# Patch RoleBinding with JSON
kubectl patch rolebinding skills-manager-binding -n anihpj --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/labels/environment", "value": "production"}
]'
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/skills-manager-binding patched
Example 2: Add Subject to User RoleBinding

This example adds a new subject to the User RoleBinding.

# Add subject using strategic merge
kubectl patch rolebinding user-reader-binding -n anihpj --type='merge' -p='
{
  "subjects": [
    {
      "kind": "Group",
      "name": "user-viewers",
      "apiGroup": "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    },
    {
      "kind": "User",
      "name": "viewer@anihpj.com",
      "apiGroup": "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"
    }
  ]
}'
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/user-reader-binding patched
Example 3: Patch Author RoleBinding Annotations

This example adds annotations to the Author RoleBinding.

# Patch annotations
kubectl patch rolebinding author-editor-binding -n anihpj --type='merge' -p='
{
  "metadata": {
    "annotations": {
      "description": "Author model editor permissions",
      "managed-by": "anihpj-controller"
    }
  }
}'
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/author-editor-binding patched
Example 4: Update Location RoleBinding Subject

This example replaces the subject in Location RoleBinding using JSON patch.

# Replace subject with JSON patch
kubectl patch rolebinding location-admin-binding -n anihpj --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/subjects/0/name", "value": "location-service-account-v2"}
]'
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/location-admin-binding patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost RoleBinding with Multiple Operations

This example applies multiple patch operations to JobPost RoleBinding.

# Multiple patch operations
kubectl patch rolebinding jobpost-publisher-binding -n anihpj --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/labels/tier", "value": "backend"},
  {"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/annotations/updated", "value": "2025-01-10"}
]'
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/jobpost-publisher-binding patched
delete - delete a RoleBinding
HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/rolebindings/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the RoleBinding
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • body: DeleteOptions
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - gracePeriodSeconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): boolean
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - propagationPolicy
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 202 (Status): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills RoleBinding

This example deletes the Skills manager RoleBinding.

# Delete RoleBinding
kubectl delete rolebinding skills-manager-binding -n anihpj
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "skills-manager-binding" deleted
Example 2: Delete User RoleBinding with Grace Period

This example deletes the User reader RoleBinding with a grace period.

# Delete with grace period
kubectl delete rolebinding user-reader-binding -n anihpj --grace-period=30
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "user-reader-binding" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author RoleBinding Immediately

This example forcefully deletes the Author editor RoleBinding without waiting.

# Force delete immediately
kubectl delete rolebinding author-editor-binding -n anihpj --grace-period=0 --force
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that objects are deleted rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "author-editor-binding" force deleted
Example 4: Delete Location RoleBinding with Dry-Run

This example simulates deletion of Location admin RoleBinding.

# Dry-run delete
kubectl delete rolebinding location-admin-binding -n anihpj --dry-run=client
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "location-admin-binding" deleted (dry run)
Example 5: Delete JobPost RoleBinding from YAML

This example deletes the JobPost RoleBinding using a YAML file.

# Delete from file
cat <<EOF | kubectl delete -f -
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: jobpost-publisher-binding
  namespace: anihpj
EOF
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "jobpost-publisher-binding" deleted
deletecollection - delete collection of RoleBinding
HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/rolebindings
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • body: DeleteOptions
  • continue (in query): string - continue
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - fieldSelector
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - gracePeriodSeconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): boolean
  • labelSelector (in query): string - labelSelector
  • limit (in query): integer - limit
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - propagationPolicy
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resourceVersion
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query): string - resourceVersionMatch
  • sendInitialEvents (in query): boolean - sendInitialEvents
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeoutSeconds
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All Skills RoleBindings

This example deletes all RoleBindings with the Skills model label.

# Delete RoleBindings by label
kubectl delete rolebindings -n anihpj -l model=skills
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "skills-manager-binding" deleted rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "skills-viewer-binding" deleted rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "skills-editor-binding" deleted
Example 2: Delete Test RoleBindings

This example deletes all RoleBindings labeled as test environment.

# Delete test RoleBindings
kubectl delete rolebindings -n anihpj -l environment=test
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "test-user-binding" deleted rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "test-author-binding" deleted
Example 3: Delete Temporary RoleBindings

This example deletes all temporary RoleBindings in the namespace.

# Delete temporary RoleBindings
kubectl delete rolebindings -n anihpj -l temporary=true
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "temp-location-binding" deleted rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "temp-jobpost-binding" deleted
Example 4: Delete RoleBindings with Field Selector

This example deletes RoleBindings using field selector.

# Delete with field selector
kubectl delete rolebindings -n anihpj --field-selector metadata.name=author-viewer-binding
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "author-viewer-binding" deleted
Example 5: Dry-Run Delete Collection

This example simulates bulk deletion of JobPost RoleBindings.

# Dry-run delete collection
kubectl delete rolebindings -n anihpj -l app=jobpost --dry-run=client
rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "jobpost-publisher-binding" deleted (dry run) rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "jobpost-approver-binding" deleted (dry run) rolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "jobpost-viewer-binding" deleted (dry run)

ClusterRoleBinding

ClusterRoleBinding Overview

ClusterRoleBindingList - Collection of ClusterRoleBindings
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: ClusterRoleBinding v1 API | API Version: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1

ClusterRoleBinding references a ClusterRole and grants cluster-wide permissions to subjects (users, groups, or service accounts).

Full Description

ClusterRoleBinding is a cluster-scoped resource that binds a ClusterRole to subjects (users, groups, or service accounts), granting them the permissions defined in the ClusterRole across the entire cluster. Unlike RoleBinding which is namespace-scoped, ClusterRoleBinding applies permissions cluster-wide, making it suitable for admin users, system components, or services that need to access resources across all namespaces. The ClusterRoleBinding references a ClusterRole by name and lists the subjects that should receive those permissions.

In your anihpj Django application, ClusterRoleBindings are essential for granting cluster-wide permissions to your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost service accounts. For example, you might create a ClusterRoleBinding that grants the Skills service account permission to read ConfigMaps across all namespaces, or allows the JobPost service to manage persistent volumes cluster-wide. This enables your Django services to operate seamlessly across namespace boundaries when needed.

Syntax

# Create a ClusterRoleBinding from YAML file
kubectl create -f clusterrolebinding.yaml
# Create a ClusterRoleBinding imperatively
kubectl create clusterrolebinding {name} --clusterrole={role} --serviceaccount={namespace}:{sa-name}
# List all ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings
# List all ClusterRoleBindings (short form)
kubectl get crb
# Get detailed information about a specific ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl describe clusterrolebinding {name}
# Get detailed information about a specific ClusterRoleBinding (short form)
kubectl describe crb {name}
# Get ClusterRoleBinding in YAML format
kubectl get clusterrolebinding {name} -o yaml
# Delete a specific ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding {name}
# Delete a specific ClusterRoleBinding (short form)
kubectl delete crb {name}

YAML Structure

apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-cluster-admin
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-skills-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: cluster-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version (rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1)
  • kind: string - Resource type (ClusterRoleBinding)
  • metadata: ObjectMeta - Standard object's metadata including name, labels, annotations
  • subjects: []Subject - List of subjects (users, groups, service accounts) to bind the ClusterRole to
  • subjects.kind: string, required - Kind of subject (User, Group, ServiceAccount)
  • subjects.name: string, required - Name of the subject
  • subjects.namespace: string - Namespace of the subject (required for ServiceAccount)
  • subjects.apiGroup: string - API group of the subject (defaults to "" for ServiceAccount)
  • roleRef: RoleRef, required - Reference to the ClusterRole to bind
  • roleRef.kind: string, required - Must be "ClusterRole"
  • roleRef.name: string, required - Name of the ClusterRole
  • roleRef.apiGroup: string, required - Must be "rbac.authorization.k8s.io"

Return Value

  • 200 (ClusterRoleBinding): OK - Request successful, ClusterRoleBinding returned
  • 201 (ClusterRoleBinding): Created - ClusterRoleBinding successfully created
  • 202 (ClusterRoleBinding): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Bind Skills Service Account to ConfigMap Reader ClusterRole

This example creates a ClusterRoleBinding granting Skills service cluster-wide ConfigMap read access.

# skills-configmap-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-configmap-reader
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    component: rbac
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-skills-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-skills-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create ClusterRoleBinding for Skills service
kubectl create -f skills-configmap-binding.yaml
# Verify the ClusterRoleBinding was created
kubectl get clusterrolebinding anihpj-skills-configmap-reader
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-configmap-reader created NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-skills-configmap-reader ClusterRole/anihpj-skills-reader 5s
Example 2: Bind User Service Account to Deployment Manager ClusterRole

This example shows binding User service account to manage deployments cluster-wide.

# user-deployment-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-deployment-manager
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    environment: production
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-user-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-user-manager
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create ClusterRoleBinding for User service
kubectl create -f user-deployment-binding.yaml
# Get detailed information about the ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl describe clusterrolebinding anihpj-user-deployment-manager
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-user-deployment-manager created Name: anihpj-user-deployment-manager Labels: app=anihpj environment=production model=user Role: Kind: ClusterRole Name: anihpj-user-manager Subjects: Kind Name Namespace ---- ---- --------- ServiceAccount anihpj-user-sa default
Example 3: Bind Multiple Authors to Aggregated ClusterRole

This example demonstrates binding multiple Author service accounts to an aggregated ClusterRole.

# author-multi-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-multi-access
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    type: multi-binding
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-author-sa
  namespace: default
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-author-readonly-sa
  namespace: jobpost
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-author-aggregated
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create multi-subject ClusterRoleBinding for Author service
kubectl create -f author-multi-binding.yaml
# Get ClusterRoleBinding details in YAML format
kubectl get clusterrolebinding anihpj-author-multi-access -o yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-author-multi-access created apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: ClusterRoleBinding metadata: name: anihpj-author-multi-access labels: app: anihpj model: author type: multi-binding subjects: - kind: ServiceAccount name: anihpj-author-sa namespace: default - kind: ServiceAccount name: anihpj-author-readonly-sa namespace: jobpost roleRef: kind: ClusterRole name: anihpj-author-aggregated apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
Example 4: Bind Location Service to Persistent Volume Admin ClusterRole

This example creates a ClusterRoleBinding for Location service to manage persistent volumes.

# location-pv-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-pv-admin
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    component: storage
  annotations:
    description: "Location service persistent volume management"
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-location-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-location-pv-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create ClusterRoleBinding for Location service
kubectl create -f location-pv-binding.yaml
# List ClusterRoleBindings for Location model
kubectl get clusterrolebinding -l model=location
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-location-pv-admin created NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-location-pv-admin ClusterRole/anihpj-location-pv-admin 3s
Example 5: Bind JobPost Service to API Discovery ClusterRole

This example shows binding JobPost service account to API discovery ClusterRole with User and Group subjects.

# jobpost-api-discovery-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    security: api-access
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-jobpost-sa
  namespace: default
- kind: User
  name: jobpost-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
- kind: Group
  name: anihpj-developers
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create ClusterRoleBinding with multiple subject types for JobPost service
kubectl create -f jobpost-api-discovery-binding.yaml
# Get detailed information about the ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl describe clusterrolebinding anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery created Name: anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery Labels: app=anihpj model=jobpost security=api-access Role: Kind: ClusterRole Name: anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery Subjects: Kind Name Namespace ---- ---- --------- ServiceAccount anihpj-jobpost-sa default User jobpost-admin Group anihpj-developers

ClusterRoleBindingList

Official Docs: ClusterRoleBindingList v1 API | API Version: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1

ClusterRoleBindingList is a collection of ClusterRoleBinding objects returned by list operations.

Full Description

ClusterRoleBindingList represents a collection of ClusterRoleBindings in your Kubernetes cluster. When you query for multiple ClusterRoleBindings using kubectl or the Kubernetes API, the response is returned as a ClusterRoleBindingList object. This is particularly useful for auditing cluster-wide permissions across all services in your anihpj Django application, allowing you to view and manage ClusterRoleBindings for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost service accounts simultaneously.

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version (rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1)
  • kind: string - Resource type (ClusterRoleBindingList)
  • metadata: ListMeta - Standard list metadata including resourceVersion and continue token
  • items: []ClusterRoleBinding, required - Array of ClusterRoleBinding objects

Return Value

  • 200 (ClusterRoleBindingList): OK - Request successful, list of ClusterRoleBindings returned
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django List Examples

Example 1: List All Skills Model ClusterRoleBindings

This example demonstrates listing all ClusterRoleBindings for the Skills model in the anihpj app.

# List all Skills ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=skills
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-skills-configmap-reader ClusterRole/anihpj-skills-reader 5m anihpj-skills-monitor-binding ClusterRole/anihpj-skills-monitor 3m
Example 2: List User Model ClusterRoleBindings with Details

This example shows listing User model ClusterRoleBindings with detailed output.

# List User ClusterRoleBindings with wide output
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=user -o wide
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-user-deployment-manager ClusterRole/anihpj-user-manager 10m anihpj-user-admin-binding ClusterRole/cluster-admin 8m
Example 3: List Author ClusterRoleBindings by App Label

This example illustrates listing Author model ClusterRoleBindings using app label selector.

# List Author ClusterRoleBindings by app
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l app=anihpj,model=author
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-author-multi-access ClusterRole/anihpj-author-aggregated 15m anihpj-author-readonly ClusterRole/anihpj-author-reader 12m
Example 4: List Location ClusterRoleBindings with JSON Output

This example demonstrates listing Location model ClusterRoleBindings in JSON format.

# List Location ClusterRoleBindings as JSON
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=location -o json
{ "apiVersion": "v1", "kind": "List", "items": [ { "apiVersion": "rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1", "kind": "ClusterRoleBinding", "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-location-pv-admin", "labels": { "app": "anihpj", "model": "location" } }, "subjects": [ { "kind": "ServiceAccount", "name": "anihpj-location-sa", "namespace": "default" } ] } ] }
Example 5: List JobPost ClusterRoleBindings with Custom Fields

This example shows listing JobPost model ClusterRoleBindings with specific field selection.

# List JobPost ClusterRoleBindings with custom fields
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=jobpost -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.roleRef.name}{"\n"}{end}'
anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery anihpj-jobpost-secret-admin anihpj-jobpost-secret-admin

ClusterRoleBinding Operations

Operations Overview

get - Read the specified ClusterRoleBinding
list - List or watch objects of kind ClusterRoleBinding
create - Create a ClusterRoleBinding
update - Replace the specified ClusterRoleBinding
patch - Partially update the specified ClusterRoleBinding
delete - Delete a ClusterRoleBinding
deletecollection - Delete collection of ClusterRoleBinding

These operations allow you to manage ClusterRoleBinding resources in your Kubernetes cluster through the Kubernetes API.

get - Read the specified ClusterRoleBinding

HTTP Request: GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterrolebindings/{name} | kubectl: kubectl get clusterrolebinding {name}
Full Description

The get operation retrieves a specific ClusterRoleBinding resource by name from your Kubernetes cluster. This operation is essential for inspecting cluster-wide role binding configurations and understanding which subjects have been granted which permissions. When you need to verify or troubleshoot cluster access permissions for your anihpj Django services, this operation provides detailed information about the binding relationship between ClusterRoles and subjects.

Syntax
# Get specific ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl get clusterrolebinding {name}
# Get with output format
kubectl get clusterrolebinding {name} -o yaml
Parameters Description
  • name: string, required - Name of the ClusterRoleBinding to retrieve
  • pretty: string - Pretty-print the output
  • exact: boolean - Exact resource version match
  • export: boolean - Export the object stripped of cluster-specific information
Return Value
  • 200 (ClusterRoleBinding): OK - ClusterRoleBinding object retrieved successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
  • 404: Not Found - ClusterRoleBinding with specified name does not exist
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Service ClusterRoleBinding

Retrieve the Skills model ClusterRoleBinding for configuration review.

# Get Skills ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl get clusterrolebinding anihpj-skills-configmap-reader
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-skills-configmap-reader ClusterRole/anihpj-skills-reader 2h
Example 2: Get User Service ClusterRoleBinding with YAML Output

Retrieve detailed YAML configuration for User model ClusterRoleBinding.

# Get User ClusterRoleBinding details
kubectl get clusterrolebinding anihpj-user-deployment-manager -o yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: ClusterRoleBinding metadata: name: anihpj-user-deployment-manager labels: app: anihpj model: user subjects: - kind: ServiceAccount name: anihpj-user-sa namespace: default roleRef: kind: ClusterRole name: anihpj-user-manager apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
Example 3: Get Author Service ClusterRoleBinding with JSON Output

Retrieve Author model ClusterRoleBinding in JSON format for API integration.

# Get Author ClusterRoleBinding as JSON
kubectl get clusterrolebinding anihpj-author-multi-access -o json
{ "apiVersion": "rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1", "kind": "ClusterRoleBinding", "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-author-multi-access", "labels": { "app": "anihpj", "model": "author" } }, "subjects": [ { "kind": "ServiceAccount", "name": "anihpj-author-sa", "namespace": "default" } ] }
Example 4: Get Location Service ClusterRoleBinding with Custom Fields

Get specific fields from Location model ClusterRoleBinding using jsonpath.

# Get Location ClusterRoleBinding subjects
kubectl get clusterrolebinding anihpj-location-pv-admin -o jsonpath='{.subjects[*].name}'
anihpj-location-sa
Example 5: Get JobPost Service ClusterRoleBinding and Describe

Retrieve and describe JobPost model ClusterRoleBinding for detailed analysis.

# Get and describe JobPost ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl get clusterrolebinding anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery
kubectl describe clusterrolebinding anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery ClusterRole/anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery 1h Name: anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery Labels: app=anihpj model=jobpost Role: Kind: ClusterRole Name: anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery Subjects: Kind Name Namespace ---- ---- --------- ServiceAccount anihpj-jobpost-sa default User jobpost-admin Group anihpj-developers

list - List or watch objects of kind ClusterRoleBinding

HTTP Request: GET /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterrolebindings | kubectl: kubectl get clusterrolebindings
Full Description

The list operation retrieves all ClusterRoleBinding resources in your Kubernetes cluster or watches for changes to ClusterRoleBindings. This is crucial for cluster-wide permission audits and monitoring role binding changes across your entire anihpj Django application infrastructure. You can filter results using label selectors to focus on specific service accounts or models in your application.

Syntax
# List all ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings
# List with label selector
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l app=anihpj
# Watch for changes
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -w
Parameters Description
  • labelSelector: string - Selector to restrict the list of returned objects by their labels
  • fieldSelector: string - Selector to restrict the list of returned objects by their fields
  • watch: boolean - Watch for changes to the described resources
  • limit: integer - Maximum number of responses to return for a list call
  • continue: string - Token for retrieving the next chunk of results
Return Value
  • 200 (ClusterRoleBindingList): OK - List of ClusterRoleBindings returned successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Skills Model ClusterRoleBindings

List all ClusterRoleBindings associated with the Skills model in anihpj app.

# List Skills ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=skills
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-skills-configmap-reader ClusterRole/anihpj-skills-reader 2h anihpj-skills-monitor-binding ClusterRole/anihpj-skills-monitor 1h
Example 2: List User Model ClusterRoleBindings with Wide Output

List User model ClusterRoleBindings with additional information display.

# List User ClusterRoleBindings with details
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=user -o wide
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-user-deployment-manager ClusterRole/anihpj-user-manager 3h anihpj-user-admin-binding ClusterRole/cluster-admin 2h
Example 3: List All anihpj App ClusterRoleBindings

List all ClusterRoleBindings for the entire anihpj Django application.

# List all anihpj ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l app=anihpj
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-skills-configmap-reader ClusterRole/anihpj-skills-reader 2h anihpj-user-deployment-manager ClusterRole/anihpj-user-manager 3h anihpj-author-multi-access ClusterRole/anihpj-author-aggregated 1h anihpj-location-pv-admin ClusterRole/anihpj-location-pv-admin 45m anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery ClusterRole/anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery 30m
Example 4: List Location ClusterRoleBindings with Custom Output

List Location model ClusterRoleBindings with custom column output format.

# List Location ClusterRoleBindings with custom columns
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=location -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ROLE:.roleRef.name,SUBJECTS:.subjects[0].name
NAME ROLE SUBJECTS anihpj-location-pv-admin anihpj-location-pv-admin anihpj-location-sa
Example 5: Watch JobPost ClusterRoleBinding Changes

Monitor real-time changes to JobPost model ClusterRoleBindings.

# Watch JobPost ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=jobpost -w
NAME ROLE AGE anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery ClusterRole/anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery 30m anihpj-jobpost-secret-admin ClusterRole/anihpj-jobpost-secret-admin 15m # Watching for changes...

create - Create a ClusterRoleBinding

HTTP Request: POST /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterrolebindings | kubectl: kubectl create -f clusterrolebinding.yaml
Full Description

The create operation creates a new ClusterRoleBinding resource in your Kubernetes cluster, establishing cluster-wide permissions for subjects by binding them to a ClusterRole. This is essential when deploying new services in your anihpj Django application that need cluster-wide access permissions. Each ClusterRoleBinding must have a unique name and properly reference existing ClusterRoles and valid subjects.

Syntax
# Create from YAML file
kubectl create -f clusterrolebinding.yaml
# Create using kubectl create clusterrolebinding command
kubectl create clusterrolebinding {name} --clusterrole={role} --serviceaccount={namespace}:{sa-name}
Parameters Description
  • body: ClusterRoleBinding, required - ClusterRoleBinding object to create
  • dryRun: string - Dry run mode (All, Server)
  • fieldManager: string - Name of the manager associated with this operation
  • pretty: string - Pretty-print the output
Return Value
  • 201 (ClusterRoleBinding): Created - ClusterRoleBinding created successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
  • 409: Conflict - ClusterRoleBinding with same name already exists
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills Service Secret Reader ClusterRoleBinding

Create a ClusterRoleBinding granting Skills service access to read secrets cluster-wide.

# skills-secret-reader.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-secret-reader
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-skills-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-skills-secret-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create Skills secret reader ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl create -f skills-secret-reader.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-secret-reader created
Example 2: Create User Service Node Reader ClusterRoleBinding

Create ClusterRoleBinding for User service to read node information across cluster.

# Create User node reader ClusterRoleBinding directly
kubectl create clusterrolebinding anihpj-user-node-reader \
  --clusterrole=anihpj-user-node-reader \
  --serviceaccount=default:anihpj-user-sa
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-user-node-reader created
Example 3: Create Author Service Network Policy ClusterRoleBinding

Create ClusterRoleBinding allowing Author service to manage network policies.

# author-network-policy.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-network-policy
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    component: networking
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-author-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-author-network-policy
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create Author network policy ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl create -f author-network-policy.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-author-network-policy created
Example 4: Create Location Service Storage Admin ClusterRoleBinding

Create ClusterRoleBinding for Location service with storage administration permissions.

# location-storage-admin.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-storage-admin
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    permission-level: admin
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-location-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-location-storage-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create Location storage admin ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl create -f location-storage-admin.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-location-storage-admin created
Example 5: Create JobPost Service Multi-Subject ClusterRoleBinding

Create ClusterRoleBinding for JobPost service with multiple subjects including users and groups.

# jobpost-multi-subject.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-multi-subject
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    type: multi-subject
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-jobpost-sa
  namespace: default
- kind: User
  name: jobpost-maintainer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
- kind: Group
  name: jobpost-developers
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-jobpost-maintainer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Create JobPost multi-subject ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl create -f jobpost-multi-subject.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-multi-subject created

update - Replace the specified ClusterRoleBinding

HTTP Request: PUT /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterrolebindings/{name} | kubectl: kubectl replace -f clusterrolebinding.yaml
Full Description

The update operation replaces an existing ClusterRoleBinding with a new configuration. This is useful when you need to change the subjects bound to a ClusterRole or modify the ClusterRole reference in your anihpj Django application. Unlike patch operations, update replaces the entire resource, so you must provide the complete ClusterRoleBinding specification including unchanged fields.

Syntax
# Replace ClusterRoleBinding from YAML file
kubectl replace -f clusterrolebinding.yaml
# Apply updated configuration
kubectl apply -f clusterrolebinding.yaml
Parameters Description
  • name: string, required - Name of the ClusterRoleBinding to replace
  • body: ClusterRoleBinding, required - Complete ClusterRoleBinding object
  • dryRun: string - Dry run mode (All, Server)
  • fieldManager: string - Name of the manager associated with this operation
Return Value
  • 200 (ClusterRoleBinding): OK - ClusterRoleBinding updated successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
  • 404: Not Found - ClusterRoleBinding does not exist
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Service ClusterRoleBinding to Add New Subject

Update Skills ClusterRoleBinding to include additional service account as subject.

# updated-skills-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-configmap-reader
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-skills-sa
  namespace: default
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-skills-readonly-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-skills-reader
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Update Skills ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl replace -f updated-skills-binding.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-configmap-reader replaced
Example 2: Update User Service ClusterRoleBinding with New ClusterRole

Update User ClusterRoleBinding to reference a different ClusterRole with enhanced permissions.

# updated-user-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-deployment-manager
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    version: v2
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-user-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-user-enhanced-manager
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Update User ClusterRoleBinding with new role
kubectl replace -f updated-user-binding.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-user-deployment-manager replaced
Example 3: Update Author Service ClusterRoleBinding Labels

Update Author ClusterRoleBinding to modify labels and metadata.

# First get current configuration
kubectl get clusterrolebinding anihpj-author-multi-access -o yaml > author-binding-update.yaml
# Edit the file to update labels
# Then apply changes
kubectl replace -f author-binding-update.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-author-multi-access replaced
Example 4: Update Location Service ClusterRoleBinding Namespace

Update Location ClusterRoleBinding to change ServiceAccount namespace reference.

# updated-location-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-pv-admin
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    component: storage
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-location-sa
  namespace: production
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-location-pv-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Update Location ClusterRoleBinding namespace
kubectl replace -f updated-location-binding.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-location-pv-admin replaced
Example 5: Update JobPost Service ClusterRoleBinding using Apply

Update JobPost ClusterRoleBinding configuration using kubectl apply for declarative management.

# updated-jobpost-binding.yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    security: enhanced
  annotations:
    last-updated: "2024-01-15T10:30:00Z"
subjects:
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-jobpost-sa
  namespace: default
- kind: ServiceAccount
  name: anihpj-jobpost-worker-sa
  namespace: default
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
# Apply JobPost ClusterRoleBinding updates
kubectl apply -f updated-jobpost-binding.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery configured

patch - Partially update the specified ClusterRoleBinding

HTTP Request: PATCH /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterrolebindings/{name} | kubectl: kubectl patch clusterrolebinding {name}
Full Description

The patch operation allows you to partially update a ClusterRoleBinding without replacing the entire resource. This is useful for making targeted changes like adding labels, updating annotations, or modifying specific subjects in your anihpj Django application's ClusterRoleBindings. You can use strategic merge patches, JSON merge patches, or JSON patches depending on your needs.

Syntax
# Strategic merge patch
kubectl patch clusterrolebinding {name} -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"new-label":"value"}}}'
# JSON merge patch
kubectl patch clusterrolebinding {name} --type=merge -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"key":"value"}}}'
# JSON patch
kubectl patch clusterrolebinding {name} --type=json -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/labels/env", "value": "prod"}]'
Parameters Description
  • name: string, required - Name of the ClusterRoleBinding to patch
  • body: Patch, required - Patch object containing the changes
  • dryRun: string - Dry run mode (All, Server)
  • fieldManager: string - Name of the manager associated with this operation
  • force: boolean - Force apply the patch
Return Value
  • 200 (ClusterRoleBinding): OK - ClusterRoleBinding patched successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
  • 404: Not Found - ClusterRoleBinding does not exist
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Service ClusterRoleBinding to Add Label

Add environment label to Skills ClusterRoleBinding using strategic merge patch.

# Add environment label to Skills ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl patch clusterrolebinding anihpj-skills-configmap-reader \
  -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"environment":"production"}}}'
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-configmap-reader patched
Example 2: Patch User Service ClusterRoleBinding with Annotation

Add descriptive annotation to User ClusterRoleBinding using merge patch.

# Add annotation to User ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl patch clusterrolebinding anihpj-user-deployment-manager \
  --type=merge \
  -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"description":"User service deployment management permissions"}}}'
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-user-deployment-manager patched
Example 3: Patch Author Service ClusterRoleBinding using JSON Patch

Add version label to Author ClusterRoleBinding using JSON patch operation.

# Add version label using JSON patch
kubectl patch clusterrolebinding anihpj-author-multi-access \
  --type=json \
  -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/labels/version", "value": "v1.2"}]'
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-author-multi-access patched
Example 4: Patch Location Service ClusterRoleBinding Multiple Labels

Add multiple labels to Location ClusterRoleBinding in a single patch operation.

# Add multiple labels to Location ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl patch clusterrolebinding anihpj-location-pv-admin \
  -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"tier":"storage","criticality":"high","team":"platform"}}}'
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-location-pv-admin patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost Service ClusterRoleBinding from File

Apply patch to JobPost ClusterRoleBinding from a patch file for complex updates.

# jobpost-patch.yaml
metadata:
  labels:
    monitoring: enabled
    backup: daily
  annotations:
    last-audit: "2024-01-15"
    compliance: "SOC2"
# Apply patch from file
kubectl patch clusterrolebinding anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery \
  --patch-file jobpost-patch.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-api-discovery patched

delete - Delete a ClusterRoleBinding

HTTP Request: DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterrolebindings/{name} | kubectl: kubectl delete clusterrolebinding {name}
Full Description

The delete operation removes a ClusterRoleBinding from your Kubernetes cluster, immediately revoking the cluster-wide permissions granted to the bound subjects. This is critical for security management in your anihpj Django application when service accounts no longer need specific permissions or when decommissioning services. Always verify the impact before deleting ClusterRoleBindings as it can immediately affect running services.

Syntax
# Delete specific ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding {name}
# Delete with confirmation
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding {name} --wait=true
# Delete from file
kubectl delete -f clusterrolebinding.yaml
Parameters Description
  • name: string, required - Name of the ClusterRoleBinding to delete
  • gracePeriodSeconds: integer - Duration in seconds before forcefully terminating
  • propagationPolicy: string - How to handle dependent objects (Foreground, Background, Orphan)
  • dryRun: string - Dry run mode (All, Server)
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - ClusterRoleBinding deleted successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
  • 404: Not Found - ClusterRoleBinding does not exist
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Service Development ClusterRoleBinding

Remove development ClusterRoleBinding for Skills service when moving to production.

# Delete Skills development ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding anihpj-skills-dev-access
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-dev-access" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Service ClusterRoleBinding with Confirmation

Delete User service ClusterRoleBinding with explicit wait confirmation.

# Delete User ClusterRoleBinding with wait
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding anihpj-user-temp-access --wait=true
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-user-temp-access" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author Service ClusterRoleBinding from File

Delete Author service ClusterRoleBinding using the original YAML file.

# Delete Author ClusterRoleBinding from file
kubectl delete -f author-network-policy.yaml
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-author-network-policy" deleted
Example 4: Delete Location Service ClusterRoleBinding with Dry Run

Test deletion of Location service ClusterRoleBinding without actual removal.

# Dry run delete Location ClusterRoleBinding
kubectl delete clusterrolebinding anihpj-location-test-binding --dry-run=server
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-location-test-binding" deleted (dry run)
Example 5: Delete Multiple JobPost Service ClusterRoleBindings by Label

Delete multiple JobPost service ClusterRoleBindings using label selector.

# Delete multiple JobPost ClusterRoleBindings by label
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l model=jobpost,environment=staging
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-staging-access" deleted clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-staging-monitor" deleted

deletecollection - Delete collection of ClusterRoleBinding

HTTP Request: DELETE /apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterrolebindings | kubectl: kubectl delete clusterrolebindings --selector={selector}
Full Description

The deletecollection operation removes multiple ClusterRoleBindings based on label selectors or field selectors in a single API call. This is extremely powerful for bulk cleanup operations in your anihpj Django application, such as removing all ClusterRoleBindings for a specific environment or model. Use this operation carefully as it can revoke permissions for multiple services simultaneously, potentially affecting cluster operations.

Syntax
# Delete collection by label selector
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l environment=staging
# Delete collection by multiple labels
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l app=anihpj,tier=test
# Delete all ClusterRoleBindings (dangerous!)
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings --all
Parameters Description
  • labelSelector: string - Selector to restrict deletion by labels
  • fieldSelector: string - Selector to restrict deletion by fields
  • gracePeriodSeconds: integer - Duration before forcefully terminating
  • propagationPolicy: string - How to handle dependent objects
  • dryRun: string - Dry run mode (All, Server)
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - Collection deleted successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All Skills Service Test ClusterRoleBindings

Remove all test environment ClusterRoleBindings for the Skills model.

# Delete all Skills test ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l model=skills,environment=test
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-access" deleted clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-monitor" deleted
Example 2: Delete All User Service Development ClusterRoleBindings

Clean up all development ClusterRoleBindings for the User model using dry run first.

# Dry run delete User development ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l model=user,environment=dev --dry-run=server
# Execute actual deletion
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l model=user,environment=dev
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-user-dev-access" deleted (dry run) clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-user-dev-debug" deleted (dry run) clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-user-dev-access" deleted clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-user-dev-debug" deleted
Example 3: Delete All Author Service Temporary ClusterRoleBindings

Remove all temporary ClusterRoleBindings for the Author model based on tier label.

# Delete temporary Author ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l model=author,tier=temporary
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-author-temp-reader" deleted clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-author-temp-writer" deleted clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-author-temp-monitor" deleted
Example 4: Delete All Location Service Version-Specific ClusterRoleBindings

Clean up old version ClusterRoleBindings for the Location model during version upgrade.

# Delete old version Location ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l model=location,version=v1.0
clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-location-v1-storage" deleted clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-location-v1-network" deleted
Example 5: Delete All JobPost Service Non-Production ClusterRoleBindings

Remove all non-production ClusterRoleBindings for JobPost model during production deployment.

# List what would be deleted
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -l model=jobpost,environment!=production
# Delete non-production JobPost ClusterRoleBindings
kubectl delete clusterrolebindings -l model=jobpost,environment!=production
NAME AGE anihpj-jobpost-dev-access 2d anihpj-jobpost-staging-monitor 1d anihpj-jobpost-test-debug 6h clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-dev-access" deleted clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-staging-monitor" deleted clusterrolebinding.rbac.authorization.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-test-debug" deleted

📜 Policy Resources

Policy resources for managing API priority and fairness in Kubernetes

FlowSchema

FlowSchema Overview

FlowSchemaSpec - Specification of FlowSchema
FlowSchemaStatus - Current status of FlowSchema
FlowSchemaList - Collection of FlowSchemas
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: FlowSchema v1 API | API Version: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1

FlowSchema defines the schema of a group of flows.

Full Description

FlowSchema defines the schema of a group of flows, enabling fine-grained control over API priority and fairness in Kubernetes. A flow is made up of a set of inbound API requests with similar attributes and is identified by a pair of strings: the name of the FlowSchema and a "flow distinguisher". This resource is essential for managing API request priorities in your anihpj Django application, ensuring that critical operations from your jobpost app components receive appropriate priority.

In production Django environments, FlowSchemas allow you to categorize and prioritize requests based on the subject making the request (ServiceAccounts, Users, Groups) and the resources being accessed. For example, you can give high priority to Skills model updates while assigning lower priority to bulk JobPost queries. This ensures your User authentication services maintain responsiveness even under heavy load from Author content creation or Location geocoding operations.

Syntax

# Create a FlowSchema from YAML file
kubectl create -f flowschema.yaml
# Apply a FlowSchema configuration
kubectl apply -f flowschema.yaml
# List all FlowSchemas
kubectl get flowschemas
# Get detailed information about a specific FlowSchema
kubectl describe flowschema {name}
# Get FlowSchema in YAML format
kubectl get flowschema {name} -o yaml
# Get FlowSchema in JSON format
kubectl get flowschema {name} -o json
# Delete a specific FlowSchema
kubectl delete flowschema {name}

YAML Structure

apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: anihpj-flow
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: api-priority
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 500
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: anihpj-skills-sa
        namespace: default
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["pods", "configmaps"]
      verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
status:
  conditions:
  - type: Ready
    status: "True"
    lastTransitionTime: "2025-11-10T10:00:00Z"
    reason: FlowSchemaInEffect
    message: This FlowSchema is in effect

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1" for FlowSchema
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "FlowSchema"
  • metadata: ObjectMeta - Standard object metadata including name, labels, and annotations for organizing FlowSchemas in your anihpj application
  • spec: FlowSchemaSpec - Specification of the desired behavior of the FlowSchema
    • distinguisherMethod: FlowDistinguisherMethod - Defines how to compute the flow distinguisher for requests matching this schema
    • matchingPrecedence: integer - Used to choose among FlowSchemas that match a request. Lower values have higher priority. Range: 1-10000
    • priorityLevelConfiguration: PriorityLevelConfigurationReference - References the PriorityLevelConfiguration that applies to this FlowSchema
    • rules: []PolicyRulesWithSubjects - Describes which requests will match this FlowSchema based on subjects and resources
  • status: FlowSchemaStatus - Current status of the FlowSchema, including conditions indicating whether it's in effect

Return Value

  • 200 (FlowSchema): OK - Request successful, FlowSchema returned
  • 201 (FlowSchema): Created - FlowSchema successfully created
  • 202 (FlowSchema): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: FlowSchema for Skills Service Requests

This example creates a FlowSchema prioritizing requests from the Skills service account.

# Create FlowSchema for Skills requests
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: skills-service-flow
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 500
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: skills-service-account
        namespace: anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"]
      verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/skills-service-flow created
Example 2: FlowSchema for User Management API

This example creates a FlowSchema for User model API requests.

# Create FlowSchema for User API
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: user-api-flow
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByNamespace
  matchingPrecedence: 800
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-low
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: Group
      group:
        name: system:serviceaccounts:anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: ["apps"]
      resources: ["deployments"]
      namespaces: ["anihpj"]
      verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/user-api-flow created
Example 3: FlowSchema for Author Publishing Workflow

This example creates a FlowSchema for Author content publishing operations.

# Create FlowSchema for Author publishing
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: author-publish-flow
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 300
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: author-publisher
        namespace: anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: ["batch"]
      resources: ["jobs"]
      verbs: ["create", "update", "patch"]
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["pods"]
      verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/author-publish-flow created
Example 4: FlowSchema for Location Service Discovery

This example creates a FlowSchema for Location service discovery requests.

# Create FlowSchema for Location discovery
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: location-discovery-flow
  labels:
    app: location
    model: location
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 1000
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: exempt
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: location-service
        namespace: anihpj
    nonResourceRules:
    - nonResourceURLs: ["/api/v1/locations/*"]
      verbs: ["get"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/location-discovery-flow created
Example 5: FlowSchema for JobPost Batch Processing

This example creates a FlowSchema for JobPost batch job processing with high priority.

# Create FlowSchema for JobPost batch jobs
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: jobpost-batch-flow
  annotations:
    description: "Priority flow for JobPost batch processing"
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 200
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: jobpost-batch-processor
        namespace: anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: ["batch"]
      resources: ["jobs", "cronjobs"]
      verbs: ["create", "update", "patch", "delete"]
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
      verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/jobpost-batch-flow created

FlowSchemaSpec

Full Description

FlowSchemaSpec describes how the FlowSchema's specification looks like, defining the matching rules and priority configuration for API request flows. In your anihpj Django application, this specification determines how requests from different services (Skills, User, Author, Location, JobPost) are categorized and prioritized.

The spec includes the distinguisher method (how to group similar requests), matching precedence (priority order), priority level configuration reference, and matching rules based on subjects and resources. This allows fine-grained control over which requests from your jobpost app components receive higher or lower priority in the cluster.

Syntax

# View FlowSchemaSpec in a FlowSchema
kubectl get flowschema {name} -o jsonpath='{.spec}'
# View FlowSchemaSpec in YAML format
kubectl get flowschema {name} -o yaml | grep -A 20 "spec:"
# Describe FlowSchema to see spec details
kubectl describe flowschema {name}

Parameters Description

  • distinguisherMethod (FlowDistinguisherMethod): defines how to compute the flow distinguisher for requests that match this schema
    • type (string), required: type of flow distinguisher method. Supported types are "ByUser" and "ByNamespace"
  • matchingPrecedence (int32): used to choose among the FlowSchemas that match a given request. Values must be ranged in [1,10000]. Default: 1000
  • priorityLevelConfiguration (PriorityLevelConfigurationReference), required: should reference a PriorityLevelConfiguration in the cluster
    • name (string), required: name of the priority level configuration being referenced
  • rules ([]PolicyRulesWithSubjects): describes which requests will match this flow schema
    • subjects ([]Subject), required: list of normal user, serviceaccount, or group
    • resourceRules ([]ResourcePolicyRule): slice of ResourcePolicyRules
    • nonResourceRules ([]NonResourcePolicyRule): list of NonResourcePolicyRules

Return Value

  • FlowSchemaSpec object: Returns the specification portion of a FlowSchema

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: FlowSchemaSpec for Skills Service

This example shows a FlowSchemaSpec prioritizing Skills model requests.

spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 500
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: anihpj-skills-sa
        namespace: default
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["configmaps"]
      verbs: ["get", "list"]
# View the spec
kubectl get flowschema skills-flow -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq
{"distinguisherMethod":{"type":"ByUser"},"matchingPrecedence":500,"priorityLevelConfiguration":{"name":"workload-high"},"rules":[{"resourceRules":[{"apiGroups":[""],"resources":["configmaps"],"verbs":["get","list"]}],"subjects":[{"kind":"ServiceAccount","serviceAccount":{"name":"anihpj-skills-sa","namespace":"default"}}]}]}
Example 2: FlowSchemaSpec for User Authentication

This example demonstrates a FlowSchemaSpec for User model authentication flows.

spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByNamespace
  matchingPrecedence: 300
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: anihpj-user-sa
        namespace: default
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["secrets"]
      verbs: ["get"]
# Describe to see spec details
kubectl describe flowschema user-auth-flow
Name: user-auth-flow API Version: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1 Kind: FlowSchema Spec: Distinguisher Method: Type: ByNamespace Matching Precedence: 300 Priority Level Configuration: Name: workload-high Rules: Resource Rules: API Groups: Resources: secrets Verbs: get
Example 3: FlowSchemaSpec for Author Publishing

This example illustrates a FlowSchemaSpec for Author content publishing operations.

spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 400
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-medium
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: Group
      group:
        name: system:serviceaccounts:anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: ["apps"]
      resources: ["deployments"]
      verbs: ["update", "patch"]
# Get spec in YAML format
kubectl get flowschema author-publish-flow -o yaml | grep -A 15 "spec:"
spec: distinguisherMethod: type: ByUser matchingPrecedence: 400 priorityLevelConfiguration: name: workload-medium rules: - resourceRules: - apiGroups: - apps resources: - deployments verbs: - update - patch
Example 4: FlowSchemaSpec for Location Geocoding

This example shows a FlowSchemaSpec for Location model geocoding services.

spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 700
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-low
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: anihpj-location-sa
        namespace: default
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["pods"]
      verbs: ["list", "watch"]
# Extract just the matchingPrecedence
kubectl get flowschema location-geocode-flow -o jsonpath='{.spec.matchingPrecedence}'
700
Example 5: FlowSchemaSpec for JobPost Queries

This example demonstrates a FlowSchemaSpec for JobPost model bulk query operations.

spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByNamespace
  matchingPrecedence: 900
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: exempt
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: anihpj-jobpost-sa
        namespace: default
    nonResourceRules:
    - verbs: ["get"]
      nonResourceURLs: ["/healthz", "/livez"]
# View priorityLevelConfiguration name
kubectl get flowschema jobpost-queries-flow -o jsonpath='{.spec.priorityLevelConfiguration.name}'
exempt

FlowSchemaStatus

Full Description

FlowSchemaStatus represents the current state of a FlowSchema, providing runtime information about whether the flow schema is active and being enforced by the API server. In your anihpj Django application, monitoring the status helps ensure your priority configurations for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services are properly applied.

The status includes a conditions array that tracks the FlowSchema's operational state. The primary condition type is "Dangling" which indicates whether the referenced PriorityLevelConfiguration exists. A healthy FlowSchema will show conditions confirming it's in effect and properly configured for your jobpost app's API priority management.

Syntax

# View FlowSchemaStatus
kubectl get flowschema {name} -o jsonpath='{.status}'
# View status conditions
kubectl get flowschema {name} -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*]}'
# Check if FlowSchema is active
kubectl describe flowschema {name} | grep -A 5 "Status:"

Parameters Description

  • conditions ([]FlowSchemaCondition): list of the current states of FlowSchema
    • type (string): type of the condition
    • status (string): status of the condition. Can be True, False, Unknown
    • lastTransitionTime (Time): last time the condition transitioned from one status to another
    • reason (string): unique, one-word, CamelCase reason for the condition's last transition
    • message (string): human-readable message indicating details about last transition

Return Value

  • FlowSchemaStatus object: Returns the status portion of a FlowSchema with current conditions

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check Skills Service FlowSchema Status

This example shows how to verify the status of a Skills service FlowSchema.

# Get FlowSchema status
kubectl get flowschema skills-flow -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{"conditions":[{"lastTransitionTime":"2025-11-10T10:00:00Z","message":"This FlowSchema is in effect","reason":"FlowSchemaInEffect","status":"True","type":"Ready"}]}
Example 2: Monitor User Authentication FlowSchema Conditions

This example demonstrates checking the conditions array for User model FlowSchema.

# View all status conditions
kubectl get flowschema user-auth-flow -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*]}' | jq
{"lastTransitionTime":"2025-11-10T09:30:00Z","message":"This FlowSchema references the PriorityLevelConfiguration object named 'workload-high' and it exists","reason":"NotDangling","status":"False","type":"Dangling"}
Example 3: Verify Author Publishing FlowSchema Is Active

This example illustrates how to confirm an Author service FlowSchema is properly configured.

# Describe to see full status
kubectl describe flowschema author-publish-flow | grep -A 10 "Status:"
Status: Conditions: Last Transition Time: 2025-11-10T08:45:00Z Message: This FlowSchema is in effect Reason: FlowSchemaInEffect Status: True Type: Ready Last Transition Time: 2025-11-10T08:45:00Z Message: This FlowSchema references the PriorityLevelConfiguration object named 'workload-medium' and it exists Reason: NotDangling Status: False
Example 4: Check Location Geocoding FlowSchema Transition Time

This example shows how to extract the last transition time from FlowSchema status.

# Get last transition time
kubectl get flowschema location-geocode-flow -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].lastTransitionTime}'
2025-11-10T07:20:00Z
Example 5: Verify JobPost Queries FlowSchema Health

This example demonstrates checking if a JobPost FlowSchema's referenced PriorityLevel exists.

# Check for Dangling condition
kubectl get flowschema jobpost-queries-flow -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Dangling")].status}'
False

FlowSchemaList

FlowSchemaList is a list of FlowSchema objects

YAML Structure

apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchemaList
metadata: ListMeta
items: []FlowSchema

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1" for FlowSchemaList
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "FlowSchemaList"
  • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata including resourceVersion and continue token for pagination
  • items ([]FlowSchema), required: List of FlowSchema objects in the collection

Return Value

  • 200 (FlowSchemaList): OK - Request successful, FlowSchemaList returned
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: List All FlowSchemas

This example lists all FlowSchemas in the cluster.

# List all FlowSchemas
kubectl get flowschemas
NAME PRIORITYLEVEL MATCHINGPRECEDENCE DISTINGUISHERMETHOD AGE skills-service-flow workload-high 500 ByUser 5d user-api-flow workload-low 800 ByNamespace 3d author-publish-flow workload-high 300 ByUser 2d location-discovery-flow exempt 1000 ByUser 1d jobpost-batch-flow workload-high 200 ByUser 12h
Example 2: List FlowSchemas with Wide Output

This example lists FlowSchemas with additional details.

# List FlowSchemas with wide output
kubectl get flowschemas -o wide
NAME PRIORITYLEVEL MATCHINGPRECEDENCE DISTINGUISHERMETHOD AGE CONDITIONS skills-service-flow workload-high 500 ByUser 5d Ready user-api-flow workload-low 800 ByNamespace 3d Ready author-publish-flow workload-high 300 ByUser 2d Ready
Example 3: Get FlowSchemas in YAML Format

This example retrieves all FlowSchemas in YAML format.

# Get FlowSchemas in YAML
kubectl get flowschemas -o yaml | head -30
apiVersion: v1 items: - apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1 kind: FlowSchema metadata: name: skills-service-flow spec: distinguisherMethod: type: ByUser matchingPrecedence: 500 priorityLevelConfiguration: name: workload-high status: conditions: - lastTransitionTime: "2025-01-05T10:30:00Z" status: "True" type: Ready kind: List metadata: resourceVersion: ""
Example 4: Count FlowSchemas

This example counts the total number of FlowSchemas.

# Count FlowSchemas
kubectl get flowschemas --no-headers | wc -l
5
Example 5: Watch FlowSchemas for Changes

This example watches for changes to FlowSchemas in real-time.

# Watch FlowSchemas
kubectl get flowschemas --watch
NAME PRIORITYLEVEL MATCHINGPRECEDENCE DISTINGUISHERMETHOD AGE skills-service-flow workload-high 500 ByUser 5d user-api-flow workload-low 800 ByNamespace 3d ADDED jobpost-realtime-flow workload-high 100 ByUser 0s

FlowSchema Operations

Operations Overview

get - Read the specified FlowSchema
get status - Read status of the specified FlowSchema
list - List FlowSchemas
create - Create a FlowSchema
update - Replace the specified FlowSchema
update status - Replace status of the specified FlowSchema
patch - Partially update the specified FlowSchema
patch status - Partially update status of the specified FlowSchema
delete - Delete a FlowSchema
deletecollection - Delete collection of FlowSchema

Operations allow you to interact with FlowSchema resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage API priority and fairness configurations for your anihpj Django application, ensuring optimal request handling for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

get - read the specified FlowSchema

Read details of a specific FlowSchema by name.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the FlowSchema
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (FlowSchema): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills FlowSchema

This example retrieves the Skills service FlowSchema details.

# Get FlowSchema details
kubectl get flowschema skills-service-flow -o yaml
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1 kind: FlowSchema metadata: name: skills-service-flow creationTimestamp: "2025-01-05T10:30:00Z" resourceVersion: "12345" spec: distinguisherMethod: type: ByUser matchingPrecedence: 500 priorityLevelConfiguration: name: workload-high rules: - subjects: - kind: ServiceAccount serviceAccount: name: skills-service-account namespace: anihpj resourceRules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["configmaps", "secrets"] verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"] status: conditions: - lastTransitionTime: "2025-01-05T10:30:00Z" message: This FlowSchema is in effect reason: FlowSchemaInEffect status: "True" type: Ready
Example 2: Describe User API FlowSchema

This example shows detailed information about the User API FlowSchema.

# Describe FlowSchema
kubectl describe flowschema user-api-flow
Name: user-api-flow API Version: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1 Kind: FlowSchema Metadata: Creation Timestamp: 2025-01-07T14:20:00Z Resource Version: 23456 Spec: Distinguisher Method: Type: ByNamespace Matching Precedence: 800 Priority Level Configuration: Name: workload-low Rules: Subjects: Group: Name: system:serviceaccounts:anihpj Kind: Group Resource Rules: Api Groups: apps Namespaces: anihpj Resources: deployments Verbs: get list watch Status: Conditions: Last Transition Time: 2025-01-07T14:20:00Z Message: This FlowSchema is in effect Reason: FlowSchemaInEffect Status: True Type: Ready Events:
Example 3: Get Author FlowSchema in JSON

This example retrieves the Author publishing FlowSchema in JSON format.

# Get FlowSchema in JSON
kubectl get flowschema author-publish-flow -o json | jq '.spec'
{ "distinguisherMethod": { "type": "ByUser" }, "matchingPrecedence": 300, "priorityLevelConfiguration": { "name": "workload-high" }, "rules": [ { "subjects": [ { "kind": "ServiceAccount", "serviceAccount": { "name": "author-publisher", "namespace": "anihpj" } } ], "resourceRules": [ { "apiGroups": ["batch"], "resources": ["jobs"], "verbs": ["create", "update", "patch"] } ] } ] }
Example 4: Get Location FlowSchema with Custom Columns

This example retrieves Location FlowSchema with custom output columns.

# Get with custom columns
kubectl get flowschema location-discovery-flow -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,PRIORITY:.spec.priorityLevelConfiguration.name,PRECEDENCE:.spec.matchingPrecedence
NAME PRIORITY PRECEDENCE location-discovery-flow exempt 1000
Example 5: Get JobPost FlowSchema Status

This example retrieves just the status section of the JobPost batch FlowSchema.

# Get status information
kubectl get flowschema jobpost-batch-flow -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0]}'
{"lastTransitionTime":"2025-11-09T18:45:00Z","message":"This FlowSchema is in effect","reason":"FlowSchemaInEffect","status":"True","type":"Ready"}

get status - read status of the specified FlowSchema

Read the status subresource of a specific FlowSchema.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the FlowSchema
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
Return Value
  • 200 (FlowSchema): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills FlowSchema Status

This example retrieves the status of the Skills service FlowSchema.

# Get status
kubectl get flowschema skills-service-flow --subresource=status -o yaml
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1 kind: FlowSchema metadata: name: skills-service-flow status: conditions: - lastTransitionTime: "2025-11-05T10:30:00Z" message: This FlowSchema is in effect reason: FlowSchemaInEffect status: "True" type: Ready
Example 2: Check User API FlowSchema Condition

This example checks if the User API FlowSchema is ready.

# Check ready condition
kubectl get flowschema user-api-flow -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}'
True
Example 3: Get Author FlowSchema Status Conditions

This example retrieves all status conditions for the Author publishing FlowSchema.

# Get all conditions
kubectl get flowschema author-publish-flow -o json | jq '.status.conditions'
[ { "lastTransitionTime": "2025-11-08T12:15:00Z", "message": "This FlowSchema is in effect", "reason": "FlowSchemaInEffect", "status": "True", "type": "Ready" } ]
Example 4: Monitor Location FlowSchema Status

This example continuously monitors the Location discovery FlowSchema status.

# Watch status changes
kubectl get flowschema location-discovery-flow --watch-only -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*].type}{" "}{.status.conditions[*].status}{"\n"}'
Ready True
Example 5: Get JobPost FlowSchema Status Timestamp

This example retrieves when the JobPost batch FlowSchema became ready.

# Get last transition time
kubectl get flowschema jobpost-batch-flow -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].lastTransitionTime}'
2025-11-09T18:45:00Z

list - list FlowSchemas

List FlowSchemas in the cluster with optional filtering and pagination.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): boolean - allowWatchBookmarks
  • continue (in query): string - continue
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - fieldSelector
  • labelSelector (in query): string - labelSelector
  • limit (in query): integer - limit
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resourceVersion
  • watch (in query): boolean - watch
Return Value
  • 200 (FlowSchemaList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Django FlowSchemas

This example lists all FlowSchemas for the Django anihpj application.

# List all FlowSchemas
kubectl get flowschemas
NAME PRIORITYLEVEL MATCHINGPRECEDENCE DISTINGUISHERMETHOD AGE skills-service-flow workload-high 500 ByUser 5d user-api-flow workload-low 800 ByNamespace 3d author-publish-flow workload-high 300 ByUser 2d location-discovery-flow exempt 1000 ByUser 1d jobpost-batch-flow workload-high 200 ByUser 12h
Example 2: List FlowSchemas with High Priority

This example lists only FlowSchemas using workload-high priority level.

# Filter by priority level
kubectl get flowschemas --field-selector spec.priorityLevelConfiguration.name=workload-high
NAME PRIORITYLEVEL MATCHINGPRECEDENCE DISTINGUISHERMETHOD AGE skills-service-flow workload-high 500 ByUser 5d author-publish-flow workload-high 300 ByUser 2d jobpost-batch-flow workload-high 200 ByUser 12h
Example 3: List FlowSchemas by Label Selector

This example lists FlowSchemas with specific labels for the jobpost models.

# Filter by labels
kubectl get flowschemas -l app=location
NAME PRIORITYLEVEL MATCHINGPRECEDENCE DISTINGUISHERMETHOD AGE location-discovery-flow exempt 1000 ByUser 1d
Example 4: List FlowSchemas Sorted by Precedence

This example lists FlowSchemas sorted by their matching precedence.

# Sort by precedence
kubectl get flowschemas --sort-by=.spec.matchingPrecedence
NAME PRIORITYLEVEL MATCHINGPRECEDENCE DISTINGUISHERMETHOD AGE jobpost-batch-flow workload-high 200 ByUser 12h author-publish-flow workload-high 300 ByUser 2d skills-service-flow workload-high 500 ByUser 5d user-api-flow workload-low 800 ByNamespace 3d location-discovery-flow exempt 1000 ByUser 1d
Example 5: List FlowSchemas with Custom Output

This example lists FlowSchemas with custom columns showing all Django models.

# Custom output columns
kubectl get flowschemas -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,PRIORITY:.spec.priorityLevelConfiguration.name,PRECEDENCE:.spec.matchingPrecedence,METHOD:.spec.distinguisherMethod.type
NAME PRIORITY PRECEDENCE METHOD skills-service-flow workload-high 500 ByUser user-api-flow workload-low 800 ByNamespace author-publish-flow workload-high 300 ByUser location-discovery-flow exempt 1000 ByUser jobpost-batch-flow workload-high 200 ByUser

create - create a FlowSchema

HTTP Request
POST /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas
Parameters
  • body: FlowSchema, required - FlowSchema to create
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • fieldManager (in query): string - fieldManager
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - fieldValidation
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
Return Value
  • 200 (FlowSchema): OK
  • 201 (FlowSchema): Created
  • 202 (FlowSchema): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills Analytics FlowSchema

This example creates a FlowSchema for Skills model analytics queries.

# Create FlowSchema for analytics
cat <<EOF | kubectl create -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: skills-analytics-flow
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    component: analytics
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 400
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-low
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: skills-analytics
        namespace: anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["pods", "pods/log"]
      verbs: ["get", "list"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/skills-analytics-flow created
Example 2: Create User Authentication FlowSchema

This example creates a FlowSchema for User model authentication operations.

# Create authentication FlowSchema
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: user-auth-flow
  annotations:
    description: "User authentication priority flow"
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 100
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: exempt
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: user-auth-service
        namespace: anihpj
    nonResourceRules:
    - nonResourceURLs: ["/api/v1/auth/*"]
      verbs: ["get", "post"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/user-auth-flow created
Example 3: Create Author Content Moderation FlowSchema

This example creates a FlowSchema for Author content moderation with medium priority.

# Create moderation FlowSchema
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
