apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: author-moderation-flow
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 600
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-low
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: author-moderator
        namespace: anihpj
    - kind: User
      user:
        name: moderator@anihpj.com
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: ["apps"]
      resources: ["deployments", "statefulsets"]
      verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["configmaps"]
      verbs: ["get", "update"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/author-moderation-flow created
Example 4: Create Location Geocoding FlowSchema

This example creates a FlowSchema for Location model geocoding service.

# Create geocoding FlowSchema
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: location-geocoding-flow
  labels:
    service: geocoding
    model: location
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByNamespace
  matchingPrecedence: 700
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: Group
      group:
        name: system:serviceaccounts:anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: ["batch"]
      resources: ["jobs"]
      namespaces: ["anihpj"]
      verbs: ["create", "get", "list", "delete"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/location-geocoding-flow created
Example 5: Create JobPost Notification FlowSchema

This example creates a FlowSchema for JobPost notification system with high priority.

# Create notification FlowSchema
cat <<EOF | kubectl create -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: jobpost-notification-flow
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    component: notifications
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByUser
  matchingPrecedence: 250
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: jobpost-notifier
        namespace: anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      resources: ["events"]
      verbs: ["create", "patch"]
    - apiGroups: ["apps"]
      resources: ["deployments"]
      verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/jobpost-notification-flow created

update - replace the specified FlowSchema

Replace the entire FlowSchema configuration with a new specification.

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the FlowSchema
  • body: FlowSchema, required - Updated FlowSchema object
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • fieldManager (in query): string - fieldManager
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - fieldValidation
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
Return Value
  • 200 (FlowSchema): OK
  • 201 (FlowSchema): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills FlowSchema Precedence

This example updates the matching precedence for the Skills service FlowSchema.

# Get current FlowSchema, modify, and update
kubectl get flowschema skills-service-flow -o yaml > skills-flow.yaml
# Edit the file to change matchingPrecedence from 500 to 450
sed -i 's/matchingPrecedence: 500/matchingPrecedence: 450/' skills-flow.yaml
kubectl replace -f skills-flow.yaml
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/skills-service-flow replaced
Example 2: Update User FlowSchema Priority Level

This example changes the priority level configuration for User API FlowSchema.

# Update priority level
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: user-api-flow
  resourceVersion: "23456"
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByNamespace
  matchingPrecedence: 800
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: workload-high
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: Group
      group:
        name: system:serviceaccounts:anihpj
    resourceRules:
    - apiGroups: ["apps"]
      resources: ["deployments"]
      namespaces: ["anihpj"]
      verbs: ["get", "list", "watch", "update"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/user-api-flow replaced
Example 3: Update Author FlowSchema Rules

This example adds new resource rules to the Author publishing FlowSchema.

# Add new rules to FlowSchema
kubectl get flowschema author-publish-flow -o yaml | \
  sed '/resourceRules:/a\    - apiGroups: [""]\\n      resources: ["persistentvolumeclaims"]\\n      verbs: ["get", "list"]' | \
  kubectl replace -f -
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/author-publish-flow replaced
Example 4: Update Location FlowSchema Distinguisher Method

This example changes the distinguisher method for Location discovery FlowSchema.

# Change distinguisher method
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: location-discovery-flow
  labels:
    app: location
    model: location
spec:
  distinguisherMethod:
    type: ByNamespace
  matchingPrecedence: 1000
  priorityLevelConfiguration:
    name: exempt
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: Group
      group:
        name: system:serviceaccounts:anihpj
    nonResourceRules:
    - nonResourceURLs: ["/api/v1/locations/*"]
      verbs: ["get"]
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/location-discovery-flow replaced
Example 5: Update JobPost FlowSchema with Additional Subjects

This example adds user subjects to the JobPost batch processing FlowSchema.

# Add user subjects
kubectl get flowschema jobpost-batch-flow -o yaml > jobpost-flow.yaml
cat <<EOF >> jobpost-flow-patch.yaml
spec:
  rules:
  - subjects:
    - kind: ServiceAccount
      serviceAccount:
        name: jobpost-batch-processor
        namespace: anihpj
    - kind: User
      user:
        name: batch-admin@anihpj.com
EOF
kubectl replace -f jobpost-flow.yaml
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/jobpost-batch-flow replaced

update status - replace status of the specified FlowSchema

Replace the status subresource of the specified FlowSchema.

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the FlowSchema
  • body: FlowSchema, required - FlowSchema with updated status
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • fieldManager (in query): string - fieldManager
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - fieldValidation
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
Return Value
  • 200 (FlowSchema): OK
  • 201 (FlowSchema): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills FlowSchema Status Condition

This example updates the status condition for Skills service FlowSchema.

# Update status condition
kubectl get flowschema skills-service-flow -o json | \
  jq '.status.conditions[0].message = "FlowSchema updated and active"' | \
  kubectl replace --subresource=status -f -
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/skills-service-flow replaced
Example 2: Mark User FlowSchema as Ready

This example updates the User API FlowSchema status to Ready.

# Set ready status
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace --subresource=status -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: user-api-flow
status:
  conditions:
  - lastTransitionTime: "2025-11-10T10:00:00Z"
    message: "User API FlowSchema is fully operational"
    reason: "FlowSchemaReady"
    status: "True"
    type: "Ready"
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/user-api-flow replaced
Example 3: Update Author FlowSchema Status Timestamp

This example updates the last transition time for Author publishing FlowSchema.

# Update timestamp
kubectl get flowschema author-publish-flow -o yaml | \
  sed "s/lastTransitionTime: .*/lastTransitionTime: $(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)/" | \
  kubectl replace --subresource=status -f -
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/author-publish-flow replaced
Example 4: Add Warning Condition to Location FlowSchema

This example adds a warning condition to Location discovery FlowSchema status.

# Add warning condition
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace --subresource=status -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: location-discovery-flow
status:
  conditions:
  - lastTransitionTime: "2025-11-10T10:15:00Z"
    message: "High request rate detected"
    reason: "HighRequestRate"
    status: "True"
    type: "Warning"
  - lastTransitionTime: "2025-11-09T08:00:00Z"
    message: "FlowSchema is operational"
    reason: "FlowSchemaInEffect"
    status: "True"
    type: "Ready"
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/location-discovery-flow replaced
Example 5: Clear JobPost FlowSchema Status Conditions

This example resets the status conditions for JobPost batch FlowSchema.

# Reset status conditions
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace --subresource=status -f -
apiVersion: flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1
kind: FlowSchema
metadata:
  name: jobpost-batch-flow
status:
  conditions:
  - lastTransitionTime: "2025-11-10T10:30:00Z"
    message: "FlowSchema reset and re-initialized"
    reason: "Reinitialized"
    status: "True"
    type: "Ready"
EOF
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/jobpost-batch-flow replaced

patch - partially update the specified FlowSchema

Partially update the specified FlowSchema using strategic merge patch.

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the FlowSchema
  • body: Patch, required - Patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • fieldManager (in query): string - fieldManager
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - fieldValidation
  • force (in query): boolean - force
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
Return Value
  • 200 (FlowSchema): OK
  • 201 (FlowSchema): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills FlowSchema Labels

This example adds labels to the Skills service FlowSchema using strategic merge patch.

# Add labels
kubectl patch flowschema skills-service-flow --type='merge' -p '
{
  "metadata": {
    "labels": {
      "environment": "production",
      "team": "skills-team"
    }
  }
}'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/skills-service-flow patched
Example 2: Patch User FlowSchema Precedence

This example updates only the matching precedence for User API FlowSchema.

# Update precedence only
kubectl patch flowschema user-api-flow --type='json' -p='[
  {
    "op": "replace",
    "path": "/spec/matchingPrecedence",
    "value": 750
  }
]'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/user-api-flow patched
Example 3: Patch Author FlowSchema Annotations

This example adds annotations to the Author publishing FlowSchema.

# Add annotations
kubectl patch flowschema author-publish-flow --type='merge' -p '
{
  "metadata": {
    "annotations": {
      "owner": "author-team@anihpj.com",
      "last-updated": "2025-11-10",
      "purpose": "Author content publishing priority"
    }
  }
}'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/author-publish-flow patched
Example 4: Patch Location FlowSchema Priority Level

This example changes the priority level for Location discovery FlowSchema using JSON patch.

# Change priority level
kubectl patch flowschema location-discovery-flow --type='json' -p='[
  {
    "op": "replace",
    "path": "/spec/priorityLevelConfiguration/name",
    "value": "workload-high"
  }
]'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/location-discovery-flow patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost FlowSchema Add Rule

This example adds a new resource rule to JobPost batch FlowSchema.

# Add new resource rule
kubectl patch flowschema jobpost-batch-flow --type='json' -p='[
  {
    "op": "add",
    "path": "/spec/rules/0/resourceRules/-",
    "value": {
      "apiGroups": [""],
      "resources": ["events"],
      "verbs": ["create", "patch"]
    }
  }
]'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/jobpost-batch-flow patched

patch status - partially update status of the specified FlowSchema

Partially update the status subresource of the specified FlowSchema.

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the FlowSchema
  • body: Patch, required - Status patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • fieldManager (in query): string - fieldManager
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - fieldValidation
  • force (in query): boolean - force
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
Return Value
  • 200 (FlowSchema): OK
  • 201 (FlowSchema): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills FlowSchema Status Message

This example updates the status message for Skills service FlowSchema.

# Update status message
kubectl patch flowschema skills-service-flow --subresource=status --type='json' -p='[
  {
    "op": "replace",
    "path": "/status/conditions/0/message",
    "value": "Skills service flow operating normally"
  }
]'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/skills-service-flow patched
Example 2: Patch User FlowSchema Add Status Condition

This example adds a new condition to User API FlowSchema status.

# Add new condition
kubectl patch flowschema user-api-flow --subresource=status --type='json' -p='[
  {
    "op": "add",
    "path": "/status/conditions/-",
    "value": {
      "type": "Degraded",
      "status": "False",
      "lastTransitionTime": "'$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)'",
      "reason": "OperatingNormally",
      "message": "No degradation detected"
    }
  }
]'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/user-api-flow patched
Example 3: Patch Author FlowSchema Status Reason

This example updates the reason field in Author publishing FlowSchema status.

# Update status reason
kubectl patch flowschema author-publish-flow --subresource=status --type='merge' -p '
{
  "status": {
    "conditions": [
      {
        "type": "Ready",
        "status": "True",
        "lastTransitionTime": "'$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)'",
        "reason": "FullyOperational",
        "message": "Author publishing flow fully operational"
      }
    ]
  }
}'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/author-publish-flow patched
Example 4: Patch Location FlowSchema Update Transition Time

This example updates the last transition time for Location discovery FlowSchema.

# Update transition time
kubectl patch flowschema location-discovery-flow --subresource=status --type='json' -p='[
  {
    "op": "replace",
    "path": "/status/conditions/0/lastTransitionTime",
    "value": "'$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)'"
  }
]'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/location-discovery-flow patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost FlowSchema Status to Warning

This example updates JobPost batch FlowSchema status to indicate a warning state.

# Set warning status
kubectl patch flowschema jobpost-batch-flow --subresource=status --type='merge' -p '
{
  "status": {
    "conditions": [
      {
        "type": "Ready",
        "status": "True",
        "lastTransitionTime": "'$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)'",
        "reason": "OperatingWithWarning",
        "message": "High load detected but operational"
      }
    ]
  }
}'
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/jobpost-batch-flow patched

delete - delete a FlowSchema

Delete a specific FlowSchema by name.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the FlowSchema
  • body: DeleteOptions - deletion options
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - gracePeriodSeconds
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - propagationPolicy
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 202 (Status): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Analytics FlowSchema

This example deletes the Skills analytics FlowSchema.

# Delete FlowSchema
kubectl delete flowschema skills-analytics-flow
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "skills-analytics-flow" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Authentication FlowSchema

This example removes the User authentication FlowSchema immediately.

# Force delete
kubectl delete flowschema user-auth-flow --grace-period=0 --force
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. The resource may continue to run on the cluster indefinitely. flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "user-auth-flow" force deleted
Example 3: Delete Author Moderation FlowSchema with Dry Run

This example shows what would happen when deleting Author moderation FlowSchema.

# Dry run delete
kubectl delete flowschema author-moderation-flow --dry-run=client
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "author-moderation-flow" deleted (dry run)
Example 4: Delete Location Geocoding FlowSchema from File

This example deletes Location geocoding FlowSchema using the original manifest file.

# Delete from file
kubectl delete -f location-geocoding-flow.yaml
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "location-geocoding-flow" deleted
Example 5: Delete JobPost Notification FlowSchema with Wait

This example deletes JobPost notification FlowSchema and waits for completion.

# Delete and wait
kubectl delete flowschema jobpost-notification-flow --wait=true
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "jobpost-notification-flow" deleted

deletecollection - delete collection of FlowSchema

Delete multiple FlowSchemas matching the specified criteria.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io/v1/flowschemas
Parameters
  • body: DeleteOptions - deletion options
  • continue (in query): string - continue
  • dryRun (in query): string - dryRun
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - fieldSelector
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - gracePeriodSeconds
  • labelSelector (in query): string - labelSelector
  • limit (in query): integer - limit
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - propagationPolicy
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resourceVersion
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All Skills Model FlowSchemas

This example deletes all FlowSchemas labeled for the Skills model.

# Delete by label selector
kubectl delete flowschemas -l model=skills
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "skills-service-flow" deleted flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "skills-analytics-flow" deleted
Example 2: Delete All User Model FlowSchemas

This example removes all FlowSchemas related to User model operations.

# Delete User FlowSchemas
kubectl delete flowschemas -l app=anihpj,component=user
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "user-api-flow" deleted flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "user-auth-flow" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author FlowSchemas with Dry Run

This example shows what would be deleted for Author model FlowSchemas.

# Dry run delete collection
kubectl delete flowschemas -l service=author --dry-run=server
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "author-publish-flow" deleted (server dry run) flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "author-moderation-flow" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete All Location Service FlowSchemas

This example deletes all FlowSchemas associated with Location service.

# Delete Location FlowSchemas
kubectl delete flowschemas -l service=geocoding,model=location
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "location-discovery-flow" deleted flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "location-geocoding-flow" deleted
Example 5: Delete All JobPost Application FlowSchemas

This example removes all FlowSchemas for the entire JobPost application.

# Delete all anihpj FlowSchemas
kubectl delete flowschemas -l app=anihpj --all-namespaces
flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "skills-service-flow" deleted flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "user-api-flow" deleted flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "author-publish-flow" deleted flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "location-discovery-flow" deleted flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "jobpost-batch-flow" deleted flowschema.flowcontrol.apiserver.k8s.io "jobpost-notification-flow" deleted

LimitRange

📋 LimitRange Overview

LimitRangeSpec - Min/max usage limits configuration
LimitRangeList - Collection of LimitRanges
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: LimitRange v1 API | API Version: v1

LimitRange sets resource usage limits for each kind of resource in a Namespace.

Full Description

LimitRange provides constraints that limit resource consumption per namespace for your anihpj Django application. It enforces minimum and maximum compute resources for each kind of resource in a namespace, enabling you to control resource usage for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

By defining LimitRanges, you can prevent resource exhaustion, ensure fair resource distribution, and set defaults for containers that don't specify resource requirements. This is crucial for multi-tenant environments where different services share the same cluster, ensuring that one service doesn't monopolize resources at the expense of others.

Syntax

# Create a LimitRange from YAML file
kubectl create -f limitrange.yaml
# List all LimitRanges in a namespace
kubectl get limitranges
# List all LimitRanges (short form)
kubectl get limits
# Get detailed information about a specific LimitRange
kubectl describe limitrange {name}
# Get LimitRange in YAML format
kubectl get limitrange {name} -o yaml
# Delete a specific LimitRange
kubectl delete limitrange {name}

YAML Structure

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: anihpj-resource-limits
  namespace: default
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "2Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    type: Container
  - max:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "4Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    type: Pod

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "v1" for LimitRange
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "LimitRange"
  • metadata: ObjectMeta - Standard object metadata
    • name: string - Name of the LimitRange
    • namespace: string - Namespace where LimitRange applies
  • spec: LimitRangeSpec - Specification of resource limits
    • limits: []LimitRangeItem - List of limit range items

Return Value

  • 200 (LimitRange): OK - Request successful, LimitRange returned
  • 201 (LimitRange): Created - LimitRange successfully created
  • 202 (LimitRange): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create LimitRange for Skills Service Namespace

This example creates resource limits for the Skills service namespace in the jobpost app.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: skills-limits
  namespace: skills-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: skills
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "50m"
      memory: "64Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "300m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    type: Container
kubectl create -f skills-limitrange.yaml
limitrange/skills-limits created
Example 2: Create LimitRange for User Authentication Service

This example sets up resource limits for User model authentication workloads.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: user-auth-limits
  namespace: user-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: user
    component: authentication
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "2Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    maxLimitRequestRatio:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "2"
    type: Container
kubectl create -f user-limitrange.yaml
kubectl describe limitrange user-auth-limits -n user-service
limitrange/user-auth-limits created Name: user-auth-limits Namespace: user-service Type Resource Min Max Default Request Default Limit Max Limit/Request Ratio ---- -------- --- --- --------------- ------------- ----------------------- Container cpu 100m 2 200m 500m 4 Container memory 128Mi 2Gi 256Mi 512Mi 2
Example 3: Create LimitRange for Author Publishing Service

This example demonstrates setting limits for the Author model content publishing.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: author-limits
  namespace: author-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: author
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: "1.5"
      memory: "1.5Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "400m"
      memory: "384Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "150m"
      memory: "192Mi"
    type: Container
  - max:
      storage: "10Gi"
    min:
      storage: "1Gi"
    type: PersistentVolumeClaim
kubectl create -f author-limitrange.yaml
limitrange/author-limits created
Example 4: Create LimitRange for Location Geocoding Service

This example creates limits for the Location model geocoding workloads.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: location-limits
  namespace: location-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: location
    component: geocoding
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "50m"
      memory: "64Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "250m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    type: Container
  - max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "2Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    type: Pod
kubectl create -f location-limitrange.yaml
kubectl get limitranges -n location-service
limitrange/location-limits created NAME CREATED AT location-limits 2025-11-10T10:00:00Z
Example 5: Create LimitRange for JobPost Batch Processing

This example sets up comprehensive resource limits for JobPost model batch processing.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: jobpost-batch-limits
  namespace: jobpost-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: jobpost
    workload: batch
spec:
  limits:
  - max:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "4Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    type: Container
  - max:
      cpu: "8"
      memory: "8Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    type: Pod
  - max:
      storage: "50Gi"
    min:
      storage: "5Gi"
    type: PersistentVolumeClaim
kubectl create -f jobpost-limitrange.yaml
kubectl get limitrange jobpost-batch-limits -n jobpost-service -o yaml
limitrange/jobpost-batch-limits created apiVersion: v1 kind: LimitRange metadata: name: jobpost-batch-limits namespace: jobpost-service labels: app: anihpj service: jobpost workload: batch spec: limits: - max: cpu: "4" memory: 4Gi min: cpu: 200m memory: 256Mi default: cpu: "1" memory: 1Gi defaultRequest: cpu: 500m memory: 512Mi type: Container

LimitRangeSpec

LimitRangeSpec defines a min/max usage limit for resources that match on kind.

Full Description

LimitRangeSpec is the specification for a LimitRange resource, containing the list of limits to enforce on resource consumption in a namespace for your anihpj Django application. Each spec defines constraints for different resource types (Container, Pod, PersistentVolumeClaim), ensuring that your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services operate within defined resource boundaries.

The spec allows you to configure minimum resources, maximum resources, default limits, default requests, and limit-to-request ratios. These settings are automatically applied to pods and containers that don't specify their own resource requirements, providing a safety net and ensuring consistent resource allocation across your jobpost application workloads.

Syntax
# View LimitRangeSpec using jsonpath
kubectl get limitrange {name} -o jsonpath='{.spec}'
# View limits array in the spec
kubectl get limitrange {name} -o jsonpath='{.spec.limits[*]}'
# Describe to see spec details
kubectl describe limitrange {name}
Parameters Description
  • limits: []LimitRangeItem (required) - List of LimitRangeItem objects that are enforced
    • type: string (required) - Type of resource (Container, Pod, PersistentVolumeClaim)
    • max: map[string]Quantity - Maximum resource values
    • min: map[string]Quantity - Minimum resource values
    • default: map[string]Quantity - Default limit values if not specified
    • defaultRequest: map[string]Quantity - Default request values if not specified
    • maxLimitRequestRatio: map[string]Quantity - Max burst ratio (limit/request)
Return Value

Returns a LimitRangeSpec object containing the resource limit configuration for the namespace.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: LimitRangeSpec for Skills Service Containers

This example shows a spec with container-level limits for the Skills model.

spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "50m"
      memory: "64Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "300m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
kubectl get limitrange skills-limits -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq
{ "limits": [ { "default": { "cpu": "300m", "memory": "256Mi" }, "defaultRequest": { "cpu": "100m", "memory": "128Mi" }, "max": { "cpu": "1", "memory": "1Gi" }, "min": { "cpu": "50m", "memory": "64Mi" }, "type": "Container" } ] }
Example 2: LimitRangeSpec for User Service with Ratio Limits

This example demonstrates spec with maxLimitRequestRatio for User authentication.

spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "2Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    maxLimitRequestRatio:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "2"
kubectl describe limitrange user-auth-limits -n user-service
Name: user-auth-limits Namespace: user-service Type Resource Min Max Default Request Default Limit Max Limit/Request Ratio ---- -------- --- --- --------------- ------------- ----------------------- Container cpu 100m 2 200m 500m 4 Container memory 128Mi 2Gi 256Mi 512Mi 2
Example 3: LimitRangeSpec for Author Service Multi-Type Limits

This example shows spec with both Container and PVC limits for Author content.

spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "1.5"
      memory: "1.5Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "400m"
      memory: "384Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "150m"
      memory: "192Mi"
  - type: PersistentVolumeClaim
    max:
      storage: "10Gi"
    min:
      storage: "1Gi"
kubectl get limitrange author-limits -n author-service -o yaml | grep -A 20 spec:
spec: limits: - default: cpu: 400m memory: 384Mi defaultRequest: cpu: 150m memory: 192Mi max: cpu: 1500m memory: 1536Mi min: cpu: 100m memory: 128Mi type: Container - max: storage: 10Gi min: storage: 1Gi type: PersistentVolumeClaim
Example 4: LimitRangeSpec for Location Service Pod Limits

This example demonstrates spec with both Container and Pod limits for Location geocoding.

spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "50m"
      memory: "64Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "250m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
  - type: Pod
    max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "2Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
kubectl get limitrange location-limits -n location-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.limits[1]}' | jq
{ "max": { "cpu": "2", "memory": "2Gi" }, "min": { "cpu": "100m", "memory": "128Mi" }, "type": "Pod" }
Example 5: LimitRangeSpec for JobPost Comprehensive Limits

This example shows comprehensive spec for JobPost batch processing with all limit types.

spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "4Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
  - type: Pod
    max:
      cpu: "8"
      memory: "8Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
  - type: PersistentVolumeClaim
    max:
      storage: "50Gi"
    min:
      storage: "5Gi"
kubectl get limitrange jobpost-batch-limits -n jobpost-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.limits}' | jq
[ { "default": { "cpu": "1", "memory": "1Gi" }, "defaultRequest": { "cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi" }, "max": { "cpu": "4", "memory": "4Gi" }, "min": { "cpu": "200m", "memory": "256Mi" }, "type": "Container" }, { "max": { "cpu": "8", "memory": "8Gi" }, "min": { "cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi" }, "type": "Pod" }, { "max": { "storage": "50Gi" }, "min": { "storage": "5Gi" }, "type": "PersistentVolumeClaim" } ]

LimitRangeList

LimitRangeList is a list of LimitRange items.

Full Description

LimitRangeList represents a collection of LimitRange resources across namespaces in your anihpj Django application cluster. This list type is returned when querying multiple LimitRanges, enabling you to view and manage resource constraints for all your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost service namespaces in a single operation.

The list includes metadata about the collection and an array of LimitRange items. This is particularly useful for cluster administrators who need to audit resource policies, ensure consistency across namespaces, or perform bulk operations on LimitRanges throughout the jobpost application infrastructure.

Syntax
# List all LimitRanges in current namespace
kubectl get limitranges
# List all LimitRanges across all namespaces
kubectl get limitranges --all-namespaces
# List with custom columns
kubectl get limitranges -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace
Parameters Description
  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "v1" for LimitRangeList
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "LimitRangeList"
  • metadata: ListMeta - Standard list metadata
    • resourceVersion: string - Version of the resource list
    • continue: string - Token for pagination
  • items: []LimitRange (required) - Array of LimitRange objects
Return Value

Returns a LimitRangeList object containing an array of LimitRange resources and associated metadata.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All LimitRanges for Skills Service

This example retrieves all LimitRanges in the Skills service namespace.

kubectl get limitranges -n skills-service
NAME CREATED AT skills-limits 2025-11-10T10:00:00Z
Example 2: List All LimitRanges Across All Namespaces

This example shows all LimitRanges for all anihpj services.

kubectl get limitranges --all-namespaces
NAMESPACE NAME CREATED AT skills-service skills-limits 2025-11-10T10:00:00Z user-service user-auth-limits 2025-11-10T10:05:00Z author-service author-limits 2025-11-10T10:10:00Z location-service location-limits 2025-11-10T10:15:00Z jobpost-service jobpost-batch-limits 2025-11-10T10:20:00Z
Example 3: List LimitRanges with Labels for Author Service

This example filters LimitRanges by label selector for Author model.

kubectl get limitranges -l app=anihpj,service=author --all-namespaces
NAMESPACE NAME CREATED AT author-service author-limits 2025-11-10T10:10:00Z
Example 4: List LimitRanges in JSON Format for Location Service

This example outputs the LimitRangeList in JSON for Location geocoding namespace.

kubectl get limitranges -n location-service -o json | jq '.items[].metadata.name'
"location-limits"
Example 5: List LimitRanges with Custom Columns for JobPost

This example displays LimitRanges with custom columns for all anihpj namespaces.

kubectl get limitranges --all-namespaces -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,\
CREATED:.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME NAMESPACE CREATED skills-limits skills-service 2025-11-10T10:00:00Z user-auth-limits user-service 2025-11-10T10:05:00Z author-limits author-service 2025-11-10T10:10:00Z location-limits location-service 2025-11-10T10:15:00Z jobpost-batch-limits jobpost-service 2025-11-10T10:20:00Z

Operations

Operations allow you to interact with LimitRange resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage resource constraints and policies for your anihpj Django application, ensuring optimal resource allocation for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

📋 LimitRange Operations Overview

get - Read the specified LimitRange
list - List or watch objects of kind LimitRange
list-all - List LimitRanges across all namespaces
create - Create a LimitRange
update - Replace the specified LimitRange
patch - Partially update the specified LimitRange
delete - Delete a LimitRange
deletecollection - Delete collection of LimitRange

get - read the specified LimitRange

Read details of a specific LimitRange by name in a namespace.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the LimitRange
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (LimitRange): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Service LimitRange

Retrieve the LimitRange configuration for Skills model namespace.

kubectl get limitrange skills-limits -n skills-service -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: LimitRange metadata: name: skills-limits namespace: skills-service spec: limits: - default: cpu: 300m memory: 256Mi defaultRequest: cpu: 100m memory: 128Mi max: cpu: "1" memory: 1Gi min: cpu: 50m memory: 64Mi type: Container
Example 2: Get User Authentication LimitRange

View the LimitRange for User service authentication workloads.

kubectl get limitrange user-auth-limits -n user-service
NAME CREATED AT user-auth-limits 2025-11-10T10:05:00Z
Example 3: Get Author Service LimitRange in JSON

Retrieve Author model LimitRange in JSON format for processing.

kubectl get limitrange author-limits -n author-service -o json | jq '.spec.limits'
[ { "default": { "cpu": "400m", "memory": "384Mi" }, "defaultRequest": { "cpu": "150m", "memory": "192Mi" }, "max": { "cpu": "1500m", "memory": "1536Mi" }, "min": { "cpu": "100m", "memory": "128Mi" }, "type": "Container" }, { "max": { "storage": "10Gi" }, "min": { "storage": "1Gi" }, "type": "PersistentVolumeClaim" } ]
Example 4: Describe Location Service LimitRange

Get detailed information about Location geocoding LimitRange.

kubectl describe limitrange location-limits -n location-service
Name: location-limits Namespace: location-service Type Resource Min Max Default Request Default Limit Max Limit/Request Ratio ---- -------- --- --- --------------- ------------- ----------------------- Container cpu 50m 1 100m 250m - Container memory 64Mi 1Gi 128Mi 256Mi - Pod cpu 100m 2 - - - Pod memory 128Mi 2Gi - - -
Example 5: Get JobPost Batch LimitRange Limits Only

Extract only the limits array from JobPost LimitRange.

kubectl get limitrange jobpost-batch-limits -n jobpost-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.limits[*]}' | jq
{ "default": { "cpu": "1", "memory": "1Gi" }, "defaultRequest": { "cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi" }, "max": { "cpu": "4", "memory": "4Gi" }, "min": { "cpu": "200m", "memory": "256Mi" }, "type": "Container" }

list - list or watch objects of kind LimitRange

List LimitRanges in a specific namespace with optional filtering and pagination.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): boolean - allowWatchBookmarks
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector
  • limit (in query): integer - limit number of results
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resource version
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout in seconds
  • watch (in query): boolean - watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (LimitRangeList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List LimitRanges in Skills Namespace

List all LimitRanges for Skills service namespace.

kubectl get limitranges -n skills-service
NAME CREATED AT skills-limits 2025-11-10T10:00:00Z
Example 2: List LimitRanges in User Namespace with Labels

Filter LimitRanges by label for User authentication service.

kubectl get limitranges -n user-service -l component=authentication
NAME CREATED AT user-auth-limits 2025-11-10T10:05:00Z
Example 3: List LimitRanges in Author Namespace with Output

List Author service LimitRanges in wide format.

kubectl get limitranges -n author-service -o wide
NAME CREATED AT author-limits 2025-11-10T10:10:00Z
Example 4: Watch LimitRanges in Location Namespace

Watch for changes to LimitRanges in Location geocoding namespace.

kubectl get limitranges -n location-service --watch
NAME CREATED AT location-limits 2025-11-10T10:15:00Z # Watching for changes...
Example 5: List LimitRanges in JobPost Namespace with JSON Path

Extract specific fields from JobPost namespace LimitRanges.

kubectl get limitranges -n jobpost-service -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.metadata.creationTimestamp}{"\n"}{end}'
jobpost-batch-limits 2025-11-10T10:20:00Z

list - list or watch objects of kind LimitRange (all namespaces)

List LimitRanges across all namespaces in the cluster.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/limitranges
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): boolean - allowWatchBookmarks
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector
  • limit (in query): integer - limit number of results
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resource version
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout in seconds
  • watch (in query): boolean - watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (LimitRangeList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All LimitRanges Across All Namespaces

View all LimitRanges for the entire anihpj application.

kubectl get limitranges --all-namespaces
NAMESPACE NAME CREATED AT skills-service skills-limits 2025-11-10T10:00:00Z user-service user-auth-limits 2025-11-10T10:05:00Z author-service author-limits 2025-11-10T10:10:00Z location-service location-limits 2025-11-10T10:15:00Z jobpost-service jobpost-batch-limits 2025-11-10T10:20:00Z
Example 2: List All LimitRanges with Label Selector

Find all LimitRanges labeled for the anihpj application.

kubectl get limitranges --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj
NAMESPACE NAME CREATED AT skills-service skills-limits 2025-11-10T10:00:00Z user-service user-auth-limits 2025-11-10T10:05:00Z author-service author-limits 2025-11-10T10:10:00Z location-service location-limits 2025-11-10T10:15:00Z jobpost-service jobpost-batch-limits 2025-11-10T10:20:00Z
Example 3: List All LimitRanges in Custom Columns

Display all LimitRanges with custom column formatting.

kubectl get limitranges --all-namespaces -o custom-columns=\
NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
CREATED:.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAMESPACE NAME CREATED skills-service skills-limits 2025-11-10T10:00:00Z user-service user-auth-limits 2025-11-10T10:05:00Z author-service author-limits 2025-11-10T10:10:00Z location-service location-limits 2025-11-10T10:15:00Z jobpost-service jobpost-batch-limits 2025-11-10T10:20:00Z
Example 4: List All LimitRanges in JSON Format

Export all LimitRanges to JSON for processing.

kubectl get limitranges --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, namespace: .metadata.namespace}'
{ "name": "skills-limits", "namespace": "skills-service" } { "name": "user-auth-limits", "namespace": "user-service" } { "name": "author-limits", "namespace": "author-service" } { "name": "location-limits", "namespace": "location-service" } { "name": "jobpost-batch-limits", "namespace": "jobpost-service" }
Example 5: Count Total LimitRanges Across All Namespaces

Get a count of all LimitRanges in the cluster.

kubectl get limitranges --all-namespaces --no-headers | wc -l
5

create - create a LimitRange

Create a new LimitRange resource in a specified namespace.

HTTP Request
POST /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • body: LimitRange, required - LimitRange object to create
  • dryRun (in query): string - perform dry run
  • fieldManager (in query): string - field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - field validation level
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (LimitRange): OK
  • 201 (LimitRange): Created
  • 202 (LimitRange): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create LimitRange for Skills Service

Create a LimitRange to constrain Skills model container resources.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: skills-limits
  namespace: skills-service
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "50m"
      memory: "64Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "300m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
kubectl create -f skills-limitrange.yaml
limitrange/skills-limits created
Example 2: Create LimitRange for User Authentication

Create a LimitRange with ratio limits for User service.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: user-auth-limits
  namespace: user-service
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "2"
      memory: "2Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    maxLimitRequestRatio:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "2"
kubectl create -f user-limitrange.yaml
kubectl describe limitrange user-auth-limits -n user-service
limitrange/user-auth-limits created Name: user-auth-limits Namespace: user-service Type Resource Min Max Default Request Default Limit Max Limit/Request Ratio ---- -------- --- --- --------------- ------------- ----------------------- Container cpu 100m 2 200m 500m 4 Container memory 128Mi 2Gi 256Mi 512Mi 2
Example 3: Create LimitRange for Author Publishing with Dry Run

Test LimitRange creation for Author model with dry run.

kubectl create -f author-limitrange.yaml --dry-run=client
limitrange/author-limits created (dry run)
Example 4: Create LimitRange for Location Service Imperatively

Create a basic LimitRange for Location geocoding using kubectl.

kubectl create limitrange location-limits -n location-service \
  --dry-run=client -o yaml | kubectl apply -f -
limitrange/location-limits created
Example 5: Create Comprehensive LimitRange for JobPost Batch

Create a multi-type LimitRange for JobPost batch processing.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: jobpost-batch-limits
  namespace: jobpost-service
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "4"
      memory: "4Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "200m"
      memory: "256Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "1"
      memory: "1Gi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
  - type: Pod
    max:
      cpu: "8"
      memory: "8Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "500m"
      memory: "512Mi"
  - type: PersistentVolumeClaim
    max:
      storage: "50Gi"
    min:
      storage: "5Gi"
kubectl create -f jobpost-limitrange.yaml
limitrange/jobpost-batch-limits created

update - replace the specified LimitRange

Replace the entire LimitRange configuration with a new specification.

HTTP Request
PUT /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the LimitRange
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • body: LimitRange, required - updated LimitRange object
  • dryRun (in query): string - perform dry run
  • fieldManager (in query): string - field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - field validation level
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (LimitRange): OK
  • 201 (LimitRange): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Service LimitRange Limits

Increase resource limits for Skills model containers.

# Edit and update
kubectl get limitrange skills-limits -n skills-service -o yaml > skills-limits.yaml
# Modify the file, then apply
kubectl replace -f skills-limits.yaml
limitrange/skills-limits replaced
Example 2: Update User Authentication LimitRange with Ratio

Modify maxLimitRequestRatio for User service containers.

apiVersion: v1
kind: LimitRange
metadata:
  name: user-auth-limits
  namespace: user-service
spec:
  limits:
  - type: Container
    max:
      cpu: "3"
      memory: "3Gi"
    min:
      cpu: "100m"
      memory: "128Mi"
    default:
      cpu: "750m"
      memory: "768Mi"
    defaultRequest:
      cpu: "250m"
      memory: "384Mi"
    maxLimitRequestRatio:
      cpu: "6"
      memory: "3"
kubectl replace -f user-limitrange-updated.yaml
limitrange/user-auth-limits replaced
Example 3: Update Author Service LimitRange Storage Limits

Increase PVC storage limits for Author content service.

# Get current config
kubectl get limitrange author-limits -n author-service -o yaml > /tmp/author-limits.yaml
# Edit storage limits
sed -i 's/storage: "10Gi"/storage: "20Gi"/' /tmp/author-limits.yaml
# Replace
kubectl replace -f /tmp/author-limits.yaml
limitrange/author-limits replaced
Example 4: Update Location Service Pod Limits

Adjust pod-level limits for Location geocoding service.

# Export, modify, and replace
kubectl get limitrange location-limits -n location-service -o yaml | \
  sed 's/max:/max: {cpu: "4", memory: "4Gi"}  # Updated/' | \
  kubectl replace -f -
limitrange/location-limits replaced
Example 5: Update JobPost Batch LimitRange with Dry Run

Test updating JobPost batch limits before applying.

kubectl replace -f jobpost-limitrange-v2.yaml --dry-run=server
limitrange/jobpost-batch-limits replaced (server dry run)

patch - partially update the specified LimitRange

Partially update a LimitRange using strategic merge patch, JSON merge patch, or JSON patch.

HTTP Request
PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the LimitRange
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • body: Patch, required - patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): string - perform dry run
  • fieldManager (in query): string - field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - field validation level
  • force (in query): boolean - force apply
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (LimitRange): OK
  • 201 (LimitRange): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Service Default CPU Limit

Update only the default CPU limit for Skills containers.

kubectl patch limitrange skills-limits -n skills-service --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/limits/0/default/cpu", "value": "400m"}
]'
limitrange/skills-limits patched
Example 2: Patch User Service Memory Limits

Modify memory constraints for User authentication containers.

kubectl patch limitrange user-auth-limits -n user-service --type merge -p '
{
  "spec": {
    "limits": [
      {
        "type": "Container",
        "max": {
          "memory": "3Gi"
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}'
limitrange/user-auth-limits patched
Example 3: Patch Author Service Add Label

Add a label to Author service LimitRange using strategic merge patch.

kubectl patch limitrange author-limits -n author-service -p '
{
  "metadata": {
    "labels": {
      "environment": "production"
    }
  }
}'
limitrange/author-limits patched
Example 4: Patch Location Service Min CPU Requirement

Update minimum CPU requirement for Location geocoding pods.

kubectl patch limitrange location-limits -n location-service --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/limits/0/min/cpu", "value": "100m"}
]'
limitrange/location-limits patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost Batch PVC Storage Limit

Increase PVC storage maximum for JobPost batch processing.

kubectl patch limitrange jobpost-batch-limits -n jobpost-service --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/limits/2/max/storage", "value": "100Gi"}
]'
limitrange/jobpost-batch-limits patched

delete - delete a LimitRange

Delete a specific LimitRange by name from a namespace.

HTTP Request
DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the LimitRange
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • body: DeleteOptions - deletion options
  • dryRun (in query): string - perform dry run
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - grace period in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): boolean
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 202 (Status): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Service LimitRange

Remove resource limits from Skills model namespace.

kubectl delete limitrange skills-limits -n skills-service
limitrange "skills-limits" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Authentication LimitRange with Confirmation

Delete User service LimitRange after confirmation.

# Check first
kubectl get limitrange user-auth-limits -n user-service
# Then delete
kubectl delete limitrange user-auth-limits -n user-service
NAME CREATED AT user-auth-limits 2025-11-10T10:05:00Z limitrange "user-auth-limits" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author Service LimitRange with Dry Run

Test deleting Author model LimitRange before actual deletion.

kubectl delete limitrange author-limits -n author-service --dry-run=server
limitrange "author-limits" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete Location Service LimitRange from YAML

Delete Location geocoding LimitRange using its manifest file.

kubectl delete -f location-limitrange.yaml
limitrange "location-limits" deleted
Example 5: Delete JobPost Batch LimitRange Immediately

Force delete JobPost LimitRange without grace period.

kubectl delete limitrange jobpost-batch-limits -n jobpost-service --grace-period=0 --force
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. limitrange "jobpost-batch-limits" force deleted

deletecollection - delete collection of LimitRange

Delete multiple LimitRanges in a namespace matching specified criteria.

HTTP Request
DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/limitranges
Parameters
  • namespace (in path): string, required - namespace
  • body: DeleteOptions - deletion options
  • continue (in query): string - continue token
  • dryRun (in query): string - perform dry run
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - field selector
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - grace period in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): boolean
  • labelSelector (in query): string - label selector
  • limit (in query): integer - limit number of results
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - resource version
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout in seconds
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All LimitRanges in Skills Namespace

Remove all resource constraints from Skills service namespace.

kubectl delete limitranges --all -n skills-service
limitrange "skills-limits" deleted
Example 2: Delete LimitRanges by Label Selector

Delete all LimitRanges labeled for User authentication.

kubectl delete limitranges -l component=authentication -n user-service
limitrange "user-auth-limits" deleted
Example 3: Delete All LimitRanges with Dry Run

Preview which Author service LimitRanges would be deleted.

kubectl delete limitranges --all -n author-service --dry-run=server
limitrange "author-limits" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete LimitRanges Matching Multiple Labels

Delete Location service LimitRanges matching specific labels.

kubectl delete limitranges -l app=anihpj,service=location -n location-service
limitrange "location-limits" deleted
Example 5: Delete All anihpj Application LimitRanges

Remove all LimitRanges for the entire jobpost application across namespaces.

# Delete from each namespace
for ns in skills-service user-service author-service location-service jobpost-service; do
  kubectl delete limitranges --all -n $ns
done
limitrange "skills-limits" deleted limitrange "user-auth-limits" deleted limitrange "author-limits" deleted limitrange "location-limits" deleted limitrange "jobpost-batch-limits" deleted

ResourceQuota

ResourceQuota Overview

ResourceQuotaSpec - Desired quota limits
ResourceQuotaStatus - Enforced quota and usage
ResourceQuotaList - Collection of ResourceQuotas
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: ResourceQuota v1 API | API Version: v1

ResourceQuota sets aggregate quota restrictions enforced per namespace.

Full Description

ResourceQuota provides a mechanism to limit the aggregate resource consumption per namespace in your anihpj Django application. By setting ResourceQuotas, you can control how much CPU, memory, storage, and other resources can be consumed by all pods in a namespace, preventing any single namespace from monopolizing cluster resources. This is essential for multi-tenant environments where your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services need to coexist without resource contention.

Each namespace in your jobpost application can have one or more ResourceQuotas that define hard limits for compute resources (CPU, memory), storage resources (persistent volume claims, storage classes), and object counts (pods, services, configmaps, secrets). Kubernetes tracks the current usage against these quotas and prevents resource requests that would exceed the limits, ensuring fair resource distribution across your application's microservices.

Syntax

# Create a ResourceQuota from YAML file
kubectl create -f resourcequota.yaml

# Create a ResourceQuota with compute limits
kubectl create quota compute-quota --hard=cpu=10,memory=20Gi --namespace=skills-service

# List all ResourceQuotas in a namespace
kubectl get resourcequotas -n user-service

# List all ResourceQuotas (short form)
kubectl get quota -n author-service

# Get detailed information about a ResourceQuota
kubectl describe resourcequota jobpost-quota -n jobpost-service

# Get ResourceQuota in YAML format
kubectl get resourcequota location-quota -n location-service -o yaml

# Delete a ResourceQuota
kubectl delete resourcequota skills-quota -n skills-service

YAML Structure

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: anihpj-compute-quota
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: resource-management
spec:
  hard:
    # Compute resources
    requests.cpu: "10"
    requests.memory: 20Gi
    limits.cpu: "20"
    limits.memory: 40Gi
    # Storage resources
    requests.storage: 100Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "10"
    # Object counts
    pods: "50"
    services: "10"
    configmaps: "20"
    secrets: "20"
    replicationcontrollers: "10"
    resourcequotas: "1"
  scopeSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - operator: In
      scopeName: PriorityClass
      values: ["high", "medium"]

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion (string): Specifies the API version, always "v1" for ResourceQuota
  • kind (string): Specifies the resource type, always "ResourceQuota"
  • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard object metadata
    • name: Name of the ResourceQuota (e.g., "anihpj-compute-quota")
    • namespace: Namespace where the quota is applied (e.g., "skills-service")
    • labels: Key-value pairs for organizing and selecting quotas
    • annotations: Additional metadata for external tools
  • spec (ResourceQuotaSpec): Defines the desired quota restrictions
    • hard (map[string]Quantity): Hard limits for each named resource
      • requests.cpu: Total CPU requests allowed (e.g., "10")
      • requests.memory: Total memory requests allowed (e.g., "20Gi")
      • limits.cpu: Total CPU limits allowed (e.g., "20")
      • limits.memory: Total memory limits allowed (e.g., "40Gi")
      • requests.storage: Total storage requests allowed (e.g., "100Gi")
      • persistentvolumeclaims: Maximum number of PVCs (e.g., "10")
      • pods: Maximum number of pods (e.g., "50")
      • services: Maximum number of services (e.g., "10")
      • configmaps: Maximum number of ConfigMaps (e.g., "20")
      • secrets: Maximum number of Secrets (e.g., "20")
    • scopeSelector (ScopeSelector): Filters which objects are tracked by quota
    • scopes ([]string): Collection of filters (e.g., ["Terminating", "NotTerminating"])
  • status (ResourceQuotaStatus): Current quota enforcement status (read-only)
    • hard: The enforced hard limits
    • used: Current observed total usage

Return Value

  • 200 OK: Returns the ResourceQuota object with current spec and status
  • 201 Created: ResourceQuota was successfully created
  • 202 Accepted: Request accepted, ResourceQuota creation is in progress
  • 401 Unauthorized: Authentication credentials are missing or invalid

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Basic Compute Quota for Skills Service

Create a ResourceQuota limiting compute resources for the Skills service namespace.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: skills-compute-quota
  namespace: skills-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: skills
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "20"
kubectl apply -f skills-quota.yaml
resourcequota/skills-compute-quota created
Example 2: Storage Quota for User Service

Limit storage consumption for User authentication data and session storage.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: user-storage-quota
  namespace: user-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: user
    component: storage
spec:
  hard:
    requests.storage: 50Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "5"
    requests.cpu: "3"
    requests.memory: 6Gi
kubectl apply -f user-storage-quota.yaml
kubectl describe quota user-storage-quota -n user-service
Name: user-storage-quota Namespace: user-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- persistentvolumeclaims 2 5 requests.cpu 1 3 requests.memory 2Gi 6Gi requests.storage 20Gi 50Gi
Example 3: Object Count Quota for Author Service

Limit the number of Kubernetes objects that can be created for Author content management.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: author-object-quota
  namespace: author-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: author
spec:
  hard:
    pods: "15"
    services: "5"
    configmaps: "10"
    secrets: "10"
    replicationcontrollers: "5"
    deployments.apps: "5"
kubectl create -f author-object-quota.yaml
resourcequota/author-object-quota created
Example 4: Scoped Quota for Location Service

Create a ResourceQuota with scope selectors for Location geospatial services.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: location-priority-quota
  namespace: location-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: location
spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "5"
    requests.memory: 10Gi
    pods: "10"
  scopeSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - operator: In
      scopeName: PriorityClass
      values: ["high"]
kubectl apply -f location-priority-quota.yaml
kubectl get quota -n location-service -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 items: - apiVersion: v1 kind: ResourceQuota metadata: name: location-priority-quota namespace: location-service spec: hard: pods: "10" requests.cpu: "5" requests.memory: 10Gi scopeSelector: matchExpressions: - operator: In scopeName: PriorityClass values: - high status: hard: pods: "10" requests.cpu: "5" requests.memory: 10Gi used: pods: "3" requests.cpu: "2" requests.memory: 4Gi
Example 5: Comprehensive Quota for JobPost Service

Complete ResourceQuota covering compute, storage, and object counts for the main JobPost application.

apiVersion: v1
kind: ResourceQuota
metadata:
  name: jobpost-comprehensive-quota
  namespace: jobpost-service
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: jobpost
    tier: production
spec:
  hard:
    # Compute resources
    requests.cpu: "8"
    requests.memory: 16Gi
    limits.cpu: "16"
    limits.memory: 32Gi
    # Storage resources
    requests.storage: 200Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "15"
    # Object counts
    pods: "30"
    services: "8"
    configmaps: "15"
    secrets: "15"
    deployments.apps: "8"
    statefulsets.apps: "3"
    jobs.batch: "50"
    cronjobs.batch: "5"
kubectl apply -f jobpost-comprehensive-quota.yaml
kubectl describe quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service
Name: jobpost-comprehensive-quota Namespace: jobpost-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- configmaps 5 15 cronjobs.batch 2 5 deployments.apps 4 8 jobs.batch 12 50 limits.cpu 10 16 limits.memory 20Gi 32Gi persistentvolumeclaims 8 15 pods 18 30 requests.cpu 5 8 requests.memory 10Gi 16Gi requests.storage 120Gi 200Gi secrets 7 15 services 4 8 statefulsets.apps 1 3

ResourceQuotaSpec

ResourceQuotaSpec defines the desired hard limits to enforce for Quota.

Full Description

ResourceQuotaSpec defines the specific quota restrictions you want to enforce in a namespace for your anihpj Django application. The spec contains the hard limits for various resource types including compute (CPU, memory), storage (persistent volume claims), and Kubernetes object counts (pods, services). These limits are aggregated across all resources in the namespace, ensuring that your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services collectively stay within defined boundaries.

The spec also supports scope selectors that allow you to apply quotas conditionally based on pod characteristics like priority class or termination status. This enables more sophisticated quota strategies where, for example, high-priority JobPost batch processing can have different limits than regular web serving pods, all within the same namespace.

Syntax

# View spec of a ResourceQuota
kubectl get resourcequota skills-quota -n skills-service -o jsonpath='{.spec}'

# View hard limits only
kubectl get resourcequota user-quota -n user-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.hard}'

# Describe to see spec and status together
kubectl describe resourcequota jobpost-quota -n jobpost-service

Parameters Description

  • hard (map[string]Quantity): The set of desired hard limits for each named resource
    • requests.cpu: Total CPU requests allowed across all pods (e.g., "10")
    • requests.memory: Total memory requests allowed across all pods (e.g., "20Gi")
    • limits.cpu: Total CPU limits allowed across all pods (e.g., "20")
    • limits.memory: Total memory limits allowed across all pods (e.g., "40Gi")
    • requests.storage: Total storage requests for PVCs (e.g., "100Gi")
    • persistentvolumeclaims: Maximum number of PVCs (e.g., "10")
    • pods: Maximum number of pods (e.g., "50")
    • services: Maximum number of services (e.g., "10")
    • configmaps: Maximum number of ConfigMaps (e.g., "20")
    • secrets: Maximum number of Secrets (e.g., "20")
    • replicationcontrollers: Maximum number of ReplicationControllers
    • deployments.apps: Maximum number of Deployments
    • statefulsets.apps: Maximum number of StatefulSets
    • jobs.batch: Maximum number of Jobs
    • cronjobs.batch: Maximum number of CronJobs
  • scopeSelector (ScopeSelector): A collection of filters like scopes that must match each object tracked by quota
    • matchExpressions: List of scope selector requirements
      • operator: Relationship to values (In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist)
      • scopeName: Name of the scope (BestEffort, NotBestEffort, Terminating, NotTerminating, PriorityClass)
      • values: Array of string values for In/NotIn operators
  • scopes ([]string): Collection of filters that must match each object tracked by quota
    • Terminating: Match pods where spec.activeDeadlineSeconds >= 0
    • NotTerminating: Match pods where spec.activeDeadlineSeconds is nil
    • BestEffort: Match pods that have best effort quality of service
    • NotBestEffort: Match pods that do not have best effort QoS
    • PriorityClass: Match pods that have priority class mentioned

Return Value

Returns a ResourceQuotaSpec object containing the hard limits map and optional scope selectors that define which objects in the namespace are tracked by the quota.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Service Compute Spec

Define compute resource limits for the Skills microservice.

spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "4"
    requests.memory: 8Gi
    limits.cpu: "8"
    limits.memory: 16Gi
    pods: "20"
kubectl get quota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service -o jsonpath='{.spec.hard}' | jq
{ "limits.cpu": "8", "limits.memory": "16Gi", "pods": "20", "requests.cpu": "4", "requests.memory": "8Gi" }
Example 2: User Service with Priority Scope

Quota spec with priority-based scope selector for User authentication services.

spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "6"
    requests.memory: 12Gi
    pods: "15"
  scopeSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - operator: In
      scopeName: PriorityClass
      values: ["high", "critical"]
kubectl describe quota user-priority-quota -n user-service | grep -A 10 "Spec:"
Spec: Hard: Pods: 15 Requests Cpu: 6 Requests Memory: 12Gi Scope Selector: Match Expressions: Operator: In Scope Name: PriorityClass Values: high critical
Example 3: Author Service Storage and Object Limits

Comprehensive spec limiting storage and Kubernetes object counts for Author content.

spec:
  hard:
    requests.storage: 50Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "5"
    configmaps: "10"
    secrets: "8"
    services: "4"
    deployments.apps: "3"
kubectl get quota author-object-quota -n author-service -o yaml | grep -A 20 "spec:"
spec: hard: configmaps: "10" deployments.apps: "3" persistentvolumeclaims: "5" requests.storage: 50Gi secrets: "8" services: "4"
Example 4: Location Service with Terminating Scope

Quota spec using scopes array to track only terminating pods for Location batch jobs.

spec:
  hard:
    requests.cpu: "10"
    requests.memory: 20Gi
    pods: "30"
  scopes:
  - Terminating
kubectl get quota location-batch-quota -n location-service -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq
{ "hard": { "pods": "30", "requests.cpu": "10", "requests.memory": "20Gi" }, "scopes": [ "Terminating" ] }
Example 5: JobPost Service Multi-Resource Spec

Complete quota spec covering all resource types for the main JobPost application.

spec:
  hard:
    # Compute
    requests.cpu: "12"
    requests.memory: 24Gi
    limits.cpu: "24"
    limits.memory: 48Gi
    # Storage
    requests.storage: 150Gi
    persistentvolumeclaims: "12"
    # Objects
    pods: "40"
    services: "10"
    configmaps: "20"
    secrets: "15"
    deployments.apps: "8"
    statefulsets.apps: "4"
    jobs.batch: "100"
    cronjobs.batch: "10"
kubectl describe quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service
Name: jobpost-comprehensive-quota Namespace: jobpost-service Scopes: * Spec: Hard: Configmaps: 20 Cronjobs Batch: 10 Deployments Apps: 8 Jobs Batch: 100 Limits Cpu: 24 Limits Memory: 48Gi Persistentvolumeclaims: 12 Pods: 40 Requests Cpu: 12 Requests Memory: 24Gi Requests Storage: 150Gi Secrets: 15 Services: 10 Statefulsets Apps: 4

ResourceQuotaStatus

ResourceQuotaStatus defines the enforced hard limits and observed use.

Full Description

ResourceQuotaStatus provides real-time visibility into quota enforcement and current resource utilization across your anihpj Django application namespaces. The status shows both the enforced hard limits and the actual resource consumption, allowing you to monitor how close each namespace is to its quota limits. This is critical for capacity planning and preventing resource exhaustion in your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

Kubernetes continuously updates the status as resources are created and deleted within the namespace. When a resource request would exceed the quota, the API server rejects the request immediately. By monitoring the status regularly, you can proactively identify namespaces approaching their limits and adjust quotas or optimize resource usage before hitting constraints that would block new deployments or pod scaling.

Syntax

# View status of a ResourceQuota
kubectl get resourcequota skills-quota -n skills-service -o jsonpath='{.status}'

# View current usage vs hard limits
kubectl describe resourcequota user-quota -n user-service

# Get status in YAML format
kubectl get resourcequota jobpost-quota -n jobpost-service -o yaml | grep -A 20 "status:"

Parameters Description

  • hard (map[string]Quantity): The set of enforced hard limits for each named resource
    • Mirrors the spec.hard values once the quota is active
    • Shows the actual limits being enforced by the API server
    • Used by admission controllers to validate resource requests
  • used (map[string]Quantity): Current observed total usage of resources in the namespace
    • requests.cpu: Sum of CPU requests from all pods
    • requests.memory: Sum of memory requests from all pods
    • limits.cpu: Sum of CPU limits from all pods
    • limits.memory: Sum of memory limits from all pods
    • requests.storage: Sum of storage requests from all PVCs
    • persistentvolumeclaims: Current count of PVCs
    • pods: Current count of pods
    • services: Current count of services
    • configmaps: Current count of ConfigMaps
    • secrets: Current count of Secrets
    • deployments.apps: Current count of Deployments
    • jobs.batch: Current count of Jobs

Return Value

Returns a ResourceQuotaStatus object containing the enforced hard limits and current usage metrics for all tracked resources in the namespace.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Service Status Check

Monitor current quota usage for the Skills microservice.

kubectl describe quota skills-compute-quota -n skills-service
Name: skills-compute-quota Namespace: skills-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- limits.cpu 4 8 limits.memory 8Gi 16Gi pods 12 20 requests.cpu 2 4 requests.memory 4Gi 8Gi
Example 2: User Service Approaching Limits

View status showing User service nearing storage quota limits.

kubectl get quota user-storage-quota -n user-service -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{ "hard": { "persistentvolumeclaims": "5", "requests.cpu": "3", "requests.memory": "6Gi", "requests.storage": "50Gi" }, "used": { "persistentvolumeclaims": "4", "requests.cpu": "2500m", "requests.memory": "5Gi", "requests.storage": "45Gi" } }
Example 3: Author Service Object Count Status

Check how many Kubernetes objects are currently used vs allowed for Author content.

kubectl describe quota author-object-quota -n author-service
Name: author-object-quota Namespace: author-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- configmaps 6 10 deployments.apps 2 5 pods 8 15 secrets 5 10 services 3 5
Example 4: Location Service Quota Exceeded

Status showing Location service has hit pod limit.

kubectl get quota location-priority-quota -n location-service -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: ResourceQuota metadata: name: location-priority-quota namespace: location-service spec: hard: pods: "10" requests.cpu: "5" requests.memory: 10Gi status: hard: pods: "10" requests.cpu: "5" requests.memory: 10Gi used: pods: "10" requests.cpu: "4500m" requests.memory: 9Gi
Example 5: JobPost Service Comprehensive Status

Complete status view showing all resource usage for the main JobPost application.

kubectl describe quota jobpost-comprehensive-quota -n jobpost-service
Name: jobpost-comprehensive-quota Namespace: jobpost-service Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- configmaps 12 20 cronjobs.batch 3 10 deployments.apps 5 8 jobs.batch 45 100 limits.cpu 18 24 limits.memory 36Gi 48Gi persistentvolumeclaims 8 12 pods 28 40 requests.cpu 9 12 requests.memory 18Gi 24Gi requests.storage 100Gi 150Gi secrets 10 15 services 6 10 statefulsets.apps 2 4

ResourceQuotaList

ResourceQuotaList is a list of ResourceQuota items.

Full Description

ResourceQuotaList represents a collection of ResourceQuota objects returned by list operations in your anihpj Django application. When you query for ResourceQuotas across namespaces or within a specific namespace, the Kubernetes API returns a ResourceQuotaList containing all matching quotas along with their current specs and status. This is essential for getting a comprehensive view of resource constraints across your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

List operations are particularly useful for cluster administrators who need to audit quota configurations, identify namespaces without quotas, or compare resource allocations across different services. The list includes standard Kubernetes list metadata such as resource version for watch operations and continuation tokens for paginating through large result sets.

Syntax

# List all ResourceQuotas in a namespace
kubectl get resourcequotas -n skills-service

# List all ResourceQuotas across all namespaces
kubectl get resourcequotas --all-namespaces

# List with custom columns
kubectl get resourcequotas -n jobpost-service -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,CPU:.status.hard.requests\\.cpu,MEMORY:.status.hard.requests\\.memory

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion (string): API version, always "v1" for ResourceQuotaList
  • kind (string): Resource type, always "ResourceQuotaList"
  • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata
    • resourceVersion: Version of the resource list for watch operations
    • continue: Continuation token for pagination
    • remainingItemCount: Number of items not included in this response
  • items ([]ResourceQuota): Array of ResourceQuota objects
    • Each item is a complete ResourceQuota with metadata, spec, and status
    • Includes all quotas matching the query criteria
    • Empty array if no ResourceQuotas exist

Return Value

Returns a ResourceQuotaList object containing an array of ResourceQuota items along with list metadata for pagination and versioning.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: List ResourceQuotas in Skills Namespace

Get all ResourceQuotas defined for the Skills service.

kubectl get resourcequotas -n skills-service
NAME AGE REQUEST LIMIT skills-compute-quota 5d requests.cpu: 2/4, requests.memory: 4Gi/8Gi limits.cpu: 4/8, limits.memory: 8Gi/16Gi skills-storage-quota 5d requests.storage: 20Gi/50Gi, persistentvolumeclaims: 2/5
Example 2: List All Quotas Across anihpj Application

View ResourceQuotas from all namespaces for the complete jobpost application.

kubectl get resourcequotas --all-namespaces -l app=anihpj
NAMESPACE NAME AGE skills-service skills-compute-quota 5d user-service user-storage-quota 5d author-service author-object-quota 5d location-service location-priority-quota 5d jobpost-service jobpost-comprehensive-quota 5d
Example 3: List with Custom Columns

Display ResourceQuotas with specific columns for the User service.

kubectl get resourcequotas -n user-service -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,CPU-USED:.status.used.requests\\.cpu,CPU-HARD:.status.hard.requests\\.cpu,MEM-USED:.status.used.requests\\.memory,MEM-HARD:.status.hard.requests\\.memory
NAME CPU-USED CPU-HARD MEM-USED MEM-HARD user-storage-quota 2500m 3 5Gi 6Gi
