kubectl get crds -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,GROUP:.spec.group,SCOPE:.spec.scope | grep anihpj
NAME GROUP SCOPE contentauthors.anihpj.io anihpj.io Cluster jobpostings.anihpj.io anihpj.io Namespaced servicelocations.anihpj.io anihpj.io Namespaced skillrequirements.anihpj.io anihpj.io Namespaced userprofiles.anihpj.io anihpj.io Namespaced
Example 3: Count anihpj CRDs
# Count total number of anihpj custom resources
kubectl get crds | grep anihpj.io | wc -l
5
Example 4: Watch CRD Changes
# Monitor CRD creation/updates in real-time
kubectl get crds --watch
NAME CREATED AT jobpostings.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:20:00Z skillrequirements.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:22:00Z (watching for changes...)
Example 5: Extract CRD Names as JSON
# Get all anihpj CRD names in JSON format
kubectl get crds -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.group=="anihpj.io") | .metadata.name'
contentauthors.anihpj.io jobpostings.anihpj.io servicelocations.anihpj.io skillrequirements.anihpj.io userprofiles.anihpj.io

Operations

The following operations are available for managing CustomResourceDefinition resources in the anihpj jobpost application for defining and managing custom API extensions.

get - Read specified CustomResourceDefinition
get (status) - Read status of specified CustomResourceDefinition
list - List all CustomResourceDefinitions
create - Create a CustomResourceDefinition
update - Replace specified CustomResourceDefinition
update (status) - Replace status of specified CustomResourceDefinition
patch - Partially update specified CustomResourceDefinition
patch (status) - Partially update status of specified CustomResourceDefinition
delete - Delete a CustomResourceDefinition
deletecollection - Delete collection of CustomResourceDefinitions

get - Read the specified CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
GET /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the CustomResourceDefinition
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200 (CustomResourceDefinition): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: Get JobPosting CRD in YAML
# Retrieve complete JobPosting CRD definition
kubectl get customresourcedefinition jobpostings.anihpj.io -o yaml
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1 kind: CustomResourceDefinition metadata: name: jobpostings.anihpj.io creationTimestamp: "2025-01-15T10:20:00Z" spec: group: anihpj.io names: kind: JobPosting plural: jobpostings shortNames: [jp, jobpost] scope: Namespaced versions: - name: v1 served: true storage: true (schema details...)
Example 2: Describe SkillRequirement CRD
# Get detailed information about SkillRequirement CRD
kubectl describe customresourcedefinition skillrequirements.anihpj.io
Name: skillrequirements.anihpj.io API Version: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1 Kind: CustomResourceDefinition Spec: Group: anihpj.io Names: Kind: SkillRequirement Plural: skillrequirements Short Names: [sr, skill] Scope: Namespaced Status: Conditions: Type: Established Status: True
Example 3: Get UserProfile CRD in JSON
# Retrieve UserProfile CRD as JSON
kubectl get crd userprofiles.anihpj.io -o json | jq '.spec.names'
{ "categories": ["all"], "kind": "UserProfile", "plural": "userprofiles", "shortNames": ["up", "user"], "singular": "userprofile" }
Example 4: Extract ContentAuthor CRD Scope
# Get scope information for ContentAuthor
kubectl get crd contentauthors.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.spec.scope}'
Cluster
Example 5: Get ServiceLocation CRD Group
# Extract API group for ServiceLocation
kubectl get crd servicelocations.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.spec.group}'
anihpj.io

get (status) - Read status of the specified CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
GET /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the CustomResourceDefinition
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200 (CustomResourceDefinition): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: Check JobPosting CRD Status
# Get status subresource for JobPosting CRD
kubectl get crd jobpostings.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{ "acceptedNames": { "kind": "JobPosting", "plural": "jobpostings", "shortNames": ["jp", "jobpost"] }, "conditions": [ { "type": "Established", "status": "True", "lastTransitionTime": "2025-01-15T10:20:05Z" } ], "storedVersions": ["v1"] }
Example 2: Verify SkillRequirement Establishment
# Check if SkillRequirement CRD is established and ready
kubectl get crd skillrequirements.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Established")].status}'
True
Example 3: Get UserProfile Accepted Names
# View names actually being used for UserProfile
kubectl get crd userprofiles.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.acceptedNames.plural}'
userprofiles
Example 4: List ContentAuthor Stored Versions
# Get all versions persisted to storage
kubectl get crd contentauthors.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.storedVersions[*]}'
v1
Example 5: Check ServiceLocation Conditions
# Get all condition types for ServiceLocation CRD
kubectl get crd servicelocations.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{range .status.conditions[*]}{.type}{": "}{.status}{"\n"}{end}'
NamesAccepted: True Established: True

list - List or watch objects of kind CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
GET /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query, boolean): Allow watch bookmarks
  • continue (in query, string): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query, string): Field selector to filter
  • labelSelector (in query, string): Label selector to filter
  • limit (in query, integer): Maximum number to return
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
  • resourceVersion (in query, string): Resource version
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query, string): Resource version match strategy
  • sendInitialEvents (in query, boolean): Send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (in query, integer): Timeout in seconds
  • watch (in query, boolean): Watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (CustomResourceDefinitionList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: List All anihpj CRDs
# Show all 5 CustomResourceDefinitions for anihpj
kubectl get customresourcedefinitions | grep anihpj.io
NAME CREATED AT contentauthors.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:25:00Z jobpostings.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:20:00Z servicelocations.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z skillrequirements.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:22:00Z userprofiles.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:27:00Z
Example 2: Custom Columns for NAME/GROUP/SCOPE
# Display custom columns for all anihpj CRDs
kubectl get crds -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,GROUP:.spec.group,SCOPE:.spec.scope | grep anihpj
NAME GROUP SCOPE contentauthors.anihpj.io anihpj.io Cluster jobpostings.anihpj.io anihpj.io Namespaced servicelocations.anihpj.io anihpj.io Namespaced skillrequirements.anihpj.io anihpj.io Namespaced userprofiles.anihpj.io anihpj.io Namespaced
Example 3: Watch CRD Changes
# Monitor CRD creation/modification in real-time
kubectl get crds --watch | grep anihpj
jobpostings.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:20:00Z skillrequirements.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:22:00Z (watching for changes...)
Example 4: Extract CRD Names
# Get all anihpj CRD names as JSON array
kubectl get crds -o json | jq -r '[.items[] | select(.spec.group=="anihpj.io") | .metadata.name]'
["contentauthors.anihpj.io","jobpostings.anihpj.io","servicelocations.anihpj.io","skillrequirements.anihpj.io","userprofiles.anihpj.io"]
Example 5: Filter CRDs by Label
# List CRDs with specific label selector
kubectl get crds -l app=anihpj
NAME CREATED AT contentauthors.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:25:00Z jobpostings.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:20:00Z servicelocations.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z skillrequirements.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:22:00Z userprofiles.anihpj.io 2025-01-15T10:27:00Z

create - Create a CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
POST /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions
Parameters
  • body (CustomResourceDefinition, required): CRD object to create
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode (All, None)
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200 (CustomResourceDefinition): OK
  • 201 (CustomResourceDefinition): Created
  • 202 (CustomResourceDefinition): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: Create JobPosting CRD
# Create JobPosting custom resource definition
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: jobpostings.anihpj.io
spec:
  group: anihpj.io
  names:
    kind: JobPosting
    plural: jobpostings
    shortNames: [jp]
  scope: Namespaced
  versions:
  - name: v1
    served: true
    storage: true
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        properties:
          spec:
            type: object
            properties:
              title: {type: string, minLength: 5}
              description: {type: string}
EOF
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/jobpostings.anihpj.io created
Example 2: Create SkillRequirement CRD with Validation
# Create SkillRequirement with CEL validation rules
kubectl apply -f skillrequirement-crd.yaml
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/skillrequirements.anihpj.io created
Example 3: Create UserProfile CRD with Dry Run
# Test CRD creation without actually creating it
kubectl create -f userprofile-crd.yaml --dry-run=server
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/userprofiles.anihpj.io created (server dry run)
Example 4: Create ContentAuthor Cluster-Scoped CRD
# Create cluster-wide ContentAuthor CRD
kubectl create -f contentauthor-crd.yaml --field-manager=kubectl-create
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/contentauthors.anihpj.io created
Example 5: Create ServiceLocation CRD with Multiple Versions
# Create CRD supporting v1 and v1beta1 versions
kubectl apply -f servicelocation-crd.yaml
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/servicelocations.anihpj.io created

update - Replace the specified CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the CustomResourceDefinition
  • body (CustomResourceDefinition, required): Updated CRD object
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode (All, None)
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200 (CustomResourceDefinition): OK
  • 201 (CustomResourceDefinition): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: Update JobPosting Schema
# Add new field to JobPosting schema
kubectl apply -f jobposting-crd-updated.yaml
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/jobpostings.anihpj.io configured
Example 2: Add Short Name to SkillRequirement
# Update SkillRequirement to add "skillreq" short name
kubectl get crd skillrequirements.anihpj.io -o yaml | \
  sed 's/shortNames: \[sr, skill\]/shortNames: [sr, skill, skillreq]/' | \
  kubectl apply -f -
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/skillrequirements.anihpj.io configured
Example 3: Update UserProfile with Status Subresource
# Enable status subresource for UserProfile
kubectl apply -f userprofile-crd-with-status.yaml
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/userprofiles.anihpj.io configured
Example 4: Update ContentAuthor with Dry Run
# Test ContentAuthor CRD update without applying
kubectl apply -f contentauthor-crd-v2.yaml --dry-run=server
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/contentauthors.anihpj.io configured (server dry run)
Example 5: Add Printer Columns to ServiceLocation
# Update ServiceLocation with custom printer columns
kubectl apply -f servicelocation-crd-with-columns.yaml
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/servicelocations.anihpj.io configured

update (status) - Replace status of the specified CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the CustomResourceDefinition
  • body (CustomResourceDefinition, required): CRD with updated status
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode (All, None)
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200 (CustomResourceDefinition): OK
  • 201 (CustomResourceDefinition): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: Update JobPosting CRD Status (Note: Status is system-managed)
# Status is typically managed by the API server, not manually updated
# Check current status instead
kubectl get crd jobpostings.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{ "acceptedNames": {"kind": "JobPosting", "plural": "jobpostings"}, "conditions": [{"type": "Established", "status": "True"}], "storedVersions": ["v1"] }
Example 2: Check SkillRequirement Status Conditions
# View status conditions (system-managed)
kubectl get crd skillrequirements.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*].type}'
NamesAccepted Established
Example 3: Monitor UserProfile Status Updates
# Watch for status changes (system updates)
kubectl get crd userprofiles.anihpj.io -w -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Established")].status}'
True (watching...)
Example 4: Verify ContentAuthor Accepted Names
# Check accepted names in status (system-set)
kubectl get crd contentauthors.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.acceptedNames}'
{"categories":["all","anihpj"],"kind":"ContentAuthor","plural":"contentauthors","shortNames":["ca","author"],"singular":"contentauthor"}
Example 5: List ServiceLocation Stored Versions
# Get stored versions from status (system-managed)
kubectl get crd servicelocations.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.storedVersions[*]}'
v1

patch - Partially update the specified CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the CustomResourceDefinition
  • body (Patch, required): Patch object
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode (All, None)
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • force (in query, boolean): Force apply
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200 (CustomResourceDefinition): OK
  • 201 (CustomResourceDefinition): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: Add Category to JobPosting CRD
# Patch JobPosting to add "jobs" category
kubectl patch crd jobpostings.anihpj.io --type=json -p='[{"op":"add","path":"/spec/names/categories/-","value":"jobs"}]'
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/jobpostings.anihpj.io patched
Example 2: Update SkillRequirement Short Name
# Add "req" as additional short name
kubectl patch crd skillrequirements.anihpj.io --type=merge -p='{"spec":{"names":{"shortNames":["sr","skill","req"]}}}'
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/skillrequirements.anihpj.io patched
Example 3: Add Label to UserProfile CRD
# Patch metadata to add environment label
kubectl patch crd userprofiles.anihpj.io -p='{"metadata":{"labels":{"environment":"production"}}}'
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/userprofiles.anihpj.io patched
Example 4: Add Annotation to ContentAuthor CRD
# Patch to add description annotation
kubectl patch crd contentauthors.anihpj.io --type=merge -p='{"metadata":{"annotations":{"description":"Content authors for anihpj platform"}}}'
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/contentauthors.anihpj.io patched
Example 5: Update ServiceLocation Conversion Strategy
# Patch to update conversion from None to Webhook (requires webhook config)
kubectl patch crd servicelocations.anihpj.io --type=merge -p='{"spec":{"conversion":{"strategy":"None"}}}'
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/servicelocations.anihpj.io patched

patch (status) - Partially update status of the specified CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the CustomResourceDefinition
  • body (Patch, required): Patch object
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode (All, None)
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • force (in query, boolean): Force apply
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200 (CustomResourceDefinition): OK
  • 201 (CustomResourceDefinition): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: View JobPosting Status (System-Managed)
# Status is managed by API server, view current state
kubectl get crd jobpostings.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*]}' | jq
[ {"type": "NamesAccepted", "status": "True", "reason": "NoConflicts"}, {"type": "Established", "status": "True", "reason": "InitialNamesAccepted"} ]
Example 2: Monitor SkillRequirement Status
# Watch for system status updates
kubectl get crd skillrequirements.anihpj.io -w -o jsonpath='{.status.acceptedNames.kind}'
SkillRequirement (watching...)
Example 3: Check UserProfile Establishment
# Verify CRD is fully established (system status)
kubectl get crd userprofiles.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Established")].message}'
the initial names have been accepted
Example 4: Get ContentAuthor Status Timestamp
# Get last transition time for Established condition
kubectl get crd contentauthors.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Established")].lastTransitionTime}'
2025-01-15T10:25:05Z
Example 5: List ServiceLocation All Status Fields
# Get complete status object (system-managed)
kubectl get crd servicelocations.anihpj.io -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq 'keys'
["acceptedNames", "conditions", "storedVersions"]

delete - Delete a CustomResourceDefinition

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the CustomResourceDefinition
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode (All, None)
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query, integer): Grace period in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query, string): Propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 202 (Status): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: Delete JobPosting CRD
# Delete JobPosting custom resource definition and all instances
kubectl delete customresourcedefinition jobpostings.anihpj.io
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "jobpostings.anihpj.io" deleted
Example 2: Delete SkillRequirement with Confirmation
# Delete SkillRequirement CRD and wait for completion
kubectl delete crd skillrequirements.anihpj.io --wait=true
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "skillrequirements.anihpj.io" deleted
Example 3: Delete UserProfile CRD with Dry Run
# Test deletion without actually deleting
kubectl delete crd userprofiles.anihpj.io --dry-run=server
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "userprofiles.anihpj.io" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete ContentAuthor CRD Immediately
# Delete without grace period
kubectl delete crd contentauthors.anihpj.io --grace-period=0 --force
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. The resource may continue to run on the cluster indefinitely. customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "contentauthors.anihpj.io" force deleted
Example 5: Delete ServiceLocation CRD from File
# Delete CRD using original definition file
kubectl delete -f servicelocation-crd.yaml
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "servicelocations.anihpj.io" deleted

deletecollection - Delete collection of CustomResourceDefinitions

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/apiextensions.k8s.io/v1/customresourcedefinitions
Parameters
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • continue (in query, string): Continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode (All, None)
  • fieldSelector (in query, string): Field selector to filter
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query, integer): Grace period in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • labelSelector (in query, string): Label selector to filter
  • limit (in query, integer): Maximum number to delete
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty-print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query, string): Propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (in query, string): Resource version
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query, string): Resource version match strategy
  • sendInitialEvents (in query, boolean): Send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (in query, integer): Timeout in seconds
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Examples
Example 1: Delete All anihpj CRDs by Label
# Delete all CRDs with app=anihpj label
kubectl delete crds -l app=anihpj
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "contentauthors.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "jobpostings.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "servicelocations.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "skillrequirements.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "userprofiles.anihpj.io" deleted
Example 2: Delete Namespaced anihpj CRDs
# Delete only namespaced-scoped CRDs (JobPosting, SkillRequirement, UserProfile, ServiceLocation)
kubectl get crds -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.group=="anihpj.io" and .spec.scope=="Namespaced") | .metadata.name' | xargs kubectl delete crd
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "jobpostings.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "servicelocations.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "skillrequirements.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "userprofiles.anihpj.io" deleted
Example 3: Delete Collection with Dry Run
# Test deletion of all anihpj CRDs without executing
kubectl delete crds -l app=anihpj --dry-run=server
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "contentauthors.anihpj.io" deleted (server dry run) customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "jobpostings.anihpj.io" deleted (server dry run) customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "servicelocations.anihpj.io" deleted (server dry run) customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "skillrequirements.anihpj.io" deleted (server dry run) customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "userprofiles.anihpj.io" deleted (server dry run)
Example 4: Delete Specific CRDs by Name Pattern
# Delete JobPosting and SkillRequirement CRDs
for crd in jobpostings skillrequirements; do kubectl delete crd ${crd}.anihpj.io; done
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "jobpostings.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "skillrequirements.anihpj.io" deleted
Example 5: Delete anihpj Group CRDs
# Delete all CRDs in anihpj.io group using field selector
kubectl get crds -o name | grep anihpj.io | xargs kubectl delete
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "contentauthors.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "jobpostings.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "servicelocations.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "skillrequirements.anihpj.io" deleted customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io "userprofiles.anihpj.io" deleted

DeviceClass v1alpha3

DeviceClassSpec - Device configuration and selectors
DeviceClassList - Collection of device classes
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: DeviceClass v1alpha3 API | API Version: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3

DeviceClass is a vendor- or admin-provided resource that contains device configuration and selectors. Cluster scoped.

Full Description

DeviceClass enables Dynamic Resource Allocation (DRA) in your anihpj Django application by defining device configurations and selectors for hardware resources. As an alpha feature requiring the DynamicResourceAllocation feature gate, DeviceClass allows you to specify which devices are suitable for allocation and how they should be configured. Each DeviceClass in your jobpost application represents a category of devices (GPUs, network interfaces, storage devices) that can be claimed by pods.

DeviceClass uses CEL (Common Expression Language) expressions to define device selectors based on device attributes and capacities. For your anihpj services, you might define a GPUClass for Skills model training workloads, a StorageClass for User model data persistence, a NetworkClass for Author model content delivery, a ComputeClass for Location model geospatial processing, and a SpecializedHardwareClass for JobPost model analytics. The spec defines configuration parameters passed to drivers and selectors that determine which devices match the class.

Syntax

# Apply a DeviceClass configuration
kubectl apply -f <deviceclass-file>.yaml

# Get details of a specific DeviceClass
kubectl get deviceclass <name> -o yaml

# List all DeviceClasses in the cluster
kubectl get deviceclasses

# Delete a DeviceClass
kubectl delete deviceclass <name>

YAML Structure

apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-gpu-class
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: skills
    model: Skills
    device-type: gpu
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel:
      expression: |
        device.driver == "gpu.example.com" &&
        device.attributes["gpu.example.com"].model == "A100" &&
        device.capacity["gpu.example.com"].memory.isGreaterThan(quantity("40Gi"))
  config:
  - opaque:
      driver: gpu.example.com
      parameters:
        kind: GPUConfig
        apiVersion: v1
        enableMPS: true
        computeMode: default
        powerLimit: 300
  extendedResourceName: nvidia.com/gpu

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3" for DeviceClass
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "DeviceClass"
  • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard object metadata including name (required), labels, annotations
  • spec (DeviceClassSpec), required: Defines device allocation criteria and configuration
    • config ([]DeviceClassConfiguration): Configuration parameters for claimed devices
      • opaque (OpaqueDeviceConfiguration): Driver-specific configuration
        • driver (string), required: DNS subdomain identifying the driver (e.g., "gpu.example.com")
        • parameters (RawExtension), required: Arbitrary driver-specific data (max 10 Ki)
    • selectors ([]DeviceSelector): Criteria for matching suitable devices
      • cel (CELDeviceSelector): CEL expression for device selection
        • expression (string), required: CEL expression evaluating device suitability (max 10 Ki), with access to device.driver, device.attributes, device.capacity, device.allowMultipleAllocations
    • extendedResourceName (string): Extended resource name for pod requests (alpha field, must be unique cluster-wide)

Return Value

  • 200 (DeviceClass): OK - Request successful
  • 201 (DeviceClass): Created - DeviceClass successfully created
  • 202 (DeviceClass): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Model GPU DeviceClass

This example demonstrates creating a DeviceClass for NVIDIA A100 GPUs used in Skills model machine learning training.

apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-gpu-class
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: skills
    model: Skills
    device-type: gpu
    gpu-model: a100
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel:
      expression: |
        device.driver == "nvidia.com/gpu" &&
        device.attributes["nvidia.com"].model == "A100-SXM4-40GB" &&
        device.capacity["nvidia.com"].memory.isGreaterThan(quantity("35Gi")) &&
        device.attributes["nvidia.com"].cudaVersion.startsWith("12")
  config:
  - opaque:
      driver: nvidia.com/gpu
      parameters:
        kind: NVIDIAGPUConfig
        apiVersion: v1
        enableMPS: true
        computeMode: default
        powerLimit: 300
        persistence: true
  extendedResourceName: nvidia.com/gpu
# Create DeviceClass for Skills GPU training
kubectl apply -f skills-gpu-deviceclass.yaml
# Verify the DeviceClass
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-skills-gpu-class -o yaml
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-gpu-class created apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3 kind: DeviceClass metadata: name: anihpj-skills-gpu-class creationTimestamp: "2024-11-10T10:30:00Z" generation: 1 uid: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7g8h-9i0j-k1l2m3n4o5p6 spec: extendedResourceName: nvidia.com/gpu selectors: - cel: expression: | device.driver == "nvidia.com/gpu" && device.attributes["nvidia.com"].model == "A100-SXM4-40GB" && device.capacity["nvidia.com"].memory.isGreaterThan(quantity("35Gi")) && device.attributes["nvidia.com"].cudaVersion.startsWith("12")
Example 2: User Model Storage DeviceClass

This example shows creating a DeviceClass for NVMe storage devices used by User model authentication service.

apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-storage-class
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: user
    model: User
    device-type: storage
    storage-type: nvme
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel:
      expression: |
        device.driver == "storage.example.com" &&
        device.attributes["storage.example.com"].type == "nvme" &&
        device.capacity["storage.example.com"].iops.isGreaterThan(quantity("100000")) &&
        device.capacity["storage.example.com"].bandwidth.isGreaterThan(quantity("3Gi"))
  config:
  - opaque:
      driver: storage.example.com
      parameters:
        kind: StorageConfig
        apiVersion: v1
        filesystem: ext4
        encryption: aes256
        cachePolicy: writethrough
  extendedResourceName: example.com/nvme-storage
# Create DeviceClass for User storage
kubectl apply -f user-storage-deviceclass.yaml
# List all storage DeviceClasses
kubectl get deviceclasses -l device-type=storage
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-user-storage-class created NAME AGE anihpj-user-storage-class 5s
Example 3: Author Model Network DeviceClass

This example illustrates creating a DeviceClass for high-speed network interfaces used by Author model content delivery.

apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-network-class
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: author
    model: Author
    device-type: network
    speed: 100g
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel:
      expression: |
        device.driver == "network.example.com" &&
        device.attributes["network.example.com"].speed == "100Gbps" &&
        device.attributes["network.example.com"].type == "ethernet" &&
        device.attributes["network.example.com"].rdma == true
  config:
  - opaque:
      driver: network.example.com
      parameters:
        kind: NetworkConfig
        apiVersion: v1
        mtu: 9000
        offload: true
        rss: true
        queues: 32
# Create DeviceClass for Author network
kubectl apply -f author-network-deviceclass.yaml
# Describe the DeviceClass
kubectl describe deviceclass anihpj-author-network-class
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-author-network-class created Name: anihpj-author-network-class Labels: app=anihpj device-type=network model=Author service=author speed=100g Annotations: API Version: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3 Kind: DeviceClass Spec: Config: Opaque: Driver: network.example.com Parameters: {"kind":"NetworkConfig","apiVersion":"v1","mtu":9000,"offload":true,"rss":true,"queues":32} Selectors: Cel: Expression: device.driver == "network.example.com" && device.attributes["network.example.com"].speed == "100Gbps" && device.attributes["network.example.com"].type == "ethernet" && device.attributes["network.example.com"].rdma == true
Example 4: Location Model Compute DeviceClass

This example shows creating a DeviceClass for FPGA accelerators used by Location model geospatial processing.

apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-fpga-class
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: location
    model: Location
    device-type: fpga
    vendor: xilinx
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel:
      expression: |
        device.driver == "fpga.xilinx.com" &&
        device.attributes["fpga.xilinx.com"].family == "Alveo" &&
        device.attributes["fpga.xilinx.com"].model.matches("U[2-5][0-9]+") &&
        device.capacity["fpga.xilinx.com"].logicCells.isGreaterThan(quantity("1000000"))
  config:
  - opaque:
      driver: fpga.xilinx.com
      parameters:
        kind: FPGAConfig
        apiVersion: v1
        bitstream: geospatial-v2.bit
        clockFrequency: 300MHz
        enablePartialReconfiguration: true
  extendedResourceName: xilinx.com/fpga
# Create DeviceClass for Location FPGA processing
kubectl apply -f location-fpga-deviceclass.yaml
# Get DeviceClass in JSON format
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-location-fpga-class -o json
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-location-fpga-class created { "apiVersion": "resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3", "kind": "DeviceClass", "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-location-fpga-class", "creationTimestamp": "2024-11-10T11:45:20Z", "generation": 1, "labels": { "app": "anihpj", "device-type": "fpga", "model": "Location", "service": "location", "vendor": "xilinx" }, "uid": "f1g2h3i4-j5k6-7l8m-9n0o-p1q2r3s4t5u6" }, "spec": { "extendedResourceName": "xilinx.com/fpga", "selectors": [ { "cel": { "expression": "device.driver == \"fpga.xilinx.com\" &&\ndevice.attributes[\"fpga.xilinx.com\"].family == \"Alveo\" &&\ndevice.attributes[\"fpga.xilinx.com\"].model.matches(\"U[2-5][0-9]+\") &&\ndevice.capacity[\"fpga.xilinx.com\"].logicCells.isGreaterThan(quantity(\"1000000\"))\n" } } ] } }
Example 5: JobPost Model Specialized Hardware DeviceClass

This example demonstrates creating a DeviceClass for AI accelerators used by JobPost model analytics.

apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    service: jobpost
    model: JobPost
    device-type: ai-accelerator
    vendor: habana
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel:
      expression: |
        device.driver == "habana.ai/gaudi" &&
        device.attributes["habana.ai"].generation.isGreaterThanOrEqualTo(2) &&
        device.capacity["habana.ai"].memory.isGreaterThan(quantity("90Gi")) &&
        device.attributes["habana.ai"].bfloat16 == true
  config:
  - opaque:
      driver: habana.ai/gaudi
      parameters:
        kind: GaudiConfig
        apiVersion: v1
        enableHabanaContainers: true
        precisionMode: mixed
        memoryOptimization: true
        distributedTraining: true
  extendedResourceName: habana.ai/gaudi
# Create DeviceClass for JobPost AI accelerator
kubectl apply -f jobpost-ai-accelerator-deviceclass.yaml
# List all anihpj DeviceClasses
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class created NAME AGE anihpj-author-network-class 5m anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class 3s anihpj-location-fpga-class 2m anihpj-skills-gpu-class 10m anihpj-user-storage-class 8m

DeviceClassSpec

DeviceClassSpec defines what devices can be allocated and how to configure them for your anihpj Django application. The spec includes selectors for matching suitable devices and configuration parameters passed to device drivers for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

Syntax
# Extract DeviceClassSpec from a DeviceClass
kubectl get deviceclass <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec}'

# Get selectors from DeviceClassSpec
kubectl get deviceclass <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.selectors[*]}'

# Get configuration from DeviceClassSpec
kubectl get deviceclass <name> -o jsonpath='{.spec.config[*]}'
Parameters
  • config ([]DeviceClassConfiguration): Configuration parameters applied to claimed devices
    • opaque (OpaqueDeviceConfiguration): Driver-specific configuration
      • driver (string), required: DNS subdomain identifying the driver vendor
      • parameters (RawExtension), required: Arbitrary driver-specific configuration data (max 10 Ki)
  • selectors ([]DeviceSelector): Criteria determining which devices satisfy the class
    • cel (CELDeviceSelector): CEL expression for device matching
      • expression (string), required: CEL expression evaluating device suitability based on device.driver, device.attributes, device.capacity, device.allowMultipleAllocations (max 10 Ki)
  • extendedResourceName (string): Extended resource name for pod requests (alpha field)
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Extract Skills GPU DeviceClassSpec

This example demonstrates extracting the complete spec from Skills GPU DeviceClass.

# Extract full spec for Skills GPU class
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-skills-gpu-class -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq .
{ "config": [ { "opaque": { "driver": "nvidia.com/gpu", "parameters": { "kind": "NVIDIAGPUConfig", "apiVersion": "v1", "enableMPS": true, "computeMode": "default", "powerLimit": 300 } } } ], "extendedResourceName": "nvidia.com/gpu", "selectors": [ { "cel": { "expression": "device.driver == \"nvidia.com/gpu\" && device.attributes[\"nvidia.com\"].model == \"A100-SXM4-40GB\"" } } ] }
Example 2: Get User Storage Selectors

This example shows extracting CEL selectors from User storage DeviceClass.

# Get selector expression for User storage
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-user-storage-class -o jsonpath='{.spec.selectors[0].cel.expression}'
device.driver == "storage.example.com" && device.attributes["storage.example.com"].type == "nvme" && device.capacity["storage.example.com"].iops.isGreaterThan(quantity("100000"))
Example 3: Extract Author Network Configuration

This example illustrates extracting device configuration from Author network DeviceClass.

# Get opaque configuration for Author network
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-author-network-class -o jsonpath='{.spec.config[0].opaque}' | jq .
{ "driver": "network.example.com", "parameters": { "kind": "NetworkConfig", "apiVersion": "v1", "mtu": 9000, "offload": true, "rss": true, "queues": 32 } }
Example 4: Show Location FPGA Extended Resource Name

This example shows extracting the extended resource name from Location FPGA DeviceClass.

# Get extended resource name for Location FPGA
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-location-fpga-class -o jsonpath='{.spec.extendedResourceName}'
xilinx.com/fpga
Example 5: List JobPost AI Accelerator Spec Fields

This example demonstrates listing all spec fields from JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass.

# List spec field names for JobPost AI accelerator
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class -o json | jq '.spec | keys'
[ "config", "extendedResourceName", "selectors" ]

DeviceClassList

DeviceClassList is a collection of DeviceClass objects used for bulk retrieval, filtering, and management of device classes in your anihpj Django application. Lists support label selectors, field selectors, pagination, and watch operations across Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost device classes.

Syntax
# List all DeviceClasses
kubectl get deviceclasses

# List DeviceClasses with custom columns
kubectl get deviceclasses -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,DRIVER:.spec.config[0].opaque.driver,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp

# Get DeviceClassList in JSON format
kubectl get deviceclasses -o json
Parameters
  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3" for DeviceClassList
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "DeviceClassList"
  • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata including resourceVersion, continue token, remainingItemCount
  • items ([]DeviceClass), required: Array of DeviceClass objects
Return Value
  • 200 (DeviceClassList): OK - Request successful, DeviceClassList returned
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All anihpj DeviceClasses

This example demonstrates listing all DeviceClasses for the anihpj application.

# List all anihpj DeviceClasses
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj
NAME AGE anihpj-author-network-class 15m anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class 10m anihpj-location-fpga-class 12m anihpj-skills-gpu-class 20m anihpj-user-storage-class 18m
Example 2: Show DeviceClasses with Custom Columns

This example shows listing DeviceClasses with device type and driver information.

# List DeviceClasses with custom columns
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,TYPE:.metadata.labels.device-type,DRIVER:.spec.config[0].opaque.driver
NAME TYPE DRIVER anihpj-author-network-class network network.example.com anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class ai-accelerator habana.ai/gaudi anihpj-location-fpga-class fpga fpga.xilinx.com anihpj-skills-gpu-class gpu nvidia.com/gpu anihpj-user-storage-class storage storage.example.com
Example 3: Count Total DeviceClasses

This example illustrates counting the total number of anihpj DeviceClasses.

# Count anihpj DeviceClasses
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj --no-headers | wc -l
5
Example 4: Watch DeviceClass Changes

This example shows watching for real-time changes to DeviceClasses.

# Watch DeviceClass changes
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj --watch
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-gpu-class 20m anihpj-user-storage-class 18m anihpj-author-network-class 15m anihpj-location-fpga-class 12m anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class 10m anihpj-skills-gpu-class 20m MODIFIED
Example 5: Extract DeviceClass Names

This example demonstrates extracting only DeviceClass names from the list.

# Extract DeviceClass names
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
anihpj-author-network-class anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class anihpj-location-fpga-class anihpj-skills-gpu-class anihpj-user-storage-class

Operations

The following operations are available for managing DeviceClass resources in the anihpj jobpost application for defining device allocation and configuration.

get - Read the specified DeviceClass
list - List or watch DeviceClasses
create - Create a DeviceClass
update - Replace the specified DeviceClass
patch - Partially update the specified DeviceClass
delete - Delete a DeviceClass
deletecollection - Delete collection of DeviceClasses

Operations allow you to interact with DeviceClass resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage device allocation and configuration for your anihpj Django application workloads.

get - read the specified DeviceClass

Retrieve details of a specific DeviceClass by name.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/deviceclasses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the DeviceClass
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (DeviceClass): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills GPU DeviceClass YAML

This example demonstrates retrieving the complete YAML for Skills GPU DeviceClass.

# Get Skills GPU DeviceClass
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-skills-gpu-class -o yaml
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3 kind: DeviceClass metadata: creationTimestamp: "2024-11-10T10:30:00Z" generation: 1 labels: app: anihpj device-type: gpu model: Skills service: skills name: anihpj-skills-gpu-class uid: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7g8h-9i0j-k1l2m3n4o5p6 spec: config: - opaque: driver: nvidia.com/gpu parameters: apiVersion: v1 computeMode: default enableMPS: true kind: NVIDIAGPUConfig powerLimit: 300 extendedResourceName: nvidia.com/gpu selectors: - cel: expression: | device.driver == "nvidia.com/gpu" && device.attributes["nvidia.com"].model == "A100-SXM4-40GB"
Example 2: Describe User Storage DeviceClass

This example shows using describe to get detailed User storage DeviceClass information.

# Describe User storage DeviceClass
kubectl describe deviceclass anihpj-user-storage-class
Name: anihpj-user-storage-class Labels: app=anihpj device-type=storage model=User service=user storage-type=nvme Annotations: API Version: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3 Kind: DeviceClass Metadata: Creation Timestamp: 2024-11-10T10:35:15Z Generation: 1 UID: b2c3d4e5-f6g7-8h9i-0j1k-l2m3n4o5p6q7 Spec: Config: Opaque: Driver: storage.example.com Parameters: {"kind":"StorageConfig","apiVersion":"v1","filesystem":"ext4","encryption":"aes256"} Extended Resource Name: example.com/nvme-storage Selectors: Cel: Expression: device.driver == "storage.example.com" && device.attributes["storage.example.com"].type == "nvme" Events:
Example 3: Get Author Network DeviceClass JSON

This example illustrates retrieving Author network DeviceClass in JSON format.

# Get Author network DeviceClass as JSON
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-author-network-class -o json | jq '.spec'
{ "config": [ { "opaque": { "driver": "network.example.com", "parameters": { "kind": "NetworkConfig", "apiVersion": "v1", "mtu": 9000, "offload": true, "rss": true, "queues": 32 } } } ], "selectors": [ { "cel": { "expression": "device.driver == \"network.example.com\" &&\ndevice.attributes[\"network.example.com\"].speed == \"100Gbps\"\n" } } ] }
Example 4: Extract Location FPGA DeviceClass Selectors

This example shows extracting CEL selectors from Location FPGA DeviceClass.

# Extract selector expression from Location FPGA DeviceClass
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-location-fpga-class -o jsonpath='{.spec.selectors[0].cel.expression}'
device.driver == "fpga.xilinx.com" && device.attributes["fpga.xilinx.com"].family == "Alveo" && device.attributes["fpga.xilinx.com"].model.matches("U[2-5][0-9]+")
Example 5: Get JobPost AI Accelerator DeviceClass Labels

This example demonstrates extracting labels from JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass.

# Get labels from JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels}' | jq .
{ "app": "anihpj", "device-type": "ai-accelerator", "model": "JobPost", "service": "jobpost", "vendor": "habana" }

list - list or watch objects of kind DeviceClass

List all DeviceClass objects in the cluster with optional filtering and watching.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/deviceclasses
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): boolean - allowWatchBookmarks requests watch events with type "BOOKMARK"
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - filter by field (e.g., "metadata.name=anihpj-gpu")
  • labelSelector (in query): string - filter by labels (e.g., "app=anihpj,device-type=gpu")
  • limit (in query): integer - maximum number of results to return
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - specific resource version to list from
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query): string - how to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query): boolean - send initial events in watch mode
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout for the list/watch call
  • watch (in query): boolean - watch for changes after listing
Return Value
  • 200 (DeviceClassList): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All anihpj DeviceClasses

This example demonstrates listing all DeviceClasses for the anihpj application.

# List all anihpj DeviceClasses
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj
NAME AGE anihpj-author-network-class 15m anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class 10m anihpj-location-fpga-class 12m anihpj-skills-gpu-class 20m anihpj-user-storage-class 18m
Example 2: List DeviceClasses with Custom Columns

This example shows listing DeviceClasses with device type and model columns.

# List DeviceClasses showing type and model
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,TYPE:.metadata.labels.device-type,MODEL:.metadata.labels.model
NAME TYPE MODEL anihpj-author-network-class network Author anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class ai-accelerator JobPost anihpj-location-fpga-class fpga Location anihpj-skills-gpu-class gpu Skills anihpj-user-storage-class storage User
Example 3: Watch DeviceClass Changes

This example illustrates watching for real-time changes to anihpj DeviceClasses.

# Watch DeviceClass changes
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj --watch
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-gpu-class 20m anihpj-user-storage-class 18m anihpj-skills-gpu-class 20m MODIFIED anihpj-location-fpga-class 12m ADDED
Example 4: Extract DeviceClass Names

This example shows extracting only the names of all anihpj DeviceClasses.

# Extract DeviceClass names
kubectl get deviceclasses -l app=anihpj -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'
anihpj-author-network-class anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class anihpj-location-fpga-class anihpj-skills-gpu-class anihpj-user-storage-class
Example 5: Filter DeviceClasses by Device Type

This example demonstrates filtering DeviceClasses by device type label.

# List only GPU DeviceClasses
kubectl get deviceclasses -l device-type=gpu
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-gpu-class 20m

create - create a DeviceClass

Create a new DeviceClass resource in the cluster.

HTTP Request
POST /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/deviceclasses
Parameters
  • body: DeviceClass, required - the DeviceClass object to create
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting (values: "All")
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager making changes
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level (values: "Ignore", "Warn", "Strict")
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (DeviceClass): OK
  • 201 (DeviceClass): Created
  • 202 (DeviceClass): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills GPU DeviceClass

This example demonstrates creating a DeviceClass for Skills model GPU training.

# Create Skills GPU DeviceClass from file
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-gpu-class
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: Skills
    device-type: gpu
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel:
      expression: |
        device.driver == "nvidia.com/gpu" &&
        device.attributes["nvidia.com"].model == "A100-SXM4-40GB"
  config:
  - opaque:
      driver: nvidia.com/gpu
      parameters:
        kind: NVIDIAGPUConfig
        apiVersion: v1
        enableMPS: true
  extendedResourceName: nvidia.com/gpu
EOF
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-gpu-class created
Example 2: Create User Storage DeviceClass

This example shows creating a DeviceClass for User model NVMe storage.

# Create User storage DeviceClass
kubectl create -f user-storage-deviceclass.yaml
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-user-storage-class created
Example 3: Create Author Network DeviceClass with Dry Run

This example illustrates creating Author network DeviceClass with dry run validation.

# Dry run create Author network DeviceClass
kubectl apply -f author-network-deviceclass.yaml --dry-run=client
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-author-network-class created (dry run)
Example 4: Create Location FPGA DeviceClass

This example shows creating a DeviceClass for Location model FPGA accelerators.

# Create Location FPGA DeviceClass
kubectl apply -f location-fpga-deviceclass.yaml
# Verify creation
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-location-fpga-class
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-location-fpga-class created NAME AGE anihpj-location-fpga-class 5s
Example 5: Create JobPost AI Accelerator DeviceClass

This example demonstrates creating a DeviceClass for JobPost model AI accelerators.

# Create JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass
kubectl apply -f jobpost-ai-accelerator-deviceclass.yaml
# Get created DeviceClass details
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class -o yaml
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class created apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3 kind: DeviceClass metadata: name: anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class labels: app: anihpj model: JobPost device-type: ai-accelerator spec: extendedResourceName: habana.ai/gaudi selectors: - cel: expression: device.driver == "habana.ai/gaudi"

update - replace the specified DeviceClass

Replace the entire DeviceClass resource with a new version.

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/deviceclasses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the DeviceClass
  • body: DeviceClass, required - the updated DeviceClass object
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager making changes
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (DeviceClass): OK
  • 201 (DeviceClass): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills GPU DeviceClass Selectors

This example demonstrates updating CEL selectors for Skills GPU DeviceClass.

# Update Skills GPU DeviceClass with modified selectors
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3
kind: DeviceClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-gpu-class
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: Skills
spec:
  selectors:
  - cel:
      expression: |
        device.driver == "nvidia.com/gpu" &&
        device.attributes["nvidia.com"].model.matches("A100.*") &&
        device.capacity["nvidia.com"].memory.isGreaterThan(quantity("35Gi"))
  extendedResourceName: nvidia.com/gpu
EOF
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-gpu-class configured
Example 2: Update User Storage Configuration

This example shows updating opaque configuration for User storage DeviceClass.

# Update User storage DeviceClass configuration
kubectl apply -f user-storage-deviceclass-updated.yaml
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-user-storage-class configured
Example 3: Update Author Network DeviceClass with Dry Run

This example illustrates updating Author network DeviceClass with validation only.

# Dry run update Author network DeviceClass
kubectl apply -f author-network-deviceclass-v2.yaml --dry-run=server
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-author-network-class configured (server dry run)
Example 4: Update Location FPGA Extended Resource Name

This example shows updating the extended resource name for Location FPGA DeviceClass.

# Update Location FPGA DeviceClass
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-location-fpga-class -o yaml | \
  sed 's/extendedResourceName: xilinx.com\/fpga/extendedResourceName: xilinx.com\/fpga-alveo/' | \
  kubectl apply -f -
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-location-fpga-class configured
Example 5: Update JobPost AI Accelerator Labels

This example demonstrates adding labels to JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass.

# Update JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass with new labels
kubectl apply -f jobpost-ai-accelerator-deviceclass-v2.yaml
# Verify updated labels
kubectl get deviceclass anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class --show-labels
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class configured NAME AGE LABELS anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class 15m app=anihpj,device-type=ai-accelerator,model=JobPost,priority=high

patch - partially update the specified DeviceClass

Partially update a DeviceClass by applying a patch to specific fields.

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/deviceclasses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the DeviceClass
  • body: Patch, required - the patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query): string - name of the manager making changes
  • fieldValidation (in query): string - validation level
  • force (in query): boolean - force apply the patch
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (DeviceClass): OK
  • 201 (DeviceClass): Created
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills GPU DeviceClass Labels

This example demonstrates adding a label to Skills GPU DeviceClass.

# Add priority label to Skills GPU DeviceClass
kubectl patch deviceclass anihpj-skills-gpu-class --type merge -p '{
  "metadata": {
    "labels": {
      "priority": "high"
    }
  }
}'
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-gpu-class patched
Example 2: Patch User Storage DeviceClass Annotation

This example shows adding an annotation to User storage DeviceClass.

# Add annotation to User storage DeviceClass
kubectl patch deviceclass anihpj-user-storage-class --type merge -p '{
  "metadata": {
    "annotations": {
      "description": "High-performance NVMe storage for User authentication"
    }
  }
}'
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-user-storage-class patched
Example 3: Patch Author Network DeviceClass Config

This example illustrates updating configuration parameters for Author network DeviceClass.

# Update Author network DeviceClass config
kubectl patch deviceclass anihpj-author-network-class --type merge -p '{
  "spec": {
    "config": [{
      "opaque": {
        "driver": "network.example.com",
        "parameters": {
          "kind": "NetworkConfig",
          "apiVersion": "v1",
          "mtu": 9000,
          "queues": 64
        }
      }
    }]
  }
}'
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-author-network-class patched
Example 4: Patch Location FPGA DeviceClass with JSON Patch

This example shows using JSON patch to modify Location FPGA DeviceClass.

# JSON patch Location FPGA DeviceClass
kubectl patch deviceclass anihpj-location-fpga-class --type json -p '[
  {
    "op": "add",
    "path": "/metadata/labels/environment",
    "value": "production"
  }
]'
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-location-fpga-class patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost AI Accelerator Selector Expression

This example demonstrates updating CEL selector for JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass.

# Patch JobPost AI accelerator selector
kubectl patch deviceclass anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class --type merge -p '{
  "spec": {
    "selectors": [{
      "cel": {
        "expression": "device.driver == \"habana.ai/gaudi\" && device.attributes[\"habana.ai\"].generation >= 2"
      }
    }]
  }
}'
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class patched

delete - delete a DeviceClass

Delete a specific DeviceClass resource from the cluster.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/deviceclasses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path): string, required - name of the DeviceClass
  • body: DeleteOptions - options for deletion
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): boolean - ignore store read errors
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - deletion propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200 (DeviceClass): OK
  • 202 (DeviceClass): Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills GPU DeviceClass

This example demonstrates deleting the Skills GPU DeviceClass.

# Delete Skills GPU DeviceClass
kubectl delete deviceclass anihpj-skills-gpu-class
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-gpu-class" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Storage DeviceClass with Wait

This example shows deleting User storage DeviceClass and waiting for completion.

# Delete User storage DeviceClass and wait
kubectl delete deviceclass anihpj-user-storage-class --wait=true
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-user-storage-class" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author Network DeviceClass with Dry Run

This example illustrates validating deletion of Author network DeviceClass.

# Dry run delete Author network DeviceClass
kubectl delete deviceclass anihpj-author-network-class --dry-run=client
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-author-network-class" deleted (dry run)
Example 4: Delete Location FPGA DeviceClass Immediately

This example shows force deleting Location FPGA DeviceClass without grace period.

# Immediately delete Location FPGA DeviceClass
kubectl delete deviceclass anihpj-location-fpga-class --grace-period=0 --force
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. The resource may continue to run on the cluster indefinitely. deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-location-fpga-class" force deleted
Example 5: Delete JobPost AI Accelerator DeviceClass from File

This example demonstrates deleting JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass using file reference.

# Delete JobPost AI accelerator DeviceClass from file
kubectl delete -f jobpost-ai-accelerator-deviceclass.yaml
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class" deleted

deletecollection - delete collection of DeviceClass

Delete multiple DeviceClass resources matching specific criteria.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/resource.k8s.io/v1alpha3/deviceclasses
Parameters
  • body: DeleteOptions - options for deletion
  • continue (in query): string - continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (in query): string - simulate the operation without persisting
  • fieldSelector (in query): string - filter by field
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): integer - grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): boolean - ignore store read errors
  • labelSelector (in query): string - filter by labels
  • limit (in query): integer - maximum number of results
  • pretty (in query): string - pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): string - deletion propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (in query): string - specific resource version
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query): string - how to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query): boolean - send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): integer - timeout for the operation
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All anihpj DeviceClasses

This example demonstrates deleting all DeviceClasses with app=anihpj label.

# Delete all anihpj DeviceClasses
kubectl delete deviceclasses -l app=anihpj
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-author-network-class" deleted deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class" deleted deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-location-fpga-class" deleted deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-gpu-class" deleted deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-user-storage-class" deleted
Example 2: Delete GPU DeviceClasses with Dry Run

This example shows validating deletion of all GPU DeviceClasses.

# Dry run delete GPU DeviceClasses
kubectl delete deviceclasses -l device-type=gpu --dry-run=client
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-gpu-class" deleted (dry run)
Example 3: Delete DeviceClasses by Model

This example illustrates deleting DeviceClasses associated with specific models.

# Delete DeviceClasses for Skills and User models
kubectl delete deviceclasses -l 'model in (Skills,User)'
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-gpu-class" deleted deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-user-storage-class" deleted
Example 4: Delete DeviceClasses by Name Pattern

This example shows deleting DeviceClasses matching a name pattern.

# Delete DeviceClasses with "network" in name
kubectl get deviceclasses -o name | grep network | xargs kubectl delete
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-author-network-class" deleted
Example 5: Delete All Non-GPU DeviceClasses

This example demonstrates deleting DeviceClasses excluding GPU type.

# Delete all non-GPU DeviceClasses
kubectl delete deviceclasses -l 'app=anihpj,device-type!=gpu'
deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-author-network-class" deleted deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-ai-accelerator-class" deleted deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-location-fpga-class" deleted deviceclass.resource.k8s.io "anihpj-user-storage-class" deleted

MutatingWebhookConfiguration v1

MutatingWebhookConfiguration Overview

MutatingWebhookConfigurationList - Collection of webhook configurations
Operations - API operations for webhook management
Official Docs: MutatingWebhookConfiguration v1 API | API Version: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1

MutatingWebhookConfiguration describes the configuration of and admission webhook that accept or reject and may change the object.

Full Description

MutatingWebhookConfiguration enables dynamic admission control for your anihpj Django application by intercepting API requests and modifying objects before they are persisted. This powerful mechanism allows you to automatically inject sidecars into Skills training pods, enforce resource limits for User data processing, add default labels and annotations to Author content delivery pods, configure network policies for Location geospatial services, and apply security policies to JobPost analytics workloads.

Each webhook configuration defines rules that specify which operations (CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE) on which resources (pods, deployments, services) trigger the webhook. The API server calls your webhook service with an AdmissionReview object containing the proposed change, and your webhook can modify the object, add defaults, inject sidecars, or enforce organizational policies. Webhooks support namespace selectors for targeting specific namespaces (like anihpj-production or anihpj-staging), object selectors for filtering based on labels, and match conditions using CEL expressions for advanced logic.

Syntax

# Create a MutatingWebhookConfiguration from YAML
kubectl apply -f webhook-config.yaml

# Get specific webhook configuration
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfiguration anihpj-sidecar-injector

# List all webhook configurations
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations

# Delete a webhook configuration
kubectl delete mutatingwebhookconfiguration anihpj-sidecar-injector

YAML Structure

apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: anihpj-sidecar-injector
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    component: admission-control
webhooks:
- name: sidecar-injector.anihpj.svc
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: anihpj-webhook-service
      namespace: anihpj-system
      path: "/mutate-pods"
      port: 443
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTiBDRVJUSUZJQ0FURS0tLS0t...
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE"]
    apiGroups: [""]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["pods"]
    scope: "Namespaced"
  namespaceSelector:
    matchLabels:
      sidecar-injection: enabled
  objectSelector:
    matchLabels:
      inject-sidecar: "true"
  failurePolicy: Fail
  sideEffects: None
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1", "v1beta1"]
  timeoutSeconds: 10
  reinvocationPolicy: Never
  matchPolicy: Equivalent

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion (string): API version - admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
  • kind (string): Resource type - MutatingWebhookConfiguration
  • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard object metadata with name, labels, annotations
  • webhooks ([]MutatingWebhook): Array of webhook configurations, each defining:
    • name (string, required): Fully qualified webhook name (e.g., sidecar.anihpj.svc)
    • clientConfig (WebhookClientConfig, required): How to communicate with webhook
      • service (ServiceReference): In-cluster service reference with name, namespace, path, port
      • url (string): External webhook URL (mutually exclusive with service)
      • caBundle ([]byte): PEM encoded CA bundle for TLS verification
    • rules ([]RuleWithOperations): When to invoke webhook
      • operations ([]string, required): CREATE, UPDATE, DELETE, CONNECT, or *
      • apiGroups ([]string, required): API groups ("" for core, * for all)
      • apiVersions ([]string, required): API versions (v1, v1beta1, or *)
      • resources ([]string, required): Resources (pods, deployments, services, etc.)
      • scope (string): Cluster, Namespaced, or * (default)
    • namespaceSelector (LabelSelector): Filter by namespace labels using matchLabels or matchExpressions
    • objectSelector (LabelSelector): Filter by object labels using matchLabels or matchExpressions
    • matchConditions ([]MatchCondition): CEL expressions for advanced filtering with name and expression fields
    • failurePolicy (string): Fail (reject on error) or Ignore (default: Fail)
    • matchPolicy (string): Exact or Equivalent (default: Equivalent)
    • sideEffects (string, required): None, NoneOnDryRun, Some, or Unknown
    • admissionReviewVersions ([]string, required): Supported AdmissionReview versions (v1, v1beta1)
    • timeoutSeconds (int32): Webhook timeout 1-30 seconds (default: 10)
    • reinvocationPolicy (string): Never or IfNeeded (default: Never)

Return Value

  • 200 (MutatingWebhookConfiguration): OK - Request successful, configuration returned
  • 201 (MutatingWebhookConfiguration): Created - Configuration successfully created
  • 202 (MutatingWebhookConfiguration): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Model Sidecar Injection Webhook

This example demonstrates configuring a mutating webhook to automatically inject monitoring sidecars into Skills training pods.

apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-sidecar-injector
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: Skills
    purpose: sidecar-injection
webhooks:
- name: skills-sidecar.anihpj.svc
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: anihpj-webhook-service
      namespace: anihpj-system
      path: "/inject-skills-sidecar"
      port: 443
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTiBDRVJUSUZJQ0FURS0tLS0tCk1JSUN...
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    apiGroups: [""]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["pods"]
    scope: "Namespaced"
  namespaceSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      environment: production
  objectSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: model
      operator: In
      values: ["Skills"]
    - key: inject-monitoring
      operator: Exists
  failurePolicy: Fail
  sideEffects: None
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
  timeoutSeconds: 15
  reinvocationPolicy: Never
  matchPolicy: Equivalent
# Create the Skills sidecar injection webhook
kubectl apply -f skills-sidecar-injector.yaml

# Verify webhook was created
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfiguration anihpj-skills-sidecar-injector

# Test with a Skills pod (webhook will inject monitoring sidecar)
kubectl apply -f skills-training-pod.yaml
mutatingwebhookconfiguration.admissionregistration.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-sidecar-injector created NAME WEBHOOKS AGE anihpj-skills-sidecar-injector 1 0s pod/anihpj-skills-trainer created (with injected sidecar)
Example 2: User Model Resource Limit Enforcement Webhook

This example shows a webhook that automatically enforces resource limits on User data processing pods to prevent resource exhaustion.

apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-resource-enforcer
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: User
    purpose: resource-enforcement
webhooks:
- name: user-resources.anihpj.svc
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: anihpj-webhook-service
      namespace: anihpj-system
      path: "/enforce-user-resources"
      port: 443
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTiBDRVJUSUZJQ0FURS0tLS0tCk1JSURN...
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE"]
    apiGroups: ["apps"]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["deployments", "statefulsets"]
    scope: "Namespaced"
  namespaceSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
  objectSelector:
    matchLabels:
      model: User
  matchConditions:
  - name: missing-resource-limits
    expression: "!has(object.spec.template.spec.containers[0].resources.limits)"
  failurePolicy: Fail
  sideEffects: None
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
  timeoutSeconds: 10
  reinvocationPolicy: Never
  matchPolicy: Equivalent
# Apply User resource enforcement webhook
kubectl apply -f user-resource-enforcer.yaml

# Create User deployment (webhook adds resource limits automatically)
kubectl apply -f user-processor-deployment.yaml

# Verify limits were added
kubectl get deployment anihpj-user-processor -o yaml | grep -A 5 limits
mutatingwebhookconfiguration.admissionregistration.k8s.io/anihpj-user-resource-enforcer created deployment.apps/anihpj-user-processor created limits: cpu: "2" memory: 4Gi requests: cpu: "1" memory: 2Gi
Example 3: Author Model Label and Annotation Injection Webhook

This example demonstrates automatically adding organizational labels and annotations to Author content delivery pods for compliance and tracking.

apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-label-injector
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: Author
    purpose: metadata-injection
webhooks:
- name: author-metadata.anihpj.svc
  clientConfig:
    url: https://webhook.anihpj.example.com:8443/inject-author-metadata
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTiBDRVJUSUZJQ0FURS0tLS0tCk1JSURa...
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE"]
    apiGroups: ["", "apps"]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["pods", "deployments", "services"]
    scope: "Namespaced"
  namespaceSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: app
      operator: In
      values: ["anihpj"]
    - key: environment
      operator: In
      values: ["production", "staging"]
  objectSelector:
    matchLabels:
      model: Author
  failurePolicy: Ignore
  sideEffects: None
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1", "v1beta1"]
  timeoutSeconds: 5
  reinvocationPolicy: Never
  matchPolicy: Equivalent
# Create Author metadata injection webhook
kubectl apply -f author-label-injector.yaml

# Deploy Author service (webhook adds compliance labels/annotations)
kubectl apply -f author-content-service.yaml

# Inspect injected metadata
kubectl get pod -l model=Author -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata}' | jq
mutatingwebhookconfiguration.admissionregistration.k8s.io/anihpj-author-label-injector created service/anihpj-author-content created { "labels": { "model": "Author", "compliance": "gdpr-enabled", "data-classification": "pii", "cost-center": "content-delivery" }, "annotations": { "audit.anihpj.com/created-by": "webhook-injector", "compliance.anihpj.com/gdpr": "true" } }
Example 4: Location Model Network Policy Injection Webhook

This example shows a webhook that automatically injects network policy configurations into Location geospatial service pods for security.

apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-network-injector
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: Location
    purpose: network-security
webhooks:
- name: location-network.anihpj.svc
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: anihpj-webhook-service
      namespace: anihpj-system
      path: "/inject-location-network"
      port: 443
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTiBDRVJUSUZJQ0FURS0tLS0tCk1JSURu...
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE"]
    apiGroups: [""]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["pods"]
    scope: "Namespaced"
  namespaceSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      security-zone: restricted
  objectSelector:
    matchExpressions:
    - key: model
      operator: In
      values: ["Location"]
    - key: network-policy
      operator: DoesNotExist
  matchConditions:
  - name: requires-network-isolation
    expression: "object.metadata.labels['security-level'] == 'high'"
  failurePolicy: Fail
  sideEffects: None
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
  timeoutSeconds: 10
  reinvocationPolicy: Never
  matchPolicy: Exact
# Apply Location network policy injection webhook
kubectl apply -f location-network-injector.yaml

# Create Location pod with high security (webhook adds network annotations)
kubectl apply -f location-geospatial-pod.yaml

# Verify network policy annotations
kubectl get pod anihpj-location-processor -o yaml | grep -A 5 annotations
mutatingwebhookconfiguration.admissionregistration.k8s.io/anihpj-location-network-injector created pod/anihpj-location-processor created annotations: network-policy.anihpj.com/egress: "database,cache" network-policy.anihpj.com/ingress: "api-gateway" network-policy.anihpj.com/isolation: "enabled" security.anihpj.com/tls-required: "true"
Example 5: JobPost Model Security Policy Enforcement Webhook

This example demonstrates a comprehensive webhook that enforces security policies on JobPost analytics workloads including pod security standards and image validation.

apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: MutatingWebhookConfiguration
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-security-enforcer
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: JobPost
    purpose: security-enforcement
webhooks:
- name: jobpost-security.anihpj.svc
  clientConfig:
    service:
      name: anihpj-webhook-service
      namespace: anihpj-system
      path: "/enforce-jobpost-security"
      port: 443
    caBundle: LS0tLS1CRUdJTiBDRVJUSUZJQ0FURS0tLS0tCk1JSURy...
  rules:
  - operations: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    apiGroups: ["", "apps", "batch"]
    apiVersions: ["v1"]
    resources: ["pods", "deployments", "jobs", "cronjobs"]
    scope: "Namespaced"
  namespaceSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      environment: production
  objectSelector:
    matchLabels:
      model: JobPost
  matchConditions:
  - name: requires-security-context
    expression: "!has(object.spec.template.spec.securityContext) || !has(object.spec.template.spec.containers[0].securityContext)"
  - name: image-not-verified
    expression: "!object.metadata.annotations.exists(a, a.startsWith('image-verified'))"
  failurePolicy: Fail
  sideEffects: None
  admissionReviewVersions: ["v1"]
  timeoutSeconds: 20
  reinvocationPolicy: IfNeeded
  matchPolicy: Equivalent
# Create JobPost security enforcement webhook
kubectl apply -f jobpost-security-enforcer.yaml

# Deploy JobPost analytics job (webhook adds security context and image verification)
kubectl apply -f jobpost-analytics-job.yaml

# Verify security enhancements
kubectl get job anihpj-jobpost-analytics -o yaml | grep -A 10 securityContext
mutatingwebhookconfiguration.admissionregistration.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-security-enforcer created job.batch/anihpj-jobpost-analytics created securityContext: runAsNonRoot: true runAsUser: 1000 fsGroup: 2000 seccompProfile: type: RuntimeDefault containers: - securityContext: allowPrivilegeEscalation: false readOnlyRootFilesystem: true capabilities: drop: ["ALL"]

MutatingWebhookConfigurationList

MutatingWebhookConfigurationList is a collection of MutatingWebhookConfiguration objects for bulk retrieval, filtering by labels and fields, pagination with continue tokens, and watching for real-time changes in webhook configurations across your anihpj cluster.

Syntax

# List all webhook configurations
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations

# List with custom columns showing webhook count and age
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,WEBHOOKS:.webhooks[*].name,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp

# Get list in JSON format for processing
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfigurations -o json

Parameters

  • apiVersion (string): API version - admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
  • kind (string): Resource type - MutatingWebhookConfigurationList
  • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata with continue token, resource version, remaining item count
  • items ([]MutatingWebhookConfiguration, required): Array of MutatingWebhookConfiguration objects in the list

Return Value
