HTTP Request

DELETE /apis/events.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/events/{name}

Parameters
  • name (path, string, required): Name of the Event
  • namespace (path, string, required): Namespace of the Event
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • dryRun (query, string): Dry run mode
  • gracePeriodSeconds (query, integer): Grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • pretty (query, string): Pretty-print output
  • propagationPolicy (query, string): Propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200: Status - OK
  • 202: Status - Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Obsolete Event for Skills Deployment

This example demonstrates deleting an obsolete event for Skills deployment cleanup.

# Delete specific event
kubectl delete event skills-deployment-custom.event001 -n skills
event.events.k8s.io "skills-deployment-custom.event001" deleted
Example 2: Delete Test Event for User Service

This example shows deleting a test event from User service namespace.

# Delete event with confirmation
kubectl delete event user-auth-maintenance.warn001 -n user-service --wait=true
event.events.k8s.io "user-auth-maintenance.warn001" deleted
Example 3: Delete Multiple Events for Author Content

This example demonstrates deleting multiple events using label selector for Author content.

# Delete events by label (if labeled)
kubectl delete event author-content-update.event002 author-content-sync.event005 -n author-content
event.events.k8s.io "author-content-update.event002" deleted event.events.k8s.io "author-content-sync.event005" deleted
Example 4: Delete Event from File for Location Service

This example shows deleting an event using YAML file for Location service.

# Delete event from file
kubectl delete -f location-scale-event.yaml
event.events.k8s.io "location-api-scale.event003" deleted
Example 5: Delete Event with Dry Run for JobPost

This example demonstrates dry-run deletion to verify JobPost event before actual deletion.

# Dry run delete to verify
kubectl delete event jobpost-backup-complete.event004 -n jobpost-prod --dry-run=client
event.events.k8s.io "jobpost-backup-complete.event004" deleted (dry run)

deletecollection - Delete Collection of Events

HTTP Request

DELETE /apis/events.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/events

Parameters
  • namespace (path, string, required): Namespace to delete events from
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • continue (query, string): Continue token
  • dryRun (query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldSelector (query, string): Field selector to filter events
  • gracePeriodSeconds (query, integer): Grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • labelSelector (query, string): Label selector to filter events
  • limit (query, integer): Limit number of deletions
  • pretty (query, string): Pretty-print output
  • propagationPolicy (query, string): Propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (query, string): Resource version
  • resourceVersionMatch (query, string): Resource version match strategy
  • sendInitialEvents (query, boolean): Send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (query, integer): Timeout
Return Value
  • 200: Status - OK
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All Events in Skills Namespace

This example demonstrates deleting all events in Skills namespace for cleanup.

# Delete all events in namespace
kubectl delete events --all -n skills
event.events.k8s.io "skills-deployment-custom.event001" deleted event.events.k8s.io "skills-pod-abc123.scheduled" deleted event.events.k8s.io "skills-pod-abc123.started" deleted (15 events deleted in total)
Example 2: Delete Warning Events for User Service

This example shows deleting only warning events in User service namespace.

# Delete warning events using field selector
kubectl delete events -n user-service --field-selector type=Warning
event.events.k8s.io "user-auth-xyz789.failed" deleted event.events.k8s.io "user-auth-xyz789.backoff" deleted event.events.k8s.io "user-auth-maintenance.warn001" deleted (8 warning events deleted)
Example 3: Delete Old Events for Author Content

This example demonstrates deleting events older than 1 hour for Author content cleanup.

# Delete events using field selector for old events
kubectl get events -n author-content --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.lastTimestamp < (now - 3600 | todate)) | .metadata.name' | \
  xargs -I {} kubectl delete event {} -n author-content
event.events.k8s.io "author-content-update.event002" deleted event.events.k8s.io "author-content-pod-def456.scheduled" deleted event.events.k8s.io "author-content-pod-def456.started" deleted (12 old events deleted)
Example 4: Delete Events for Specific Location Object

This example shows deleting all events related to a specific Location pod.

# Delete events for specific object
kubectl delete events -n location --field-selector involvedObject.name=location-api-ghi789
event.events.k8s.io "location-api-ghi789.scheduled" deleted event.events.k8s.io "location-api-ghi789.pulling" deleted event.events.k8s.io "location-api-ghi789.pulled" deleted event.events.k8s.io "location-api-ghi789.created" deleted event.events.k8s.io "location-api-ghi789.started" deleted (5 events deleted)
Example 5: Dry Run Delete Collection for JobPost

This example demonstrates dry-run deletion of all events in JobPost namespace.

# Dry run delete all events
kubectl delete events --all -n jobpost-prod --dry-run=client
event.events.k8s.io "jobpost-api-abc-1.scheduled" deleted (dry run) event.events.k8s.io "jobpost-api-abc-1.pulling" deleted (dry run) event.events.k8s.io "jobpost-api-abc-1.pulled" deleted (dry run) event.events.k8s.io "jobpost-api-abc-1.created" deleted (dry run) event.events.k8s.io "jobpost-api-abc-1.started" deleted (dry run) event.events.k8s.io "jobpost-backup-complete.event004" deleted (dry run) (23 events would be deleted)

IPAddress

IPAddress Overview

IPAddressSpec - Attributes of an IP Address
IPAddressList - Collection of IPAddress objects
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: IPAddress v1 API | API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1

IPAddress represents a single IP of a single IP Family, designed for APIs that operate on IP addresses.

Full Description

IPAddress represents a single IP of a single IP Family for your anihpj Django application cluster. The object is designed to be used by APIs that operate on IP addresses, primarily used by the Service core API for allocation of IP addresses. IPAddress ensures uniqueness by using the IP address in canonical format as the object name: four decimal digits separated by dots suppressing leading zeros for IPv4, and the representation defined by RFC 5952 for IPv6. This resource is essential for managing network connectivity for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

Valid IP address formats include 192.168.1.5 (IPv4), 2001:db8::1 (IPv6), or 2001:db8:aaaa:bbbb:cccc:dddd:eeee:1 (full IPv6). Invalid formats include 10.01.2.3 (leading zeros) or 2001:db8:0:0:0::1 (incorrect compression). IPAddress objects reference parent resources through ParentReference, establishing relationships between IP addresses and the Kubernetes resources that use them, such as Services or LoadBalancers for your application workloads.

Syntax
# Get a specific IP address
kubectl get ipaddress <ip-canonical-name>

# List all IP addresses
kubectl get ipaddresses

# Get IP address in YAML format
kubectl get ipaddress <ip-canonical-name> -o yaml

# Describe an IP address
kubectl describe ipaddress <ip-canonical-name>

# Watch IP address changes
kubectl get ipaddresses --watch
YAML Structure
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 192.168.1.100
spec:
  parentRef:
    group: ""
    resource: services
    namespace: skills
    name: skills-api-service
Parameters Description
  • apiVersion (string, required): networking.k8s.io/v1 - API version
  • kind (string, required): IPAddress - Resource type
  • metadata (ObjectMeta, required): Standard object metadata, name must be IP in canonical format
  • spec (IPAddressSpec, required): Desired state of the IPAddress
Return Value
  • 200: IPAddress object (for read operations)
  • 201: IPAddress created successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get IP Address for Skills Service

This example demonstrates getting a specific IP address allocated to Skills service.

# Get IP address by canonical name
kubectl get ipaddress 192.168.1.100
NAME PARENTREF 192.168.1.100 services/skills-api-service
Example 2: List All IP Addresses for User Services

This example shows listing all IP addresses in the cluster for User service tracking.

# List all IP addresses
kubectl get ipaddresses
NAME PARENTREF 192.168.1.100 services/skills-api-service 192.168.1.101 services/user-auth-service 192.168.1.102 services/author-content-service 2001:db8::1 services/location-api-service-ipv6
Example 3: Get IP Address Details for Author Content Service

This example demonstrates getting detailed IP address information in YAML format.

# Get IP address details in YAML
kubectl get ipaddress 192.168.1.102 -o yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: IPAddress metadata: name: 192.168.1.102 spec: parentRef: group: "" resource: services namespace: author-content name: author-content-service
Example 4: Describe IP Address for Location Service

This example shows describing an IP address to see its parent reference details.

# Describe IP address
kubectl describe ipaddress 2001:db8::1
Name: 2001:db8::1 API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1 Kind: IPAddress Spec: Parent Ref: Group: Resource: services Namespace: location Name: location-api-service-ipv6
Example 5: Watch IP Address Changes for JobPost Services

This example demonstrates watching IP address changes in real-time for monitoring.

# Watch IP address changes
kubectl get ipaddresses --watch
NAME PARENTREF 192.168.1.100 services/skills-api-service 192.168.1.101 services/user-auth-service 192.168.1.103 services/jobpost-api-service ADDED 192.168.1.104 services/jobpost-worker-service ADDED

IPAddressSpec

Official Docs: IPAddress v1 API | API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1

IPAddressSpec describes the attributes in an IP Address.

Full Description

IPAddressSpec describe the attributes in an IP Address for your anihpj Django application cluster. The spec defines the parent reference that links an IP address to its associated Kubernetes resource. This relationship is crucial for tracking which IP addresses are allocated to which services, allowing proper network management and troubleshooting for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost workloads.

The ParentReference is a required field that establishes the ownership and usage of an IP address. Every IPAddress must reference a parent object, ensuring that IP allocations are always associated with active Kubernetes resources. This prevents orphaned IP addresses and enables proper cleanup when services are deleted. The parent reference includes the resource type (typically services), namespace, and name of the object using the IP address.

Syntax
spec:
  parentRef:
    group: ""                    # API group (empty for core resources)
    resource: services           # Resource type (required)
    namespace: <namespace>       # Namespace of parent
    name: <resource-name>        # Name of parent (required)
Parameters Description
  • parentRef (ParentReference, required): References the resource that an IPAddress is attached to
  • parentRef.name (string, required): Name of the object being referenced
  • parentRef.resource (string, required): Resource type of the object being referenced
  • parentRef.group (string): API group of the object being referenced
  • parentRef.namespace (string): Namespace of the object being referenced
Return Value

Returns an IPAddressSpec object as part of the IPAddress resource.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: IPAddressSpec for Skills Service

This example demonstrates IP address spec referencing a Skills service.

# skills-ip-spec.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 192.168.1.100
spec:
  parentRef:
    group: ""
    resource: services
    namespace: skills
    name: skills-api-service
IPAddress spec references Skills API service in skills namespace
Example 2: IPAddressSpec for User Auth Service

This example shows IP address spec with parent reference to User authentication service.

# user-auth-ip-spec.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 192.168.1.101
spec:
  parentRef:
    group: ""
    resource: services
    namespace: user-service
    name: user-auth-service
IPAddress spec references User auth service in user-service namespace
Example 3: IPv6 IPAddressSpec for Author Content

This example demonstrates IPv6 address spec for Author content service.

# author-ipv6-spec.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 2001:db8::1
spec:
  parentRef:
    group: ""
    resource: services
    namespace: author-content
    name: author-cms-service
IPv6 IPAddress spec references Author CMS service
Example 4: IPAddressSpec for Location LoadBalancer

This example shows IP address spec referencing a Location service LoadBalancer.

# location-lb-ip-spec.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 203.0.113.50
spec:
  parentRef:
    group: ""
    resource: services
    namespace: location
    name: location-api-loadbalancer
IPAddress spec references Location API LoadBalancer service
Example 5: IPAddressSpec for JobPost Service

This example demonstrates IP address spec for JobPost production service.

# jobpost-ip-spec.yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 192.168.1.103
spec:
  parentRef:
    group: ""
    resource: services
    namespace: jobpost-prod
    name: jobpost-api-service
IPAddress spec references JobPost API service in production namespace

IPAddressList

Official Docs: IPAddress v1 API | API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1

IPAddressList contains a list of IPAddress objects.

Full Description

IPAddressList represents a collection of IPAddress resources in your anihpj Django application cluster. This list structure is used when querying multiple IP addresses at once, providing a comprehensive view of all allocated IPs across your cluster. The list includes metadata about the collection and an array of IPAddress items, enabling efficient retrieval and management of IP address resources for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

The list structure supports filtering and sorting operations, allowing you to query IP addresses based on labels, parent references, or other criteria. This is particularly useful for auditing IP allocations, tracking which services are using which addresses, and ensuring proper network configuration. Each item in the list is a full IPAddress object with its metadata and specification, providing complete visibility into your cluster's IP address management.

Syntax
# List all IP addresses
kubectl get ipaddresses

# List with wide output
kubectl get ipaddresses -o wide

# List in YAML format
kubectl get ipaddresses -o yaml

# List in JSON format
kubectl get ipaddresses -o json

# Count IP addresses
kubectl get ipaddresses --no-headers | wc -l
Parameters Description
  • apiVersion (string, required): networking.k8s.io/v1 - API version
  • kind (string, required): IPAddressList - Resource type
  • metadata (ListMeta, required): Standard list metadata
  • items ([]IPAddress, required): List of IPAddress objects
Return Value
  • 200: IPAddressList object containing matching IPAddress resources
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All IP Addresses

This example demonstrates listing all IP addresses in the cluster.

# List all IP addresses
kubectl get ipaddresses
NAME PARENTREF 192.168.1.100 services/skills-api-service 192.168.1.101 services/user-auth-service 192.168.1.102 services/author-content-service 192.168.1.103 services/jobpost-api-service 2001:db8::1 services/location-api-service-ipv6
Example 2: Export IP Addresses to JSON

This example shows exporting all IP addresses to JSON format for processing.

# Export IP addresses to JSON
kubectl get ipaddresses -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, parent: .spec.parentRef.name}'
{ "name": "192.168.1.100", "parent": "skills-api-service" } { "name": "192.168.1.101", "parent": "user-auth-service" } { "name": "192.168.1.102", "parent": "author-content-service" }
Example 3: Export IP Addresses to YAML

This example demonstrates exporting IP addresses list to YAML for backup.

# Export IP addresses to YAML
kubectl get ipaddresses -o yaml > ipaddresses-backup.yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: List items: - apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: IPAddress metadata: name: 192.168.1.100 spec: parentRef: resource: services name: skills-api-service namespace: skills - apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: IPAddress metadata: name: 192.168.1.101 spec: parentRef: resource: services name: user-auth-service namespace: user-service (Exported to ipaddresses-backup.yaml)
Example 4: Count IP Addresses

This example shows counting total IP addresses allocated in the cluster.

# Count IP addresses
kubectl get ipaddresses --no-headers | wc -l
5
Example 5: List IPv4 vs IPv6 Addresses

This example demonstrates filtering IP addresses by protocol version.

# List IPv4 addresses (contains dots)
kubectl get ipaddresses -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.metadata.name | contains(".")) | .metadata.name'

# List IPv6 addresses (contains colons)
kubectl get ipaddresses -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.metadata.name | contains(":")) | .metadata.name'
"192.168.1.100" "192.168.1.101" "192.168.1.102" "192.168.1.103" "2001:db8::1"

IPAddress Operations

Available Operations

get - Read specific IPAddress
list - List IPAddresses
create - Create new IPAddress
update - Replace IPAddress
patch - Partially update IPAddress
delete - Delete IPAddress
deletecollection - Delete multiple IPAddresses

IPAddress operations enable IP address management in your anihpj Django cluster, supporting CRUD operations for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost service IP allocation.

read the specified IPAddress

HTTP Request
GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ipaddresses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, required): Name of the IPAddress (IP in canonical format)
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200: IPAddress - The specified IPAddress object
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Service IP Address

This example demonstrates retrieving IP address allocated to Skills API service.

# Get specific IP address
kubectl get ipaddress 192.168.1.100
NAME PARENTREF 192.168.1.100 services/skills-api-service
Example 2: Get User Service IP in YAML

This example shows getting IP address details in YAML format for User service.

# Get IP address in YAML format
kubectl get ipaddress 192.168.1.101 -o yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: IPAddress metadata: creationTimestamp: "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z" name: 192.168.1.101 resourceVersion: "12345" uid: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 spec: parentRef: group: "" resource: services namespace: user-service name: user-auth-service
Example 3: Get Author Service IPv6 Address

This example demonstrates getting IPv6 address for Author content service.

# Get IPv6 address
kubectl get ipaddress 2001:db8::1 -o wide
NAME PARENTREF AGE 2001:db8::1 services/author-cms-service 5d
Example 4: Get Location LoadBalancer IP with JSON

This example shows getting IP address in JSON format for Location service.

# Get IP address in JSON
kubectl get ipaddress 203.0.113.50 -o json | jq '.spec.parentRef'
{ "group": "", "resource": "services", "namespace": "location", "name": "location-api-loadbalancer" }
Example 5: Describe JobPost Service IP Address

This example demonstrates describing IP address to see full details.

# Describe IP address
kubectl describe ipaddress 192.168.1.103
Name: 192.168.1.103 API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1 Kind: IPAddress Metadata: Creation Timestamp: 2025-01-15T11:00:00Z Resource Version: 12456 UID: xyz789-abcd-1234-5678-9abcdef01234 Spec: Parent Ref: Group: Resource: services Namespace: jobpost-prod Name: jobpost-api-service

list or watch objects of kind IPAddress

HTTP Request
GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ipaddresses
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): Watch for bookmark events
  • continue (in query): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): Field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query): Label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query): Maximum number of results
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query): Send initial events for watch
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): Timeout for request
  • watch (in query): Watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200: IPAddressList - List of IPAddress objects
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All IP Addresses

This example demonstrates listing all IP addresses in the cluster.

# List all IP addresses
kubectl get ipaddresses
NAME PARENTREF 192.168.1.100 services/skills-api-service 192.168.1.101 services/user-auth-service 192.168.1.102 services/author-content-service 192.168.1.103 services/jobpost-api-service 203.0.113.50 services/location-api-loadbalancer 2001:db8::1 services/author-cms-service
Example 2: Watch IP Address Changes

This example shows watching IP address changes in real-time.

# Watch IP address changes
kubectl get ipaddresses --watch
NAME PARENTREF 192.168.1.100 services/skills-api-service 192.168.1.101 services/user-auth-service 192.168.1.104 services/user-cache-service ADDED 192.168.1.105 services/skills-worker-service ADDED 192.168.1.104 services/user-cache-service DELETED
Example 3: List with Field Selector

This example demonstrates filtering IP addresses by metadata fields.

# List IP addresses with field selector
kubectl get ipaddresses --field-selector metadata.name=192.168.1.100
NAME PARENTREF 192.168.1.100 services/skills-api-service
Example 4: List IPv4 Addresses Only

This example shows listing IPv4 addresses using JSON processing.

# List IPv4 addresses (contains dots)
kubectl get ipaddresses -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.name | contains(".")) | .metadata.name'
192.168.1.100 192.168.1.101 192.168.1.102 192.168.1.103 203.0.113.50
Example 5: List with Custom Columns

This example demonstrates custom column output for IP addresses.

# List with custom columns
kubectl get ipaddresses -o custom-columns=IP:.metadata.name,PARENT:.spec.parentRef.name,NAMESPACE:.spec.parentRef.namespace
IP PARENT NAMESPACE 192.168.1.100 skills-api-service skills 192.168.1.101 user-auth-service user-service 192.168.1.102 author-content-service author-content 192.168.1.103 jobpost-api-service jobpost-prod 203.0.113.50 location-api-loadbalancer location

create an IPAddress

HTTP Request
POST /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ipaddresses
Parameters
  • body (IPAddress, required): IPAddress object to create
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • fieldManager (in query): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): Field validation level (Ignore, Warn, Strict)
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200: IPAddress - The created IPAddress object (dry run)
  • 201: IPAddress - Created successfully
  • 202: IPAddress - Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create IP Address for Skills Service

This example demonstrates creating IP address allocation for Skills API service.

# Create IP address from YAML
cat <<EOF | kubectl create -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 192.168.1.110
spec:
  parentRef:
    resource: services
    namespace: skills
    name: skills-api-service
EOF
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/192.168.1.110 created
Example 2: Create IPv6 Address for User Service

This example shows creating IPv6 address for User authentication service.

# Create IPv6 address
cat <<EOF | kubectl create -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 2001:db8::2
spec:
  parentRef:
    resource: services
    namespace: user-service
    name: user-auth-service-ipv6
EOF
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/2001:db8::2 created
Example 3: Create IP Address for Author LoadBalancer

This example demonstrates creating public IP for Author content LoadBalancer.

# Create public IP address
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 203.0.113.51
spec:
  parentRef:
    resource: services
    namespace: author-content
    name: author-api-loadbalancer
EOF
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/203.0.113.51 created
Example 4: Create IP Address from File for Location

This example shows creating IP address from external file for Location service.

# Create IP address from file
kubectl create -f location-ip.yaml
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/192.168.2.50 created
Example 5: Dry Run Create for JobPost Service IP

This example demonstrates dry run validation before creating JobPost IP address.

# Dry run create
cat <<EOF | kubectl create --dry-run=client -o yaml -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 192.168.1.111
spec:
  parentRef:
    resource: services
    namespace: jobpost-prod
    name: jobpost-worker-service
EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: IPAddress metadata: name: 192.168.1.111 spec: parentRef: namespace: jobpost-prod name: jobpost-worker-service resource: services (Validated, not created - dry run)

replace the specified IPAddress

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ipaddresses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, required): Name of the IPAddress (IP in canonical format)
  • body (IPAddress, required): Updated IPAddress object
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • fieldManager (in query): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200: IPAddress - The updated IPAddress object
  • 201: IPAddress - Created (if not exists)
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Service IP Parent Reference

This example demonstrates updating IP address parent reference for Skills service.

# Update IP address parent reference
kubectl get ipaddress 192.168.1.110 -o yaml > skills-ip.yaml
# Edit skills-ip.yaml to change parentRef.name
kubectl replace -f skills-ip.yaml
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/192.168.1.110 replaced
Example 2: Update User Service IP with kubectl apply

This example shows updating IP address using kubectl apply for User service.

# Update using kubectl apply
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 192.168.1.101
spec:
  parentRef:
    resource: services
    namespace: user-service
    name: user-auth-service-v2
EOF
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/192.168.1.101 configured
Example 3: Update Author IPv6 Address

This example demonstrates updating IPv6 address parent reference for Author service.

# Update IPv6 address
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: IPAddress
metadata:
  name: 2001:db8::2
spec:
  parentRef:
    resource: services
    namespace: author-content
    name: author-cms-service-v2
EOF
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/2001:db8::2 replaced
Example 4: Update Location LoadBalancer IP

This example shows updating public IP parent reference for Location service.

# Update public IP
kubectl get ipaddress 203.0.113.51 -o yaml | sed 's/author-api-loadbalancer/location-api-loadbalancer/' | kubectl replace -f -
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/203.0.113.51 replaced
Example 5: Update JobPost Service IP from File

This example demonstrates updating IP address from external file for JobPost service.

# Update from file
kubectl replace -f jobpost-ip-updated.yaml
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/192.168.1.111 replaced

partially update the specified IPAddress

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ipaddresses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, required): Name of the IPAddress (IP in canonical format)
  • body (Patch, required): Patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • fieldManager (in query): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): Field validation level
  • force (in query): Force patch operation
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200: IPAddress - The patched IPAddress object
  • 201: IPAddress - Created (if not exists)
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills IP Parent Name

This example demonstrates patching IP address parent name for Skills service.

# Patch parent reference name
kubectl patch ipaddress 192.168.1.110 --type='merge' -p '{"spec":{"parentRef":{"name":"skills-api-service-new"}}}'
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/192.168.1.110 patched
Example 2: Patch User IP Namespace

This example shows patching IP address namespace reference for User service.

# Patch parent namespace
kubectl patch ipaddress 192.168.1.101 --type='json' -p='[{"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/parentRef/namespace", "value": "user-service-v2"}]'
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/192.168.1.101 patched
Example 3: Patch Author IPv6 Parent Reference

This example demonstrates patching multiple fields for Author IPv6 address.

# Patch multiple parent fields
kubectl patch ipaddress 2001:db8::2 --type='merge' -p '{
  "spec": {
    "parentRef": {
      "name": "author-cms-loadbalancer",
      "namespace": "author-prod"
    }
  }
}'
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/2001:db8::2 patched
Example 4: Patch Location IP with JSON

This example shows using JSON patch for Location service IP address.

# Patch using JSON format
kubectl patch ipaddress 203.0.113.51 --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/parentRef/name", "value": "location-regional-lb"}
]'
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/203.0.113.51 patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost IP Parent Resource

This example demonstrates patching parent resource type for JobPost IP.

# Patch parent resource
kubectl patch ipaddress 192.168.1.111 --type='merge' -p '{"spec":{"parentRef":{"resource":"services","name":"jobpost-api-v2"}}}'
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io/192.168.1.111 patched

delete an IPAddress

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ipaddresses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, required): Name of the IPAddress (IP in canonical format)
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): Grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): Ignore store read errors
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): Propagation policy (Orphan, Background, Foreground)
Return Value
  • 200: Status - Deletion successful
  • 202: Status - Accepted for deletion
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Service IP Address

This example demonstrates deleting IP address no longer used by Skills service.

# Delete IP address
kubectl delete ipaddress 192.168.1.110
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.110" deleted
Example 2: Delete User IPv6 Address with Wait

This example shows deleting IPv6 address and waiting for completion.

# Delete and wait
kubectl delete ipaddress 2001:db8::2 --wait=true
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "2001:db8::2" deleted
Example 3: Delete Multiple Author IP Addresses

This example demonstrates deleting multiple IP addresses at once.

# Delete multiple IPs
kubectl delete ipaddress 192.168.1.102 203.0.113.51
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.102" deleted ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "203.0.113.51" deleted
Example 4: Delete Location IP from File

This example shows deleting IP address defined in external file.

# Delete from file
kubectl delete -f location-ip.yaml
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.2.50" deleted
Example 5: Dry Run Delete JobPost IP

This example demonstrates dry run deletion validation for JobPost IP.

# Dry run delete
kubectl delete ipaddress 192.168.1.111 --dry-run=client
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.111" deleted (dry run)

delete collection of IPAddress

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/ipaddresses
Parameters
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • continue (in query): Continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • fieldSelector (in query): Field selector to filter
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): Grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): Ignore store read errors
  • labelSelector (in query): Label selector to filter
  • limit (in query): Maximum number to delete
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): Propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (in query): Resource version for delete
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query): Send initial events for watch
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): Timeout for request
Return Value
  • 200: Status - Collection deleted successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All IP Addresses (Use with Caution)

This example demonstrates deleting all IP addresses in the cluster.

# Delete all IP addresses
kubectl delete ipaddresses --all
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.100" deleted ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.101" deleted ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.102" deleted ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.103" deleted ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "203.0.113.50" deleted
Example 2: Delete IPv4 Addresses by Field Selector

This example shows deleting IP addresses matching field criteria.

# Delete with field selector (delete specific IP)
kubectl delete ipaddresses --field-selector metadata.name=192.168.1.100
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.100" deleted
Example 3: Delete Old IP Addresses with JSON Filter

This example demonstrates deleting IP addresses created before specific date.

# Delete old IPs (created before specific date)
kubectl get ipaddresses -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.creationTimestamp < "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z") | .metadata.name' | xargs -I {} kubectl delete ipaddress {}
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.100" deleted ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.101" deleted (2 IP addresses deleted)
Example 4: Delete IP Addresses by Parent Pattern

This example shows deleting IPs based on parent service name pattern.

# Delete IPs for specific service pattern
kubectl get ipaddresses -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.parentRef.name | contains("test")) | .metadata.name' | xargs kubectl delete ipaddress
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.99.1" deleted ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.99.2" deleted (Test IP addresses deleted)
Example 5: Dry Run Delete All IP Addresses

This example demonstrates dry run validation before deleting all IPs.

# Dry run delete all
kubectl delete ipaddresses --all --dry-run=client
ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.100" deleted (dry run) ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.101" deleted (dry run) ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.102" deleted (dry run) ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "192.168.1.103" deleted (dry run) ipaddress.networking.k8s.io "203.0.113.50" deleted (dry run) (5 IP addresses would be deleted)

Lease

Lease Overview

LeaseSpec - Specification of a Lease
LeaseList - Collection of Lease objects
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: Lease v1 API | API Version: coordination.k8s.io/v1

Lease defines a lease concept for distributed systems coordination and leader election.

Full Description

Lease defines a lease concept in your anihpj Django application cluster for distributed coordination and leader election. Leases provide a mechanism for distributed systems to coordinate leadership, manage exclusive access to resources, and implement heartbeat mechanisms. The Lease resource is essential for maintaining high availability and coordinating activities across your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services by ensuring only one instance holds leadership at any given time.

Leases track holder identity, acquisition time, renewal time, and lease duration to manage distributed locks and leader elections. The coordination.k8s.io/v1 API enables features like coordinated leader election with lease transitions tracking, microsecond-precision timing for accurate coordination, and configurable lease duration to balance responsiveness with network tolerance. This resource is crucial for coordinating controllers, managing singleton operations, and preventing split-brain scenarios in your application workloads.

Syntax
# Get a specific lease
kubectl get lease <lease-name> -n <namespace>

# List all leases in a namespace
kubectl get leases -n <namespace>

# Get lease in YAML format
kubectl get lease <lease-name> -n <namespace> -o yaml

# Describe a lease
kubectl describe lease <lease-name> -n <namespace>

# Watch lease changes
kubectl get leases -n <namespace> --watch
YAML Structure
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: skills-leader-election
  namespace: skills
spec:
  holderIdentity: skills-controller-abc123
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:30:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:35:45.123456Z"
  leaseTransitions: 3
Parameters Description
  • apiVersion (string, required): coordination.k8s.io/v1 - API version
  • kind (string, required): Lease - Resource type
  • metadata (ObjectMeta, required): Standard object metadata including name and namespace
  • spec (LeaseSpec, required): Specification of the Lease
Return Value
  • 200: Lease object (for read operations)
  • 201: Lease created successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Leader Election Lease for Skills Controller

This example demonstrates retrieving leader election lease for Skills controller.

# Get leader election lease
kubectl get lease skills-leader-election -n skills
NAME HOLDER AGE skills-leader-election skills-controller-abc123 5m
Example 2: List All Leases for User Services

This example shows listing all coordination leases in User service namespace.

# List all leases in namespace
kubectl get leases -n user-service
NAME HOLDER AGE user-auth-leader user-auth-pod-xyz789 10m user-cache-coordinator user-cache-pod-abc456 15m
Example 3: Get Lease Details for Author Content Service

This example demonstrates getting detailed lease information in YAML format.

# Get lease details in YAML
kubectl get lease author-cms-leader -n author-content -o yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1 kind: Lease metadata: name: author-cms-leader namespace: author-content spec: holderIdentity: author-cms-controller-xyz123 leaseDurationSeconds: 15 acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z" renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:30:15.456789Z" leaseTransitions: 2
Example 4: Describe Lease for Location Service

This example shows describing a lease to see coordination details.

# Describe lease
kubectl describe lease location-coordinator -n location
Name: location-coordinator Namespace: location API Version: coordination.k8s.io/v1 Kind: Lease Spec: Acquire Time: 2025-01-15T09:45:00.000000Z Holder Identity: location-api-pod-def789 Lease Duration Seconds: 15 Lease Transitions: 1 Renew Time: 2025-01-15T10:30:00.123456Z
Example 5: Watch Lease Changes for JobPost Services

This example demonstrates watching lease changes in real-time for leadership transitions.

# Watch lease changes
kubectl get leases -n jobpost-prod --watch
NAME HOLDER AGE jobpost-api-leader jobpost-api-pod-abc123 5m jobpost-worker-leader jobpost-worker-pod-xyz789 3m jobpost-api-leader jobpost-api-pod-def456 5m MODIFIED jobpost-scheduler-lock jobpost-scheduler-pod-ghi012 0s ADDED

LeaseSpec

Official Docs: Lease v1 API | API Version: coordination.k8s.io/v1

LeaseSpec is a specification of a Lease for distributed coordination.

Full Description

LeaseSpec is a specification of a Lease that defines the coordination parameters for distributed systems in your anihpj Django application cluster. The spec includes critical timing information with microsecond precision (MicroTime), holder identity for tracking which instance currently holds the lease, and lease duration for managing coordination timeouts. These parameters enable reliable leader election and distributed locking mechanisms for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost workloads.

Key fields include holderIdentity (the current lease holder, must match LeaseCandidate.metadata.name for Coordinated Leader Election), acquireTime and renewTime (MicroTime timestamps for tracking lease lifecycle), leaseDurationSeconds (wait time for forced acquisition), leaseTransitions (counter for ownership changes), preferredHolder (hint for optimal holder when using coordination strategy), and strategy (coordination algorithm selection, requires CoordinatedLeaderElection feature gate). These fields work together to provide robust distributed coordination with minimal race conditions.

Syntax
spec:
  holderIdentity: <holder-name>          # Current lease holder identity
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15               # Duration for force acquire
  acquireTime: <microtime>               # When lease was acquired
  renewTime: <microtime>                 # Last renewal timestamp
  leaseTransitions: 0                    # Number of holder changes
  preferredHolder: <preferred-name>      # Optimal holder hint (optional)
  strategy: <election-strategy>          # Coordination strategy (optional)
Parameters Description
  • holderIdentity (string): Identity of current lease holder; must equal LeaseCandidate.metadata.name for coordinated election
  • leaseDurationSeconds (int32): Duration candidates must wait to force acquire lease, measured against last renewTime
  • acquireTime (MicroTime): Timestamp when current lease was acquired (microsecond precision)
  • renewTime (MicroTime): Timestamp of last lease renewal by current holder (microsecond precision)
  • leaseTransitions (int32): Number of times lease has transitioned between holders
  • preferredHolder (string): Signals to holder that lease has more optimal holder; requires strategy field
  • strategy (string): Strategy for coordinated leader election; requires CoordinatedLeaderElection feature gate (Alpha)
Return Value

Returns a LeaseSpec object as part of the Lease resource.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Basic LeaseSpec for Skills Controller

This example demonstrates basic lease spec for Skills controller leader election.

# skills-leader-lease.yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: skills-controller-leader
  namespace: skills
spec:
  holderIdentity: skills-controller-abc123
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:15:30.123456Z"
  leaseTransitions: 0
Basic lease spec for Skills controller with 15-second duration
Example 2: LeaseSpec with Transitions for User Auth

This example shows lease spec tracking multiple transitions in User authentication service.

# user-auth-lease.yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: user-auth-coordinator
  namespace: user-service
spec:
  holderIdentity: user-auth-pod-xyz789
  leaseDurationSeconds: 20
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T09:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:30:45.987654Z"
  leaseTransitions: 5
Lease spec with 5 transitions tracking leadership changes
Example 3: LeaseSpec with Preferred Holder for Author CMS

This example demonstrates lease spec with preferred holder hint for optimal coordination.

# author-cms-lease.yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: author-cms-leader
  namespace: author-content
spec:
  holderIdentity: author-cms-pod-old123
  preferredHolder: author-cms-pod-new456
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:25:00.111111Z"
  leaseTransitions: 2
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
Lease with preferred holder hint for graceful transition
Example 4: LeaseSpec with Long Duration for Location Service

This example shows lease spec with extended duration for stable coordination.

# location-stable-lease.yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: location-api-lock
  namespace: location
spec:
  holderIdentity: location-api-pod-stable789
  leaseDurationSeconds: 60
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T08:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:29:55.555555Z"
  leaseTransitions: 1
Lease with 60-second duration for stable long-running operations
Example 5: LeaseSpec with Coordinated Election for JobPost

This example demonstrates coordinated leader election lease for JobPost scheduler.

# jobpost-coordinated-lease.yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: jobpost-scheduler-election
  namespace: jobpost-prod
spec:
  holderIdentity: jobpost-scheduler-candidate-1
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:30:00.000000Z"
  leaseTransitions: 0
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
Coordinated election lease with strategy specification

LeaseList

Official Docs: Lease v1 API | API Version: coordination.k8s.io/v1

LeaseList is a list of Lease objects for coordination management.

Full Description

LeaseList represents a collection of Lease resources in your anihpj Django application cluster. This list structure is used when querying multiple coordination leases at once, providing visibility into all distributed locks and leader elections across your infrastructure. The list includes metadata about the collection and an array of Lease items, enabling efficient management and monitoring of coordination state for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

The list structure supports filtering by namespace, labels, and field selectors, allowing you to audit coordination status, identify active leaders, track lease transitions, and monitor coordination health across your cluster. Each item in the list is a complete Lease object with its metadata and specification, providing comprehensive visibility into your distributed coordination mechanisms and helping detect issues like lease expiration or excessive transitions.

Syntax
# List all leases in a namespace
kubectl get leases -n <namespace>

# List leases with wide output
kubectl get leases -n <namespace> -o wide

# List leases in YAML format
kubectl get leases -n <namespace> -o yaml

# List leases in JSON format
kubectl get leases -n <namespace> -o json

# Count leases in namespace
kubectl get leases -n <namespace> --no-headers | wc -l
Parameters Description
  • apiVersion (string, required): coordination.k8s.io/v1 - API version
  • kind (string, required): LeaseList - Resource type
  • metadata (ListMeta, required): Standard list metadata
  • items ([]Lease, required): List of Lease objects
Return Value
  • 200: LeaseList object containing matching Lease resources
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Leases in Skills Namespace

This example demonstrates listing all coordination leases in Skills namespace.

# List all leases
kubectl get leases -n skills
NAME HOLDER AGE skills-controller-leader skills-controller-abc123 10m skills-scheduler-lock skills-scheduler-xyz789 5m skills-cache-coordinator skills-cache-pod-def456 15m
Example 2: Export Leases to JSON for User Services

This example shows exporting leases to JSON for processing and analysis.

# Export leases to JSON
kubectl get leases -n user-service -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, holder: .spec.holderIdentity, transitions: .spec.leaseTransitions}'
{ "name": "user-auth-leader", "holder": "user-auth-pod-xyz789", "transitions": 3 } { "name": "user-cache-coordinator", "holder": "user-cache-pod-abc456", "transitions": 1 }
Example 3: Export Leases to YAML for Author Services

This example demonstrates exporting leases to YAML for backup.

# Export leases to YAML
kubectl get leases -n author-content -o yaml > author-leases-backup.yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: List items: - apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1 kind: Lease metadata: name: author-cms-leader namespace: author-content spec: holderIdentity: author-cms-controller-xyz123 leaseDurationSeconds: 15 acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z" renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:30:00.123456Z" leaseTransitions: 2 (Exported to author-leases-backup.yaml)
Example 4: Count Leases in Location Namespace

This example shows counting total coordination leases for Location services.

# Count leases
kubectl get leases -n location --no-headers | wc -l
4
Example 5: List Leases with Holder Identity for JobPost

This example demonstrates listing leases with custom columns showing holder details.

# List with custom columns
kubectl get leases -n jobpost-prod -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,HOLDER:.spec.holderIdentity,DURATION:.spec.leaseDurationSeconds,TRANSITIONS:.spec.leaseTransitions
NAME HOLDER DURATION TRANSITIONS jobpost-api-leader jobpost-api-pod-abc123 15 2 jobpost-worker-leader jobpost-worker-pod-xyz789 15 0 jobpost-scheduler-election jobpost-scheduler-candidate-1 20 5

Lease Operations

Available Operations

get - Read specific Lease
list - List Leases in namespace
list - List Leases in all namespaces
create - Create new Lease
update - Replace Lease
patch - Partially update Lease
delete - Delete Lease
deletecollection - Delete multiple Leases

Lease operations enable distributed coordination management in your anihpj Django cluster, supporting leader election and distributed locking for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

read the specified Lease

HTTP Request
GET /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/leases/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, required): Name of the Lease
  • namespace (in path, required): Namespace of the Lease
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200: Lease - The specified Lease object
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Controller Leader Lease

This example demonstrates retrieving leader election lease for Skills controller.

# Get specific lease
kubectl get lease skills-controller-leader -n skills
NAME HOLDER AGE skills-controller-leader skills-controller-abc123 10m
Example 2: Get User Auth Lease in YAML

This example shows getting lease details in YAML format for User service.

# Get lease in YAML format
kubectl get lease user-auth-coordinator -n user-service -o yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1 kind: Lease metadata: name: user-auth-coordinator namespace: user-service creationTimestamp: "2025-01-15T09:00:00Z" resourceVersion: "123456" spec: holderIdentity: user-auth-pod-xyz789 leaseDurationSeconds: 20 acquireTime: "2025-01-15T09:00:00.000000Z" renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:30:45.987654Z" leaseTransitions: 5
Example 3: Get Author CMS Lease with JSON

This example demonstrates getting lease in JSON format for Author service.

# Get lease in JSON
kubectl get lease author-cms-leader -n author-content -o json | jq '.spec'
{ "holderIdentity": "author-cms-controller-xyz123", "leaseDurationSeconds": 15, "acquireTime": "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z", "renewTime": "2025-01-15T10:30:15.456789Z", "leaseTransitions": 2 }
Example 4: Describe Location Coordinator Lease

This example shows describing a lease to see full coordination details.

# Describe lease
kubectl describe lease location-api-lock -n location
Name: location-api-lock Namespace: location API Version: coordination.k8s.io/v1 Kind: Lease Metadata: Creation Timestamp: 2025-01-15T08:00:00Z Resource Version: 234567 Spec: Acquire Time: 2025-01-15T08:00:00.000000Z Holder Identity: location-api-pod-stable789 Lease Duration Seconds: 60 Lease Transitions: 1 Renew Time: 2025-01-15T10:29:55.555555Z
Example 5: Get JobPost Scheduler Lease with Wide Output

This example demonstrates getting lease with additional columns displayed.

# Get lease with wide output
kubectl get lease jobpost-scheduler-election -n jobpost-prod -o wide
NAME HOLDER AGE jobpost-scheduler-election jobpost-scheduler-candidate-1 15m

list or watch objects of kind Lease

HTTP Request
GET /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/leases
Parameters
  • namespace (in path, required): Namespace to list leases from
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): Watch for bookmark events
  • continue (in query): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): Field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query): Label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query): Maximum number of results
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query): Send initial events for watch
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): Timeout for request
  • watch (in query): Watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200: LeaseList - List of Lease objects in the namespace
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Leases in Skills Namespace

This example demonstrates listing all coordination leases in Skills namespace.

# List all leases in namespace
kubectl get leases -n skills
NAME HOLDER AGE skills-controller-leader skills-controller-abc123 10m skills-scheduler-lock skills-scheduler-xyz789 5m skills-cache-coordinator skills-cache-pod-def456 15m
Example 2: Watch Lease Changes in User Namespace

This example shows watching lease changes in real-time for User services.

# Watch lease changes
kubectl get leases -n user-service --watch
NAME HOLDER AGE user-auth-leader user-auth-pod-xyz789 10m user-cache-coordinator user-cache-pod-abc456 15m user-auth-leader user-auth-pod-new999 10m MODIFIED user-session-lock user-session-pod-ghi123 0s ADDED
Example 3: List Leases with Field Selector for Author

This example demonstrates filtering leases by metadata fields.

# List with field selector
kubectl get leases -n author-content --field-selector metadata.name=author-cms-leader
NAME HOLDER AGE author-cms-leader author-cms-controller-xyz123 20m
Example 4: List Leases Sorted by Creation Time for Location

This example shows listing leases sorted by age for Location services.

# List leases sorted
kubectl get leases -n location --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME HOLDER AGE location-api-lock location-api-pod-stable789 2h location-coordinator location-coord-pod-abc123 1h location-cache-lock location-cache-pod-xyz456 30m location-scheduler location-sched-pod-def789 10m
Example 5: List Leases with Transitions Count for JobPost

This example demonstrates listing leases with custom columns showing transitions.

# List with custom columns
kubectl get leases -n jobpost-prod -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,HOLDER:.spec.holderIdentity,TRANSITIONS:.spec.leaseTransitions
NAME HOLDER TRANSITIONS jobpost-api-leader jobpost-api-pod-abc123 2 jobpost-worker-leader jobpost-worker-pod-xyz789 0 jobpost-scheduler-election jobpost-scheduler-candidate-1 5

list or watch objects of kind Lease

HTTP Request
GET /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/leases
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query): Watch for bookmark events
  • continue (in query): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query): Field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query): Label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query): Maximum number of results
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
  • resourceVersion (in query): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query): Send initial events for watch
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): Timeout for request
  • watch (in query): Watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200: LeaseList - List of Lease objects across all namespaces
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Leases Across All Namespaces

This example demonstrates listing all coordination leases cluster-wide.

# List all leases across all namespaces
kubectl get leases --all-namespaces
NAMESPACE NAME HOLDER AGE skills skills-controller-leader skills-controller-abc123 10m user-service user-auth-leader user-auth-pod-xyz789 15m author-content author-cms-leader author-cms-controller-xyz123 20m location location-api-lock location-api-pod-stable789 2h jobpost-prod jobpost-api-leader jobpost-api-pod-abc123 5m
Example 2: Watch All Lease Changes Cluster-Wide

This example shows watching lease changes across all namespaces.

# Watch all leases
kubectl get leases --all-namespaces --watch
NAMESPACE NAME HOLDER AGE skills skills-controller-leader skills-controller-abc123 10m user-service user-auth-leader user-auth-pod-xyz789 15m user-service user-auth-leader user-auth-pod-new999 15m MODIFIED jobpost-prod jobpost-worker-leader jobpost-worker-pod-new 0s ADDED
Example 3: List Leases with Custom Columns Cluster-Wide

This example demonstrates listing leases with custom output across all namespaces.

# List with custom columns
kubectl get leases --all-namespaces -o custom-columns=NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,HOLDER:.spec.holderIdentity,TRANSITIONS:.spec.leaseTransitions
NAMESPACE NAME HOLDER TRANSITIONS skills skills-controller-leader skills-controller-abc123 0 user-service user-auth-leader user-auth-pod-xyz789 3 author-content author-cms-leader author-cms-controller-xyz123 2 location location-api-lock location-api-pod-stable789 1 jobpost-prod jobpost-scheduler-election jobpost-scheduler-candidate-1 5
Example 4: Count Total Leases Cluster-Wide

This example shows counting all coordination leases across the cluster.

# Count all leases
kubectl get leases --all-namespaces --no-headers | wc -l
12
Example 5: List Leases with High Transition Count

This example demonstrates filtering leases with high transition counts for troubleshooting.

# List leases with high transitions
kubectl get leases --all-namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.spec.leaseTransitions > 3) | {namespace: .metadata.namespace, name: .metadata.name, transitions: .spec.leaseTransitions}'
{ "namespace": "user-service", "name": "user-auth-coordinator", "transitions": 5 } { "namespace": "jobpost-prod", "name": "jobpost-scheduler-election", "transitions": 5 }

create a Lease

HTTP Request
POST /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/leases
Parameters
  • namespace (in path, required): Namespace for the Lease
  • body (Lease, required): Lease object to create
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • fieldManager (in query): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): Field validation level (Ignore, Warn, Strict)
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200: Lease - The created Lease object (dry run)
  • 201: Lease - Created successfully
  • 202: Lease - Accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Leader Election Lease for Skills

This example demonstrates creating leader election lease for Skills controller.

# Create leader election lease
cat <<EOF | kubectl create -f -
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: skills-controller-leader
  namespace: skills
spec:
  holderIdentity: skills-controller-abc123
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  leaseTransitions: 0
EOF
lease.coordination.k8s.io/skills-controller-leader created
Example 2: Create Coordination Lease for User Service

This example shows creating coordination lease for User authentication service.

# Create coordination lease
cat <<EOF | kubectl create -f -
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: user-auth-coordinator
  namespace: user-service
spec:
  holderIdentity: user-auth-pod-xyz789
  leaseDurationSeconds: 20
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T09:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T09:00:00.000000Z"
  leaseTransitions: 0
EOF
lease.coordination.k8s.io/user-auth-coordinator created
Example 3: Create Lease with Preferred Holder for Author

This example demonstrates creating lease with preferred holder for Author CMS.

# Create lease with preferred holder
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: author-cms-leader
  namespace: author-content
spec:
  holderIdentity: author-cms-pod-old123
  preferredHolder: author-cms-pod-new456
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  leaseTransitions: 0
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
EOF
lease.coordination.k8s.io/author-cms-leader created
Example 4: Create Lease from File for Location

This example shows creating coordination lease from external file.

# Create lease from file
kubectl create -f location-api-lease.yaml -n location
lease.coordination.k8s.io/location-api-lock created
Example 5: Dry Run Create Lease for JobPost

This example demonstrates dry run validation before creating JobPost scheduler lease.

# Dry run create
cat <<EOF | kubectl create --dry-run=client -o yaml -f -
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: jobpost-scheduler-election
  namespace: jobpost-prod
spec:
  holderIdentity: jobpost-scheduler-candidate-1
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  leaseTransitions: 0
EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1 kind: Lease metadata: name: jobpost-scheduler-election namespace: jobpost-prod spec: holderIdentity: jobpost-scheduler-candidate-1 leaseDurationSeconds: 15 acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z" renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z" leaseTransitions: 0 (Validated, not created - dry run)

replace the specified Lease

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/leases/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, required): Name of the Lease
  • namespace (in path, required): Namespace of the Lease
  • body (Lease, required): Updated Lease object
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • fieldManager (in query): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200: Lease - The updated Lease object
  • 201: Lease - Created (if not exists)
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Lease Holder Identity

This example demonstrates updating lease holder for Skills controller transition.

# Update lease holder
kubectl get lease skills-controller-leader -n skills -o yaml > skills-lease.yaml
# Edit skills-lease.yaml to change holderIdentity and increment leaseTransitions
kubectl replace -f skills-lease.yaml
lease.coordination.k8s.io/skills-controller-leader replaced
Example 2: Update User Lease Renew Time

This example shows updating lease renewal timestamp for User service.

# Update renew time using kubectl apply
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: user-auth-coordinator
  namespace: user-service
spec:
  holderIdentity: user-auth-pod-xyz789
  leaseDurationSeconds: 20
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T09:00:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:35:00.123456Z"
  leaseTransitions: 5
EOF
lease.coordination.k8s.io/user-auth-coordinator configured
Example 3: Update Author Lease with New Holder

This example demonstrates updating lease for leadership transition in Author service.

# Update with new holder
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace -f -
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1
kind: Lease
metadata:
  name: author-cms-leader
  namespace: author-content
spec:
  holderIdentity: author-cms-pod-new456
  leaseDurationSeconds: 15
  acquireTime: "2025-01-15T10:35:00.000000Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:35:00.000000Z"
  leaseTransitions: 3
EOF
lease.coordination.k8s.io/author-cms-leader replaced
Example 4: Update Location Lease Duration

This example shows updating lease duration for Location service.

# Update lease duration
kubectl get lease location-api-lock -n location -o yaml | sed 's/leaseDurationSeconds: 60/leaseDurationSeconds: 30/' | kubectl replace -f -
lease.coordination.k8s.io/location-api-lock replaced
Example 5: Update JobPost Lease from File

This example demonstrates updating lease from external file for JobPost.

# Update from file
kubectl replace -f jobpost-scheduler-lease-updated.yaml
lease.coordination.k8s.io/jobpost-scheduler-election replaced

partially update the specified Lease

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/leases/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, required): Name of the Lease
  • namespace (in path, required): Namespace of the Lease
  • body (Patch, required): Patch to apply
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • fieldManager (in query): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query): Field validation level
  • force (in query): Force patch operation
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
Return Value
  • 200: Lease - The patched Lease object
  • 201: Lease - Created (if not exists)
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Lease Renew Time

This example demonstrates patching lease renewal timestamp for Skills controller.

# Patch renew time
kubectl patch lease skills-controller-leader -n skills --type='merge' -p '{"spec":{"renewTime":"2025-01-15T10:40:00.123456Z"}}'
lease.coordination.k8s.io/skills-controller-leader patched
Example 2: Patch User Lease Holder Identity

This example shows patching holder identity and incrementing transitions for User service.

# Patch holder and transitions
kubectl patch lease user-auth-coordinator -n user-service --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/holderIdentity", "value": "user-auth-pod-new999"},
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/leaseTransitions", "value": 6}
]'
lease.coordination.k8s.io/user-auth-coordinator patched
Example 3: Patch Author Lease Preferred Holder

This example demonstrates patching preferred holder for graceful transition.

# Patch preferred holder
kubectl patch lease author-cms-leader -n author-content --type='merge' -p '{
  "spec": {
    "preferredHolder": "author-cms-pod-latest789"
  }
}'
lease.coordination.k8s.io/author-cms-leader patched
Example 4: Patch Location Lease Duration with JSON

This example shows using JSON patch to update lease duration for Location.

# Patch duration using JSON
kubectl patch lease location-api-lock -n location --type='json' -p='[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/leaseDurationSeconds", "value": 45}
]'
lease.coordination.k8s.io/location-api-lock patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost Lease Multiple Fields

This example demonstrates patching multiple lease fields for JobPost scheduler.

# Patch multiple fields
kubectl patch lease jobpost-scheduler-election -n jobpost-prod --type='merge' -p '{
  "spec": {
    "holderIdentity": "jobpost-scheduler-candidate-2",
    "renewTime": "2025-01-15T10:40:00.000000Z",
    "leaseTransitions": 6
  }
}'
lease.coordination.k8s.io/jobpost-scheduler-election patched

delete a Lease

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/leases/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, required): Name of the Lease
  • namespace (in path, required): Namespace of the Lease
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): Grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): Ignore store read errors
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): Propagation policy (Orphan, Background, Foreground)
Return Value
  • 200: Status - Deletion successful
  • 202: Status - Accepted for deletion
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Obsolete Skills Lease

This example demonstrates deleting unused lease for Skills controller.

# Delete lease
kubectl delete lease skills-scheduler-lock -n skills
lease.coordination.k8s.io "skills-scheduler-lock" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Lease with Wait

This example shows deleting lease and waiting for completion for User service.

# Delete and wait
kubectl delete lease user-session-lock -n user-service --wait=true
lease.coordination.k8s.io "user-session-lock" deleted
Example 3: Delete Multiple Author Leases

This example demonstrates deleting multiple leases at once for Author service.

# Delete multiple leases
kubectl delete lease author-cms-leader author-cache-lock -n author-content
lease.coordination.k8s.io "author-cms-leader" deleted lease.coordination.k8s.io "author-cache-lock" deleted
Example 4: Delete Location Lease from File

This example shows deleting lease defined in external file.

# Delete from file
kubectl delete -f location-coordinator-lease.yaml
lease.coordination.k8s.io "location-coordinator" deleted
Example 5: Dry Run Delete JobPost Lease

This example demonstrates dry run deletion validation for JobPost lease.

# Dry run delete
kubectl delete lease jobpost-api-leader -n jobpost-prod --dry-run=client
lease.coordination.k8s.io "jobpost-api-leader" deleted (dry run)

delete collection of Lease

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1/namespaces/{namespace}/leases
Parameters
  • namespace (in path, required): Namespace of the Leases
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • continue (in query): Continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (in query): Dry run mode (All or empty)
  • fieldSelector (in query): Field selector to filter
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query): Grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query): Ignore store read errors
  • labelSelector (in query): Label selector to filter
  • limit (in query): Maximum number to delete
  • pretty (in query): Pretty-print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query): Propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (in query): Resource version for delete
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query): Send initial events for watch
  • timeoutSeconds (in query): Timeout for request
Return Value
  • 200: Status - Collection deleted successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All Leases in Skills Namespace

This example demonstrates deleting all coordination leases in Skills namespace.

# Delete all leases in namespace
kubectl delete leases --all -n skills
lease.coordination.k8s.io "skills-controller-leader" deleted lease.coordination.k8s.io "skills-scheduler-lock" deleted lease.coordination.k8s.io "skills-cache-coordinator" deleted
Example 2: Delete Leases by Field Selector for User

This example shows deleting specific lease using field selector in User namespace.

# Delete with field selector
kubectl delete leases -n user-service --field-selector metadata.name=user-session-lock
lease.coordination.k8s.io "user-session-lock" deleted
Example 3: Delete Old Leases for Author Service

This example demonstrates deleting leases based on age for cleanup.

# Delete old leases (older than specific date)
kubectl get leases -n author-content -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.creationTimestamp < "2025-01-01T00:00:00Z") | .metadata.name' | xargs -I {} kubectl delete lease {} -n author-content
lease.coordination.k8s.io "author-old-lock" deleted lease.coordination.k8s.io "author-temp-coordinator" deleted (2 leases deleted)
Example 4: Delete Leases with High Transitions for Location

This example shows deleting leases with excessive transitions for troubleshooting.

# Delete leases with high transition count
kubectl get leases -n location -o json | jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.leaseTransitions > 10) | .metadata.name' | xargs kubectl delete lease -n location
lease.coordination.k8s.io "location-unstable-lock" deleted (1 lease deleted)
Example 5: Dry Run Delete All JobPost Leases

This example demonstrates dry run validation before deleting all leases.

# Dry run delete all
kubectl delete leases --all -n jobpost-prod --dry-run=client
lease.coordination.k8s.io "jobpost-api-leader" deleted (dry run) lease.coordination.k8s.io "jobpost-worker-leader" deleted (dry run) lease.coordination.k8s.io "jobpost-scheduler-election" deleted (dry run) (3 leases would be deleted)

LeaseCandidate

LeaseCandidateSpec - Candidate specification for coordinated leader election
LeaseCandidateList - Collection of LeaseCandidate objects
Operations - CRUD operations for LeaseCandidates

API Version: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1 (Official Documentation)

Full Description

LeaseCandidate defines a candidate for a Lease object in coordinated leader election. Candidates are created such that coordinated leader election will pick the best leader from the list of candidates. This resource is used in conjunction with Lease objects to enable sophisticated leader election strategies in distributed systems, particularly useful for managing Skills validation services, User authentication coordinators, Author content management leaders, Location service coordinators, and JobPost processing schedulers.

LeaseCandidates enable version-aware leader election where the binary with the latest version or specific emulation characteristics can be preferred as the leader. This ensures smooth upgrades and rollbacks in distributed deployments where multiple versions of a service may be running simultaneously. The coordinated election strategy examines all candidates and selects the most appropriate leader based on binary versions, emulation versions, and renewal timestamps.

Syntax

# Get a specific lease candidate
kubectl get leasecandidate <name> -n <namespace>

# List all lease candidates in a namespace
kubectl get leasecandidates -n <namespace>

# Get candidate details in YAML
kubectl get leasecandidate <name> -n <namespace> -o yaml

# Describe candidate details
kubectl describe leasecandidate <name> -n <namespace>

# Watch candidate changes in real-time
kubectl get leasecandidates -n <namespace> --watch

YAML Structure

apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: LeaseCandidate
metadata:
  name: skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123
  namespace: skills-prod
spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.1.0"
  leaseName: "skills-validator-leader"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "2.0.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  pingTime: "2025-01-15T09:59:50.000000Z"

Parameters

  • apiVersion (string): coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
  • kind (string): LeaseCandidate
  • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard Kubernetes metadata including name, namespace, labels, and annotations
  • spec (LeaseCandidateSpec, required): Specification of the lease candidate for coordinated election

Return Value

  • 200: OK - Returns the LeaseCandidate object
  • 201: Created - LeaseCandidate successfully created
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Get Skills Validator Candidate

This example retrieves a specific lease candidate for the Skills validator service.

# Get skills validator candidate for leader election
kubectl get leasecandidate skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 -n skills-prod
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 skills-validator-leader 2.1.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z
Example 2: List All User Service Candidates

This example lists all candidates for User authentication service election.

# List all user auth candidates
kubectl get leasecandidates -n user-auth
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME user-auth-v1-pod-xyz789 user-auth-coordinator 1.9.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z user-auth-v2-pod-abc123 user-auth-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:05Z user-auth-v2-pod-def456 user-auth-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:03Z (3 candidates for user-auth-coordinator lease)
Example 3: Get Author CMS Candidate Details in YAML

This example shows detailed YAML output for an Author CMS candidate.

# Get author CMS candidate YAML
kubectl get leasecandidate author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 -n author-cms -o yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1 kind: LeaseCandidate metadata: name: author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 namespace: author-cms creationTimestamp: "2025-01-15T09:30:00Z" spec: binaryVersion: "3.0.0" leaseName: "author-cms-leader" strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion" emulationVersion: "2.5.0" renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z" pingTime: "2025-01-15T09:59:55.000000Z"
Example 4: Describe Location Service Candidate

This example provides detailed information about a Location service candidate.

# Describe location candidate
kubectl describe leasecandidate location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 -n location-prod
Name: location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 Namespace: location-prod API Version: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1 Kind: LeaseCandidate Metadata: Creation Timestamp: 2025-01-15T09:00:00Z Spec: Binary Version: 2.2.0 Emulation Version: 2.1.0 Lease Name: location-api-coordinator Ping Time: 2025-01-15T09:59:50.000000Z Renew Time: 2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z Strategy: OldestEmulationVersion Events: <none>
Example 5: Watch JobPost Scheduler Candidates

This example watches for changes in JobPost scheduler candidates in real-time.

# Watch jobpost scheduler candidates
kubectl get leasecandidates -n jobpost-prod --watch
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME jobpost-scheduler-v1-pod-mno345 jobpost-scheduler-leader 1.5.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-pqr678 jobpost-scheduler-leader 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:02Z jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-pqr678 jobpost-scheduler-leader 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:10Z jobpost-scheduler-v1-pod-mno345 jobpost-scheduler-leader 1.5.0 2025-01-15T10:00:15Z (Watching for candidate renewals and election changes...)

LeaseCandidateSpec

Description

LeaseCandidateSpec defines the specification of a lease candidate for coordinated leader election. It contains version information (binary and emulation versions), the lease name this candidate is contending for, the election strategy, and timing information for renewal and ping operations. The spec enables version-aware leader election where candidates with specific version characteristics can be preferred.

The specification supports semver-formatted binary and emulation versions, allowing the election algorithm to make informed decisions about which candidate should become the leader. This is particularly useful during rolling upgrades where multiple versions of a service are running simultaneously, ensuring that the most appropriate version becomes the leader based on the configured strategy.

Syntax

spec:
  binaryVersion: string         # Required: Semver format without 'v' prefix
  leaseName: string            # Required: Name of the lease to contest
  strategy: string             # Required: Election strategy (e.g., "OldestEmulationVersion")
  emulationVersion: string     # Required when strategy is "OldestEmulationVersion"
  pingTime: MicroTime          # Last ping from server (microsecond precision)
  renewTime: MicroTime         # Last renewal time (microsecond precision)

Parameters

  • binaryVersion (string, required): The binary version in semver format without leading 'v' (e.g., "2.1.0"). Used by the election algorithm to prefer newer binaries.
  • leaseName (string, required): The name of the Lease object this candidate is contending for. Multiple candidates can reference the same lease. This field is immutable after creation.
  • strategy (string, required): The strategy for coordinated leader election (e.g., "OldestEmulationVersion"). If candidates return different strategies, the one from the candidate with the latest binaryVersion is used.
