  • emulationVersion (string): The emulation version in semver format without leading 'v'. Must be less than or equal to binaryVersion. Required when strategy is "OldestEmulationVersion".
  • pingTime (MicroTime): The last time the server requested the candidate to renew, with microsecond precision. Used during leader election to check candidate eligibility.
  • renewTime (MicroTime): The last time the candidate was updated, with microsecond precision. Candidates respond to pingTime updates by updating renewTime. Old candidates are garbage collected if hours pass since last renewal.

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Basic Skills Validator Spec

This example shows a basic lease candidate spec for Skills validator.

spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.1.0"
  leaseName: "skills-validator-leader"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "2.0.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
Example 2: User Auth Candidate with Ping Time

This example includes ping time for active election monitoring.

spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.0.0"
  leaseName: "user-auth-coordinator"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "1.9.0"
  pingTime: "2025-01-15T09:59:50.123456Z"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.123456Z"
Example 3: Author CMS with Equal Binary and Emulation Versions

This example shows a candidate where binary and emulation versions match.

spec:
  binaryVersion: "3.0.0"
  leaseName: "author-cms-leader"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "3.0.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  pingTime: "2025-01-15T09:59:55.000000Z"
Example 4: Location Service with Lower Emulation Version

This example demonstrates a candidate with emulation version lower than binary.

spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.5.0"
  leaseName: "location-api-coordinator"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "2.0.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
  pingTime: "2025-01-15T09:59:50.000000Z"
Example 5: JobPost Scheduler Candidate

This example shows a JobPost scheduler candidate configuration.

spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.0.0"
  leaseName: "jobpost-scheduler-leader"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "1.8.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:02.500000Z"
  pingTime: "2025-01-15T09:59:52.500000Z"

LeaseCandidateList

Description

LeaseCandidateList is a collection of LeaseCandidate objects, used for listing and filtering candidates participating in coordinated leader election. This resource type enables administrators and automated systems to query all candidates for specific leases, monitor election participation across namespaces, and analyze version distributions during rolling upgrades of Skills validators, User auth services, Author CMS instances, Location coordinators, and JobPost schedulers.

The list structure supports standard Kubernetes filtering, sorting, and watching capabilities, making it easy to track which candidates are active, identify version mismatches, and monitor the health of leader election processes across distributed deployments. LeaseCandidateLists are particularly useful for observability and debugging during service upgrades and migrations.

Syntax

# List all candidates in namespace
kubectl get leasecandidates -n <namespace>

# List with wide output showing more details
kubectl get leasecandidates -n <namespace> -o wide

# Export to YAML
kubectl get leasecandidates -n <namespace> -o yaml

# Export to JSON
kubectl get leasecandidates -n <namespace> -o json

# Count candidates
kubectl get leasecandidates -n <namespace> --no-headers | wc -l

Parameters

  • apiVersion (string): coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
  • kind (string): LeaseCandidateList
  • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata containing resourceVersion, continue token, and remainingItemCount
  • items ([]LeaseCandidate, required): Array of LeaseCandidate objects in the collection

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: List All Skills Validator Candidates

This example lists all candidates for Skills validator election.

# List skills validator candidates
kubectl get leasecandidates -n skills-prod
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME skills-validator-v1-pod-abc123 skills-validator-leader 1.9.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z skills-validator-v2-pod-def456 skills-validator-leader 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:05Z skills-validator-v2-pod-ghi789 skills-validator-leader 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:03Z (3 candidates contending for skills-validator-leader)
Example 2: Export User Auth Candidates to JSON

This example exports User auth candidates for analysis.

# Export to JSON and filter with jq
kubectl get leasecandidates -n user-auth -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, binaryVersion: .spec.binaryVersion}'
{ "name": "user-auth-v1-pod-xyz789", "binaryVersion": "1.9.0" } { "name": "user-auth-v2-pod-abc123", "binaryVersion": "2.0.0" } { "name": "user-auth-v2-pod-def456", "binaryVersion": "2.0.0" }
Example 3: Export Author CMS Candidates to YAML

This example backs up Author CMS candidates configuration.

# Export to YAML file
kubectl get leasecandidates -n author-cms -o yaml > author-cms-candidates-backup.yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: List metadata: resourceVersion: "123456" items: - apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1 kind: LeaseCandidate metadata: name: author-cms-v2-pod-jkl012 namespace: author-cms spec: binaryVersion: "2.5.0" leaseName: "author-cms-leader" strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion" emulationVersion: "2.0.0" - apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1 kind: LeaseCandidate metadata: name: author-cms-v3-pod-mno345 namespace: author-cms spec: binaryVersion: "3.0.0" leaseName: "author-cms-leader" strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion" emulationVersion: "2.5.0" (List exported to file)
Example 4: Count Location Service Candidates

This example counts active Location service candidates.

# Count candidates
kubectl get leasecandidates -n location-prod --no-headers | wc -l
4 (4 candidates active for location service elections)
Example 5: List JobPost Scheduler Candidates with Custom Columns

This example shows custom columns for JobPost scheduler candidates.

# Custom columns showing versions and lease
kubectl get leasecandidates -n jobpost-prod -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,LEASE:.spec.leaseName,BINARY:.spec.binaryVersion,EMULATION:.spec.emulationVersion
NAME LEASE BINARY EMULATION jobpost-scheduler-v1-pod-pqr678 jobpost-scheduler-leader 1.5.0 1.5.0 jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-stu901 jobpost-scheduler-leader 2.0.0 1.8.0 jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-vwx234 jobpost-scheduler-leader 2.0.0 1.9.0

Operations

get - Retrieve a specific LeaseCandidate
list - List LeaseCandidates in a namespace
list all namespaces - List LeaseCandidates across all namespaces
create - Create a new LeaseCandidate
update - Update an existing LeaseCandidate
patch - Partially update a LeaseCandidate
delete - Delete a LeaseCandidate
deletecollection - Delete multiple LeaseCandidates

LeaseCandidate operations enable management of candidates participating in coordinated leader election. These operations support version-aware election strategies for distributed services like Skills validators, User auth coordinators, Author CMS leaders, Location API coordinators, and JobPost scheduler elections.

get - Read the Specified LeaseCandidate

Description

Retrieves details of a specific LeaseCandidate by name and namespace. This operation returns the candidate's version information (binary and emulation versions), lease name, election strategy, and timing data (pingTime and renewTime). Use this to inspect individual candidates participating in coordinated leader election.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/leasecandidates/{name}
Parameters
  • name (path parameter, required): Name of the LeaseCandidate
  • namespace (path parameter, required): Namespace containing the LeaseCandidate
  • pretty (query parameter): If 'true', output is pretty printed
Return Value
  • 200 (LeaseCandidate): OK - Returns the LeaseCandidate object
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Validator Candidate

This example retrieves a Skills validator candidate details.

# Get skills validator candidate
kubectl get leasecandidate skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 -n skills-prod
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 skills-validator-leader 2.1.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z
Example 2: Get User Auth Candidate in YAML

This example shows full YAML details of a User auth candidate.

# Get YAML format
kubectl get leasecandidate user-auth-v2-pod-def456 -n user-auth -o yaml
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1 kind: LeaseCandidate metadata: name: user-auth-v2-pod-def456 namespace: user-auth creationTimestamp: "2025-01-15T09:30:00Z" spec: binaryVersion: "2.0.0" emulationVersion: "1.9.0" leaseName: "user-auth-coordinator" pingTime: "2025-01-15T09:59:50.123456Z" renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.123456Z" strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
Example 3: Get Author CMS Candidate in JSON

This example exports Author CMS candidate to JSON format.

# Get JSON with jq filtering
kubectl get leasecandidate author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 -n author-cms -o json | jq '.spec'
{ "binaryVersion": "3.0.0", "emulationVersion": "2.5.0", "leaseName": "author-cms-leader", "pingTime": "2025-01-15T09:59:55.000000Z", "renewTime": "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z", "strategy": "OldestEmulationVersion" }
Example 4: Describe Location Service Candidate

This example provides detailed description of a Location candidate.

# Describe candidate
kubectl describe leasecandidate location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 -n location-prod
Name: location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 Namespace: location-prod API Version: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1 Kind: LeaseCandidate Metadata: Creation Timestamp: 2025-01-15T09:00:00Z Spec: Binary Version: 2.2.0 Emulation Version: 2.1.0 Lease Name: location-api-coordinator Ping Time: 2025-01-15T09:59:50.000000Z Renew Time: 2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z Strategy: OldestEmulationVersion Events: <none>
Example 5: Get JobPost Scheduler Candidate Wide Output

This example shows wide output with additional columns.

# Get with wide output
kubectl get leasecandidate jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345 -n jobpost-prod -o wide
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION EMULATION RENEWTIME AGE jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345 jobpost-scheduler-leader 2.0.0 1.8.0 2025-01-15T10:00:02Z 2h

list - List or Watch Objects of Kind LeaseCandidate

Description

Lists all LeaseCandidates in a specific namespace. This operation supports filtering by field and label selectors, pagination with limit and continue tokens, and real-time watching for changes. Use this to monitor all candidates participating in elections within a namespace, analyze version distributions during upgrades, and track candidate renewals.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/leasecandidates
Parameters
  • namespace (path parameter, required): Namespace to list LeaseCandidates from
  • allowWatchBookmarks (query parameter): Enable watch bookmarks
  • continue (query parameter): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (query parameter): Filter by fields (e.g., metadata.name=candidate-name)
  • labelSelector (query parameter): Filter by labels
  • limit (query parameter): Maximum number of results to return
  • pretty (query parameter): Pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (query parameter): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (query parameter): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (query parameter): Send initial events in watch
  • timeoutSeconds (query parameter): Timeout for list/watch operation
  • watch (query parameter): Watch for changes to objects
Return Value
  • 200 (LeaseCandidateList): OK - Returns list of LeaseCandidates
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Skills Validator Candidates

This example lists all candidates in Skills validator namespace.

# List all candidates
kubectl get leasecandidates -n skills-prod
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME skills-validator-v1-pod-abc123 skills-validator-leader 1.9.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z skills-validator-v2-pod-def456 skills-validator-leader 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:05Z skills-validator-v2-pod-ghi789 skills-validator-leader 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:03Z (3 candidates for election)
Example 2: Watch User Auth Candidates in Real-Time

This example watches for changes to User auth candidates.

# Watch for changes
kubectl get leasecandidates -n user-auth --watch
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME user-auth-v2-pod-abc123 user-auth-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z user-auth-v2-pod-def456 user-auth-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:02Z user-auth-v2-pod-abc123 user-auth-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:10Z user-auth-v2-pod-def456 user-auth-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:12Z (Watching for renewals and new candidates...)
Example 3: Filter Author CMS Candidates by Field Selector

This example filters candidates using field selector.

# Filter by candidate name
kubectl get leasecandidates -n author-cms --field-selector metadata.name=author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 author-cms-leader 3.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z (1 candidate matched filter)
Example 4: List Location Candidates Sorted by Creation Time

This example lists candidates sorted by age.

# Sort by creation timestamp
kubectl get leasecandidates -n location-prod --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME location-api-v1-pod-jkl012 location-api-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z location-api-v2-pod-mno345 location-api-coordinator 2.2.0 2025-01-15T10:00:05Z location-api-v2-pod-pqr678 location-api-coordinator 2.2.0 2025-01-15T10:00:03Z location-api-v2-pod-stu901 location-api-coordinator 2.2.0 2025-01-15T10:00:08Z (4 candidates sorted by age)
Example 5: List JobPost Candidates with Custom Columns

This example shows custom columns for JobPost scheduler.

# Custom columns
kubectl get leasecandidates -n jobpost-prod -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,BINARY:.spec.binaryVersion,EMULATION:.spec.emulationVersion,LEASE:.spec.leaseName
NAME BINARY EMULATION LEASE jobpost-scheduler-v1-pod-vwx234 1.5.0 1.5.0 jobpost-scheduler-leader jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-yza567 2.0.0 1.8.0 jobpost-scheduler-leader jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-bcd890 2.0.0 1.9.0 jobpost-scheduler-leader

list - List or Watch Objects of Kind LeaseCandidate (All Namespaces)

Description

Lists all LeaseCandidates across all namespaces in the cluster. This operation provides cluster-wide visibility into coordinated leader election, useful for monitoring distributed systems spanning multiple namespaces. Supports filtering, pagination, and watching like the namespace-scoped list operation.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1/leasecandidates
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (query parameter): Enable watch bookmarks
  • continue (query parameter): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (query parameter): Filter by fields
  • labelSelector (query parameter): Filter by labels
  • limit (query parameter): Maximum number of results
  • pretty (query parameter): Pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (query parameter): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (query parameter): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (query parameter): Send initial events in watch
  • timeoutSeconds (query parameter): Timeout for operation
  • watch (query parameter): Watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (LeaseCandidateList): OK - Returns list of LeaseCandidates from all namespaces
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Candidates Cluster-Wide

This example lists candidates from all namespaces.

# List all candidates
kubectl get leasecandidates --all-namespaces
NAMESPACE NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME skills-prod skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 skills-validator-leader 2.1.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z user-auth user-auth-v2-pod-def456 user-auth-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:05Z author-cms author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 author-cms-leader 3.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:03Z location-prod location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 location-api-coordinator 2.2.0 2025-01-15T10:00:08Z jobpost-prod jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345 jobpost-scheduler-leader 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:02Z (5 candidates across cluster)
Example 2: Watch All Candidates Cluster-Wide

This example watches for any candidate changes across the cluster.

# Watch all namespaces
kubectl get leasecandidates --all-namespaces --watch
NAMESPACE NAME LEASE BINARYVERSION RENEWTIME skills-prod skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 skills-validator-leader 2.1.0 2025-01-15T10:00:00Z user-auth user-auth-v2-pod-def456 user-auth-coordinator 2.0.0 2025-01-15T10:00:05Z skills-prod skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 skills-validator-leader 2.1.0 2025-01-15T10:00:10Z (Watching for cluster-wide changes...)
Example 3: List with Custom Columns Showing Versions

This example shows version distribution across the cluster.

# Custom columns cluster-wide
kubectl get leasecandidates --all-namespaces -o custom-columns=NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,BINARY:.spec.binaryVersion,EMULATION:.spec.emulationVersion
NAMESPACE NAME BINARY EMULATION skills-prod skills-validator-v1-pod-old 1.9.0 1.9.0 skills-prod skills-validator-v2-pod-abc 2.1.0 2.0.0 user-auth user-auth-v2-pod-def 2.0.0 1.9.0 author-cms author-cms-v3-pod-ghi 3.0.0 2.5.0 location-prod location-api-v2-pod-jkl 2.2.0 2.1.0
Example 4: Count Total Candidates Across Cluster

This example counts all active candidates.

# Count all candidates
kubectl get leasecandidates --all-namespaces --no-headers | wc -l
12 (12 candidates active cluster-wide)
Example 5: Filter High Binary Version Candidates

This example finds candidates with binary version 2.0 or higher using grep.

# Filter by binary version pattern
kubectl get leasecandidates --all-namespaces -o custom-columns=NAMESPACE:.metadata.namespace,NAME:.metadata.name,VERSION:.spec.binaryVersion | grep "2\.[0-9]"
NAMESPACE NAME VERSION skills-prod skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 2.1.0 user-auth user-auth-v2-pod-def456 2.0.0 location-prod location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 2.2.0 jobpost-prod jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345 2.0.0 (4 candidates with version 2.x)

create - Create a LeaseCandidate

Description

Creates a new LeaseCandidate for participation in coordinated leader election. The candidate must specify a binary version, lease name to contest, and election strategy. When created, the candidate becomes eligible for selection by the election algorithm based on its version characteristics and the configured strategy.

HTTP Request
POST /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/leasecandidates
Parameters
  • namespace (path parameter, required): Namespace for the LeaseCandidate
  • body (LeaseCandidate, required): The LeaseCandidate object to create
  • dryRun (query parameter): Validate without persisting (client/server/none)
  • fieldManager (query parameter): Manager name for field ownership tracking
  • fieldValidation (query parameter): Field validation level (Strict/Warn/Ignore)
  • pretty (query parameter): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (LeaseCandidate): OK - LeaseCandidate already exists
  • 201 (LeaseCandidate): Created - LeaseCandidate successfully created
  • 202 (LeaseCandidate): Accepted - Request accepted, processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills Validator Candidate

This example creates a new Skills validator candidate for election.

# Create from inline YAML
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: LeaseCandidate
metadata:
  name: skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123
  namespace: skills-prod
spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.1.0"
  leaseName: "skills-validator-leader"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "2.0.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
EOF
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 created
Example 2: Create User Auth Candidate with Latest Binary

This example creates a User auth candidate with latest binary version.

# Create with v2.0 binary
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: LeaseCandidate
metadata:
  name: user-auth-v2-pod-def456
  namespace: user-auth
spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.0.0"
  leaseName: "user-auth-coordinator"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "1.9.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
EOF
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/user-auth-v2-pod-def456 created
Example 3: Create Author CMS Candidate with Matching Versions

This example creates an Author CMS candidate where binary and emulation versions match.

# Create with matching versions
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: LeaseCandidate
metadata:
  name: author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789
  namespace: author-cms
spec:
  binaryVersion: "3.0.0"
  leaseName: "author-cms-leader"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "3.0.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
EOF
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 created
Example 4: Create Location Service Candidate from File

This example creates a Location candidate from a YAML file.

# Create from file
kubectl apply -f location-api-candidate.yaml
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 created # File contents (location-api-candidate.yaml): apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1 kind: LeaseCandidate metadata: name: location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 namespace: location-prod spec: binaryVersion: "2.2.0" leaseName: "location-api-coordinator" strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion" emulationVersion: "2.1.0"
Example 5: Dry Run Create JobPost Scheduler Candidate

This example validates candidate creation without persisting.

# Dry run validation
kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: LeaseCandidate
metadata:
  name: jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345
  namespace: jobpost-prod
spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.0.0"
  leaseName: "jobpost-scheduler-leader"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "1.8.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00.000000Z"
EOF
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345 created (dry run)

update - Replace the Specified LeaseCandidate

Description

Updates an existing LeaseCandidate by replacing it with a new version. This operation is used to update timing fields (renewTime, pingTime) in response to election signals, or to modify version information during rolling upgrades. The entire candidate specification is replaced with the provided definition.

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/leasecandidates/{name}
Parameters
  • name (path parameter, required): Name of the LeaseCandidate to update
  • namespace (path parameter, required): Namespace containing the LeaseCandidate
  • body (LeaseCandidate, required): Updated LeaseCandidate object
  • dryRun (query parameter): Validate without persisting
  • fieldManager (query parameter): Manager name for field ownership
  • fieldValidation (query parameter): Field validation level
  • pretty (query parameter): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (LeaseCandidate): OK - LeaseCandidate successfully updated
  • 201 (LeaseCandidate): Created - LeaseCandidate created as it didn't exist
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Validator Renew Time

This example updates the renewTime in response to a ping.

# Update renew time
kubectl get leasecandidate skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 -n skills-prod -o yaml | \
  sed 's/renewTime: .*/renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:05:00.000000Z"/' | \
  kubectl apply -f -
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 configured (RenewTime updated to 2025-01-15T10:05:00.000000Z)
Example 2: Update User Auth Candidate Version

This example updates binary version during a rolling upgrade.

# Update binary version
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: LeaseCandidate
metadata:
  name: user-auth-v2-pod-def456
  namespace: user-auth
spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.1.0"
  leaseName: "user-auth-coordinator"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "2.0.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:05:00.000000Z"
EOF
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/user-auth-v2-pod-def456 configured (Binary version updated to 2.1.0)
Example 3: Update Author CMS Emulation Version

This example updates emulation version for backward compatibility.

# Update emulation version
kubectl get leasecandidate author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 -n author-cms -o yaml | \
  sed 's/emulationVersion: .*/emulationVersion: "2.8.0"/' | \
  kubectl apply -f -
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 configured (Emulation version updated to 2.8.0)
Example 4: Update Location Candidate from File

This example applies updates from a modified YAML file.

# Update from file
kubectl apply -f location-api-candidate-updated.yaml
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 configured # Updated file includes new renewTime and pingTime (Candidate timing updated)
Example 5: Update JobPost Scheduler Both Timing Fields

This example updates both renewTime and pingTime.

# Update timing fields
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1
kind: LeaseCandidate
metadata:
  name: jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345
  namespace: jobpost-prod
spec:
  binaryVersion: "2.0.0"
  leaseName: "jobpost-scheduler-leader"
  strategy: "OldestEmulationVersion"
  emulationVersion: "1.8.0"
  renewTime: "2025-01-15T10:05:00.500000Z"
  pingTime: "2025-01-15T10:04:50.500000Z"
EOF
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345 configured (Timing fields updated)

patch - Partially Update the Specified LeaseCandidate

Description

Partially updates a LeaseCandidate by applying a patch to specific fields. This operation is more efficient than full updates when only certain fields need modification, such as updating renewTime or pingTime without affecting other candidate properties. Supports JSON Patch, Merge Patch, and Strategic Merge Patch formats.

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/leasecandidates/{name}
Parameters
  • name (path parameter, required): Name of the LeaseCandidate to patch
  • namespace (path parameter, required): Namespace containing the LeaseCandidate
  • body (Patch, required): Patch data
  • dryRun (query parameter): Validate without persisting
  • fieldManager (query parameter): Manager name for field ownership
  • fieldValidation (query parameter): Field validation level
  • force (query parameter): Force apply the patch
  • pretty (query parameter): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (LeaseCandidate): OK - LeaseCandidate successfully patched
  • 201 (LeaseCandidate): Created - LeaseCandidate created by patch
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Validator Renew Time

This example patches only the renewTime field.

# Merge patch renew time
kubectl patch leasecandidate skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 -n skills-prod --type merge -p '{
  "spec": {
    "renewTime": "2025-01-15T10:10:00.000000Z"
  }
}'
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/skills-validator-v2-pod-abc123 patched (RenewTime updated)
Example 2: JSON Patch User Auth Candidate Multiple Fields

This example uses JSON patch for multiple field updates.

# JSON patch array operations
kubectl patch leasecandidate user-auth-v2-pod-def456 -n user-auth --type json -p '[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/renewTime", "value": "2025-01-15T10:10:00.123456Z"},
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/pingTime", "value": "2025-01-15T10:09:50.123456Z"}
]'
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/user-auth-v2-pod-def456 patched (RenewTime and pingTime updated)
Example 3: Patch Author CMS Emulation Version

This example patches emulation version for compatibility adjustment.

# Merge patch emulation version
kubectl patch leasecandidate author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 -n author-cms --type merge -p '{
  "spec": {
    "emulationVersion": "2.9.0"
  }
}'
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/author-cms-v3-pod-ghi789 patched (Emulation version updated to 2.9.0)
Example 4: JSON Patch Location Binary Version

This example patches binary version using JSON patch.

# JSON patch binary version
kubectl patch leasecandidate location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 -n location-prod --type json -p '[
  {"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/binaryVersion", "value": "2.3.0"}
]'
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/location-api-v2-pod-jkl012 patched (Binary version updated to 2.3.0)
Example 5: Patch JobPost Scheduler All Timing Fields

This example patches both timing fields in one operation.

# Merge patch multiple timing fields
kubectl patch leasecandidate jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345 -n jobpost-prod --type merge -p '{
  "spec": {
    "renewTime": "2025-01-15T10:10:00.500000Z",
    "pingTime": "2025-01-15T10:09:50.500000Z"
  }
}'
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io/jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-mno345 patched (Timing fields updated)

delete - Delete a LeaseCandidate

Description

Deletes a specific LeaseCandidate, removing it from participation in coordinated leader election. Use this operation when a pod is being terminated, when manually removing a candidate from election, or when cleaning up stale candidates. The candidate is immediately removed from consideration by the election algorithm.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/leasecandidates/{name}
Parameters
  • name (path parameter, required): Name of the LeaseCandidate to delete
  • namespace (path parameter, required): Namespace containing the LeaseCandidate
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • dryRun (query parameter): Validate without persisting
  • gracePeriodSeconds (query parameter): Grace period for deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (query parameter): Ignore store read errors
  • pretty (query parameter): Pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (query parameter): Deletion propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - LeaseCandidate successfully deleted
  • 202 (Status): Accepted - Deletion request accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Validator Candidate

This example deletes a Skills validator candidate on pod termination.

# Delete candidate
kubectl delete leasecandidate skills-validator-v1-pod-old -n skills-prod
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "skills-validator-v1-pod-old" deleted (Candidate removed from election)
Example 2: Delete User Auth Candidate with Wait

This example deletes a User auth candidate and waits for completion.

# Delete with wait
kubectl delete leasecandidate user-auth-v1-pod-xyz789 -n user-auth --wait=true
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "user-auth-v1-pod-xyz789" deleted (Deletion confirmed, candidate no longer participating)
Example 3: Delete Multiple Author CMS Candidates

This example deletes multiple candidates in one command.

# Delete multiple candidates
kubectl delete leasecandidate author-cms-v1-pod-old1 author-cms-v1-pod-old2 -n author-cms
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "author-cms-v1-pod-old1" deleted leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "author-cms-v1-pod-old2" deleted (2 old candidates removed)
Example 4: Delete Location Candidate from File

This example deletes a candidate specified in a YAML file.

# Delete from file
kubectl delete -f location-api-old-candidate.yaml
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "location-api-v1-pod-old" deleted (Candidate removed based on file specification)
Example 5: Dry Run Delete JobPost Scheduler Candidate

This example validates deletion without actually removing the candidate.

# Dry run delete
kubectl delete leasecandidate jobpost-scheduler-v1-pod-old -n jobpost-prod --dry-run=client
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "jobpost-scheduler-v1-pod-old" deleted (dry run) (Validation successful, candidate would be deleted)

deletecollection - Delete Collection of LeaseCandidate

Description

Deletes multiple LeaseCandidates matching specified criteria within a namespace. This operation supports field and label selectors for filtering candidates to delete. Useful for cleanup operations such as removing all candidates for a specific lease, deleting candidates with old versions, or bulk cleanup during service migration or namespace teardown.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/coordination.k8s.io/v1beta1/namespaces/{namespace}/leasecandidates
Parameters
  • namespace (path parameter, required): Namespace to delete candidates from
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options for the operation
  • continue (query parameter): Continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (query parameter): Validate without persisting
  • fieldSelector (query parameter): Filter by fields (e.g., metadata.name=candidate-name)
  • gracePeriodSeconds (query parameter): Grace period before deletion
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (query parameter): Ignore store read errors
  • labelSelector (query parameter): Filter by labels
  • limit (query parameter): Maximum number to delete
  • pretty (query parameter): Pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (query parameter): Deletion propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (query parameter): Resource version for delete
  • resourceVersionMatch (query parameter): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (query parameter): Send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (query parameter): Timeout for operation
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - Collection successfully deleted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All Skills Validator Candidates

This example deletes all candidates in the Skills namespace.

# Delete all candidates
kubectl delete leasecandidates --all -n skills-prod
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "skills-validator-v1-pod-abc" deleted leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "skills-validator-v2-pod-def" deleted leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "skills-validator-v2-pod-ghi" deleted (3 candidates deleted)
Example 2: Delete User Auth Candidates by Field Selector

This example deletes specific candidates using field selector.

# Delete by field selector
kubectl delete leasecandidates -n user-auth --field-selector metadata.name=user-auth-v1-pod-xyz789
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "user-auth-v1-pod-xyz789" deleted (1 candidate deleted)
Example 3: Delete Old Author CMS Candidates by Creation Time

This example deletes candidates created before a specific date.

# Delete old candidates (using kubectl and jq for filtering)
kubectl get leasecandidates -n author-cms -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.metadata.creationTimestamp < "2025-01-01") | .metadata.name' | \
  xargs -I {} kubectl delete leasecandidate {} -n author-cms
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "author-cms-v1-pod-old1" deleted leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "author-cms-v1-pod-old2" deleted (2 old candidates deleted)
Example 4: Delete Location Candidates for Specific Lease

This example deletes all candidates contending for a specific lease.

# Delete candidates for specific lease
kubectl get leasecandidates -n location-prod -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.leaseName == "location-api-old-lease") | .metadata.name' | \
  xargs -I {} kubectl delete leasecandidate {} -n location-prod
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "location-api-v1-pod-abc" deleted leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "location-api-v1-pod-def" deleted (2 candidates for old lease deleted)
Example 5: Dry Run Delete All JobPost Scheduler Candidates

This example validates bulk deletion without removing candidates.

# Dry run delete all
kubectl delete leasecandidates --all -n jobpost-prod --dry-run=client
leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "jobpost-scheduler-v1-pod-pqr" deleted (dry run) leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-stu" deleted (dry run) leasecandidate.coordination.k8s.io "jobpost-scheduler-v2-pod-vwx" deleted (dry run) (3 candidates would be deleted)

Namespace

Namespace Overview

NamespaceSpec - Namespace specification
NamespaceStatus - Namespace status information
NamespaceList - Collection of Namespaces
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: Namespace v1 API | API Version: v1

Namespace provides a scope for Names, enabling resource isolation and multi-tenancy within a Kubernetes cluster.

Full Description

Namespace provides a scope for Names, creating logical boundaries within a Kubernetes cluster for resource isolation and organization. Use of multiple namespaces is optional but recommended for multi-tenancy, environment separation (development, staging, production), and team-based resource management. Namespaces enable resource quotas, network policies, and RBAC boundaries, ensuring that Skills validation services, User authentication systems, Author content management tools, Location tracking services, and JobPost scheduling systems operate in isolated environments. Namespaces are cluster-scoped resources that provide the fundamental isolation mechanism for all namespace-scoped resources, supporting secure multi-tenant architectures and preventing resource name conflicts across teams and applications.

Syntax

# Create a Namespace from YAML file
kubectl create -f namespace.yaml
# Create a Namespace imperatively
kubectl create namespace {name}
# List all Namespaces
kubectl get namespaces
# List all Namespaces (short form)
kubectl get ns
# Get detailed information about a specific Namespace
kubectl describe namespace {name}
# Get detailed information about a specific Namespace (short form)
kubectl describe ns {name}
# Get Namespace in YAML format
kubectl get namespace {name} -o yaml
# Delete a specific Namespace
kubectl delete namespace {name}
# Delete a specific Namespace (short form)
kubectl delete ns {name}

YAML Structure

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-namespace
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    environment: production
    team: platform
spec:
  finalizers:
    - kubernetes
status:
  phase: Active

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion (string): v1
  • kind (string): Namespace
  • metadata (ObjectMeta): Standard object's metadata including name, labels, annotations. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#metadata
  • spec (NamespaceSpec): Defines the behavior of the Namespace, including finalizers for cleanup hooks. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#spec-and-status
  • status (NamespaceStatus): Describes the current status including phase (Active/Terminating) and conditions. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#spec-and-status

Return Value

  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Request successful
  • 201 (Namespace): Created - Namespace successfully created
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create Namespace for Skills Model Service

This example demonstrates creating a dedicated Namespace for the Skills model service in the jobpost app.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    component: validation
kubectl create -f skills-namespace.yaml
kubectl get namespace anihpj-skills
namespace/anihpj-skills created NAME STATUS AGE anihpj-skills Active 5s
Example 2: Create Namespace for User Authentication Service

This example shows creating a Namespace for Django User model authentication with labels.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-auth
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    component: auth
    environment: production
kubectl create -f user-namespace.yaml
kubectl describe namespace anihpj-user-auth
namespace/anihpj-user-auth created Name: anihpj-user-auth Labels: app=anihpj component=auth environment=production model=user Annotations: Status: Active
Example 3: Create Namespace for Author Content Service

This example illustrates creating a Namespace for the Author model with finalizers.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    component: content
spec:
  finalizers:
    - kubernetes
kubectl create -f author-namespace.yaml
kubectl get namespace anihpj-author -o yaml
namespace/anihpj-author created apiVersion: v1 kind: Namespace metadata: name: anihpj-author labels: app: anihpj model: author component: content spec: finalizers: - kubernetes status: phase: Active
Example 4: Create Namespace for Location Lookup Service

This example demonstrates creating a Namespace for the Location model geolocation service.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    component: geolocation
kubectl create namespace anihpj-location
kubectl label namespace anihpj-location app=anihpj model=location component=geolocation
namespace/anihpj-location created namespace/anihpj-location labeled
Example 5: Create Namespace for JobPost Scheduler Service

This example shows creating a Namespace for the JobPost model scheduler with annotations.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    component: scheduler
  annotations:
    description: "Namespace for JobPost scheduling and management"
kubectl create -f jobpost-namespace.yaml
kubectl get namespace anihpj-jobpost
namespace/anihpj-jobpost created NAME STATUS AGE anihpj-jobpost Active 5s

NamespaceSpec

NamespaceSpec describes the attributes on a Namespace, primarily focusing on finalizers that control cleanup behavior. Finalizers are opaque values that must be cleared before the namespace can be permanently removed from storage, enabling controllers to perform cleanup operations for Skills validation resources, User authentication data, Author content artifacts, Location tracking records, and JobPost scheduling state. When a namespace is deleted, it enters Terminating phase and waits for all finalizers to be removed by their respective controllers, ensuring graceful cleanup of all namespace-scoped resources and preventing resource leaks across the cluster.

Syntax

spec:
  finalizers:
    - kubernetes
    - custom-controller.example.com/cleanup

Parameters

  • finalizers ([]string): Atomic field (replaced during merge). Opaque list of values that must be empty to permanently remove object from storage. Common values include "kubernetes" (default finalizer) and custom controller finalizers. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/namespaces/

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Basic Skills Namespace Spec
spec:
  finalizers:
    - kubernetes
Example 2: User Auth Namespace with Custom Finalizer
spec:
  finalizers:
    - kubernetes
    - user-auth-controller.anihpj.com/cleanup
Example 3: Author CMS Namespace with Multiple Finalizers
spec:
  finalizers:
    - kubernetes
    - author-cms-controller.anihpj.com/content-cleanup
    - author-cms-controller.anihpj.com/storage-cleanup
Example 4: Location Namespace Empty Finalizers
spec:
  finalizers: []
Example 5: JobPost Namespace with Scheduler Finalizer
spec:
  finalizers:
    - kubernetes
    - jobpost-scheduler.anihpj.com/job-cleanup

NamespaceStatus

NamespaceStatus is information about the current status of a Namespace, including its lifecycle phase and conditions. The phase indicates whether the namespace is Active (available for use) or Terminating (undergoing graceful deletion), while conditions provide detailed observations about namespace state. When a namespace is deleted, it transitions to Terminating phase and waits for all finalizers to be cleared by controllers managing Skills validation cleanup, User authentication teardown, Author content archival, Location tracking finalization, and JobPost scheduling state removal, ensuring all namespace-scoped resources are properly cleaned up before permanent deletion.

Syntax

status:
  phase: Active
  conditions:
    - type: NamespaceDeletionDiscoveryFailure
      status: "False"
      lastTransitionTime: "2025-01-15T10:00:00Z"
      reason: DiscoverySucceeded
      message: All resources discovered successfully

Parameters

  • phase (string): Current lifecycle phase of the namespace. Possible values:
    • "Active": Namespace is available for use in the system
    • "Terminating": Namespace is undergoing graceful termination, waiting for finalizers to be cleared
    More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/namespaces/
  • conditions ([]NamespaceCondition): Patch strategy (merge on key type), Map (unique values on key type kept during merge). Represents the latest available observations of a namespace's current state. Each condition contains:
    • status (string, required): Status of the condition, one of True, False, Unknown
    • type (string, required): Type of namespace controller condition
    • lastTransitionTime (Time): Last time the condition transitioned from one status to another. Time supports correct marshaling to YAML and JSON
    • message (string): Human-readable message indicating details about last transition
    • reason (string): Unique, one-word, CamelCase reason for the condition's last transition

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Skills Namespace Active Status
status:
  phase: Active
Example 2: User Auth Namespace Terminating Status
status:
  phase: Terminating
  conditions:
    - type: NamespaceDeletionContentFailure
      status: "True"
      lastTransitionTime: "2025-01-15T10:05:00Z"
      reason: ResourcesRemaining
      message: Some content in the namespace has finalizers remaining
Example 3: Author CMS Namespace with Discovery Condition
status:
  phase: Active
  conditions:
    - type: NamespaceDeletionDiscoveryFailure
      status: "False"
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-11-01T10:00:00Z"
      reason: DiscoverySucceeded
      message: All resources discovered successfully
Example 4: Location Namespace with Multiple Conditions
status:
  phase: Active
  conditions:
    - type: NamespaceDeletionDiscoveryFailure
      status: "False"
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-12-01T08:00:00Z"
      reason: DiscoverySucceeded
    - type: NamespaceDeletionContentFailure
      status: "False"
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-12-01T08:00:00Z"
      reason: NoContentRemaining
Example 5: JobPost Namespace Terminating with Finalizer Wait
status:
  phase: Terminating
  conditions:
    - type: NamespaceContentRemaining
      status: "True"
      lastTransitionTime: "2025-01-15T11:00:00Z"
      reason: SomeResourcesRemain
      message: Jobs remain in namespace pending cleanup

NamespaceList

NamespaceList is a list of Namespaces, providing a comprehensive view of all isolation boundaries within the cluster. List operations return all namespaces regardless of their status, enabling administrators to monitor Active namespaces for Skills validation, User authentication, Author content management, Location tracking, and JobPost scheduling, as well as Terminating namespaces undergoing cleanup. NamespaceList supports filtering, sorting, and watching capabilities for real-time monitoring of namespace lifecycle changes across the entire cluster.

Syntax

# List all namespaces
kubectl get namespaces

# List with wide output
kubectl get namespaces -o wide

# List in YAML format
kubectl get namespaces -o yaml

# List in JSON format
kubectl get namespaces -o json

# Count namespaces
kubectl get namespaces --no-headers | wc -l

Parameters

  • apiVersion (string): v1
  • kind (string): NamespaceList
  • metadata (ListMeta): Standard list metadata. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#types-kinds
  • items ([]Namespace, required): Items is the list of Namespace objects in the list. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/overview/working-with-objects/namespaces/

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: List All Skills-Related Namespaces
# List namespaces with Skills in the name
kubectl get namespaces | grep skills

Expected Output:

NAME                STATUS   AGE
skills-dev          Active   90d
skills-staging      Active   75d
skills-validation   Active   45d
Example 2: Export User Namespaces to JSON
# Export User namespaces to JSON with status information
kubectl get namespaces -o json | jq '.items[] | select(.metadata.name | contains("user"))'

Expected Output:

{
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "kind": "Namespace",
  "metadata": {
    "name": "user-auth-dev",
    "creationTimestamp": "2024-10-15T08:00:00Z"
  },
  "spec": {
    "finalizers": ["kubernetes"]
  },
  "status": {
    "phase": "Active"
  }
}
Example 3: Backup All Author CMS Namespaces to YAML
# Backup Author namespaces to YAML file
kubectl get namespaces -l team=content -o yaml > author-namespaces-backup.yaml

Expected Output:

apiVersion: v1
items:
- apiVersion: v1
  kind: Namespace
  metadata:
    name: author-cms
    labels:
      team: content
  spec:
    finalizers:
    - kubernetes
  status:
    phase: Active
kind: List
metadata:
  resourceVersion: ""
Example 4: Count Location Tracking Namespaces
# Count how many Location namespaces exist
kubectl get namespaces | grep location | wc -l

Expected Output:

4
Example 5: List JobPost Namespaces with Custom Columns
# List JobPost namespaces with phase and age
kubectl get namespaces -l app=jobpost -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp

Expected Output:

NAME                STATUS   AGE
jobpost-dev         Active   2024-11-20T09:00:00Z
jobpost-staging     Active   2024-11-25T14:30:00Z
jobpost-scheduler   Active   2024-12-01T10:00:00Z

Namespace Operations

Operations Overview

get - Read the specified Namespace
get (status) - Read status of the specified Namespace
list - List all Namespaces
create - Create a Namespace
update - Replace the specified Namespace
update (finalize) - Update finalization of the specified Namespace
update (status) - Replace status of the specified Namespace
patch - Partially update the specified Namespace
patch (status) - Partially update status of the specified Namespace
delete - Delete a Namespace

Operations allow you to interact with Namespace resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage isolation boundaries and resource scopes for your anihpj Django application services, including Skills validation, User authentication, Author content management, Location tracking, and JobPost scheduling.

get - read the specified Namespace

Read the specified Namespace to retrieve its configuration including finalizers, labels, and current status. This operation returns the complete Namespace object with metadata, spec, and status, enabling administrators to verify isolation boundaries for Skills validation, User authentication, Author content management, Location tracking, and JobPost scheduling services. Use this operation to inspect namespace configuration before deploying resources or troubleshooting isolation issues.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/namespaces/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Namespace
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Namespace successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Validation Namespace

This example retrieves the Skills validation namespace.

# Get Skills namespace
kubectl get namespace skills-validation
NAME STATUS AGE skills-validation Active 45d
Example 2: Get User Auth Namespace YAML

This example retrieves User namespace in YAML format with all details.

# Get User namespace YAML
kubectl get namespace user-auth-prod -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: Namespace metadata: name: user-auth-prod labels: environment: production team: identity creationTimestamp: "2024-10-15T08:00:00Z" spec: finalizers: - kubernetes status: phase: Active
Example 3: Get Author CMS Namespace JSON

This example retrieves Author namespace in JSON format.

# Get Author namespace JSON
kubectl get namespace author-cms -o json
{ "apiVersion": "v1", "kind": "Namespace", "metadata": { "name": "author-cms", "labels": { "environment": "production", "team": "content" }, "creationTimestamp": "2024-11-01T10:00:00Z" }, "spec": { "finalizers": ["kubernetes"] }, "status": { "phase": "Active" } }
Example 4: Describe Location Tracking Namespace

This example describes Location namespace with detailed information.

# Describe Location namespace
kubectl describe namespace location-tracking
Name: location-tracking Labels: environment=production team=infrastructure Annotations: <none> Status: Active Resource Quotas: Name: location-quota Resource Used Hard -------- ---- ---- cpu 2 4 memory 4Gi 8Gi No LimitRange resource.
Example 5: Get JobPost Namespace with Wide Output

This example retrieves JobPost namespace with wide output format.

# Get JobPost namespace wide output
kubectl get namespace jobpost-scheduler -o wide
NAME STATUS AGE jobpost-scheduler Active 30d

get - read status of the specified Namespace

Read status of the specified Namespace using the status subresource endpoint to retrieve phase (Active/Terminating) and conditions without full namespace configuration. This operation is useful for monitoring namespace lifecycle transitions during deletion, tracking when Skills validation, User authentication, Author content management, Location tracking, and JobPost scheduling namespaces enter Terminating phase while waiting for finalizer cleanup. Use this operation for status-only queries that don't require full namespace spec.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/namespaces/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Namespace
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Namespace status successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Check Skills Namespace Status

This example checks Skills namespace status phase.

# Check Skills namespace status
kubectl get namespace skills-validation -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'
Active
Example 2: Monitor User Auth Namespace Deletion Status

This example monitors User namespace during deletion.

# Monitor User namespace status during deletion
kubectl get namespace user-auth-old -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}{"\n"}{.status.conditions}'
Terminating [{"type":"NamespaceDeletionContentFailure","status":"True","lastTransitionTime":"2025-01-15T10:05:00Z","reason":"ResourcesRemaining","message":"Some content remains"}]
Example 3: Get Author CMS Namespace Full Status

This example retrieves full status object for Author namespace.

# Get Author namespace full status
kubectl get namespace author-cms -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{ "phase": "Active" }
Example 4: Check Location Namespace Conditions

This example checks conditions on Location namespace.

# Check Location namespace conditions
kubectl get namespace location-tracking -o yaml | grep -A 10 "status:"
status: phase: Active
Example 5: Watch JobPost Namespace Status Changes

This example watches JobPost namespace for status changes.

# Watch JobPost namespace status
kubectl get namespace jobpost-scheduler -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}' --watch
Active Active

list - list or watch objects of kind Namespace

List or watch objects of kind Namespace to retrieve all isolation boundaries across the cluster. This cluster-wide operation returns all namespaces regardless of their status, enabling administrators to monitor Active namespaces for Skills validation, User authentication, Author content management, Location tracking, and JobPost scheduling, as well as Terminating namespaces undergoing cleanup. Supports filtering by labels, field selectors, and watch mode for real-time monitoring of namespace lifecycle changes across the entire cluster.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/namespaces
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query, boolean): Allow watch bookmarks for efficient watching
  • continue (in query, string): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query, string): Field selector to filter namespaces
  • labelSelector (in query, string): Label selector to filter namespaces
  • limit (in query, integer): Maximum number of namespaces to return
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query, string): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query, string): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query, boolean): Send initial events in watch mode
  • timeoutSeconds (in query, integer): Timeout for list operation
  • watch (in query, boolean): Watch for changes to namespaces
Return Value
  • 200 (NamespaceList): OK - Namespace list successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Namespaces for Skills Services

This example lists all namespaces in the cluster.

# List all namespaces
kubectl get namespaces
NAME STATUS AGE default Active 180d kube-system Active 180d skills-validation Active 45d user-auth-prod Active 60d author-cms Active 75d
Example 2: Watch User Namespaces for Real-Time Changes

This example watches User namespaces for creation and deletion.

# Watch User namespaces
kubectl get namespaces -l team=identity --watch
NAME STATUS AGE user-auth-dev Active 60d user-auth-prod Active 60d user-auth-staging Active 50d
Example 3: Filter Author Namespaces by Label

This example filters Author namespaces using label selector.

# Filter Author namespaces by team label
kubectl get namespaces -l team=content
NAME STATUS AGE author-cms Active 75d
Example 4: List Location Namespaces Sorted by Age

This example lists Location namespaces sorted by creation time.

# List Location namespaces sorted by age
kubectl get namespaces -l app=location --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME STATUS AGE location-tracking Active 90d location-geo-dev Active 60d location-geo-staging Active 45d
Example 5: List JobPost Namespaces with Custom Columns

This example lists JobPost namespaces with phase and labels.

# List JobPost namespaces with custom columns
kubectl get namespaces -l app=jobpost -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,STATUS:.status.phase,ENV:.metadata.labels.environment
NAME STATUS ENV jobpost-dev Active development jobpost-staging Active staging jobpost-scheduler Active production

create - create a Namespace

Create a Namespace to establish a new isolation boundary within the cluster for resource organization and multi-tenancy. This operation initializes a new namespace with specified finalizers, labels, and metadata, creating a dedicated scope for Skills validation resources, User authentication services, Author content management systems, Location tracking components, or JobPost scheduling infrastructure. Newly created namespaces enter Active phase immediately and remain until deleted, at which point they transition to Terminating phase while waiting for finalizer cleanup.

HTTP Request
POST /api/v1/namespaces
Parameters
  • body (Namespace, required): Namespace object to create with metadata, spec, and optional status
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode (All, Server) for validation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name for tracking field ownership
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Namespace successfully created
  • 201 (Namespace): Created - Namespace successfully created
  • 202 (Namespace): Accepted - Namespace creation accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills Validation Namespace

This example creates a new Skills validation namespace.

# Create Skills namespace
kubectl create namespace skills-validation
namespace/skills-validation created
Example 2: Create User Auth Namespace with Labels

This example creates User namespace with environment and team labels.

# Create User namespace with labels
kubectl create namespace user-auth-prod
kubectl label namespace user-auth-prod environment=production team=identity
namespace/user-auth-prod created namespace/user-auth-prod labeled
Example 3: Create Author CMS Namespace from YAML

This example creates Author namespace from YAML file with custom finalizers.

# Create Author namespace from YAML
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: author-cms
  labels:
    environment: production
    team: content
spec:
  finalizers:
    - kubernetes
    - author-cms-controller.anihpj.com/content-cleanup
EOF
namespace/author-cms created
Example 4: Create Location Namespace from File

This example creates Location namespace from external file.

# Create Location namespace from file
kubectl create -f location-namespace.yaml
namespace/location-tracking created
Example 5: Dry Run Create JobPost Namespace

This example validates JobPost namespace creation without persisting.

# Dry run create JobPost namespace
kubectl create namespace jobpost-scheduler --dry-run=client -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: Namespace metadata: name: jobpost-scheduler spec: finalizers: - kubernetes

update - replace the specified Namespace

Replace the specified Namespace to update its configuration including finalizers, labels, and annotations. This operation performs a full replacement of the namespace spec, commonly used to update labels for Skills validation categorization, User authentication environment markers, Author content management team assignments, Location tracking metadata, or JobPost scheduling tags. Note that the namespace name cannot be changed and status updates require the separate status subresource endpoint.

HTTP Request
PUT /api/v1/namespaces/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Namespace
  • body (Namespace, required): Updated Namespace object
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode for validation
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Namespace successfully updated
  • 201 (Namespace): Created - Namespace successfully updated
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Namespace Labels

This example updates labels on Skills namespace.

# Update Skills namespace labels
kubectl label namespace skills-validation version=v2 --overwrite
namespace/skills-validation labeled
Example 2: Update User Auth Namespace Annotations

This example adds annotations to User namespace.

# Update User namespace annotations
kubectl annotate namespace user-auth-prod contact=identity-team@anihpj.com
namespace/user-auth-prod annotated
Example 3: Update Author CMS Namespace Finalizers

This example updates finalizers on Author namespace from YAML.

# Update Author namespace finalizers
kubectl get namespace author-cms -o yaml > author-ns.yaml
# Edit author-ns.yaml to add finalizer
# spec:
#   finalizers:
#     - kubernetes
#     - author-cms-controller.anihpj.com/storage-cleanup
kubectl replace -f author-ns.yaml
namespace/author-cms replaced
Example 4: Update Location Namespace from File

This example updates Location namespace configuration from file.

# Update Location namespace from file
kubectl replace -f location-namespace-updated.yaml
namespace/location-tracking replaced
Example 5: Update JobPost Namespace with Multiple Labels

This example updates JobPost namespace with multiple new labels.

# Update JobPost namespace labels
kubectl label namespace jobpost-scheduler tier=critical monitoring=enabled --overwrite
namespace/jobpost-scheduler labeled

update - replace finalize of the specified Namespace

Replace finalize of the specified Namespace using the finalize subresource to manage finalizers that control namespace deletion behavior. This operation is used by controllers to add or remove finalizers, ensuring proper cleanup of Skills validation resources, User authentication data, Author content artifacts, Location tracking state, and JobPost scheduling jobs before namespace deletion completes. Controllers add finalizers when creating resources and remove them after cleanup, allowing the namespace to transition from Terminating to permanent deletion once all finalizers are cleared.

HTTP Request
PUT /api/v1/namespaces/{name}/finalize
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Namespace
  • body (Namespace, required): Namespace object with updated finalizers
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode for validation
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Namespace finalizers successfully updated
  • 201 (Namespace): Created - Namespace finalizers successfully updated
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Add Skills Controller Finalizer

This example adds a custom finalizer for Skills controller.

# Add Skills controller finalizer
kubectl patch namespace skills-validation --type=json -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/finalizers/-", "value": "skills-controller.anihpj.com/cleanup"}]'
namespace/skills-validation patched
Example 2: Remove User Auth Finalizer After Cleanup

This example removes finalizer after User resources cleaned up.

# Remove User auth finalizer
kubectl get namespace user-auth-old -o json | \
  jq '.spec.finalizers = []' | \
  kubectl replace --raw /api/v1/namespaces/user-auth-old/finalize -f -
namespace/user-auth-old finalized
Example 3: Update Author CMS Finalizers List

This example updates entire finalizers list for Author namespace.

# Update Author finalizers list
kubectl patch namespace author-cms --type=merge -p '{"spec":{"finalizers":["kubernetes","author-cms-controller.anihpj.com/content-cleanup","author-cms-controller.anihpj.com/storage-cleanup"]}}'
namespace/author-cms patched
Example 4: Clear Location Namespace Finalizers

This example clears all finalizers to allow immediate deletion.

# Clear Location finalizers
kubectl patch namespace location-old --type=json -p='[{"op": "replace", "path": "/spec/finalizers", "value": []}]'
namespace/location-old patched
Example 5: Add JobPost Scheduler Finalizer

This example adds JobPost controller finalizer for job cleanup.

# Add JobPost scheduler finalizer
kubectl patch namespace jobpost-scheduler --type=json -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/finalizers/-", "value": "jobpost-scheduler.anihpj.com/job-cleanup"}]'
namespace/jobpost-scheduler patched

update - replace status of the specified Namespace

Replace status of the specified Namespace using the status subresource to update phase and conditions. This operation is typically used by Kubernetes controllers to transition namespaces between Active and Terminating phases, update deletion conditions for Skills validation cleanup, User authentication teardown, Author content archival, Location tracking finalization, and JobPost scheduling state removal. Status updates are separate from spec updates, following the Kubernetes status subresource pattern for clean separation of desired state and observed state.

HTTP Request
PUT /api/v1/namespaces/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Namespace
  • body (Namespace, required): Namespace object with updated status
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode for validation
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Namespace status successfully updated
  • 201 (Namespace): Created - Namespace status successfully updated
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Check Skills Namespace Status After Update

This example verifies Skills namespace status remains Active.

# Check Skills namespace status
kubectl get namespace skills-validation -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'
Active
Example 2: Monitor User Namespace Terminating Status

This example monitors User namespace during deletion.

# Monitor User namespace status
kubectl get namespace user-auth-old -o yaml | grep -A 5 "status:"
status: conditions: - lastTransitionTime: "2025-01-15T10:05:00Z" message: Some content remains reason: ResourcesRemaining status: "True" type: NamespaceDeletionContentFailure phase: Terminating
Example 3: View Author Namespace Status Conditions

This example views status conditions on Author namespace.

# View Author namespace status
kubectl get namespace author-cms -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{ "phase": "Active" }
Example 4: Check Location Namespace Phase Transition

This example checks Location namespace phase during deletion.

# Check Location namespace phase
kubectl get namespace location-old -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,PHASE:.status.phase,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME PHASE AGE location-old Terminating 2024-11-01T10:00:00Z
Example 5: Watch JobPost Namespace Status Changes

This example watches JobPost namespace for status updates.

# Watch JobPost namespace status
kubectl get namespace jobpost-scheduler -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}' --watch
Active Active

patch - partially update the specified Namespace

Partially update the specified Namespace to modify specific fields without replacing the entire object. This operation supports strategic merge patch, JSON merge patch, and JSON patch formats, enabling targeted updates to labels, annotations, or finalizers for Skills validation configuration, User authentication metadata, Author content management tags, Location tracking markers, or JobPost scheduling attributes. Patch operations are more efficient than full updates when modifying only a few fields and preserve fields not included in the patch.

HTTP Request
PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Namespace
  • body (Patch, required): Patch object containing changes
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode for validation
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • force (in query, boolean): Force patch operation
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Namespace successfully patched
  • 201 (Namespace): Created - Namespace successfully patched
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Namespace Label

This example patches a single label on Skills namespace.

# Patch Skills namespace label
kubectl patch namespace skills-validation -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"tier":"critical"}}}'
namespace/skills-validation patched
Example 2: JSON Patch User Namespace Annotations

This example uses JSON patch to update User namespace annotations.

# JSON patch User namespace
kubectl patch namespace user-auth-prod --type=json -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/annotations/last-updated", "value": "2025-01-15"}]'
namespace/user-auth-prod patched
Example 3: Merge Patch Author Namespace Multiple Labels

This example merge patches multiple labels on Author namespace.

# Merge patch Author namespace labels
kubectl patch namespace author-cms --type=merge -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"version":"v2","monitoring":"enabled"}}}'
namespace/author-cms patched
Example 4: JSON Patch Location Namespace Finalizer

This example uses JSON patch to add finalizer to Location namespace.

# JSON patch Location finalizer
kubectl patch namespace location-tracking --type=json -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/finalizers/-", "value": "location-controller.anihpj.com/cleanup"}]'
namespace/location-tracking patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost Namespace Annotation

This example patches annotation on JobPost namespace.

# Patch JobPost namespace annotation
kubectl patch namespace jobpost-scheduler -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"backup":"enabled"}}}'
namespace/jobpost-scheduler patched

patch - partially update status of the specified Namespace

Partially update status of the specified Namespace using the status subresource to modify phase or conditions without replacing the entire status object. This operation is used by controllers to update namespace status fields during deletion, adding conditions about Skills validation cleanup progress, User authentication teardown state, Author content archival status, Location tracking finalization, and JobPost scheduling removal. Status patches follow the same patch formats as regular patches (strategic merge, JSON merge, JSON patch) but only affect the status subresource.

HTTP Request
PATCH /api/v1/namespaces/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Namespace
  • body (Patch, required): Patch object containing status changes
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode for validation
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • force (in query, boolean): Force patch operation
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Namespace): OK - Namespace status successfully patched
  • 201 (Namespace): Created - Namespace status successfully patched
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Verify Skills Namespace Status Patch

This example verifies Skills namespace status after operation.

# Verify Skills namespace status
kubectl get namespace skills-validation -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}'
Active
Example 2: Patch User Namespace Status Condition

This example patches status condition on User namespace.

# Patch User namespace status (typically done by controllers)
# This is示 demonstrative - status is usually managed by controllers
kubectl get namespace user-auth-prod -o jsonpath='{.status}'
{"phase":"Active"}
Example 3: Monitor Author Namespace Status Patch

This example monitors Author namespace status for changes.

# Monitor Author namespace status
kubectl get namespace author-cms -o yaml | grep -A 3 "status:"
status: phase: Active
Example 4: Check Location Namespace Status After Patch

This example checks Location namespace status conditions.

# Check Location namespace status
kubectl get namespace location-tracking -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}{"\n"}'
Active
Example 5: Watch JobPost Namespace Status Patches

This example watches JobPost namespace for status updates.

# Watch JobPost namespace status
kubectl get namespace jobpost-scheduler --watch -o jsonpath='{.status.phase}{"\n"}'
Active Active

delete - delete a Namespace

Delete a Namespace to remove the isolation boundary and all resources within it. This operation transitions the namespace to Terminating phase and waits for all finalizers to be cleared by controllers responsible for cleaning up Skills validation resources, User authentication data, Author content artifacts, Location tracking state, and JobPost scheduling jobs. Once all finalizers are removed and all namespace-scoped resources are deleted, the namespace is permanently removed from the cluster. Note that namespace deletion is cascading by default, meaning all resources within the namespace are also deleted.

HTTP Request
DELETE /api/v1/namespaces/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Namespace
  • body (DeleteOptions): Options for deletion including grace period and propagation policy
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode for validation
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query, integer): Grace period for deletion in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query, string): Propagation policy (Foreground, Background, Orphan)
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - Namespace successfully deleted
  • 202 (Status): Accepted - Namespace deletion accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Old Skills Development Namespace

This example deletes an old Skills development namespace.

# Delete Skills dev namespace
kubectl delete namespace skills-dev-old
namespace "skills-dev-old" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Auth Namespace with Wait

This example deletes User namespace and waits for completion.

# Delete User namespace with wait
kubectl delete namespace user-auth-test --wait=true
namespace "user-auth-test" deleted
Example 3: Delete Multiple Author Namespaces

This example deletes multiple Author namespaces by label.

# Delete multiple Author namespaces
kubectl delete namespaces -l environment=staging,team=content
namespace "author-cms-staging-old" deleted namespace "author-cms-staging-test" deleted
Example 4: Delete Location Namespace from File

This example deletes Location namespace using YAML file.

# Delete Location namespace from file
kubectl delete -f location-old-namespace.yaml
namespace "location-geo-old" deleted
Example 5: Dry Run Delete JobPost Namespace

This example validates JobPost namespace deletion without removing it.

# Dry run delete JobPost namespace
kubectl delete namespace jobpost-test --dry-run=client
namespace "jobpost-test" deleted (dry run)

Node

Node Overview

NodeSpec - Node specification describing node attributes
NodeStatus - Current observed status of the node
NodeList - Collection of Nodes
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: Node v1 API | API Version: v1

Node is a worker node in Kubernetes, each with a unique identifier for running your anihpj Django application pods.

Full Description

Node is a worker node in Kubernetes. Each node will have a unique identifier in the cache (i.e. in etcd). Nodes are the physical or virtual machines where your anihpj Django application containers run, hosting the Skills validation service, User authentication service, Author content management, Location tracking, and JobPost scheduling workloads. The Kubernetes control plane manages and monitors these nodes to ensure your application pods are running as expected.

Nodes provide the computational resources (CPU, memory, storage) required by your application. Each node runs the kubelet agent that communicates with the control plane, the container runtime (like Docker or containerd) that runs your containers, and kube-proxy that manages networking. Understanding node configuration and status is crucial for capacity planning, troubleshooting, and ensuring your anihpj services have adequate resources.

Syntax

# Create a Node from YAML file
kubectl create -f node.yaml
# Create a Node imperatively
kubectl create node {name}
# List all Nodes
kubectl get nodes
# List all Nodes (short form)
kubectl get no
# Get detailed information about a specific Node
kubectl describe node {name}
# Get detailed information about a specific Node (short form)
kubectl describe no {name}
# Get Node in YAML format
kubectl get node {name} -o yaml
# Delete a specific Node
kubectl delete node {name}
# Delete a specific Node (short form)
kubectl delete no {name}

YAML Structure

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-worker-node-1
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    role: worker
    environment: production
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.1.0/24
  providerID: aws:///us-east-1a/i-0123456789abcdef0
  taints:
  - effect: NoSchedule
    key: dedicated
    value: anihpj-services
status:
  capacity:
    cpu: "4"
    memory: 16Gi
    pods: "110"
  allocatable:
    cpu: "4"
    memory: 15Gi
