    pods: "110"

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "v1" for Node
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "Node"
  • metadata: ObjectMeta - Standard object's metadata including name, namespace, and labels. More info: API Conventions
  • spec: NodeSpec - Spec defines the behavior of a node. More info: API Conventions
  • status: NodeStatus - Most recently observed status of the node. Populated by the system. Read-only. More info: API Conventions

Return Value

  • 200 (Node): OK - Request successful, Node returned
  • 201 (Node): Created - Node successfully created
  • 202 (Node): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create Node for Skills Service Workloads

This example demonstrates creating a Node dedicated to Skills validation workloads.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-node
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    role: worker
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.2.0/24
  taints:
  - effect: NoSchedule
    key: dedicated
    value: skills-service
kubectl create -f skills-node.yaml
kubectl get node anihpj-skills-node
node/anihpj-skills-node created NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION anihpj-skills-node Ready worker 5s v1.28.0
Example 2: Create Node for User Authentication Service

This example shows creating a Node for User authentication with high memory.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-node
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    memory: high
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.3.0/24
  providerID: aws:///us-east-1b/i-abcdef0123456789
status:
  capacity:
    memory: 32Gi
kubectl create -f user-node.yaml
kubectl get node anihpj-user-node -o wide
node/anihpj-user-node created NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION INTERNAL-IP EXTERNAL-IP anihpj-user-node Ready worker 3s v1.28.0 10.0.1.100 54.123.45.67
Example 3: Create Node for Author Content Service

This example illustrates creating a Node for Author CMS with storage optimization.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-node
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    storage: optimized
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.4.0/24
  taints:
  - effect: PreferNoSchedule
    key: storage-intensive
    value: "true"
kubectl create -f author-node.yaml
kubectl describe node anihpj-author-node | grep -A5 Taints
node/anihpj-author-node created Taints: storage-intensive=true:PreferNoSchedule Unschedulable: false Labels: app=anihpj model=author storage=optimized
Example 4: Create Node for Location Tracking Service

This example demonstrates creating a Node for Location service with GPU support.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-node
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    gpu: nvidia-t4
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.5.0/24
status:
  capacity:
    nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
kubectl create -f location-node.yaml
kubectl get node anihpj-location-node -o jsonpath='{.status.capacity}'
node/anihpj-location-node created {"cpu":"8","memory":"64Gi","nvidia.com/gpu":"1","pods":"110"}
Example 5: Create Node for JobPost Scheduler Service

This example shows creating a Node for JobPost scheduler with multiple zones.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-node
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    zone: us-east-1a
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.6.0/24
  providerID: aws:///us-east-1a/i-xyz123456789
  unschedulable: false
kubectl create -f jobpost-node.yaml
kubectl get node anihpj-jobpost-node --show-labels
node/anihpj-jobpost-node created NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION LABELS anihpj-jobpost-node Ready worker 2s v1.28.0 app=anihpj,model=jobpost,zone=us-east-1a

NodeSpec

Official Docs: NodeSpec v1 API | API Version: v1

NodeSpec describes the attributes that a node is created with for your anihpj application infrastructure.

Full Description

NodeSpec describes the attributes that a node is created with. It defines the desired configuration for the node including pod CIDR ranges, provider identification, taints for pod scheduling control, and other node-specific settings. For your anihpj Django application, NodeSpec allows you to configure nodes to handle specific workloads like Skills validation, User authentication, or JobPost scheduling with appropriate resource allocations and scheduling constraints.

Parameters Description

  • configSource: NodeConfigSource - Deprecated. Previously used to specify the source of the node's configuration for the DynamicKubeletConfig feature. This feature is removed.
  • externalID: string - Deprecated. Not all kubelets will set this field. Remove field after 1.13
  • podCIDR: string - PodCIDR represents the pod IP range assigned to the node
  • podCIDRs: []string - Represents the IP ranges assigned to the node for usage by Pods. May contain at most 1 value for each of IPv4 and IPv6
  • providerID: string - ID of the node assigned by the cloud provider in the format: <ProviderName>://<ProviderSpecificNodeID>
  • taints: []Taint - If specified, the node's taints that affect pod scheduling
  • unschedulable: boolean - Controls node schedulability of new pods. By default, node is schedulable

Return Value

  • 200 (NodeSpec): OK - NodeSpec successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View Skills Node Spec Configuration

This example demonstrates viewing the NodeSpec configuration for Skills worker node.

# Get Skills node spec
kubectl get node anihpj-skills-node -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq
{ "podCIDR": "10.244.2.0/24", "podCIDRs": ["10.244.2.0/24"], "taints": [ { "effect": "NoSchedule", "key": "dedicated", "value": "skills-service" } ], "unschedulable": false }
Example 2: Check User Node Provider ID

This example shows checking User node's cloud provider identification.

# Get User node provider ID
kubectl get node anihpj-user-node -o jsonpath='{.spec.providerID}'
aws:///us-east-1b/i-0abc123def456
Example 3: View Author Node Pod CIDR

This example illustrates viewing Author node's pod IP range configuration.

# Get Author node pod CIDR
kubectl get node anihpj-author-node -o jsonpath='{.spec.podCIDR}'
10.244.4.0/24
Example 4: Check Location Node Taints

This example demonstrates viewing Location node's taint configuration.

# Get Location node taints
kubectl get node anihpj-location-node -o jsonpath='{.spec.taints}' | jq
[ { "effect": "PreferNoSchedule", "key": "storage-intensive", "value": "true" } ]
Example 5: Verify JobPost Node Schedulability

This example shows checking JobPost node's schedulability status.

# Check JobPost node schedulability
kubectl get node anihpj-jobpost-node -o jsonpath='{.spec.unschedulable}'
false

NodeStatus

Official Docs: NodeStatus v1 API | API Version: v1

NodeStatus provides real-time information about node health and resource availability for your anihpj services.

Full Description

NodeStatus is information about the current status of a node. It provides real-time information about the node's health, capacity, available resources, running container images, and system information. For your anihpj Django application, monitoring NodeStatus helps you understand resource availability for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services, detect node issues, and make informed decisions about pod placement and scaling.

Parameters Description

  • addresses: []NodeAddress - List of addresses reachable to the node. Queried from cloud provider, if available
  • allocatable: map[string]Quantity - Resources of a node that are available for scheduling. Defaults to Capacity
  • capacity: map[string]Quantity - Total resources of a node
  • conditions: []NodeCondition - Array of current observed node conditions
  • daemonEndpoints: NodeDaemonEndpoints - Endpoints of daemons running on the Node
  • features: NodeFeatures - Set of features implemented by the CRI implementation
  • images: []ContainerImage - List of container images on this node
  • nodeInfo: NodeSystemInfo - Set of ids/uuids to uniquely identify the node
  • phase: string - NodePhase is the recently observed lifecycle phase of the node (deprecated)
  • runtimeHandlers: []NodeRuntimeHandler - The available runtime handlers
  • volumesAttached: []AttachedVolume - List of volumes that are attached to the node
  • volumesInUse: []string - List of attachable volumes in use (mounted) by the node

Return Value

  • 200 (NodeStatus): OK - NodeStatus successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check Skills Node Capacity and Allocatable

This example demonstrates viewing Skills node's capacity and allocatable resources.

# Get Skills node capacity and allocatable
kubectl get node anihpj-skills-node -o jsonpath='{.status.capacity}' | jq
kubectl get node anihpj-skills-node -o jsonpath='{.status.allocatable}' | jq
{"cpu":"4","memory":"16Gi","pods":"110"} {"cpu":"4","memory":"15Gi","pods":"110"}
Example 2: View User Node Conditions

This example shows checking User node's health conditions.

# Get User node conditions
kubectl get node anihpj-user-node -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")]}' | jq
{ "type": "Ready", "status": "True", "lastHeartbeatTime": "2026-01-08T10:40:00Z", "lastTransitionTime": "2026-01-07T08:00:00Z", "reason": "KubeletReady", "message": "kubelet is posting ready status" }
Example 3: Check Author Node System Info

This example illustrates viewing Author node's system information.

# Get Author node system info
kubectl get node anihpj-author-node -o jsonpath='{.status.nodeInfo}' | jq
{ "architecture": "amd64", "bootID": "abc-123-def", "containerRuntimeVersion": "containerd://1.6.8", "kernelVersion": "5.15.0-1023-aws", "kubeletVersion": "v1.28.0", "operatingSystem": "linux", "osImage": "Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS" }
Example 4: List Location Node Addresses

This example demonstrates viewing Location node's network addresses.

# Get Location node addresses
kubectl get node anihpj-location-node -o jsonpath='{.status.addresses}' | jq
[ {"type":"InternalIP","address":"10.0.1.50"}, {"type":"ExternalIP","address":"54.123.78.90"}, {"type":"Hostname","address":"anihpj-location-node"} ]
Example 5: View JobPost Node Images

This example shows listing container images cached on JobPost node.

# Get JobPost node cached images
kubectl get node anihpj-jobpost-node -o jsonpath='{.status.images[*].names[0]}' | tr ' ' '\n' | head -5
registry.k8s.io/kube-proxy:v1.28.0 registry.k8s.io/pause:3.9 anihpj/jobpost-scheduler:latest anihpj/nginx:stable postgres:15-alpine

NodeList

Official Docs: NodeList v1 API | API Version: v1

NodeList is a collection of Node objects for managing multiple nodes in your anihpj cluster.

Full Description

NodeList is the whole list of all Nodes which have been registered with the Kubernetes cluster. This resource provides a convenient way to query multiple nodes at once, apply filters, and retrieve information about your entire cluster infrastructure. For your anihpj Django application, NodeList operations help you monitor all worker nodes running Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services, check cluster capacity, and manage node labels and taints across your infrastructure.

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - APIVersion defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object. Servers should convert recognized schemas to the latest internal value, and may reject unrecognized values
  • kind: string - Kind is a string value representing the REST resource this object represents. Servers may infer this from the endpoint the client submits requests to. Cannot be updated. In CamelCase
  • metadata: ListMeta - Standard list metadata. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#types-kinds
  • items: []Node - List of nodes. More info: https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/architecture/nodes/

Return Value

  • 200 (NodeList): OK - NodeList successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django List Examples

Example 1: List Skills Nodes by Label

This example demonstrates listing nodes with Skills service label.

# List Skills nodes
kubectl get nodes -l service=skills -o wide
NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION INTERNAL-IP EXTERNAL-IP OS-IMAGE KERNEL-VERSION CONTAINER-RUNTIME anihpj-skills-node Ready 30d v1.28.0 10.0.1.10 52.123.45.67 Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS 5.15.0-1023-aws containerd://1.6.8 anihpj-skills-node-2 Ready 25d v1.28.0 10.0.1.11 52.123.45.68 Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS 5.15.0-1023-aws containerd://1.6.8
Example 2: List User Nodes in JSON Format

This example shows retrieving User nodes in JSON for API integration.

# Get User nodes as JSON
kubectl get nodes -l service=user -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, status: .status.conditions[] | select(.type=="Ready") | .status}'
{ "name": "anihpj-user-node", "status": "True" } { "name": "anihpj-user-node-2", "status": "True" }
Example 3: List Author Nodes with Custom Columns

This example illustrates custom column display for Author nodes.

# List Author nodes with custom columns
kubectl get nodes -l service=author -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,CPU:.status.capacity.cpu,MEMORY:.status.capacity.memory,PODS:.status.capacity.pods
NAME CPU MEMORY PODS anihpj-author-node 4 16Gi 110 anihpj-author-node-2 8 32Gi 110
Example 4: List Location Nodes Sorted by Creation Time

This example demonstrates sorting Location nodes by age.

# List Location nodes sorted by creation time
kubectl get nodes -l service=location --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION anihpj-location-node Ready 35d v1.28.0 anihpj-location-node-2 Ready 30d v1.28.0 anihpj-location-node-3 Ready 20d v1.28.0
Example 5: List All JobPost Nodes with Ready Status

This example shows filtering JobPost nodes by Ready condition.

# List all JobPost nodes that are ready
kubectl get nodes -l service=jobpost --field-selector status.phase=Running -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@.status.conditions[0].type=="Ready")]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.conditions[0].status}{"\n"}{end}'
anihpj-jobpost-node True anihpj-jobpost-node-2 True anihpj-jobpost-node-3 True

Node Operations

Operations Overview

get - Read the specified Node
get (status) - Read status of the specified Node
list - List all Nodes
create - Create a Node
update - Replace the specified Node
update (status) - Replace status of the specified Node
patch - Partially update the specified Node
patch (status) - Partially update status of the specified Node
delete - Delete a Node
deletecollection - Delete collection of Nodes

Operations allow you to interact with Node resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage the worker nodes hosting your anihpj Django application, monitoring node health, capacity, and resource availability for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

get - read the specified Node

Read details of a specific Node by name to retrieve its complete configuration, capacity, allocatable resources, and current status. This operation returns the full Node object including metadata, spec, and status, enabling you to monitor the node hosting your anihpj application workloads. Use this operation to verify node health, check resource availability, review node labels and taints, and troubleshoot scheduling or capacity issues for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/nodes/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Node
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Node): OK - Node successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Node Details

This example retrieves details of the node running Skills validation pods.

# Get Skills node
kubectl get node anihpj-skills-node -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: Node metadata: name: anihpj-skills-node labels: app: anihpj model: skills status: capacity: cpu: "4" memory: 16Gi conditions: - status: "True" type: Ready
Example 2: Get User Node Resource Capacity

This example shows getting User node capacity and allocatable resources.

# Get User node resources
kubectl describe node anihpj-user-node | grep -A10 "Capacity\|Allocatable"
Capacity: cpu: 8 memory: 32Gi pods: 110 Allocatable: cpu: 8 memory: 31Gi pods: 110
Example 3: Get Author Node Status JSON

This example illustrates retrieving Author node in JSON format.

# Get Author node as JSON
kubectl get node anihpj-author-node -o json | jq .status.conditions
[ { "type": "Ready", "status": "True", "lastHeartbeatTime": "2026-01-08T10:30:00Z", "reason": "KubeletReady", "message": "kubelet is posting ready status" } ]
Example 4: Get Location Node Addresses

This example demonstrates retrieving Location node IP addresses.

# Get Location node addresses
kubectl get node anihpj-location-node -o jsonpath='{.status.addresses[*]}'
{"address":"10.0.1.50","type":"InternalIP"} {"address":"54.123.78.90","type":"ExternalIP"} {"address":"anihpj-location-node","type":"Hostname"}
Example 5: Get JobPost Node System Info

This example shows retrieving JobPost node system information.

# Get JobPost node system info
kubectl get node anihpj-jobpost-node -o jsonpath='{.status.nodeInfo}' | jq
{ "architecture": "amd64", "kubeletVersion": "v1.28.0", "operatingSystem": "linux", "osImage": "Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS", "containerRuntimeVersion": "containerd://1.6.8" }

get - read status of the specified Node

Read the status of a specific Node to retrieve only the current state information without the full specification. This operation returns the Node's status subresource including conditions, capacity, allocatable resources, addresses, system info, and running images. Use this operation when you only need runtime status information to monitor node health and resource availability for your anihpj application without retrieving the complete node configuration.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/nodes/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Node
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Node): OK - Node status successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Node Status

This example retrieves status of Skills node to monitor readiness.

# Get Skills node status
kubectl get node anihpj-skills-node -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")]}'
{"type":"Ready","status":"True","lastHeartbeatTime":"2026-01-08T10:35:00Z","lastTransitionTime":"2026-01-07T08:00:00Z","reason":"KubeletReady","message":"kubelet is posting ready status"}
Example 2: Get User Node Allocatable Resources

This example shows checking User node available resources.

# Get User node allocatable
kubectl get node anihpj-user-node -o jsonpath='{.status.allocatable}' | jq
{ "cpu": "8", "memory": "31Gi", "pods": "110", "ephemeral-storage": "100Gi" }
Example 3: Get Author Node Container Images

This example illustrates retrieving images cached on Author node.

# Get Author node images
kubectl get node anihpj-author-node -o jsonpath='{.status.images[*].names[0]}' | tr ' ' '\n' | head -5
registry.k8s.io/kube-proxy:v1.28.0 registry.k8s.io/pause:3.9 anihpj/author-cms:latest anihpj/nginx:stable postgres:15-alpine
Example 4: Get Location Node Conditions

This example demonstrates checking Location node health conditions.

# Get Location node conditions
kubectl describe node anihpj-location-node | grep -A15 "Conditions:"
Conditions: Type Status LastHeartbeatTime Reason ---- ------ ----------------- ------ MemoryPressure False Wed, 08 Jan 2026 10:35:00 +0000 KubeletHasSufficientMemory DiskPressure False Wed, 08 Jan 2026 10:35:00 +0000 KubeletHasNoDiskPressure PIDPressure False Wed, 08 Jan 2026 10:35:00 +0000 KubeletHasSufficientPID Ready True Wed, 08 Jan 2026 10:35:00 +0000 KubeletReady
Example 5: Get JobPost Node Volumes

This example shows retrieving volumes attached to JobPost node.

# Get JobPost node volumes
kubectl get node anihpj-jobpost-node -o jsonpath='{.status.volumesAttached}' | jq
[ { "name": "kubernetes.io/csi/ebs.csi.aws.com^vol-0abc123", "devicePath": "/dev/xvda" } ]

list - list or watch objects of kind Node

List all Nodes in the cluster to get an overview of your infrastructure. This operation returns a NodeList containing all registered worker nodes, enabling you to monitor the complete infrastructure hosting your anihpj Django application. Use this operation to view cluster capacity, identify nodes by labels, filter nodes by field selectors, or watch for node changes in real-time. Essential for capacity planning, infrastructure monitoring, and ensuring adequate resources for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

HTTP Request
GET /api/v1/nodes
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query, boolean): allowWatchBookmarks requests watch events with type "BOOKMARK"
  • continue (in query, string): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query, string): Field selector to filter nodes
  • labelSelector (in query, string): Label selector to filter nodes
  • limit (in query, integer): Maximum number of results to return
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query, string): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query, string): How to match resource version
  • sendInitialEvents (in query, boolean): Send initial events in watch mode
  • timeoutSeconds (in query, integer): Timeout for the list/watch call
  • watch (in query, boolean): Watch for changes to nodes
Return Value
  • 200 (NodeList): OK - NodeList successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Nodes

This example lists all nodes in the cluster.

# List all nodes
kubectl get nodes
NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION anihpj-skills-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 anihpj-user-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 anihpj-author-node Ready worker 4d v1.28.0 anihpj-location-node Ready worker 3d v1.28.0 anihpj-jobpost-node Ready worker 2d v1.28.0
Example 2: List Nodes with Wide Output

This example shows listing nodes with additional details.

# List nodes with wide output
kubectl get nodes -o wide
NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION INTERNAL-IP EXTERNAL-IP OS-IMAGE KERNEL-VERSION anihpj-skills-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 10.0.1.10 54.123.45.10 Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS 5.15.0-1023-aws anihpj-user-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 10.0.1.20 54.123.45.20 Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS 5.15.0-1023-aws anihpj-author-node Ready worker 4d v1.28.0 10.0.1.30 54.123.45.30 Ubuntu 22.04.1 LTS 5.15.0-1023-aws
Example 3: List Nodes by Label

This example illustrates filtering nodes by app label.

# List anihpj nodes
kubectl get nodes -l app=anihpj
NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION anihpj-skills-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 anihpj-user-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 anihpj-author-node Ready worker 4d v1.28.0 anihpj-location-node Ready worker 3d v1.28.0 anihpj-jobpost-node Ready worker 2d v1.28.0
Example 4: List Nodes with Custom Columns

This example demonstrates listing nodes with custom output.

# List nodes with custom columns
kubectl get nodes -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,CPU:.status.capacity.cpu,MEMORY:.status.capacity.memory
NAME CPU MEMORY anihpj-skills-node 4 16Gi anihpj-user-node 8 32Gi anihpj-author-node 4 16Gi anihpj-location-node 8 64Gi anihpj-jobpost-node 4 16Gi
Example 5: Watch Node Changes

This example shows watching for node status changes in real-time.

# Watch node changes
kubectl get nodes --watch
NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION anihpj-skills-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 anihpj-user-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 anihpj-skills-node Ready worker 5d v1.28.0 anihpj-location-node NotReady worker 3d v1.28.0 anihpj-location-node Ready worker 3d v1.28.0

create - create a Node

Create a new Node in the cluster by registering it with the Kubernetes API server. This operation allows you to manually register nodes or create custom node configurations for your anihpj Django application infrastructure. Typically, nodes are automatically registered by the kubelet, but manual node creation is useful for testing, cloud provider integrations, or custom node provisioning workflows. Once created, the node becomes available for scheduling pods running your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

HTTP Request
POST /api/v1/nodes
Parameters
  • body (Node, required): Node object to create
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode for validation without persisting
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name for tracking field ownership
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level (Strict, Warn, Ignore)
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Node): OK - Node object returned
  • 201 (Node): Created - Node successfully created
  • 202 (Node): Accepted - Node creation accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills Worker Node

This example creates a dedicated worker node for Skills validation workloads.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-worker-01
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    nodeType: worker
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.10.0/24
kubectl create -f skills-worker-node.yaml
node/anihpj-skills-worker-01 created
Example 2: Create User Node with High Memory

This example shows creating a node for User authentication with high memory capacity.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-highmem-01
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    memory: high
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.11.0/24
  providerID: aws:///us-east-1a/i-0abc123
kubectl create -f user-highmem-node.yaml
node/anihpj-user-highmem-01 created
Example 3: Create Author Node with Taints

This example illustrates creating an Author node with dedicated taints.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-dedicated-01
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.12.0/24
  taints:
  - effect: NoSchedule
    key: dedicated
    value: author-cms
kubectl create -f author-dedicated-node.yaml
node/anihpj-author-dedicated-01 created
Example 4: Create Location Node with GPU

This example demonstrates creating a Location node with GPU support.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-gpu-01
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    gpu: nvidia-t4
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.13.0/24
kubectl create -f location-gpu-node.yaml
kubectl label node anihpj-location-gpu-01 accelerator=nvidia-t4
node/anihpj-location-gpu-01 created node/anihpj-location-gpu-01 labeled
Example 5: Create JobPost Node with Dry Run

This example shows validating JobPost node creation without persisting.

apiVersion: v1
kind: Node
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-worker-01
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
spec:
  podCIDR: 10.244.14.0/24
  unschedulable: false
kubectl create -f jobpost-worker-node.yaml --dry-run=client -o yaml
apiVersion: v1 kind: Node metadata: name: anihpj-jobpost-worker-01 labels: app: anihpj model: jobpost spec: podCIDR: 10.244.14.0/24 unschedulable: false

update - replace the specified Node

Replace the entire specification of an existing Node with a new configuration. This operation performs a full update of the Node resource, replacing all fields in the spec. Use this operation to modify node configuration such as pod CIDR ranges, taints, or schedulability. Note that most node fields are managed by the kubelet and should not be manually updated unless you understand the implications. Prefer using patch operations for selective updates to avoid overwriting kubelet-managed fields.

HTTP Request
PUT /api/v1/nodes/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Node
  • body (Node, required): Updated Node object
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode for validation
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Node): OK - Node successfully updated
  • 201 (Node): Created - Node created (if didn't exist)
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Node CIDR

This example updates the Skills node pod CIDR range.

# Get, modify, and update Skills node
kubectl get node anihpj-skills-node -o yaml > skills-node.yaml
# Edit skills-node.yaml to change spec.podCIDR
kubectl replace -f skills-node.yaml
node/anihpj-skills-node replaced
Example 2: Update User Node Schedulability

This example shows marking User node as unschedulable.

# Update User node spec
kubectl get node anihpj-user-node -o yaml | \
  sed 's/unschedulable: false/unschedulable: true/' | \
  kubectl replace -f -
node/anihpj-user-node replaced
Example 3: Update Author Node Taints

This example illustrates updating Author node taints configuration.

# Get current node config
kubectl get node anihpj-author-node -o yaml > author-node.yaml
# Edit to modify spec.taints
kubectl replace -f author-node.yaml
node/anihpj-author-node replaced
Example 4: Update Location Node Provider ID

This example demonstrates updating Location node provider information.

# Update Location node
kubectl get node anihpj-location-node -o yaml | \
  sed 's|providerID: .*|providerID: aws:///us-west-2a/i-new123|' | \
  kubectl replace -f -
node/anihpj-location-node replaced
Example 5: Update JobPost Node Labels via Replace

This example shows updating JobPost node configuration with new labels.

# Get and modify JobPost node
kubectl get node anihpj-jobpost-node -o yaml > jobpost-node.yaml
# Edit metadata.labels in jobpost-node.yaml
kubectl replace -f jobpost-node.yaml
node/anihpj-jobpost-node replaced

update - replace status of the specified Node

Replace the status subresource of an existing Node. This operation updates only the status field of the Node resource, which contains runtime information like conditions, capacity, allocatable resources, addresses, and system info. Status updates are typically managed by the kubelet and node controllers, not by end users. This operation is useful for custom controllers or automation tools that need to update node status information for your anihpj infrastructure monitoring.

HTTP Request
PUT /api/v1/nodes/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Node
  • body (Node, required): Node object with updated status
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Node): OK - Node status successfully updated
  • 201 (Node): Created - Node status created
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills Node Conditions

This example updates Skills node status conditions.

# Update Skills node status (typically done by kubelet)
kubectl get node anihpj-skills-node -o json | \
  jq '.status.conditions[0].status = "False"' | \
  kubectl replace -f - --subresource=status
node/anihpj-skills-node status replaced
Example 2: Update User Node Capacity

This example shows updating User node capacity information.

# Update User node capacity status
kubectl get node anihpj-user-node -o yaml > user-status.yaml
# Modify status.capacity in user-status.yaml
kubectl replace -f user-status.yaml --subresource=status
node/anihpj-user-node status replaced
Example 3: Update Author Node Addresses

This example illustrates updating Author node address information.

# Update Author node addresses
kubectl get node anihpj-author-node -o json | \
  jq '.status.addresses += [{"type":"ExternalDNS","address":"author.anihpj.com"}]' | \
  kubectl replace -f - --subresource=status
node/anihpj-author-node status replaced
Example 4: Update Location Node Images

This example demonstrates updating Location node cached images.

# Update Location node images list (managed by kubelet)
kubectl get node anihpj-location-node -o yaml > location-status.yaml
# Modify status.images
kubectl replace -f location-status.yaml --subresource=status
node/anihpj-location-node status replaced
Example 5: Update JobPost Node System Info

This example shows updating JobPost node system information.

# Update JobPost node system info
kubectl get node anihpj-jobpost-node -o json | \
  jq '.status.nodeInfo.kubeletVersion = "v1.28.1"' | \
  kubectl replace -f - --subresource=status
node/anihpj-jobpost-node status replaced

patch - partially update the specified Node

Partially update a Node by applying a patch to specific fields without replacing the entire resource. This operation allows you to make targeted modifications to node configuration such as adding labels, updating taints, or changing schedulability. Patch operations are preferred over full updates because they avoid overwriting kubelet-managed fields and allow multiple actors to modify different fields independently. Use this operation to make incremental updates to nodes hosting your anihpj Django application workloads.

HTTP Request
PATCH /api/v1/nodes/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Node
  • body (Patch, required): Patch object containing changes
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • force (in query, boolean): Force apply the patch
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Node): OK - Node successfully patched
  • 201 (Node): Created - Node created via patch
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Node Add Label

This example adds a label to Skills node using patch.

# Add label to Skills node
kubectl patch node anihpj-skills-node -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"tier":"production"}}}'
node/anihpj-skills-node patched
Example 2: Patch User Node Mark Unschedulable

This example shows cordoning User node via patch.

# Mark User node unschedulable
kubectl patch node anihpj-user-node -p '{"spec":{"unschedulable":true}}'
# Or use kubectl cordon
kubectl cordon anihpj-user-node
node/anihpj-user-node patched node/anihpj-user-node cordoned
Example 3: Patch Author Node Add Taint

This example illustrates adding a taint to Author node.

# Add taint to Author node
kubectl taint node anihpj-author-node dedicated=author-cms:NoSchedule
# Or via patch
kubectl patch node anihpj-author-node --type=json -p='[{"op":"add","path":"/spec/taints/-","value":{"key":"dedicated","value":"author-cms","effect":"NoSchedule"}}]'
node/anihpj-author-node tainted node/anihpj-author-node patched
Example 4: Patch Location Node Update Annotation

This example demonstrates adding annotation to Location node.

# Add annotation to Location node
kubectl annotate node anihpj-location-node monitoring=enabled
# Or via patch
kubectl patch node anihpj-location-node -p '{"metadata":{"annotations":{"monitoring":"enabled"}}}'
node/anihpj-location-node annotated node/anihpj-location-node patched
Example 5: Patch JobPost Node Make Schedulable

This example shows uncordoning JobPost node via patch.

# Make JobPost node schedulable
kubectl patch node anihpj-jobpost-node -p '{"spec":{"unschedulable":false}}'
# Or use kubectl uncordon
kubectl uncordon anihpj-jobpost-node
node/anihpj-jobpost-node patched node/anihpj-jobpost-node uncordoned

patch - partially update status of the specified Node

Partially update the status subresource of a Node by applying a patch to specific status fields. This operation allows you to make targeted modifications to node status information like conditions, capacity, or addresses without affecting other status fields or the node spec. Status patches are typically performed by the kubelet, node controllers, or custom operators managing your anihpj infrastructure. Use this operation for incremental status updates to monitor and maintain node health for your Django application workloads.

HTTP Request
PATCH /api/v1/nodes/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Node
  • body (Patch, required): Patch object for status changes
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • force (in query, boolean): Force apply the patch
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (Node): OK - Node status successfully patched
  • 201 (Node): Created - Node status created via patch
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Node Condition

This example patches Skills node Ready condition.

# Patch Skills node condition (typically kubelet-managed)
kubectl patch node anihpj-skills-node --subresource=status --type=json -p='[{"op":"replace","path":"/status/conditions/0/status","value":"True"}]'
node/anihpj-skills-node patched (status)
Example 2: Patch User Node Allocatable

This example shows patching User node allocatable resources.

# Patch User node allocatable
kubectl patch node anihpj-user-node --subresource=status -p '{"status":{"allocatable":{"memory":"30Gi"}}}'
node/anihpj-user-node patched (status)
Example 3: Patch Author Node Add Address

This example illustrates patching Author node addresses.

# Add address to Author node status
kubectl patch node anihpj-author-node --subresource=status --type=json -p='[{"op":"add","path":"/status/addresses/-","value":{"type":"InternalDNS","address":"author-node.internal"}}]'
node/anihpj-author-node patched (status)
Example 4: Patch Location Node Capacity

This example demonstrates patching Location node capacity.

# Patch Location node GPU capacity
kubectl patch node anihpj-location-node --subresource=status -p '{"status":{"capacity":{"nvidia.com/gpu":"2"}}}'
node/anihpj-location-node patched (status)
Example 5: Patch JobPost Node Info

This example shows patching JobPost node system information.

# Patch JobPost node info
kubectl patch node anihpj-jobpost-node --subresource=status --type=json -p='[{"op":"replace","path":"/status/nodeInfo/kubeletVersion","value":"v1.28.2"}]'
node/anihpj-jobpost-node patched (status)

delete - delete a Node

Delete a Node from the cluster, removing it from the Kubernetes API and making it unavailable for scheduling new pods. This operation removes the node registration but does not affect the physical or virtual machine itself. Pods running on the deleted node will be rescheduled to other available nodes if they are managed by controllers (Deployments, ReplicaSets, etc.). Use this operation when decommissioning nodes, scaling down your infrastructure, or removing failed nodes hosting your anihpj Django application services.

HTTP Request
DELETE /api/v1/nodes/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the Node
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options including grace period
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query, integer): Grace period for deletion in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query, string): Propagation policy (Foreground, Background, Orphan)
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - Node successfully deleted
  • 202 (Status): Accepted - Node deletion accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Old Skills Node

This example deletes an old Skills node being decommissioned.

# Delete old Skills node
kubectl delete node anihpj-skills-old-01
node "anihpj-skills-old-01" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Node with Drain

This example shows safely deleting User node by draining first.

# Drain and delete User node
kubectl drain anihpj-user-node --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
kubectl delete node anihpj-user-node
node/anihpj-user-node cordoned node/anihpj-user-node drained node "anihpj-user-node" deleted
Example 3: Delete Failed Author Node

This example illustrates deleting a failed Author node immediately.

# Delete failed Author node
kubectl delete node anihpj-author-failed-01 --grace-period=0 --force
warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. The resource may continue to run on the cluster indefinitely. node "anihpj-author-failed-01" force deleted
Example 4: Delete Location Node from YAML

This example demonstrates deleting Location node using YAML file.

# Delete Location node from file
kubectl delete -f location-node-old.yaml
node "anihpj-location-old" deleted
Example 5: Dry Run Delete JobPost Node

This example shows validating JobPost node deletion without removing it.

# Dry run delete JobPost node
kubectl delete node anihpj-jobpost-node --dry-run=client
node "anihpj-jobpost-node" deleted (dry run)

deletecollection - delete collection of Node

Delete multiple Nodes at once based on label selectors or field selectors. This bulk operation allows you to efficiently decommission or remove multiple nodes from your cluster simultaneously, useful when scaling down infrastructure, retiring old hardware, or removing nodes from specific availability zones. Exercise caution with this operation as it can significantly impact your anihpj Django application's capacity and availability. Always ensure adequate capacity remains for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services before deleting node collections.

HTTP Request
DELETE /api/v1/nodes
Parameters
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • continue (in query, string): Continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldSelector (in query, string): Field selector to filter nodes
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query, integer): Grace period in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • labelSelector (in query, string): Label selector to filter nodes
  • limit (in query, integer): Maximum number to delete
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query, string): Propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (in query, string): Resource version
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query, string): Resource version match strategy
  • sendInitialEvents (in query, boolean): Send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (in query, integer): Timeout in seconds
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - Nodes successfully deleted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Old Skills Nodes by Label

This example deletes all old Skills nodes marked for decommission.

# Delete old Skills nodes
kubectl delete nodes -l app=anihpj,model=skills,status=decommissioned
node "anihpj-skills-old-01" deleted node "anihpj-skills-old-02" deleted node "anihpj-skills-old-03" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Nodes in Specific Zone

This example shows deleting User nodes from a retiring availability zone.

# Delete User nodes in zone
kubectl delete nodes -l app=anihpj,model=user,zone=us-east-1c
node "anihpj-user-zone-c-01" deleted node "anihpj-user-zone-c-02" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author Dev Nodes

This example illustrates deleting all Author development nodes.

# Delete Author dev nodes
kubectl delete nodes -l app=anihpj,model=author,environment=development
node "anihpj-author-dev-01" deleted node "anihpj-author-dev-02" deleted
Example 4: Delete Location Test Nodes

This example demonstrates deleting Location testing nodes.

# Delete Location test nodes
kubectl delete nodes -l app=anihpj,model=location,tier=testing --dry-run=client
node "anihpj-location-test-01" deleted (dry run) node "anihpj-location-test-02" deleted (dry run)
Example 5: Delete JobPost Staging Nodes

This example shows deleting JobPost staging infrastructure nodes.

# Delete JobPost staging nodes
kubectl drain -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost,environment=staging --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data
kubectl delete nodes -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost,environment=staging
node/anihpj-jobpost-staging-01 drained node/anihpj-jobpost-staging-02 drained node "anihpj-jobpost-staging-01" deleted node "anihpj-jobpost-staging-02" deleted

RuntimeClass

RuntimeClass Overview

RuntimeClassList - Collection of RuntimeClasses
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: RuntimeClass v1 API | API Version: node.k8s.io/v1

RuntimeClass defines a class of container runtime supported in the cluster for running your anihpj Django pods.

Full Description

RuntimeClass defines a class of container runtime supported in the cluster. The RuntimeClass is used to determine which container runtime is used to run all containers in a pod. RuntimeClasses are manually defined by a user or cluster provisioner, and referenced in the PodSpec. The Kubelet is responsible for resolving the RuntimeClassName reference before running the pod.

For your anihpj Django application, RuntimeClass enables you to specify different container runtimes for different workloads. For example, you might use a security-focused runtime like gVisor or Kata Containers for sensitive Skills validation services, standard containerd for general User and Author services, and specialized runtimes optimized for Location processing or JobPost scheduling tasks. This flexibility allows you to balance security, performance, and resource utilization based on each service's specific requirements.

Syntax

# Create a RuntimeClass from YAML file
kubectl create -f runtimeclass.yaml
# Get all RuntimeClasses
kubectl get runtimeclasses
# Get all RuntimeClasses (short form)
kubectl get rc
# Get detailed information about a specific RuntimeClass
kubectl describe runtimeclass {name}
# Get detailed information about a specific RuntimeClass (short form)
kubectl describe rc {name}
# Get RuntimeClass in YAML format
kubectl get runtimeclass {name} -o yaml
# Delete a specific RuntimeClass
kubectl delete runtimeclass {name}
# Delete a specific RuntimeClass (short form)
kubectl delete rc {name}

YAML Structure

apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-secure
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    security: high
handler: gvisor
overhead:
  podFixed:
    cpu: "100m"
    memory: "128Mi"
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    security-level: high
    runtime: gvisor
  tolerations:
  - key: "runtime"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "gvisor"
    effect: "NoSchedule"

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "node.k8s.io/v1" for RuntimeClass
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "RuntimeClass"
  • metadata: ObjectMeta - Standard object metadata including name, labels, and annotations
  • handler: string (required) - Specifies the underlying runtime and configuration that the CRI implementation will use to handle pods of this class. Must be lowercase, conform to DNS Label (RFC 1123) requirements, and is immutable
  • overhead: Overhead - Represents the resource overhead associated with running a pod for a given RuntimeClass
  • scheduling: Scheduling - Holds the scheduling constraints to ensure that pods running with this RuntimeClass are scheduled to nodes that support it

Return Value

  • 200 (RuntimeClass): OK - Request successful, returns RuntimeClass object
  • 201 (RuntimeClass): Created - RuntimeClass successfully created
  • 202 (RuntimeClass): Accepted - Request accepted for processing

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create Secure RuntimeClass for Skills Service

This example creates a RuntimeClass using gVisor for high-security Skills validation.

# Create secure runtime YAML
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-secure
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    security: high
handler: gvisor
overhead:
  podFixed:
    cpu: "100m"
    memory: "128Mi"
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    security-level: high
    runtime: gvisor
  tolerations:
  - key: "runtime"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "gvisor"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF

# Verify creation
kubectl get runtimeclass anihpj-skills-secure
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-secure created NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-skills-secure gvisor 5s
Example 2: Create Standard RuntimeClass for User Service

This example demonstrates creating a standard containerd RuntimeClass for User management.

# Create standard runtime
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-standard
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    performance: standard
handler: runc
EOF

# Get details
kubectl describe rc anihpj-user-standard
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-user-standard created Name: anihpj-user-standard Labels: app=anihpj model=user performance=standard Handler: runc
Example 3: Create Kata Containers RuntimeClass for Author Service

This example illustrates using Kata Containers for isolated Author content processing.

# Create Kata runtime
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-isolated
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    isolation: vm-based
handler: kata
overhead:
  podFixed:
    cpu: "250m"
    memory: "256Mi"
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    runtime: kata
EOF
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-author-isolated created
Example 4: Create GPU-Optimized RuntimeClass for Location Service

This example shows creating a runtime for GPU-accelerated Location mapping.

# Create GPU runtime
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-gpu
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    hardware: gpu
handler: nvidia
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    hardware: gpu
    nvidia.com/gpu: "true"
  tolerations:
  - key: "nvidia.com/gpu"
    operator: "Exists"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF

# List all runtimes
kubectl get runtimeclasses
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-location-gpu created NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-skills-secure gvisor 5m anihpj-user-standard runc 4m anihpj-author-isolated kata 3m anihpj-location-gpu nvidia 2s
Example 5: Create Performance RuntimeClass for JobPost Scheduler

This example demonstrates a performance-optimized runtime for JobPost scheduling.

# Create performance runtime
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-perf
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    optimization: performance
handler: crun
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    performance: high
    cpu-type: intel
  tolerations:
  - key: "performance"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "high"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF

# View in YAML
kubectl get rc anihpj-jobpost-perf -o yaml
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-perf created apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1 handler: crun kind: RuntimeClass metadata: name: anihpj-jobpost-perf labels: app: anihpj model: jobpost optimization: performance scheduling: nodeSelector: cpu-type: intel performance: high tolerations: - effect: NoSchedule key: performance operator: Equal value: high

RuntimeClassList

Official Docs: RuntimeClassList v1 API | API Version: node.k8s.io/v1

RuntimeClassList is a collection of RuntimeClass objects for managing multiple runtime classes in your anihpj cluster.

Full Description

RuntimeClassList is a list of RuntimeClass objects. This resource provides a convenient way to query and manage multiple runtime classes at once, enabling you to view all available container runtimes configured in your cluster. For your anihpj Django application, RuntimeClassList operations help you inventory available runtime options across different security levels, performance profiles, and hardware configurations for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - APIVersion defines the versioned schema of this representation of an object. Always "node.k8s.io/v1" for RuntimeClassList
  • kind: string - Kind is a string value representing the REST resource this object represents. Always "RuntimeClassList"
  • metadata: ListMeta - Standard list metadata. More info: https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/devel/sig-architecture/api-conventions.md#metadata
  • items: []RuntimeClass - List of RuntimeClass objects in the cluster

Return Value

  • 200 (RuntimeClassList): OK - RuntimeClassList successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django List Examples

Example 1: List All RuntimeClasses for anihpj Services

This example demonstrates listing all RuntimeClasses with anihpj label.

# List all anihpj runtimes
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj -o wide
NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-skills-secure gvisor 30d anihpj-user-standard runc 25d anihpj-author-isolated kata 20d anihpj-location-gpu nvidia 15d anihpj-jobpost-perf crun 10d
Example 2: List Skills RuntimeClasses in JSON Format

This example shows retrieving Skills RuntimeClasses in JSON for API integration.

# Get Skills runtimes as JSON
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=skills -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, handler: .handler}'
{ "name": "anihpj-skills-secure", "handler": "gvisor" } { "name": "anihpj-skills-standard", "handler": "runc" }
Example 3: List User and Author RuntimeClasses with Custom Columns

This example illustrates custom column display for User and Author runtimes.

# List with custom columns
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l 'app=anihpj,model in (user,author)' -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,HANDLER:.handler,MODEL:.metadata.labels.model,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME HANDLER MODEL AGE anihpj-user-standard runc user 2026-01-05T10:00:00Z anihpj-author-isolated kata author 2026-01-03T14:30:00Z
Example 4: List Location RuntimeClasses Sorted by Handler

This example demonstrates sorting Location runtimes by handler type.

# List Location runtimes sorted by handler
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=location --sort-by=.handler
NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-location-gpu nvidia 15d anihpj-location-standard runc 20d
Example 5: List All JobPost RuntimeClasses with Overhead Details

This example shows viewing JobPost runtimes with resource overhead.

# List JobPost runtimes with overhead
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.handler}{"\t"}{.overhead.podFixed.cpu}{"\t"}{.overhead.podFixed.memory}{"\n"}{end}' | column -t
anihpj-jobpost-perf crun 50m 64Mi anihpj-jobpost-standard runc - -

RuntimeClass Operations

Operations Overview

get - Read the specified RuntimeClass
list - List all RuntimeClasses
create - Create a RuntimeClass
update - Replace the specified RuntimeClass
patch - Partially update the specified RuntimeClass
delete - Delete a RuntimeClass
deletecollection - Delete collection of RuntimeClasses

Operations allow you to interact with RuntimeClass resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage container runtime configurations for your anihpj Django application, defining different runtime classes for different security, performance, and isolation requirements across your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

get - read the specified RuntimeClass

Read details of a specific RuntimeClass by name to retrieve its complete configuration including handler, overhead, and scheduling constraints. This operation returns the full RuntimeClass object, enabling you to verify runtime configuration, check scheduling requirements, and understand resource overhead for pods using this runtime. Use this operation to audit runtime configurations, troubleshoot pod scheduling issues, and ensure the correct runtime is configured for your anihpj application workloads.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/runtimeclasses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the RuntimeClass
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (RuntimeClass): OK - RuntimeClass successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills Secure RuntimeClass

This example retrieves the secure runtime configuration for Skills service.

# Get Skills secure runtime
kubectl get runtimeclass anihpj-skills-secure -o yaml
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1 handler: gvisor kind: RuntimeClass metadata: name: anihpj-skills-secure labels: app: anihpj model: skills security: high overhead: podFixed: cpu: 100m memory: 128Mi scheduling: nodeSelector: runtime: gvisor security-level: high
Example 2: Get User Standard RuntimeClass Handler

This example shows checking the handler for User service runtime.

# Get User runtime handler
kubectl get runtimeclass anihpj-user-standard -o jsonpath='{.handler}'
runc
Example 3: Get Author Isolated RuntimeClass Overhead

This example illustrates viewing overhead for Author runtime.

# Get Author runtime overhead
kubectl get runtimeclass anihpj-author-isolated -o jsonpath='{.overhead.podFixed}' | jq
{ "cpu": "250m", "memory": "256Mi" }
Example 4: Get Location GPU RuntimeClass Scheduling

This example demonstrates viewing scheduling constraints for Location runtime.

# Get Location runtime scheduling
kubectl get runtimeclass anihpj-location-gpu -o jsonpath='{.scheduling}' | jq
{ "nodeSelector": { "hardware": "gpu", "nvidia.com/gpu": "true" }, "tolerations": [ { "effect": "NoSchedule", "key": "nvidia.com/gpu", "operator": "Exists" } ] }
Example 5: Describe JobPost Performance RuntimeClass

This example shows getting detailed information for JobPost runtime.

# Describe JobPost runtime
kubectl describe runtimeclass anihpj-jobpost-perf
Name: anihpj-jobpost-perf Labels: app=anihpj model=jobpost optimization=performance Handler: crun Scheduling: Node Selector: cpu-type: intel performance: high Tolerations: Key: performance Operator: Equal Value: high Effect: NoSchedule

list - list or watch objects of kind RuntimeClass

List all RuntimeClasses in the cluster, optionally filtered by labels or field selectors. This operation returns a RuntimeClassList containing all runtime configurations available in your cluster. Use this operation to inventory available runtimes, monitor runtime configurations across your infrastructure, and verify that required runtimes are properly configured for your anihpj Django application services. You can filter results to find runtimes by specific characteristics such as security level, performance profile, or hardware requirements.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/runtimeclasses
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query, boolean): Allow watch bookmarks
  • continue (in query, string): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query, string): Field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query, string): Label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query, integer): Maximum number of results to return
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query, string): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query, string): Resource version match strategy
  • sendInitialEvents (in query, boolean): Send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (in query, integer): Timeout for list operation
  • watch (in query, boolean): Watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (RuntimeClassList): OK - List successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Skills RuntimeClasses

This example lists all RuntimeClasses configured for Skills service.

# List Skills runtimes
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=skills
NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-skills-secure gvisor 30d anihpj-skills-standard runc 28d
Example 2: List User RuntimeClasses with Wide Output

This example shows listing User runtimes with additional details.

# List User runtimes wide
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=user -o wide
NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-user-standard runc 25d anihpj-user-debug runc 20d
Example 3: List Author RuntimeClasses in JSON

This example illustrates listing Author runtimes in JSON format.

# List Author runtimes as JSON
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=author -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, handler: .handler, created: .metadata.creationTimestamp}'
{ "name": "anihpj-author-isolated", "handler": "kata", "created": "2026-01-03T14:30:00Z" } { "name": "anihpj-author-standard", "handler": "runc", "created": "2026-01-05T09:15:00Z" }
Example 4: List Location RuntimeClasses with Label Filter

This example demonstrates filtering Location runtimes by hardware label.

# List Location GPU runtimes
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=location,hardware=gpu
NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-location-gpu nvidia 15d
Example 5: Watch JobPost RuntimeClasses for Changes

This example shows watching JobPost runtimes for configuration changes.

# Watch JobPost runtimes
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost --watch
NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-jobpost-perf crun 10d anihpj-jobpost-standard runc 12d anihpj-jobpost-perf crun 10d (MODIFIED)

create - create a RuntimeClass

Create a new RuntimeClass to define a container runtime configuration for your cluster. This operation allows you to configure new runtime handlers with specific overhead calculations and scheduling constraints. Use this operation to add support for new container runtimes, configure specialized runtime environments for specific workloads, or define custom runtime configurations optimized for your anihpj Django application's security, performance, or isolation requirements across Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

HTTP Request
POST /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/runtimeclasses
Parameters
  • body (RuntimeClass, required): RuntimeClass object to create
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (RuntimeClass): OK - RuntimeClass already exists
  • 201 (RuntimeClass): Created - RuntimeClass successfully created
  • 202 (RuntimeClass): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Firecracker RuntimeClass for Skills Microservices

This example creates a Firecracker-based runtime for Skills microVM isolation.

# Create Firecracker runtime
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-firecracker
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    isolation: microvm
handler: firecracker
overhead:
  podFixed:
    cpu: "125m"
    memory: "192Mi"
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    runtime: firecracker
  tolerations:
  - key: "runtime"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "firecracker"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-firecracker created
Example 2: Create WebAssembly RuntimeClass for User Functions

This example shows creating a WASM runtime for lightweight User functions.

# Create WASM runtime
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-wasm
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    type: wasm
handler: wasmtime
overhead:
  podFixed:
    cpu: "10m"
    memory: "32Mi"
EOF
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-user-wasm created
Example 3: Create Confidential Computing RuntimeClass for Author Data

This example illustrates creating a runtime with SGX for Author sensitive data.

# Create SGX runtime
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-sgx
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    security: confidential
handler: sgx
overhead:
  podFixed:
    cpu: "200m"
    memory: "384Mi"
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    sgx.intel.com/epc: "true"
  tolerations:
  - key: "sgx"
    operator: "Exists"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-author-sgx created
Example 4: Create FPGA RuntimeClass for Location Processing

This example demonstrates creating a runtime for FPGA-accelerated Location processing.

# Create FPGA runtime
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-fpga
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    hardware: fpga
handler: xilinx
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    fpga: xilinx
    hardware: fpga
  tolerations:
  - key: "fpga"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "xilinx"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF

# Verify creation
kubectl get rc anihpj-location-fpga
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-location-fpga created NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-location-fpga xilinx 2s
Example 5: Create ARM RuntimeClass for JobPost Edge Nodes

This example shows creating a runtime for ARM-based JobPost edge deployment.

# Create ARM runtime
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-arm
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    arch: arm64
handler: runc
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    kubernetes.io/arch: arm64
EOF

# List all runtimes
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-arm created NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-skills-secure gvisor 30d anihpj-skills-firecracker firecracker 2m anihpj-user-standard runc 25d anihpj-user-wasm wasmtime 1m anihpj-author-isolated kata 20d anihpj-author-sgx sgx 1m anihpj-location-gpu nvidia 15d anihpj-location-fpga xilinx 30s anihpj-jobpost-perf crun 10d anihpj-jobpost-arm runc 5s

update - replace the specified RuntimeClass

Replace an existing RuntimeClass with a new configuration. This operation performs a complete replacement of the RuntimeClass object, updating all fields to match the provided specification. Use this operation when you need to modify runtime configurations comprehensively, such as changing overhead calculations, updating scheduling constraints, or modifying handler settings. Note that the handler field is immutable and cannot be changed after creation. For your anihpj Django application, this operation helps maintain optimal runtime configurations as requirements evolve for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

HTTP Request
PUT /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/runtimeclasses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the RuntimeClass
  • body (RuntimeClass, required): Updated RuntimeClass object
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (RuntimeClass): OK - RuntimeClass successfully updated
  • 201 (RuntimeClass): Created - RuntimeClass created (if it didn't exist)
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills RuntimeClass Overhead

This example updates overhead values for Skills secure runtime.

# Get current config
kubectl get runtimeclass anihpj-skills-secure -o yaml > skills-runtime.yaml

# Edit overhead values
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-secure
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    security: high
handler: gvisor
overhead:
  podFixed:
    cpu: "150m"
    memory: "192Mi"
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    runtime: gvisor
    security-level: high
  tolerations:
  - key: "runtime"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "gvisor"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-secure configured
Example 2: Update User RuntimeClass Node Selector

This example shows adding node selectors to User runtime.

# Update User runtime with node selector
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-standard
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    performance: standard
handler: runc
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    node-type: general-purpose
    cpu-generation: modern
EOF
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-user-standard configured
Example 3: Update Author RuntimeClass Tolerations

This example illustrates adding tolerations to Author runtime.

# Update Author runtime tolerations
kubectl get runtimeclass anihpj-author-isolated -o yaml | \
  sed '/tolerations:/a\  - key: "workload"\n    operator: "Equal"\n    value: "author"\n    effect: "NoSchedule"' | \
  kubectl apply -f -
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-author-isolated configured
Example 4: Update Location RuntimeClass Labels

This example demonstrates updating labels for Location runtime.

# Update Location runtime labels
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-gpu
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    hardware: gpu
    gpu-type: nvidia-a100
    performance: high
handler: nvidia
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    hardware: gpu
    nvidia.com/gpu: "true"
    gpu-model: a100
  tolerations:
  - key: "nvidia.com/gpu"
    operator: "Exists"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-location-gpu configured
Example 5: Update JobPost RuntimeClass with Overhead

This example shows adding overhead to JobPost performance runtime.

# Update JobPost runtime
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: node.k8s.io/v1
kind: RuntimeClass
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-perf
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    optimization: performance
handler: crun
overhead:
  podFixed:
    cpu: "75m"
    memory: "96Mi"
scheduling:
  nodeSelector:
    performance: high
    cpu-type: intel
  tolerations:
  - key: "performance"
    operator: "Equal"
    value: "high"
    effect: "NoSchedule"
EOF

# Verify update
kubectl get rc anihpj-jobpost-perf -o jsonpath='{.overhead.podFixed}'
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-perf configured {"cpu":"75m","memory":"96Mi"}

patch - partially update the specified RuntimeClass

Partially update a RuntimeClass by applying changes to specific fields without replacing the entire object. This operation is more efficient than update when you only need to modify specific fields like labels, annotations, overhead, or scheduling constraints. Use strategic merge patch, JSON merge patch, or JSON patch formats to make targeted modifications. For your anihpj Django application, this operation allows fine-grained updates to runtime configurations while preserving unchanged settings for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

HTTP Request
PATCH /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/runtimeclasses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the RuntimeClass
  • body (Patch, required): Patch object
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldManager (in query, string): Field manager name
  • fieldValidation (in query, string): Field validation level
  • force (in query, boolean): Force update
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (RuntimeClass): OK - RuntimeClass successfully patched
  • 201 (RuntimeClass): Created - RuntimeClass created
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills RuntimeClass Labels

This example adds new labels to Skills runtime without modifying other fields.

# Patch Skills runtime labels
kubectl patch runtimeclass anihpj-skills-secure --type=merge -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"environment":"production","tier":"security"}}}'
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-secure patched
Example 2: Patch User RuntimeClass Overhead

This example shows updating only overhead values for User runtime.

# Patch User runtime overhead
kubectl patch runtimeclass anihpj-user-standard --type=merge -p '{
  "overhead": {
    "podFixed": {
      "cpu": "50m",
      "memory": "64Mi"
    }
  }
}'
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-user-standard patched
Example 3: Patch Author RuntimeClass Node Selector

This example illustrates adding node selector to Author runtime.

# Patch Author runtime node selector
kubectl patch runtimeclass anihpj-author-isolated --type=merge -p '{
  "scheduling": {
    "nodeSelector": {
      "runtime": "kata",
      "isolation": "vm",
      "security-zone": "dmz"
    }
  }
}'
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-author-isolated patched
Example 4: Patch Location RuntimeClass Annotation

This example demonstrates adding annotations to Location runtime.

# Patch Location runtime annotations
kubectl patch runtimeclass anihpj-location-gpu --type=merge -p '{
  "metadata": {
    "annotations": {
      "description": "NVIDIA GPU runtime for location mapping",
      "maintained-by": "platform-team",
      "last-updated": "2026-01-08"
    }
  }
}'

# Verify patch
kubectl get rc anihpj-location-gpu -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}'
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-location-gpu patched {"description":"NVIDIA GPU runtime for location mapping","last-updated":"2026-01-08","maintained-by":"platform-team"}
Example 5: Patch JobPost RuntimeClass Tolerations

This example shows adding tolerations to JobPost runtime using JSON patch.

# Patch JobPost runtime tolerations
kubectl patch runtimeclass anihpj-jobpost-perf --type=json -p '[
  {
    "op": "add",
    "path": "/scheduling/tolerations/-",
    "value": {
      "key": "workload-type",
      "operator": "Equal",
      "value": "batch",
      "effect": "NoExecute",
      "tolerationSeconds": 3600
    }
  }
]'

# Check tolerations
kubectl get rc anihpj-jobpost-perf -o jsonpath='{.scheduling.tolerations}' | jq
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-perf patched [ { "effect": "NoSchedule", "key": "performance", "operator": "Equal", "value": "high" }, { "effect": "NoExecute", "key": "workload-type", "operator": "Equal", "tolerationSeconds": 3600, "value": "batch" } ]

delete - delete a RuntimeClass

Delete a RuntimeClass from the cluster. This operation removes the runtime configuration, making it unavailable for new pod scheduling. Existing pods using the RuntimeClass will continue to run, but new pods cannot be created with the deleted RuntimeClass. Use this operation to clean up unused runtime configurations, remove deprecated runtimes, or decommission runtime classes that are no longer needed. Exercise caution when deleting RuntimeClasses that may still be referenced by pod templates or deployments in your anihpj Django application.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/runtimeclasses/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the RuntimeClass
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query, integer): Grace period in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query, string): Propagation policy
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - RuntimeClass successfully deleted
  • 202 (Status): Accepted - Deletion request accepted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Deprecated Skills RuntimeClass

This example deletes an old Skills runtime that's no longer needed.

# Check if runtime is in use
kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@.spec.runtimeClassName=="anihpj-skills-old")]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}'

# Delete deprecated runtime
kubectl delete runtimeclass anihpj-skills-old
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-old" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Test RuntimeClass

This example shows deleting a User testing runtime after tests complete.

# Delete test runtime
kubectl delete runtimeclass anihpj-user-test

# Verify deletion
kubectl get rc anihpj-user-test
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-user-test" deleted Error from server (NotFound): runtimeclasses.node.k8s.io "anihpj-user-test" not found
Example 3: Dry Run Delete for Author RuntimeClass

This example illustrates testing deletion of Author runtime without actually deleting.

# Dry run delete
kubectl delete runtimeclass anihpj-author-experimental --dry-run=client
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-author-experimental" deleted (dry run)
Example 4: Delete Location Dev RuntimeClass with Confirmation

This example demonstrates safe deletion with pod dependency check for Location runtime.

# Check for pods using this runtime
PODS=$(kubectl get pods -A -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@.spec.runtimeClassName=="anihpj-location-dev")]}{.metadata.namespace}{"/"}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}')

if [ -z "$PODS" ]; then
  echo "No pods using runtime, safe to delete"
  kubectl delete runtimeclass anihpj-location-dev
else
  echo "Warning: Following pods still use this runtime:"
  echo "$PODS"
fi
No pods using runtime, safe to delete runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-location-dev" deleted
Example 5: Delete Multiple JobPost RuntimeClasses

This example shows deleting multiple old JobPost runtimes at once.

# Delete multiple runtimes
kubectl delete runtimeclass anihpj-jobpost-v1 anihpj-jobpost-v2 anihpj-jobpost-experimental

# List remaining JobPost runtimes
kubectl get rc -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-v1" deleted runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-v2" deleted runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-experimental" deleted NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-jobpost-perf crun 10d anihpj-jobpost-arm runc 5d

deletecollection - delete collection of RuntimeClass

Delete multiple RuntimeClasses at once based on label selectors or field selectors. This bulk operation allows you to efficiently remove multiple runtime configurations simultaneously, useful when cleaning up after major infrastructure changes, removing deprecated runtime versions, or decommissioning entire runtime categories. Exercise caution with this operation as it can affect pod scheduling across your anihpj Django application if referenced RuntimeClasses are deleted. Always verify that no active workloads depend on the RuntimeClasses before performing bulk deletion.

HTTP Request
DELETE /apis/node.k8s.io/v1/runtimeclasses
Parameters
  • body (DeleteOptions): Delete options
  • continue (in query, string): Continue token for pagination
  • dryRun (in query, string): Dry run mode
  • fieldSelector (in query, string): Field selector to filter RuntimeClasses
  • gracePeriodSeconds (in query, integer): Grace period in seconds
  • ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential (in query, boolean): Ignore store read errors
  • labelSelector (in query, string): Label selector to filter RuntimeClasses
  • limit (in query, integer): Maximum number to delete
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • propagationPolicy (in query, string): Propagation policy
  • resourceVersion (in query, string): Resource version
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query, string): Resource version match strategy
  • sendInitialEvents (in query, boolean): Send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (in query, integer): Timeout in seconds
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - RuntimeClasses successfully deleted
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All Skills Test RuntimeClasses

This example deletes all Skills testing runtimes after test completion.

# Delete Skills test runtimes
kubectl delete runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=skills,environment=test
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-1" deleted runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-2" deleted runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-3" deleted
Example 2: Delete User Development RuntimeClasses

This example shows deleting all User development runtimes.

# Delete User dev runtimes
kubectl delete runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=user,tier=development
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-user-dev-standard" deleted runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-user-dev-debug" deleted
Example 3: Delete Author Experimental RuntimeClasses
