This example illustrates deleting experimental Author runtimes.

# Delete Author experimental runtimes
kubectl delete runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=author,status=experimental --dry-run=client
runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-author-exp-1" deleted (dry run) runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-author-exp-2" deleted (dry run)
Example 4: Delete Deprecated Location RuntimeClasses

This example demonstrates deleting deprecated Location runtimes.

# List deprecated runtimes first
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=location,deprecated=true

# Delete after confirmation
kubectl delete runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=location,deprecated=true

# Verify remaining runtimes
kubectl get rc -l app=anihpj,model=location
NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-location-old-v1 runc 90d anihpj-location-old-v2 runc 60d runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-location-old-v1" deleted runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-location-old-v2" deleted NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-location-gpu nvidia 15d anihpj-location-standard runc 20d
Example 5: Delete Old JobPost RuntimeClass Versions

This example shows cleaning up old JobPost runtime versions.

# Check which runtimes will be deleted
kubectl get runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost,version!=current

# Delete old versions
kubectl delete runtimeclasses -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost,version!=current

# List current JobPost runtimes
kubectl get rc -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost,version=current
NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-jobpost-v1 runc 120d anihpj-jobpost-v2 runc 90d anihpj-jobpost-v3 crun 60d runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-v1" deleted runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-v2" deleted runtimeclass.node.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-v3" deleted NAME HANDLER AGE anihpj-jobpost-perf crun 10d anihpj-jobpost-arm runc 5d

ServiceCIDR

ServiceCIDR Overview

ServiceCIDRSpec - Specification for IP allocation
ServiceCIDRStatus - Current status information
ServiceCIDRList - Collection of ServiceCIDRs
Operations - API operations for management
Official Docs: ServiceCIDR v1 API | API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1

ServiceCIDR defines a range of IP addresses using CIDR format for allocating ClusterIPs to Service objects in your anihpj cluster.

Full Description

ServiceCIDR defines a range of IP addresses using CIDR format (e.g. 192.168.0.0/24 or 2001:db8::/64). This range is used to allocate ClusterIPs to Service objects. ServiceCIDR resources enable fine-grained control over IP address allocation for cluster services, supporting both IPv4 and IPv6 address families. This is essential for managing network resources in large clusters or environments with specific networking requirements.

For your anihpj Django application, ServiceCIDR helps manage IP allocation for internal services exposing your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost APIs. You can define separate CIDRs for different service tiers, environments (production, staging, development), or geographic regions. This ensures predictable IP allocation, prevents IP exhaustion, and enables better network planning and security policies for your microservices architecture.

Syntax

# Create a ServiceCIDR from YAML file
kubectl create -f servicecidr.yaml
# Get all ServiceCIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs
# Get detailed information about a specific ServiceCIDR
kubectl describe servicecidr {name}
# Get ServiceCIDR in YAML format
kubectl get servicecidr {name} -o yaml
# Get ServiceCIDR status
kubectl get servicecidr {name} -o jsonpath='{.status}'
# Delete a specific ServiceCIDR
kubectl delete servicecidr {name}
# Watch ServiceCIDRs for changes
kubectl get servicecidrs --watch

YAML Structure

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    tier: api
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.0.0/16"
  - "fd00:10:96::/112"

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - API version, always "networking.k8s.io/v1" for ServiceCIDR
  • kind: string - Resource type, always "ServiceCIDR"
  • metadata: ObjectMeta - Standard object metadata including name, labels, and annotations
  • spec: ServiceCIDRSpec - Desired state of the ServiceCIDR defining IP blocks for ClusterIP allocation
  • status: ServiceCIDRStatus - Current state of the ServiceCIDR with conditions

Return Value

  • 200 (ServiceCIDR): OK - Request successful, returns ServiceCIDR object
  • 201 (ServiceCIDR): Created - ServiceCIDR successfully created
  • 202 (ServiceCIDR): Accepted - Request accepted for processing

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Create ServiceCIDR for Skills API Services

This example creates a dedicated CIDR for Skills validation services.

# Create Skills services CIDR
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    tier: api
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.0.0/20"
EOF

# Verify creation
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-services created NAME AGE anihpj-skills-services 5s
Example 2: Create Dual-Stack ServiceCIDR for User Services

This example demonstrates creating IPv4 and IPv6 CIDRs for User management services.

# Create dual-stack User services CIDR
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    network: dual-stack
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.16.0/20"
  - "fd00:10:96::/112"
EOF

# Describe the resource
kubectl describe servicecidr anihpj-user-services
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-user-services created Name: anihpj-user-services Labels: app=anihpj model=user network=dual-stack Spec: Cidrs: 10.96.16.0/20 fd00:10:96::/112 Status: Conditions: Type: Ready Status: True
Example 3: Create ServiceCIDR for Author Content Services

This example illustrates creating a CIDR for Author content API services.

# Create Author services CIDR
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    environment: production
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.32.0/20"
EOF

# View in YAML
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services -o yaml
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-author-services created apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: ServiceCIDR metadata: name: anihpj-author-services labels: app: anihpj model: author environment: production spec: cidrs: - 10.96.32.0/20
Example 4: Create Regional ServiceCIDR for Location Services

This example shows creating region-specific CIDR for Location mapping services.

# Create regional Location services CIDR
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-us-east
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    region: us-east-1
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.48.0/20"
EOF

# List all ServiceCIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-location-us-east created NAME AGE anihpj-skills-services 5m anihpj-user-services 4m anihpj-author-services 3m anihpj-location-us-east 2s
Example 5: Create ServiceCIDR for JobPost Scheduler Services

This example demonstrates creating CIDR for JobPost scheduling and matching services.

# Create JobPost services CIDR
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    component: scheduler
  annotations:
    description: "CIDR for JobPost scheduling and matching services"
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.64.0/20"
EOF

# Check status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0]}' | jq
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-services created { "lastTransitionTime": "2026-01-08T10:00:00Z", "message": "ServiceCIDR is ready", "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready" }

ServiceCIDRSpec

Official Docs: ServiceCIDRSpec v1 API | API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1

ServiceCIDRSpec defines the CIDRs the user wants to use for allocating ClusterIPs for Services.

Full Description

ServiceCIDRSpec define the CIDRs the user wants to use for allocating ClusterIPs for Services. The specification allows defining IP blocks in CIDR notation from which Kubernetes allocates service cluster IPs. A maximum of two CIDRs is allowed, one for each IP family (IPv4 and IPv6). This field is immutable after creation, ensuring stable IP allocation policies for your anihpj Django services.

Parameters Description

  • cidrs: []string - CIDRs defines the IP blocks in CIDR notation (e.g. "192.168.0.0/24" or "2001:db8::/64") from which to assign service cluster IPs. Max of two CIDRs is allowed, one of each IP family. This field is immutable

Return Value

  • 200 (ServiceCIDRSpec): OK - Spec successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: View Skills ServiceCIDR Spec

This example retrieves the CIDR specification for Skills services.

# Get Skills ServiceCIDR spec
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services -o jsonpath='{.spec}' | jq
{ "cidrs": [ "10.96.0.0/20" ] }
Example 2: Extract User ServiceCIDR CIDRs

This example shows extracting CIDR blocks from User services.

# Get User CIDR blocks
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services -o jsonpath='{.spec.cidrs[*]}'
10.96.16.0/20 fd00:10:96::/112
Example 3: Check Author ServiceCIDR IPv4 Range

This example illustrates checking IPv4 CIDR for Author services.

# Get Author IPv4 CIDR
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services -o jsonpath='{.spec.cidrs[0]}'
10.96.32.0/20
Example 4: Verify Location ServiceCIDR Immutability

This example demonstrates that CIDR blocks cannot be changed after creation.

# Attempt to modify Location CIDRs (will fail)
kubectl patch servicecidr anihpj-location-us-east --type=merge -p '{
  "spec": {
    "cidrs": ["10.96.48.0/21"]
  }
}'
Error from server: spec.cidrs field is immutable
Example 5: List All JobPost ServiceCIDR Ranges

This example shows listing CIDR ranges for all JobPost services.

# List JobPost CIDR ranges
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.cidrs[*]}{"\n"}{end}' | column -t
anihpj-jobpost-services 10.96.64.0/20

ServiceCIDRStatus

Official Docs: ServiceCIDRStatus v1 API | API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1

ServiceCIDRStatus describes the current state of the ServiceCIDR with conditions.

Full Description

ServiceCIDRStatus describes the current state of the ServiceCIDR. It contains conditions that indicate the operational state and readiness of the CIDR allocation range. This status information is crucial for monitoring IP address allocation health and identifying any issues with service IP assignment in your anihpj Django cluster.

Parameters Description

  • conditions: []Condition - Array of metav1.Condition that describe the state of the ServiceCIDR. Each condition includes type, status, reason, message, lastTransitionTime, and observedGeneration fields to provide detailed state information

Return Value

  • 200 (ServiceCIDRStatus): OK - Status successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django Examples

Example 1: Check Skills ServiceCIDR Status

This example retrieves the complete status for Skills service CIDR.

# Get Skills ServiceCIDR status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{ "conditions": [ { "lastTransitionTime": "2026-01-08T09:00:00Z", "message": "ServiceCIDR is ready for IP allocation", "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready", "observedGeneration": 1 } ] }
Example 2: View User ServiceCIDR Conditions

This example shows checking conditions for User service CIDR.

# Get User ServiceCIDR conditions
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*]}' | jq
{ "lastTransitionTime": "2026-01-08T09:15:00Z", "message": "Dual-stack CIDR allocation ready", "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready", "observedGeneration": 1 }
Example 3: Monitor Author ServiceCIDR Readiness

This example illustrates monitoring readiness status for Author services.

# Check Author ServiceCIDR ready status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}'
True
Example 4: View Location ServiceCIDR Condition Details

This example demonstrates viewing detailed condition information for Location services.

# Get Location ServiceCIDR condition details
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-us-east -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0]}' | jq
{ "lastTransitionTime": "2026-01-08T09:30:00Z", "message": "Regional CIDR allocation active", "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready", "observedGeneration": 1 }
Example 5: Watch JobPost ServiceCIDR Status Changes

This example shows watching for status changes in JobPost service CIDR.

# Watch JobPost ServiceCIDR status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].message}' --watch
ServiceCIDR is ready ServiceCIDR is ready

ServiceCIDRList

Official Docs: ServiceCIDRList v1 API | API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1

ServiceCIDRList contains a collection of ServiceCIDR objects for managing multiple CIDR ranges in your anihpj cluster.

Full Description

ServiceCIDRList contains a list of ServiceCIDR objects. This resource provides a convenient way to query and manage multiple service CIDR allocations at once, enabling you to view all IP ranges configured for service allocation across your cluster. For your anihpj Django application, ServiceCIDRList operations help you audit CIDR allocations, ensure non-overlapping IP ranges, and manage network segmentation for your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services.

Parameters Description

  • apiVersion: string - APIVersion defines the versioned schema. Always "networking.k8s.io/v1" for ServiceCIDRList
  • kind: string - Kind is the REST resource type. Always "ServiceCIDRList"
  • metadata: ListMeta - Standard list metadata with resourceVersion and continue token
  • items: []ServiceCIDR - List of ServiceCIDR objects in the cluster

Return Value

  • 200 (ServiceCIDRList): OK - ServiceCIDRList successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required

5 Practical Django List Examples

Example 1: List All anihpj ServiceCIDRs

This example demonstrates listing all ServiceCIDRs for anihpj services.

# List all anihpj ServiceCIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-services 30m anihpj-user-services 25m anihpj-author-services 20m anihpj-location-us-east 15m anihpj-jobpost-services 10m
Example 2: List ServiceCIDRs with CIDR Ranges

This example shows retrieving ServiceCIDRs with their CIDR blocks in custom format.

# List with CIDR ranges
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,CIDRS:.spec.cidrs[*]
NAME CIDRS anihpj-skills-services 10.96.0.0/20 anihpj-user-services 10.96.16.0/20 fd00:10:96::/112 anihpj-author-services 10.96.32.0/20 anihpj-location-us-east 10.96.48.0/20 anihpj-jobpost-services 10.96.64.0/20
Example 3: List ServiceCIDRs in JSON Format

This example illustrates listing ServiceCIDRs in JSON for API integration.

# Get ServiceCIDRs as JSON
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, cidrs: .spec.cidrs, ready: .status.conditions[0].status}'
{ "name": "anihpj-skills-services", "cidrs": ["10.96.0.0/20"], "ready": "True" } { "name": "anihpj-user-services", "cidrs": ["10.96.16.0/20", "fd00:10:96::/112"], "ready": "True" } { "name": "anihpj-author-services", "cidrs": ["10.96.32.0/20"], "ready": "True" }
Example 4: List Dual-Stack ServiceCIDRs

This example demonstrates filtering ServiceCIDRs that support dual-stack networking.

# List dual-stack ServiceCIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,network=dual-stack
NAME AGE anihpj-user-services 25m
Example 5: List ServiceCIDRs Sorted by Creation Time

This example shows listing ServiceCIDRs sorted by age.

# List ServiceCIDRs sorted by creation
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-services 30m anihpj-user-services 25m anihpj-author-services 20m anihpj-location-us-east 15m anihpj-jobpost-services 10m

ServiceCIDR Operations

Operations Overview

get - Read the specified ServiceCIDR
get (status) - Read status of the specified ServiceCIDR
list - List all ServiceCIDRs
create - Create a ServiceCIDR
update - Replace the specified ServiceCIDR
update (status) - Replace status of the specified ServiceCIDR
patch - Partially update the specified ServiceCIDR
patch (status) - Partially update status of the specified ServiceCIDR
delete - Delete a ServiceCIDR
deletecollection - Delete collection of ServiceCIDRs

Operations allow you to interact with ServiceCIDR resources through the Kubernetes API. These operations enable you to manage IP address allocation ranges for your anihpj Django application services, defining CIDR blocks for ClusterIP assignment across your Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost microservices.

get - read the specified ServiceCIDR

Read details of a specific ServiceCIDR by name to retrieve its complete configuration including CIDR blocks and status. This operation returns the full ServiceCIDR object, enabling you to verify IP allocation ranges, check readiness conditions, and audit network configuration for your anihpj application. Use this operation to troubleshoot service IP allocation issues, validate CIDR configurations, and ensure proper network segmentation for your microservices.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs/{name}
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the ServiceCIDR
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ServiceCIDR): OK - ServiceCIDR successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills ServiceCIDR Details

This example retrieves complete configuration for Skills service CIDR.

# Get Skills ServiceCIDR
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services -o yaml
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: ServiceCIDR metadata: name: anihpj-skills-services labels: app: anihpj model: skills tier: api spec: cidrs: - 10.96.0.0/20 status: conditions: - lastTransitionTime: "2026-01-08T09:00:00Z" message: ServiceCIDR is ready for IP allocation reason: Ready status: "True" type: Ready
Example 2: Get User ServiceCIDR CIDR Blocks

This example shows extracting CIDR blocks from User service configuration.

# Get User CIDR blocks
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services -o jsonpath='{.spec.cidrs}' | jq
["10.96.16.0/20","fd00:10:96::/112"]
Example 3: Describe Author ServiceCIDR

This example illustrates getting detailed information for Author service CIDR.

# Describe Author ServiceCIDR
kubectl describe servicecidr anihpj-author-services
Name: anihpj-author-services Labels: app=anihpj environment=production model=author Spec: Cidrs: 10.96.32.0/20 Status: Conditions: Type: Ready Status: True Message: ServiceCIDR is ready for IP allocation Reason: Ready
Example 4: Get Location ServiceCIDR in JSON

This example demonstrates retrieving Location service CIDR in JSON format.

# Get Location ServiceCIDR as JSON
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-us-east -o json | jq '{name: .metadata.name, cidrs: .spec.cidrs, ready: .status.conditions[0].status}'
{ "name": "anihpj-location-us-east", "cidrs": ["10.96.48.0/20"], "ready": "True" }
Example 5: Get JobPost ServiceCIDR with Custom Output

This example shows custom formatted output for JobPost service CIDR.

# Get JobPost ServiceCIDR custom output
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,CIDR:.spec.cidrs[0],STATUS:.status.conditions[0].type,READY:.status.conditions[0].status
NAME CIDR STATUS READY anihpj-jobpost-services 10.96.64.0/20 Ready True

get - read status of the specified ServiceCIDR

Read the status of a specific ServiceCIDR to monitor its operational state and readiness. This operation returns only the status portion of the ServiceCIDR object, which includes conditions that indicate whether the CIDR is ready for IP allocation. Use this operation to check ServiceCIDR health, monitor condition changes over time, and ensure IP allocation ranges are functioning correctly for your anihpj Django services.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs/{name}/status
Parameters
  • name (in path, string, required): Name of the ServiceCIDR
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
Return Value
  • 200 (ServiceCIDR): OK - Status successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Get Skills ServiceCIDR Status

This example retrieves status information for Skills service CIDR.

# Get Skills ServiceCIDR status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{ "conditions": [ { "lastTransitionTime": "2026-01-08T09:00:00Z", "message": "ServiceCIDR is ready for IP allocation", "observedGeneration": 1, "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready" } ] }
Example 2: Check User ServiceCIDR Ready Condition

This example shows checking the ready status for User service CIDR.

# Check User ServiceCIDR ready condition
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")]}' | jq
{ "lastTransitionTime": "2026-01-08T09:15:00Z", "message": "Dual-stack CIDR allocation ready", "observedGeneration": 1, "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready" }
Example 3: Monitor Author ServiceCIDR Status Message

This example illustrates extracting status message for Author service CIDR.

# Get Author status message
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].message}'
ServiceCIDR is ready for IP allocation
Example 4: Check Location ServiceCIDR Last Transition Time

This example demonstrates checking when Location service CIDR last changed state.

# Get Location last transition time
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-us-east -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].lastTransitionTime}'
2026-01-08T09:30:00Z
Example 5: Watch JobPost ServiceCIDR Status Changes

This example shows watching for status changes in JobPost service CIDR.

# Watch JobPost ServiceCIDR status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services --watch -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].status}'
True True

list - list or watch objects of kind ServiceCIDR

List all ServiceCIDRs in the cluster, optionally filtered by labels or field selectors. This operation returns a ServiceCIDRList containing all CIDR configurations available for service IP allocation. Use this operation to inventory IP allocation ranges, audit CIDR configurations across your cluster, verify non-overlapping ranges, and monitor IP address space utilization for your anihpj Django application. You can filter results by labels to find CIDRs for specific models, environments, or regions.

HTTP Request
GET /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs
Parameters
  • allowWatchBookmarks (in query, boolean): Allow watch bookmarks
  • continue (in query, string): Continue token for pagination
  • fieldSelector (in query, string): Field selector to filter results
  • labelSelector (in query, string): Label selector to filter results
  • limit (in query, integer): Maximum number of results to return
  • pretty (in query, string): Pretty print output
  • resourceVersion (in query, string): Resource version for list
  • resourceVersionMatch (in query, string): Resource version match strategy
  • sendInitialEvents (in query, boolean): Send initial events
  • timeoutSeconds (in query, integer): Timeout for list operation
  • watch (in query, boolean): Watch for changes
Return Value
  • 200 (ServiceCIDRList): OK - List successfully retrieved
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List All Skills ServiceCIDRs

This example lists all ServiceCIDRs configured for Skills services.

# List Skills ServiceCIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=skills
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-services 30m
Example 2: List User ServiceCIDRs with Wide Output

This example shows listing User ServiceCIDRs with additional details.

# List User ServiceCIDRs with CIDR blocks
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=user -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,CIDRS:.spec.cidrs[*],AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME CIDRS AGE anihpj-user-services 10.96.16.0/20 fd00:10:96::/112 2026-01-08T09:15:00Z
Example 3: List Author ServiceCIDRs in JSON

This example illustrates listing Author ServiceCIDRs in JSON format.

# List Author ServiceCIDRs as JSON
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=author -o json | jq '.items[] | {name: .metadata.name, cidrs: .spec.cidrs}'
{ "name": "anihpj-author-services", "cidrs": ["10.96.32.0/20"] }
Example 4: List Location ServiceCIDRs by Region

This example demonstrates filtering Location ServiceCIDRs by region label.

# List Location ServiceCIDRs for us-east
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=location,region=us-east-1
NAME AGE anihpj-location-us-east 15m
Example 5: Watch All JobPost ServiceCIDRs

This example shows watching JobPost ServiceCIDRs for configuration changes.

# Watch JobPost ServiceCIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost --watch
NAME AGE anihpj-jobpost-services 10m anihpj-jobpost-services 10m (MODIFIED)

create - create a ServiceCIDR

Creating a ServiceCIDR defines a new IP address range in CIDR format for allocating ClusterIPs to Service objects. Once created, the cidrs field is immutable and cannot be modified. You can specify up to two CIDRs (one for IPv4 and one for IPv6) to support dual-stack networking. ServiceCIDRs are cluster-scoped resources managed by Kubernetes controllers to ensure proper IP allocation and prevent conflicts.

HTTP Request

POST /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs

Parameters
  • body: ServiceCIDR (required) - The ServiceCIDR configuration to create
  • dryRun: string - When present, indicates that modifications should not be persisted (all, none)
  • fieldManager: string - Name of the manager handling this operation
  • fieldValidation: string - Determines how server handles unrecognized fields (Ignore, Warn, Strict)
  • pretty: string - If 'true', output is pretty printed
Return Value
  • 200 (ServiceCIDR): OK - ServiceCIDR created successfully
  • 201 (ServiceCIDR): Created - ServiceCIDR created successfully
  • 202 (ServiceCIDR): Accepted - Request accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills API Services CIDR

This example creates a ServiceCIDR for the anihpj Skills API with IPv4 range.

# Create Skills Services CIDR
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    environment: production
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.0.0/20"
EOF
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-services created
Example 2: Create User Management Dual-Stack CIDR

This example creates a dual-stack ServiceCIDR for User management with both IPv4 and IPv6.

# Create User dual-stack CIDR
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
    environment: production
    networking: dual-stack
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.16.0/20"
  - "fd00:10:96::/112"
EOF

# Verify creation
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-user-services created NAME AGE anihpj-user-services 1s
Example 3: Create Author Content Production CIDR

This example creates a production ServiceCIDR for Author content services with annotations.

# Create Author production CIDR
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    environment: production
    tier: content
  annotations:
    description: "Production CIDR for author content services"
    managed-by: "platform-team"
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.32.0/20"
EOF

# Check status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0]}'
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-author-services created {"lastTransitionTime":"2025-01-15T10:30:00Z","message":"ServiceCIDR is ready","reason":"Ready","status":"True","type":"Ready"}
Example 4: Create Location Services Regional CIDR

This example creates a regional ServiceCIDR for Location services in us-east region.

# Create Location regional CIDR
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-services-useast
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    environment: production
    region: us-east
  annotations:
    region: "us-east-1"
    allocated-date: "2025-01-15"
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.48.0/20"
EOF

# Describe the created CIDR
kubectl describe servicecidr anihpj-location-services-useast
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-location-services-useast created Name: anihpj-location-services-useast Namespace: Labels: app=anihpj environment=production model=location region=us-east Annotations: allocated-date: 2025-01-15 region: us-east-1 API Version: networking.k8s.io/v1 Kind: ServiceCIDR Spec: Cidrs: 10.96.48.0/20 Status: Conditions: Type: Ready Status: True Reason: Ready
Example 5: Create JobPost Staging CIDR with Validation

This example creates a staging ServiceCIDR for JobPost services with dry-run validation first.

# First validate with dry-run
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-services-staging
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    environment: staging
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.64.0/20"
EOF

# Create if validation passes
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-services-staging
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    environment: staging
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.64.0/20"
EOF
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-services-staging created (dry run) servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-services-staging created

update - replace the specified ServiceCIDR

Updating a ServiceCIDR replaces its entire configuration. Important: The spec.cidrs field is immutable and cannot be changed after creation. You can only update mutable fields like labels, annotations, and metadata. This operation is useful for updating administrative metadata, changing labels for better organization, or adding annotations for documentation. Any attempt to modify the cidrs field will result in an error.

HTTP Request

PUT /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs/{name}

Parameters
  • name: string (required) - Name of the ServiceCIDR to update
  • body: ServiceCIDR (required) - The updated ServiceCIDR configuration
  • dryRun: string - When present, indicates that modifications should not be persisted
  • fieldManager: string - Name of the manager handling this operation
  • fieldValidation: string - Determines how server handles unrecognized fields
  • pretty: string - If 'true', output is pretty printed
Return Value
  • 200 (ServiceCIDR): OK - ServiceCIDR updated successfully
  • 201 (ServiceCIDR): Created - ServiceCIDR created if it didn't exist
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Update Skills CIDR Labels

This example updates the labels on the Skills ServiceCIDR without modifying CIDRs.

# Get current configuration
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services -o yaml > skills-cidr.yaml

# Edit labels (not cidrs)
# Add new label: tier=api

# Apply updated configuration
kubectl apply -f skills-cidr.yaml

# Verify update
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services --show-labels
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-services configured NAME AGE LABELS anihpj-skills-services 5h app=anihpj,environment=production,model=skills,tier=api
Example 2: Update User CIDR Annotations

This example adds documentation annotations to the User ServiceCIDR.

# Update User CIDR with annotations
kubectl annotate servicecidr anihpj-user-services \
  documentation="Dual-stack CIDR for user management services" \
  contact-team="user-services-team" \
  --overwrite

# View annotations
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}'
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-user-services annotated {"contact-team":"user-services-team","documentation":"Dual-stack CIDR for user management services"}
Example 3: Attempt to Update Author CIDR Range (Will Fail)

This example demonstrates that updating the cidrs field fails due to immutability.

# Try to update CIDR range (will fail)
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: ServiceCIDR
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-services
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    environment: production
spec:
  cidrs:
  - "10.96.40.0/20"  # Attempting to change from 10.96.32.0/20
EOF
The ServiceCIDR "anihpj-author-services" is invalid: spec.cidrs: Forbidden: field is immutable
Example 4: Update Location CIDR with Additional Labels

This example adds operational labels to the Location ServiceCIDR for better management.

# Add operational labels
kubectl label servicecidr anihpj-location-services-useast \
  monitored=true \
  alert-level=critical \
  backup-enabled=true

# Get full configuration with updated labels
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-services-useast -o yaml | grep -A 10 labels
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-location-services-useast labeled labels: alert-level: critical app: anihpj backup-enabled: "true" environment: production model: location monitored: "true" region: us-east
Example 5: Update JobPost CIDR Metadata

This example updates multiple metadata fields on the JobPost staging CIDR.

# Update labels and annotations together
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services-staging -o yaml > jobpost-cidr.yaml

# Edit to add:
# labels: responsible-team=jobpost-dev
# annotations: last-reviewed=2025-01-15

kubectl apply -f jobpost-cidr.yaml

# Verify all metadata
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services-staging -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
LABELS:.metadata.labels,\
ANNOTATIONS:.metadata.annotations
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-services-staging configured NAME LABELS ANNOTATIONS anihpj-jobpost-services-staging map[app:anihpj environment:staging model:jobpost responsible-team:jobpost-dev] map[last-reviewed:2025-01-15]

update - replace status of the specified ServiceCIDR

Updating the status of a ServiceCIDR replaces the entire status subresource. This operation is typically performed by Kubernetes controllers to report the operational state of the CIDR allocation. The status includes conditions that indicate readiness and any issues with IP allocation. Regular users rarely need to manually update status; it's primarily managed by the system controllers.

HTTP Request

PUT /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs/{name}/status

Parameters
  • name: string (required) - Name of the ServiceCIDR
  • body: ServiceCIDR (required) - The ServiceCIDR object with updated status
  • dryRun: string - When present, indicates that modifications should not be persisted
  • fieldManager: string - Name of the manager handling this operation
  • fieldValidation: string - Determines how server handles unrecognized fields
  • pretty: string - If 'true', output is pretty printed
Return Value
  • 200 (ServiceCIDR): OK - Status updated successfully
  • 201 (ServiceCIDR): Created - Status created if it didn't exist
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Monitor Skills CIDR Status Updates

This example watches status changes on the Skills ServiceCIDR.

# Watch status changes
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services --watch-only -o jsonpath='{.status}'

# In another terminal, view current status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services -o jsonpath='{.status}' | jq
{ "conditions": [ { "lastTransitionTime": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", "message": "ServiceCIDR is ready", "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready" } ] }
Example 2: Check User CIDR Ready Condition

This example extracts the ready condition from User ServiceCIDR status.

# Get ready condition status
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}'

# Get ready condition message
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].message}'
True Dual-stack ServiceCIDR is ready and allocating IPs
Example 3: View Author CIDR Status History

This example shows all status transitions for the Author ServiceCIDR.

# View full status with all conditions
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services -o yaml | grep -A 20 status:

# Extract condition transition times
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services \
  -o jsonpath='{range .status.conditions[*]}{.type}{"\t"}{.lastTransitionTime}{"\n"}{end}'
status: conditions: - lastTransitionTime: "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z" message: ServiceCIDR is ready observedGeneration: 1 reason: Ready status: "True" type: Ready Ready 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z
Example 4: Monitor Location CIDR for Status Changes

This example continuously monitors the Location ServiceCIDR status for any changes.

# Watch status field continuously
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-services-useast \
  --watch -o jsonpath='{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.conditions[0].status}{"\t"}{.status.conditions[0].message}{"\n"}'
anihpj-location-services-useast True ServiceCIDR is ready
Example 5: Check JobPost CIDR Status with Description

This example retrieves detailed status information for the JobPost staging CIDR.

# Get status with custom format
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services-staging \
  -o custom-columns=\
NAME:.metadata.name,\
STATUS:.status.conditions[0].status,\
REASON:.status.conditions[0].reason,\
MESSAGE:.status.conditions[0].message,\
LAST_TRANSITION:.status.conditions[0].lastTransitionTime
NAME STATUS REASON MESSAGE LAST_TRANSITION anihpj-jobpost-services-staging True Ready ServiceCIDR is ready 2025-01-15T10:35:00Z

patch - partially update the specified ServiceCIDR

Patching a ServiceCIDR allows you to partially update specific fields without replacing the entire object. This is the preferred method for making incremental changes to labels, annotations, or other metadata fields. Important: The spec.cidrs field remains immutable and cannot be patched. Patches can be applied in different formats: strategic merge patch, JSON merge patch, or JSON patch. This operation is more efficient than a full update when only modifying a few fields.

HTTP Request

PATCH /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs/{name}

Parameters
  • name: string (required) - Name of the ServiceCIDR to patch
  • body: Patch (required) - The patch to apply
  • dryRun: string - When present, indicates that modifications should not be persisted
  • fieldManager: string - Name of the manager handling this operation
  • fieldValidation: string - Determines how server handles unrecognized fields
  • force: boolean - Force apply if conflicts occur
  • pretty: string - If 'true', output is pretty printed
Return Value
  • 200 (ServiceCIDR): OK - ServiceCIDR patched successfully
  • 201 (ServiceCIDR): Created - ServiceCIDR created if it didn't exist
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills CIDR with New Label

This example adds a monitoring label to the Skills ServiceCIDR using JSON merge patch.

# Patch Skills CIDR to add monitoring label
kubectl patch servicecidr anihpj-skills-services \
  --type=merge \
  -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"monitoring":"enabled"}}}'

# Verify the patch
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels.monitoring}'
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-services patched enabled
Example 2: Patch User CIDR Annotation

This example updates an annotation on the User ServiceCIDR using strategic merge.

# Add/update last-audited annotation
kubectl patch servicecidr anihpj-user-services \
  --type=strategic \
  -p '{
    "metadata": {
      "annotations": {
        "last-audited": "2025-01-15",
        "audit-result": "passed"
      }
    }
  }'

# View updated annotations
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}' | jq
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-user-services patched { "audit-result": "passed", "contact-team": "user-services-team", "documentation": "Dual-stack CIDR for user management services", "last-audited": "2025-01-15" }
Example 3: Patch Author CIDR with Multiple Labels

This example adds multiple operational labels to the Author ServiceCIDR in one patch.

# Add multiple labels at once
kubectl patch servicecidr anihpj-author-services \
  --type=merge \
  -p '{
    "metadata": {
      "labels": {
        "cost-center": "content-production",
        "compliance": "required",
        "backup": "daily"
      }
    }
  }'

# List all labels
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services --show-labels
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-author-services patched NAME AGE LABELS anihpj-author-services 6h app=anihpj,backup=daily,compliance=required,cost-center=content-production,environment=production,model=author,tier=content
Example 4: Patch Location CIDR Using JSON Patch

This example uses JSON Patch format to add a new annotation to Location ServiceCIDR.

# Use JSON Patch to add annotation
kubectl patch servicecidr anihpj-location-services-useast \
  --type=json \
  -p '[
    {
      "op": "add",
      "path": "/metadata/annotations/network-policy",
      "value": "strict"
    }
  ]'

# Verify the annotation
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-services-useast \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.network-policy}'
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-location-services-useast patched strict
Example 5: Patch JobPost CIDR with Dry Run

This example tests patching the JobPost ServiceCIDR with dry-run before applying.

# Test patch with dry-run
kubectl patch servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services-staging \
  --type=merge \
  -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"tested":"true","version":"v2"}}}' \
  --dry-run=client

# Apply if validation passes
kubectl patch servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services-staging \
  --type=merge \
  -p '{"metadata":{"labels":{"tested":"true","version":"v2"}}}'

# Verify changes
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services-staging \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.labels}' | jq
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-services-staging patched (dry run) servicecidr.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-services-staging patched { "app": "anihpj", "environment": "staging", "model": "jobpost", "responsible-team": "jobpost-dev", "tested": "true", "version": "v2" }

patch - partially update status of the specified ServiceCIDR

Patching the status subresource of a ServiceCIDR allows partial updates to the status conditions without affecting the spec or metadata. This operation is primarily used by Kubernetes controllers to update operational state information. The status typically contains conditions array with information about the CIDR readiness, allocation status, and any error conditions. Manual status patches are rarely needed and should be used with caution.

HTTP Request

PATCH /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs/{name}/status

Parameters
  • name: string (required) - Name of the ServiceCIDR
  • body: Patch (required) - The status patch to apply
  • dryRun: string - When present, indicates that modifications should not be persisted
  • fieldManager: string - Name of the manager handling this operation
  • fieldValidation: string - Determines how server handles unrecognized fields
  • force: boolean - Force apply if conflicts occur
  • pretty: string - If 'true', output is pretty printed
Return Value
  • 200 (ServiceCIDR): OK - Status patched successfully
  • 201 (ServiceCIDR): Created - Status created if it didn't exist
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Monitor Skills CIDR Status Patches

This example watches for status patch operations on the Skills ServiceCIDR.

# Watch status changes in real-time
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services \
  --watch -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*].type}{"\t"}{.status.conditions[*].status}{"\n"}'

# View current status conditions
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-services \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[*]}' | jq
Ready True [ { "lastTransitionTime": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z", "message": "ServiceCIDR is ready", "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready" } ]
Example 2: Check User CIDR Status Patch Results

This example verifies status patches applied to the User ServiceCIDR.

# Get status with observedGeneration
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0]}' | jq

# Check if status matches current generation
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-user-services \
  -o jsonpath='Gen:{.metadata.generation} ObservedGen:{.status.conditions[0].observedGeneration}'
{ "lastTransitionTime": "2025-01-15T10:31:00Z", "message": "Dual-stack ServiceCIDR is ready and allocating IPs", "observedGeneration": 1, "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready" } Gen:1 ObservedGen:1
Example 3: View Author CIDR Status Condition Details

This example extracts detailed condition information from Author ServiceCIDR status.

# Get all condition fields
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services \
  -o custom-columns=\
TYPE:.status.conditions[0].type,\
STATUS:.status.conditions[0].status,\
REASON:.status.conditions[0].reason,\
MESSAGE:.status.conditions[0].message,\
TRANSITION:.status.conditions[0].lastTransitionTime

# Check status age
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-author-services -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[0].lastTransitionTime}'
TYPE STATUS REASON MESSAGE TRANSITION Ready True Ready ServiceCIDR is ready 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z
Example 4: Monitor Location CIDR Status Changes Over Time

This example tracks status condition transitions for the Location ServiceCIDR.

# Watch with timestamp for status changes
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-services-useast \
  --watch -o json | jq -r '.status.conditions[] | "\(.lastTransitionTime) \(.type)=\(.status) \(.message)"'

# Get current status summary
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-services-useast \
  -o jsonpath='Status: {.status.conditions[0].status}, Last Update: {.status.conditions[0].lastTransitionTime}'
2025-01-15T10:30:00Z Ready=True ServiceCIDR is ready Status: True, Last Update: 2025-01-15T10:30:00Z
Example 5: Verify JobPost CIDR Status After Patches

This example validates that status patches are correctly applied to the JobPost staging CIDR.

# Get full status in YAML format
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services-staging -o yaml | grep -A 15 "status:"

# Check specific condition by type
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-jobpost-services-staging \
  -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")]}'  | jq
status: conditions: - lastTransitionTime: "2025-01-15T10:35:00Z" message: ServiceCIDR is ready observedGeneration: 1 reason: Ready status: "True" type: Ready { "lastTransitionTime": "2025-01-15T10:35:00Z", "message": "ServiceCIDR is ready", "observedGeneration": 1, "reason": "Ready", "status": "True", "type": "Ready" }

delete - delete a ServiceCIDR

Deleting a ServiceCIDR removes the IP address range allocation from the cluster. Warning: Before deleting a ServiceCIDR, ensure that no active Services are using IP addresses from this range, as deletion may disrupt network connectivity. The operation supports graceful deletion with configurable grace periods and can perform dry-run validation before actual deletion. Always verify dependencies and backup configurations before deleting production ServiceCIDRs.

HTTP Request

DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs/{name}

Parameters
  • name: string (required) - Name of the ServiceCIDR to delete
  • body: DeleteOptions - Options for deletion behavior
  • dryRun: string - When present, indicates that modifications should not be persisted
  • gracePeriodSeconds: integer - Duration in seconds before the object should be deleted
  • pretty: string - If 'true', output is pretty printed
  • propagationPolicy: string - How deletion should cascade (Orphan, Background, Foreground)
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - ServiceCIDR deleted successfully
  • 202 (Status): Accepted - Deletion accepted for processing
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Deprecated Skills Test CIDR

This example deletes a test ServiceCIDR for Skills that is no longer needed.

# Check if CIDR exists
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-test-services

# Delete the test CIDR
kubectl delete servicecidr anihpj-skills-test-services

# Verify deletion
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-skills-test-services
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-test-services 30d servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-services" deleted Error from server (NotFound): servicecidrs.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-services" not found
Example 2: Delete User Development CIDR

This example removes a development ServiceCIDR for User services after migration.

# List User CIDRs before deletion
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=user

# Delete development CIDR
kubectl delete servicecidr anihpj-user-services-dev

# Confirm only production CIDR remains
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=user
NAME AGE anihpj-user-services 7d anihpj-user-services-dev 45d servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-user-services-dev" deleted NAME AGE anihpj-user-services 7d
Example 3: Delete Author CIDR with Dry Run

This example validates deletion of an Author ServiceCIDR using dry-run before actual deletion.

# Test deletion with dry-run
kubectl delete servicecidr anihpj-author-services-old --dry-run=client

# Check for dependent services (none should exist)
kubectl get services -A -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.spec.clusterIP | startswith("10.96.32"))'

# Proceed with actual deletion
kubectl delete servicecidr anihpj-author-services-old
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-author-services-old" deleted (dry run) servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-author-services-old" deleted
Example 4: Delete Location CIDR After Dependency Check

This example performs thorough dependency checking before deleting a Location ServiceCIDR.

# Check for services using this CIDR range
kubectl get services -A -o json | \
  jq -r '.items[] | select(.spec.clusterIP | startswith("10.96.48")) | 
  "\(.metadata.namespace)/\(.metadata.name) - \(.spec.clusterIP)"'

# Verify no active services
# If clear, delete the CIDR
kubectl delete servicecidr anihpj-location-services-westus

# Confirm deletion
kubectl get servicecidr anihpj-location-services-westus
(no output - no services using this range) servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-location-services-westus" deleted Error from server (NotFound): servicecidrs.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-location-services-westus" not found
Example 5: Delete Multiple JobPost Test CIDRs

This example removes several test ServiceCIDRs for JobPost after testing completion.

# List all JobPost test CIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost,environment=test

# Delete all test CIDRs
kubectl delete servicecidr \
  anihpj-jobpost-services-test1 \
  anihpj-jobpost-services-test2 \
  anihpj-jobpost-services-test3

# Verify only production and staging remain
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost
NAME AGE anihpj-jobpost-services-test1 15d anihpj-jobpost-services-test2 12d anihpj-jobpost-services-test3 8d servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-services-test1" deleted servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-services-test2" deleted servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-services-test3" deleted NAME AGE anihpj-jobpost-services 10d anihpj-jobpost-services-staging 5d

deletecollection - delete collection of ServiceCIDR

Deleting a collection of ServiceCIDRs removes multiple CIDR allocations at once based on label selectors or field selectors. This operation is useful for bulk cleanup of test environments, deprecated configurations, or expired CIDR ranges. Critical Warning: Exercise extreme caution with deletecollection operations, as they can remove multiple ServiceCIDRs simultaneously and potentially disrupt network services. Always use dry-run first and verify label selectors before executing.

HTTP Request

DELETE /apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs

Parameters
  • body: DeleteOptions - Options for deletion behavior
  • continue: string - Continue token for pagination
  • dryRun: string - When present, indicates that modifications should not be persisted
  • fieldSelector: string - Filter based on field values
  • gracePeriodSeconds: integer - Duration before deletion
  • labelSelector: string - Filter based on labels
  • limit: integer - Maximum number of objects to delete
  • pretty: string - If 'true', output is pretty printed
  • propagationPolicy: string - How deletion should cascade
  • resourceVersion: string - Resource version for optimistic concurrency
  • timeoutSeconds: integer - Timeout for the request
Return Value
  • 200 (Status): OK - Collection deleted successfully
  • 401: Unauthorized - Authentication required
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete All Skills Test ServiceCIDRs

This example removes all test ServiceCIDRs for Skills model after testing completion.

# List Skills test CIDRs before deletion
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=skills,environment=test

# Delete all Skills test CIDRs
kubectl delete servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=skills,environment=test

# Verify production CIDRs remain
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=skills,environment=production
NAME AGE anihpj-skills-test-cidr-1 20d anihpj-skills-test-cidr-2 15d anihpj-skills-test-cidr-3 10d servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-cidr-1" deleted servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-cidr-2" deleted servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-skills-test-cidr-3" deleted NAME AGE anihpj-skills-services 25d
Example 2: Delete User Development ServiceCIDRs

This example removes all development ServiceCIDRs for User management after migration.

# Check what will be deleted
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=user,environment=development

# Delete development CIDRs
kubectl delete servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=user,environment=development

# Confirm deletion
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=user
NAME AGE anihpj-user-dev-cidr-1 45d anihpj-user-dev-cidr-2 30d servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-user-dev-cidr-1" deleted servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-user-dev-cidr-2" deleted NAME AGE anihpj-user-services 20d
Example 3: Delete Author Experimental ServiceCIDRs with Dry Run

This example validates bulk deletion of experimental Author ServiceCIDRs before actual removal.

# Test deletion with dry-run
kubectl delete servicecidrs \
  -l app=anihpj,model=author,experimental=true \
  --dry-run=client

# List what would be deleted
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=author,experimental=true

# Proceed with actual deletion if validation passes
kubectl delete servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=author,experimental=true
servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-author-experimental-1" deleted (dry run) servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-author-experimental-2" deleted (dry run) NAME AGE anihpj-author-experimental-1 60d anihpj-author-experimental-2 45d servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-author-experimental-1" deleted servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-author-experimental-2" deleted
Example 4: Delete Deprecated Location Regional ServiceCIDRs

This example removes all deprecated regional ServiceCIDRs for Location services.

# List deprecated Location CIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs \
  -l app=anihpj,model=location,status=deprecated \
  -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,REGION:.metadata.labels.region,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp

# Delete all deprecated Location CIDRs
kubectl delete servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=location,status=deprecated

# Show remaining active Location CIDRs
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=location,status!=deprecated
NAME REGION AGE anihpj-location-services-uswest-old us-west 90d anihpj-location-services-euwest-old eu-west 85d servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-location-services-uswest-old" deleted servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-location-services-euwest-old" deleted NAME AGE anihpj-location-services-useast 15d anihpj-location-services-eucentral 12d
Example 5: Delete Old JobPost ServiceCIDR Versions

This example cleans up old versioned ServiceCIDRs for JobPost, keeping only current versions.

# List all JobPost CIDRs with version labels
kubectl get servicecidrs \
  -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost \
  -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,VERSION:.metadata.labels.version,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp

# Delete old versions (v1, v2)
kubectl delete servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost,version=v1
kubectl delete servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost,version=v2

# Verify only v3 remains
kubectl get servicecidrs -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost
NAME VERSION AGE anihpj-jobpost-services-v1 v1 120d anihpj-jobpost-services-v2 v2 90d anihpj-jobpost-services-v3 v3 30d servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-services-v1" deleted servicecidr.networking.k8s.io "anihpj-jobpost-services-v2" deleted NAME AGE anihpj-jobpost-services-v3 30d

📐 Common Definitions

Common Definitions are fundamental data types and structures shared across all Kubernetes API resources. These definitions provide consistent patterns for metadata, selectors, references, and operational parameters used throughout the Kubernetes API. Understanding these common structures is essential for working with any Kubernetes resource effectively.

DeleteOptions - Options for resource deletion behavior and constraints
LabelSelector - Query mechanism for selecting resources by labels
ListMeta - Metadata for resource lists and collections
LocalObjectReference - Reference to object in same namespace
NodeSelectorRequirement - Node selection criteria with operators
ObjectFieldSelector - Field selector for resource properties
ObjectMeta - Standard metadata for all resources
ObjectReference - Reference to any Kubernetes object
Patch - Partial update operation payload
Quantity - Fixed-point number representation
ResourceFieldSelector - Container resource selector
Status - Standard error and status response
TypedLocalObjectReference - Typed reference within namespace

DeleteOptions

Official Documentation: Kubernetes DeleteOptions

API Group: meta/v1

DeleteOptions may be provided when deleting an API object to control deletion behavior.

Full Description

DeleteOptions provides fine-grained control over resource deletion operations in Kubernetes. When deleting resources, you can specify parameters such as grace period seconds for graceful shutdown, propagation policy for cascading deletes, and dry-run mode for validation. These options are particularly important when deleting resources that have dependencies or require specific cleanup procedures.

The propagation policy determines how dependent resources are handled: Foreground deletion waits for all dependents to be deleted first, Background deletion removes the owner immediately and deletes dependents asynchronously, and Orphan leaves dependents intact. Understanding these options is crucial for safely managing resource lifecycles in production environments, especially for the anihpj Django application's Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost workloads.

Key Fields
  • gracePeriodSeconds: integer - Duration in seconds before the object should be deleted
  • propagationPolicy: string - How garbage collection is performed (Orphan, Background, Foreground)
  • dryRun: array of strings - Indicates modifications should not be persisted
  • preconditions: Preconditions - Must be fulfilled before deletion
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Delete Skills Deployment with Grace Period

This example deletes a Skills deployment with a custom grace period for safe shutdown.

# Delete with 60-second grace period for graceful shutdown
kubectl delete deployment anihpj-skills-api --grace-period=60

# Verify deletion status
kubectl get deployment anihpj-skills-api
deployment.apps "anihpj-skills-api" deleted Error from server (NotFound): deployments.apps "anihpj-skills-api" not found
Example 2: Delete User Service with Foreground Propagation

This example uses foreground propagation to delete User service and wait for all endpoints to clear.

# Delete with foreground propagation (waits for dependents)
kubectl delete service anihpj-user-service --cascade=foreground

# Check if endpoints are cleared
kubectl get endpoints anihpj-user-service
service "anihpj-user-service" deleted Error from server (NotFound): endpoints "anihpj-user-service" not found
Example 3: Delete Author Pods with Dry Run

This example validates Author pod deletion using dry-run without actually deleting.

# Test deletion with dry-run
kubectl delete pod -l app=anihpj,model=author --dry-run=client

# If validation passes, proceed with actual deletion
kubectl delete pod -l app=anihpj,model=author --grace-period=30
pod "anihpj-author-api-7f8b9c5d6-abc12" deleted (dry run) pod "anihpj-author-api-7f8b9c5d6-def34" deleted (dry run) pod "anihpj-author-api-7f8b9c5d6-abc12" deleted pod "anihpj-author-api-7f8b9c5d6-def34" deleted
Example 4: Delete Location ConfigMap with Orphan Policy

This example orphans Location ConfigMap consumers instead of deleting them.

# Delete ConfigMap but orphan dependent pods
kubectl delete configmap anihpj-location-config --cascade=orphan

# Verify pods still running without owner reference
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=location -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.ownerReferences}'
configmap "anihpj-location-config" deleted []
Example 5: Delete JobPost StatefulSet with Preconditions

This example deletes JobPost StatefulSet with immediate grace period after validation.

# Delete immediately (grace-period=0) for quick cleanup
kubectl delete statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers --grace-period=0 --force

# Verify StatefulSet and pods are removed
kubectl get statefulset,pod -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost
Warning: Immediate deletion does not wait for confirmation that the running resource has been terminated. statefulset.apps "anihpj-jobpost-workers" force deleted No resources found in default namespace.

LabelSelector

Official Documentation: Kubernetes LabelSelector

API Group: meta/v1

A label selector is a label query over a set of resources.

Full Description

LabelSelector provides a powerful mechanism for querying and filtering Kubernetes resources based on labels. It supports two types of selectors: matchLabels for exact equality matching and matchExpressions for more complex queries using operators like In, NotIn, Exists, and DoesNotExist. Label selectors are fundamental to Kubernetes' organizational model and are used extensively in services, replica sets, deployments, and network policies.

For the anihpj Django application, label selectors enable precise targeting of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost workloads. You can select resources by model name, environment, version, or any custom labels. This flexibility is essential for implementing blue-green deployments, canary releases, targeted scaling, and network policy enforcement. Mastering label selectors is crucial for effective Kubernetes resource management and operational excellence.

Key Fields
  • matchLabels: map[string]string - Key-value pairs for exact label matching
  • matchExpressions: array of LabelSelectorRequirement - List of label selector requirements with operators
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Select Skills Pods by Exact Labels

This example uses matchLabels to select all Skills API pods in production.

# Get Skills pods with exact label match
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=skills,environment=production

# Show labels for verification
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=skills --show-labels
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-skills-api-7f8b9c5d6-abc12 1/1 Running 0 5h anihpj-skills-api-7f8b9c5d6-def34 1/1 Running 0 5h NAME LABELS anihpj-skills-api-7f8b9c5d6-abc12 app=anihpj,environment=production,model=skills,version=v1 anihpj-skills-api-7f8b9c5d6-def34 app=anihpj,environment=production,model=skills,version=v1
Example 2: Select User Deployments with IN Operator

This example uses matchExpressions with IN operator to select User workloads across multiple environments.

# Select User deployments in staging or production
kubectl get deployments -l 'app=anihpj,model=user,environment in (staging,production)'

# Show deployment details with custom columns
kubectl get deployments -l 'app=anihpj,model=user' \
  -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,ENV:.metadata.labels.environment,REPLICAS:.spec.replicas
NAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGE anihpj-user-api-prod 3/3 3 3 10d anihpj-user-api-staging 2/2 2 2 7d NAME ENV REPLICAS anihpj-user-api-prod production 3 anihpj-user-api-staging staging 2
Example 3: Select Author Services with NOTIN Operator

This example excludes test Author services using NotIn operator.

# Get Author services excluding test environment
kubectl get services -l 'app=anihpj,model=author,environment notin (test,development)'

# Verify services are production and staging only
kubectl get services -l app=anihpj,model=author -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.metadata.labels.environment}{"\n"}{end}'
NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE anihpj-author-api-prod ClusterIP 10.96.100.50 8000/TCP 15d anihpj-author-api-staging ClusterIP 10.96.100.51 8000/TCP 12d anihpj-author-api-prod production anihpj-author-api-staging staging
Example 4: Select Location ConfigMaps with EXISTS Operator

This example selects Location ConfigMaps that have a region label, regardless of value.

# Get Location ConfigMaps with region label (any value)
kubectl get configmaps -l 'app=anihpj,model=location,region'

# Show all labels including region
kubectl get configmaps -l app=anihpj,model=location \
  -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,REGION:.metadata.labels.region,AGE:.metadata.creationTimestamp
NAME DATA AGE anihpj-location-config-useast 5 20d anihpj-location-config-euwest 5 18d anihpj-location-config-apac 5 15d NAME REGION AGE anihpj-location-config-useast us-east 2025-12-19T10:00:00Z anihpj-location-config-euwest eu-west 2025-12-21T14:30:00Z anihpj-location-config-apac asia-pac 2025-12-24T08:15:00Z
Example 5: Complex JobPost Selector with Multiple Expressions

This example combines multiple selector expressions for JobPost worker pods.

# Complex selector: JobPost, production or staging, version v2 or v3
kubectl get pods -l 'app=anihpj,model=jobpost,environment in (production,staging),version in (v2,v3)'

# Use in NetworkPolicy for precise targeting
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-network-policy
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
    matchExpressions:
    - key: environment
      operator: In
      values: [production, staging]
    - key: version
      operator: NotIn
      values: [v1]
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
EOF
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-jobpost-workers-v2-abc12 1/1 Running 0 3d anihpj-jobpost-workers-v3-def34 1/1 Running 0 1d networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-jobpost-network-policy created

ListMeta

Official Documentation: Kubernetes ListMeta

API Group: meta/v1

ListMeta describes metadata that synthetic resources must have, including lists and various status objects.

Full Description

ListMeta provides essential pagination and consistency metadata for resource list operations in Kubernetes. It includes fields like resourceVersion for consistency control, continue token for pagination, and remainingItemCount for result set size information. These fields enable efficient handling of large resource collections by allowing clients to retrieve results in manageable chunks while maintaining consistency.

For the anihpj Django application, ListMeta is crucial when listing large numbers of Skills, User, Author, Location, or JobPost pods, especially in production clusters with hundreds or thousands of resources. The continue token allows you to page through results without missing updates, while resourceVersion ensures you're working with a consistent snapshot. Understanding ListMeta is essential for building scalable CLI tools, dashboards, and automation scripts that interact with Kubernetes resources.

Key Fields
  • resourceVersion: string - Version of the resource list for consistency
  • continue: string - Token for retrieving next page of results
  • remainingItemCount: integer - Approximate number of items remaining
  • selfLink: string - Deprecated URL representing this object
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: List Skills Pods with Resource Version

This example lists Skills pods and captures the resource version for consistency.

# Get Skills pods and show resource version
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=skills -o jsonpath='{.metadata.resourceVersion}'

# Watch from specific resource version for new changes
RESOURCE_VERSION=$(kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=skills -o jsonpath='{.metadata.resourceVersion}')
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=skills --watch --resource-version=$RESOURCE_VERSION
123456 NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-skills-api-7f8b9c5d6-abc12 1/1 Running 0 5h
Example 2: Paginate User Pods with Limit and Continue

This example paginates through User pods using limit and continue token.

# Get first page with limit=2
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=user --limit=2 -o json | \
  jq '{continue: .metadata.continue, items: [.items[].metadata.name]}'

# Get next page using continue token
CONTINUE_TOKEN=$(kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=user --limit=2 -o jsonpath='{.metadata.continue}')
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=user --limit=2 --continue=$CONTINUE_TOKEN
{ "continue": "eyJ2IjoibWV0YS5rOHMuaW8vdjEiLCJydiI6MTIzNDU2fQ", "items": [ "anihpj-user-api-abc12", "anihpj-user-api-def34" ] } NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-user-api-ghi56 1/1 Running 0 4h anihpj-user-api-jkl78 1/1 Running 0 4h
Example 3: Check Author Deployment List Metadata

This example extracts complete ListMeta for Author deployments.

# Get full list metadata
kubectl get deployments -l app=anihpj,model=author -o json | \
  jq '.metadata | {resourceVersion, remainingItemCount, continue}'

# Show resource version for caching
kubectl get deployments -l app=anihpj,model=author -o jsonpath='{.metadata.resourceVersion}'
{ "resourceVersion": "789012", "remainingItemCount": null, "continue": "" } 789012
Example 4: List Location Services with Remaining Count

This example shows remaining item count when listing Location services with pagination.

# List with limit to see remaining count
kubectl get services -l app=anihpj,model=location --limit=5 -o json | \
  jq '.metadata | {itemCount: (.items | length), remainingItemCount, continue}'

# Get total count by listing all
kubectl get services -l app=anihpj,model=location --no-headers | wc -l
{ "itemCount": 5, "remainingItemCount": 3, "continue": "eyJ2IjoibWV0YS5rOHMuaW8vdjEiLCJydiI6OTAxMjM0fQ" } 8
Example 5: Watch JobPost Pods from Resource Version

This example uses ListMeta resource version to watch JobPost pods efficiently.

# Get current resource version
RV=$(kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost -o jsonpath='{.metadata.resourceVersion}')
echo "Starting watch from resource version: $RV"

# Watch for changes starting from this version
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost --watch --resource-version=$RV --output-watch-events
Starting watch from resource version: 345678 ADDED anihpj-jobpost-workers-mno90 1/1 Running 0 1s MODIFIED anihpj-jobpost-workers-pqr12 1/1 Running 1 2s

LocalObjectReference

Official Documentation: Kubernetes LocalObjectReference

API Group: core/v1

LocalObjectReference contains enough information to let you locate the referenced object inside the same namespace.

Full Description

LocalObjectReference is a lightweight reference structure used to point to another Kubernetes object within the same namespace. Unlike ObjectReference, it only contains the object's name field, making it simpler and more efficient when the namespace context is already known. This type is commonly used in resource specifications where cross-namespace references are not needed, such as referencing Secrets in Pod volumes, ConfigMaps in environment variables, and ServiceAccounts in Pod specifications.

For the anihpj Django application, LocalObjectReference is extensively used to connect Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost workloads with their configuration resources. For example, a Skills API deployment might reference a Secret for database credentials, or a User service might reference a ConfigMap for application settings. Understanding how to properly construct and use LocalObjectReference is fundamental to building secure, maintainable Kubernetes configurations.

Key Fields
  • name: string - Name of the referenced object in the same namespace
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Reference Skills Secret in Deployment

This example references a Skills database secret using LocalObjectReference in a deployment.

# Create Skills database secret
kubectl create secret generic anihpj-skills-db-secret \
  --from-literal=db-password=skills_secure_pass_123

# Create deployment referencing the secret
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: skills
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1
        env:
        - name: DB_PASSWORD
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-skills-db-secret  # LocalObjectReference
              key: db-password
EOF

# Verify secret reference
kubectl describe deployment anihpj-skills-api | grep -A 3 "Environment"
secret/anihpj-skills-db-secret created deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api created Environment: DB_PASSWORD: Optional: false
Example 2: Reference User ConfigMap in Pod

This example mounts a User ConfigMap as a volume using LocalObjectReference.

# Create User configuration ConfigMap
kubectl create configmap anihpj-user-config \
  --from-literal=app-settings='{"max_users": 10000, "cache_ttl": 300}'

# Create pod with ConfigMap volume reference
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api-pod
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
spec:
  containers:
  - name: user-api
    image: anihpj/user-api:v1
    volumeMounts:
    - name: config-volume
      mountPath: /etc/config
  volumes:
  - name: config-volume
    configMap:
      name: anihpj-user-config  # LocalObjectReference
EOF

# Verify ConfigMap is mounted
kubectl exec anihpj-user-api-pod -- ls /etc/config
configmap/anihpj-user-config created pod/anihpj-user-api-pod created app-settings
Example 3: Reference Author ServiceAccount

This example assigns an Author ServiceAccount to pods using LocalObjectReference.

# Create Author ServiceAccount with specific permissions
kubectl create serviceaccount anihpj-author-sa

# Create deployment using the ServiceAccount
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: author
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: author
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: anihpj-author-sa  # LocalObjectReference
      containers:
      - name: author-api
        image: anihpj/author-api:v1
EOF

# Verify ServiceAccount is assigned
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=author \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[0].spec.serviceAccountName}'
serviceaccount/anihpj-author-sa created deployment.apps/anihpj-author-api created anihpj-author-sa
Example 4: Reference Location PersistentVolumeClaim

This example references a Location PVC in a StatefulSet using LocalObjectReference.

# Create PVC for Location data
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-data-pvc
spec:
  accessModes: [ReadWriteOnce]
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 5Gi
EOF

# Create StatefulSet referencing the PVC
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-api
spec:
  serviceName: location-api
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: location
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: location
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: location-api
        image: anihpj/location-api:v1
        volumeMounts:
        - name: location-data
          mountPath: /data
      volumes:
      - name: location-data
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: anihpj-location-data-pvc  # LocalObjectReference
EOF

# Verify PVC is bound
kubectl get pvc anihpj-location-data-pvc
persistentvolumeclaim/anihpj-location-data-pvc created statefulset.apps/anihpj-location-api created NAME STATUS VOLUME CAPACITY ACCESS MODES anihpj-location-data-pvc Bound pvc-a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890 5Gi RWO
Example 5: Reference JobPost Image Pull Secret

This example uses LocalObjectReference for private registry authentication in JobPost deployments.

# Create image pull secret for private registry
kubectl create secret docker-registry anihpj-registry-secret \
  --docker-server=registry.anihpj.com \
  --docker-username=anihpj-user \
  --docker-password=secure_token_123 \
  --docker-email=deploy@anihpj.com

# Create deployment with image pull secret reference
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      imagePullSecrets:
      - name: anihpj-registry-secret  # LocalObjectReference
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: registry.anihpj.com/jobpost-worker:v2
EOF

# Verify image pull secret is referenced
kubectl describe deployment anihpj-jobpost-workers | grep "Image Pull Secrets"
secret/anihpj-registry-secret created deployment.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created Image Pull Secrets: anihpj-registry-secret

NodeSelectorRequirement

Official Documentation: Kubernetes NodeSelectorRequirement

API Group: core/v1

A node selector requirement is a selector that contains values, a key, and an operator that relates the key and values.

Full Description

NodeSelectorRequirement defines rules for selecting nodes based on their labels using operators like In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt (greater than), and Lt (less than). This structure is used in node affinity and node selector terms to control pod placement on specific nodes. The key field specifies the node label to evaluate, the operator defines how to compare, and the values array contains the comparison values (required for In, NotIn, Gt, Lt operators).
