For the anihpj Django application, NodeSelectorRequirement enables precise control over where Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost pods run. You can schedule workloads on nodes with specific hardware (GPU, SSD), in particular zones or regions, or with specific capacity characteristics. This is essential for performance optimization, cost management, and compliance requirements in production environments.

Key Fields
  • key: string (required) - Label key that the selector applies to
  • operator: string (required) - Operator to use (In, NotIn, Exists, DoesNotExist, Gt, Lt)
  • values: array of strings - Values for comparison (required for In, NotIn, Gt, Lt)
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Schedule Skills Pods on SSD Nodes

This example uses NodeSelectorRequirement to schedule Skills API on nodes with SSD storage.

# Create deployment with node affinity for SSD
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: skills
    spec:
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
            - matchExpressions:
              - key: disktype
                operator: In
                values:
                - ssd
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1
EOF

# Verify pods are scheduled on SSD nodes
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=skills -o wide
deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api created NAME READY STATUS NODE anihpj-skills-api-abc12 1/1 Running node-ssd-1 anihpj-skills-api-def34 1/1 Running node-ssd-2 anihpj-skills-api-ghi56 1/1 Running node-ssd-1
Example 2: Schedule User Pods in Specific Zones

This example schedules User services in us-east-1a or us-east-1b zones.

# Deploy User API with zone affinity
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
spec:
  replicas: 4
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: user
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: user
    spec:
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          preferredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
          - weight: 100
            preference:
              matchExpressions:
              - key: topology.kubernetes.io/zone
                operator: In
                values:
                - us-east-1a
                - us-east-1b
      containers:
      - name: user-api
        image: anihpj/user-api:v1
EOF

# Check distribution across zones
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=user -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.nodeName}{"\n"}{end}'
deployment.apps/anihpj-user-api created anihpj-user-api-jkl78 node-useast1a-1 anihpj-user-api-mno90 node-useast1b-2 anihpj-user-api-pqr12 node-useast1a-3 anihpj-user-api-stu34 node-useast1b-1
Example 3: Exclude Author Pods from Spot Instances

This example prevents Author workloads from running on spot/preemptible nodes.

# Deploy Author API avoiding spot instances
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-api
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: author
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: author
    spec:
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
            - matchExpressions:
              - key: node.kubernetes.io/instance-type
                operator: NotIn
                values:
                - spot
                - preemptible
      containers:
      - name: author-api
        image: anihpj/author-api:v1
EOF

# Verify nodes are not spot instances
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=author -o wide
deployment.apps/anihpj-author-api created NAME READY STATUS NODE anihpj-author-api-vwx56 1/1 Running node-ondemand-1 anihpj-author-api-yz890 1/1 Running node-ondemand-2
Example 4: Schedule Location Pods on GPU Nodes

This example uses Exists operator to schedule Location services on nodes with GPU.

# Deploy Location API with GPU requirement
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-ml-api
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: location
      tier: ml
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: location
        tier: ml
    spec:
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
            - matchExpressions:
              - key: accelerator
                operator: Exists
      containers:
      - name: location-ml-api
        image: anihpj/location-ml-api:v1
        resources:
          limits:
            nvidia.com/gpu: 1
EOF

# Check GPU node assignment
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=location,tier=ml -o wide
deployment.apps/anihpj-location-ml-api created NAME READY STATUS NODE anihpj-location-ml-api-abc12 1/1 Running node-gpu-p100-1 anihpj-location-ml-api-def34 1/1 Running node-gpu-v100-2
Example 5: Schedule JobPost Workers on High-Memory Nodes

This example uses Gt operator to schedule JobPost workers on nodes with sufficient memory.

# Deploy JobPost workers requiring high memory nodes
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      affinity:
        nodeAffinity:
          requiredDuringSchedulingIgnoredDuringExecution:
            nodeSelectorTerms:
            - matchExpressions:
              - key: node.kubernetes.io/memory-gb
                operator: Gt
                values:
                - "32"
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "8Gi"
EOF

# Verify nodes meet memory requirement
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost -o wide
deployment.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created NAME READY STATUS NODE anihpj-jobpost-workers-ghi56 1/1 Running node-64gb-1 anihpj-jobpost-workers-jkl78 1/1 Running node-128gb-2 anihpj-jobpost-workers-mno90 1/1 Running node-64gb-3

ObjectFieldSelector

Official Documentation: Kubernetes ObjectFieldSelector

API Group: core/v1

ObjectFieldSelector selects an APIVersioned field of an object.

Full Description

ObjectFieldSelector provides a mechanism to reference specific fields from the Kubernetes API object itself, commonly used in the Downward API to expose pod and container metadata to containers as environment variables or files. It allows containers to access information like pod name, namespace, labels, annotations, node name, and service account without making API calls. The fieldPath specifies which field to select using a dot-notation path (e.g., metadata.name, metadata.namespace).

For the anihpj Django application, ObjectFieldSelector is invaluable for injecting runtime context into Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost containers. Applications can discover their pod identity, namespace, node assignment, and labels without requiring Kubernetes API permissions. This enables better logging, monitoring, service discovery, and dynamic configuration based on the deployment context.

Key Fields
  • apiVersion: string - API version of the object field (defaults to v1)
  • fieldPath: string (required) - Path to the field to select (e.g., metadata.name, metadata.namespace)
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Inject Skills Pod Name and Namespace

This example exposes Skills pod name and namespace as environment variables.

# Create deployment with pod metadata exposed
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: skills
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1
        env:
        - name: POD_NAME
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.name
        - name: POD_NAMESPACE
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.namespace
        - name: POD_IP
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: status.podIP
EOF

# Verify environment variables are set
kubectl exec deployment/anihpj-skills-api -- env | grep POD_
deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api created POD_NAME=anihpj-skills-api-abc12 POD_NAMESPACE=default POD_IP=10.244.1.15
Example 2: Inject User Pod Labels

This example exposes User pod labels for dynamic configuration.

# Deploy User API with labels exposed
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: user
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: user
        environment: production
        version: v2
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: user-api
        image: anihpj/user-api:v2
        env:
        - name: APP_LABEL
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.labels['app']
        - name: MODEL_LABEL
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.labels['model']
        - name: VERSION_LABEL
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.labels['version']
EOF

# Check label injection
kubectl exec deployment/anihpj-user-api -- printenv | grep _LABEL
deployment.apps/anihpj-user-api created APP_LABEL=anihpj MODEL_LABEL=user VERSION_LABEL=v2
Example 3: Inject Author Node Information

This example exposes Author pod's node name and host IP for monitoring.

# Deploy Author API with node info
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-api
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: author
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: author
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: author-api
        image: anihpj/author-api:v1
        env:
        - name: NODE_NAME
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: spec.nodeName
        - name: HOST_IP
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: status.hostIP
        - name: SERVICE_ACCOUNT
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: spec.serviceAccountName
EOF

# Verify node information is accessible
kubectl exec deployment/anihpj-author-api -- sh -c 'echo "Node: $NODE_NAME, Host IP: $HOST_IP"'
deployment.apps/anihpj-author-api created Node: node-worker-3, Host IP: 192.168.1.103
Example 4: Write Location Pod Metadata to Files

This example writes Location pod metadata to files using Downward API volumes.

# Deploy Location API with metadata volume
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    tier: api
spec:
  containers:
  - name: location-api
    image: anihpj/location-api:v1
    volumeMounts:
    - name: podinfo
      mountPath: /etc/podinfo
  volumes:
  - name: podinfo
    downwardAPI:
      items:
      - path: "name"
        fieldRef:
          fieldPath: metadata.name
      - path: "namespace"
        fieldRef:
          fieldPath: metadata.namespace
      - path: "labels"
        fieldRef:
          fieldPath: metadata.labels
      - path: "annotations"
        fieldRef:
          fieldPath: metadata.annotations
EOF

# Read metadata from files
kubectl exec anihpj-location-api -- cat /etc/podinfo/name
kubectl exec anihpj-location-api -- cat /etc/podinfo/labels
pod/anihpj-location-api created anihpj-location-api app="anihpj" model="location" tier="api"
Example 5: Inject JobPost Pod UID and Creation Time

This example exposes JobPost pod UID and creation timestamp for tracking.

# Deploy JobPost workers with UID tracking
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
spec:
  replicas: 4
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1
        env:
        - name: POD_UID
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.uid
        - name: POD_NAME
          valueFrom:
            fieldRef:
              fieldPath: metadata.name
        command: ["sh", "-c"]
        args:
        - |
          echo "JobPost Worker started"
          echo "Pod UID: $POD_UID"
          echo "Pod Name: $POD_NAME"
          # Start application
          /app/start-worker.sh
EOF

# Check logs to verify UID injection
kubectl logs deployment/anihpj-jobpost-workers --tail=5
deployment.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created JobPost Worker started Pod UID: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-1234-567890abcdef Pod Name: anihpj-jobpost-workers-pqr56 Starting worker process...

ObjectMeta

Official Documentation: Kubernetes ObjectMeta

API Group: meta/v1

ObjectMeta is metadata that all persisted resources must have, which includes all objects users must create.

Full Description

ObjectMeta is the foundational metadata structure present in every Kubernetes resource. It contains essential identification and organizational fields including name, namespace, labels, annotations, uid, resourceVersion, generation, creationTimestamp, and ownerReferences. Labels enable resource selection and grouping, while annotations store arbitrary non-identifying metadata. The UID provides a unique identifier across space and time, and resourceVersion enables optimistic concurrency control.

For the anihpj Django application, properly structuring ObjectMeta is critical for organizing Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost resources. Labels like app, model, environment, and version enable precise queries and service discovery. Annotations can store deployment metadata, configuration hashes, or external tool information. Understanding ObjectMeta fields is fundamental to effective Kubernetes resource management, automation, and troubleshooting.

Key Fields
  • name: string - Unique name within a namespace
  • namespace: string - Namespace containing the object
  • labels: map[string]string - Labels for organization and selection
  • annotations: map[string]string - Non-identifying metadata
  • uid: string - Unique identifier across space and time
  • resourceVersion: string - Version for optimistic concurrency
  • generation: integer - Sequence number for spec updates
  • creationTimestamp: Time - Creation time
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Create Skills Deployment with Complete Metadata

This example demonstrates comprehensive ObjectMeta structure for Skills deployment.

# Create Skills deployment with rich metadata
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
  namespace: default
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    environment: production
    version: v1
    tier: backend
  annotations:
    description: "Skills API for job matching and recommendations"
    owner: "platform-team@anihpj.com"
    deployment.kubernetes.io/revision: "5"
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port: "9090"
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: skills
        environment: production
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1
EOF

# View complete metadata
kubectl get deployment anihpj-skills-api -o yaml | grep -A 20 metadata:
deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api created metadata: annotations: deployment.kubernetes.io/revision: "5" description: Skills API for job matching and recommendations owner: platform-team@anihpj.com prometheus.io/port: "9090" prometheus.io/scrape: "true" creationTimestamp: "2026-01-08T10:00:00Z" generation: 1 labels: app: anihpj environment: production model: skills tier: backend version: v1 name: anihpj-skills-api namespace: default resourceVersion: "123456" uid: a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-1234-567890abcdef
Example 2: Query User Resources by Labels

This example uses ObjectMeta labels to query and filter User resources.

# List all User pods with specific labels
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=user,environment=production

# Get User services by model label
kubectl get services -l model=user --show-labels

# Complex query with multiple label conditions
kubectl get all -l 'app=anihpj,model=user,version in (v1,v2)' -o wide
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-user-api-prod-abc12 1/1 Running 0 5h anihpj-user-api-prod-def34 1/1 Running 0 5h NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP LABELS anihpj-user-service ClusterIP 10.96.100.20 app=anihpj,model=user,version=v2 NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE IP NODE pod/anihpj-user-api-v2-ghi56 1/1 Running 0 3h 10.244.1.20 node-1
Example 3: Use Author Annotations for Configuration

This example stores Author deployment configuration in annotations.

# Create Author deployment with configuration annotations
kubectl create deployment anihpj-author-api --image=anihpj/author-api:v1

# Add annotations for configuration tracking
kubectl annotate deployment anihpj-author-api \
  config-hash="a1b2c3d4e5f6" \
  last-updated="2026-01-08T10:30:00Z" \
  updated-by="deployment-bot" \
  rollout-strategy="canary"

# Retrieve annotations
kubectl get deployment anihpj-author-api -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}' | jq

# Use annotations in automation scripts
CONFIG_HASH=$(kubectl get deployment anihpj-author-api -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations.config-hash}')
echo "Current config hash: $CONFIG_HASH"
deployment.apps/anihpj-author-api created deployment.apps/anihpj-author-api annotated { "config-hash": "a1b2c3d4e5f6", "deployment.kubernetes.io/revision": "1", "last-updated": "2026-01-08T10:30:00Z", "rollout-strategy": "canary", "updated-by": "deployment-bot" } Current config hash: a1b2c3d4e5f6
Example 4: Track Location Resource Versions

This example uses resourceVersion for optimistic concurrency control with Location resources.

# Get current resourceVersion
kubectl get configmap anihpj-location-config \
  -o jsonpath='{.metadata.resourceVersion}'

# Watch for changes from specific version
RV=$(kubectl get configmap anihpj-location-config -o jsonpath='{.metadata.resourceVersion}')
echo "Watching from resourceVersion: $RV"
kubectl get configmap anihpj-location-config --watch --resource-version=$RV

# Check generation for spec changes
kubectl get deployment anihpj-location-api \
  -o jsonpath='Generation: {.metadata.generation}, ObservedGeneration: {.status.observedGeneration}'
567890 Watching from resourceVersion: 567890 NAME DATA AGE anihpj-location-config 3 10m anihpj-location-config 4 10m (MODIFIED) Generation: 5, ObservedGeneration: 5
Example 5: Use JobPost OwnerReferences for Cleanup

This example demonstrates ownerReferences in ObjectMeta for garbage collection.

# Create JobPost StatefulSet
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1
EOF

# Check ownerReferences on pods
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.ownerReferences}' | jq

# Delete StatefulSet (cascade delete pods)
kubectl delete statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers

# Verify pods are deleted automatically
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created [ { "apiVersion": "apps/v1", "blockOwnerDeletion": true, "controller": true, "kind": "StatefulSet", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-workers", "uid": "b2c3d4e5-f6g7-8901-2345-678901bcdefg" } ] statefulset.apps "anihpj-jobpost-workers" deleted No resources found in default namespace.

ObjectReference

Official Documentation: Kubernetes ObjectReference

API Group: core/v1

ObjectReference contains enough information to let you inspect or modify the referred object.

Full Description

ObjectReference provides a complete reference to any Kubernetes object, including fields for kind, namespace, name, uid, apiVersion, resourceVersion, and fieldPath. Unlike LocalObjectReference which only contains a name, ObjectReference can point to objects in different namespaces and different API groups. It's commonly used in Events to reference the object that triggered the event, in OwnerReferences for garbage collection, and in various controllers to track related resources.

For the anihpj Django application, ObjectReference is essential for establishing relationships between Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost resources. Events generated by your workloads reference the pods or deployments that caused them. Horizontal Pod Autoscalers reference the deployments they scale. Understanding ObjectReference is crucial for debugging, tracing resource relationships, and implementing custom controllers.

Key Fields
  • kind: string - Kind of the referenced object
  • namespace: string - Namespace of the referenced object
  • name: string - Name of the referenced object
  • uid: string - UID of the referenced object
  • apiVersion: string - API version of the referenced object
  • resourceVersion: string - Resource version of the referenced object
  • fieldPath: string - Path to a specific field in the object
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: View Skills Pod Events with References

This example shows how events reference Skills pods using ObjectReference.

# Get Skills pod events
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-skills-api-abc12

# View event details with object reference
kubectl get events -o json | \
  jq '.items[] | select(.involvedObject.name | contains("skills")) | 
  {message: .message, object: .involvedObject}'
LAST SEEN TYPE REASON OBJECT MESSAGE 5m Normal Scheduled pod/anihpj-skills-api-abc12 Successfully assigned to node-1 5m Normal Pulling pod/anihpj-skills-api-abc12 Pulling image "anihpj/skills-api:v1" 5m Normal Pulled pod/anihpj-skills-api-abc12 Successfully pulled image 4m Normal Started pod/anihpj-skills-api-abc12 Started container skills-api { "message": "Successfully assigned to node-1", "object": { "kind": "Pod", "namespace": "default", "name": "anihpj-skills-api-abc12", "uid": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890", "apiVersion": "v1", "resourceVersion": "123456" } }
Example 2: Check User HPA Object Reference

This example shows how Horizontal Pod Autoscaler references User deployment.

# Create HPA for User API
kubectl autoscale deployment anihpj-user-api --cpu-percent=70 --min=2 --max=10

# View HPA with object reference
kubectl get hpa anihpj-user-api -o yaml | grep -A 10 "scaleTargetRef:"

# Get referenced deployment details
kubectl get hpa anihpj-user-api -o jsonpath='{.spec.scaleTargetRef}' | jq
horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/anihpj-user-api autoscaled scaleTargetRef: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: anihpj-user-api minReplicas: 2 maxReplicas: 10 targetCPUUtilizationPercentage: 70 { "apiVersion": "apps/v1", "kind": "Deployment", "name": "anihpj-user-api" }
Example 3: Examine Author Pod Owner References

This example shows how Author pods reference their owning ReplicaSet.

# Get Author pod owner references
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=author \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.ownerReferences}' | jq

# Verify owner ReplicaSet exists
RS_NAME=$(kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=author \
  -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.ownerReferences[0].name}')
kubectl get replicaset $RS_NAME
[ { "apiVersion": "apps/v1", "blockOwnerDeletion": true, "controller": true, "kind": "ReplicaSet", "name": "anihpj-author-api-7f8b9c5d6", "uid": "c3d4e5f6-g7h8-9012-3456-789012cdefgh" } ] NAME DESIRED CURRENT READY AGE anihpj-author-api-7f8b9c5d6 2 2 2 10m
Example 4: Create Location PodDisruptionBudget with Reference

This example creates a PDB that references Location deployment using ObjectReference.

# Create PodDisruptionBudget for Location API
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-api-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: location
EOF

# View PDB status with observed generation
kubectl get pdb anihpj-location-api-pdb -o yaml | grep -A 5 "status:"

# Check which pods are covered
kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=location --show-labels
poddisruptionbudget.policy/anihpj-location-api-pdb created status: currentHealthy: 3 desiredHealthy: 2 disruptionsAllowed: 1 expectedPods: 3 observedGeneration: 1 NAME READY STATUS LABELS anihpj-location-api-abc12 1/1 Running app=anihpj,model=location,version=v1 anihpj-location-api-def34 1/1 Running app=anihpj,model=location,version=v1 anihpj-location-api-ghi56 1/1 Running app=anihpj,model=location,version=v1
Example 5: Trace JobPost ReplicaSet to Deployment

This example follows ObjectReference chain from JobPost pod to deployment.

# Get JobPost pod
POD=$(kubectl get pods -l app=anihpj,model=jobpost -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}')

# Get owning ReplicaSet from pod
RS=$(kubectl get pod $POD -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences[0].name}')

# Get owning Deployment from ReplicaSet
DEPLOY=$(kubectl get replicaset $RS -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences[0].name}')

echo "Reference chain: Pod -> $RS -> $DEPLOY"

# Verify complete chain
kubectl get pod $POD -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences}' | jq
kubectl get rs $RS -o jsonpath='{.metadata.ownerReferences}' | jq
Reference chain: Pod -> anihpj-jobpost-workers-6d7c8b9f5 -> anihpj-jobpost-workers [ { "apiVersion": "apps/v1", "blockOwnerDeletion": true, "controller": true, "kind": "ReplicaSet", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-workers-6d7c8b9f5", "uid": "d4e5f6g7-h8i9-0123-4567-890123defghi" } ] [ { "apiVersion": "apps/v1", "blockOwnerDeletion": true, "controller": true, "kind": "Deployment", "name": "anihpj-jobpost-workers", "uid": "e5f6g7h8-i9j0-1234-5678-901234efghij" } ]

Patch

Official Documentation: Kubernetes Patch

API Group: meta/v1

Patch is provided to give a concrete name and type to the Kubernetes PATCH request body.

Full Description

Patch represents a partial update operation on a Kubernetes resource. Unlike PUT operations which replace the entire resource, PATCH allows selective updates to specific fields. Kubernetes supports three patch strategies: Strategic Merge Patch (smart field merging), JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7386), and JSON Patch (RFC 6902). Strategic Merge Patch is the default and most commonly used, intelligently merging arrays and maps based on resource-specific merge keys.

For the anihpj Django application, patch operations enable efficient updates to Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost resources without replacing entire configurations. You can add labels, update annotations, modify specific container images, or adjust replica counts without touching other fields. This reduces risk, improves performance, and enables atomic updates in production environments.

Patch Types
  • application/strategic-merge-patch+json: Strategic merge with smart field handling
  • application/merge-patch+json: JSON Merge Patch (RFC 7386)
  • application/json-patch+json: JSON Patch (RFC 6902)
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Patch Skills Deployment Image

This example updates Skills container image using strategic merge patch.

# Patch Skills deployment to update image version
kubectl patch deployment anihpj-skills-api \
  --type='strategic' \
  -p '{
    "spec": {
      "template": {
        "spec": {
          "containers": [{
            "name": "skills-api",
            "image": "anihpj/skills-api:v2"
          }]
        }
      }
    }
  }'

# Verify image update
kubectl get deployment anihpj-skills-api \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.spec.containers[0].image}'
deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api patched anihpj/skills-api:v2
Example 2: Patch User Deployment Replicas

This example scales User deployment using JSON merge patch.

# Scale User deployment with merge patch
kubectl patch deployment anihpj-user-api \
  --type='merge' \
  -p '{"spec":{"replicas":5}}'

# Check replica count
kubectl get deployment anihpj-user-api \
  -o jsonpath='Replicas: {.spec.replicas}, Ready: {.status.readyReplicas}'
deployment.apps/anihpj-user-api patched Replicas: 5, Ready: 5
Example 3: Patch Author Service Labels with JSON Patch

This example adds labels to Author service using JSON Patch operations.

# Add labels using JSON Patch
kubectl patch service anihpj-author-service \
  --type='json' \
  -p '[
    {"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/labels/monitoring", "value": "enabled"},
    {"op": "add", "path": "/metadata/labels/tier", "value": "backend"}
  ]'

# Verify labels added
kubectl get service anihpj-author-service --show-labels
service/anihpj-author-service patched NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP LABELS anihpj-author-service ClusterIP 10.96.100.30 app=anihpj,model=author,monitoring=enabled,tier=backend
Example 4: Patch Location ConfigMap Data

This example updates Location ConfigMap configuration using strategic merge.

# Update ConfigMap with new configuration
kubectl patch configmap anihpj-location-config \
  --type='strategic' \
  -p '{
    "data": {
      "max-locations": "50000",
      "cache-enabled": "true"
    }
  }'

# View updated configuration
kubectl get configmap anihpj-location-config -o jsonpath='{.data}' | jq
configmap/anihpj-location-config patched { "cache-enabled": "true", "max-locations": "50000", "region": "us-east-1" }
Example 5: Patch JobPost StatefulSet with Multiple Changes

This example patches JobPost StatefulSet with resource limits and annotations.

# Complex patch with resources and metadata
kubectl patch statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers \
  --type='strategic' \
  -p '{
    "metadata": {
      "annotations": {
        "last-patched": "2026-01-11T11:00:00Z",
        "patch-reason": "resource-optimization"
      }
    },
    "spec": {
      "template": {
        "spec": {
          "containers": [{
            "name": "jobpost-worker",
            "resources": {
              "requests": {
                "cpu": "500m",
                "memory": "1Gi"
              },
              "limits": {
                "cpu": "1000m",
                "memory": "2Gi"
              }
            }
          }]
        }
      }
    }
  }'

# Verify patch applied
kubectl describe statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers | grep -A 5 "Limits\|Annotations"
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers patched Annotations: last-patched: 2026-01-11T11:00:00Z patch-reason: resource-optimization Limits: cpu: 1000m memory: 2Gi Requests: cpu: 500m memory: 1Gi

Quantity

Official Documentation: Kubernetes Quantity

API Group: resource

Quantity is a fixed-point representation of a number.

Full Description

Quantity represents resource amounts using decimal SI units (m, K, M, G, T, P, E) or binary IEC units (Ki, Mi, Gi, Ti, Pi, Ei) for precise resource specification. It's used extensively for CPU, memory, storage, and custom resource quantities throughout Kubernetes. CPU can be expressed in millicores (m) where 1000m = 1 CPU, while memory and storage use bytes with appropriate suffixes. Quantities are designed to avoid floating-point arithmetic issues and ensure consistent resource calculations.

For the anihpj Django application, understanding Quantity is essential for properly configuring resource requests and limits for Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost workloads. Correct quantity specifications ensure optimal pod scheduling, prevent resource starvation, enable quality of service guarantees, and maintain cluster stability. Misconfigurations can lead to pods failing to schedule or experiencing OOM kills.

Common Formats
  • CPU: 100m (0.1 CPU), 500m (0.5 CPU), 1 (1 CPU), 2.5 (2.5 CPUs)
  • Memory (SI): 128M (128 megabytes), 1G (1 gigabyte), 2T (2 terabytes)
  • Memory (IEC): 128Mi (128 mebibytes), 1Gi (1 gibibyte), 2Ti (2 tebibytes)
  • Storage: 10Gi (10 gibibytes), 100Gi (100 gibibytes), 1Ti (1 tebibyte)
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Set Skills Pod Resource Requests and Limits

This example configures appropriate resource quantities for Skills API pods.

# Create Skills deployment with resource specifications
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: skills
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "250m"
            memory: "512Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1000m"
            memory: "1Gi"
EOF

# Verify resource configuration
kubectl describe deployment anihpj-skills-api | grep -A 6 "Limits:"
deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api created Limits: cpu: 1 memory: 1Gi Requests: cpu: 250m memory: 512Mi
Example 2: Configure User Pod with High Memory

This example sets high memory quantities for User service pods.

# Deploy User API with high memory
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api-highmem
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
spec:
  containers:
  - name: user-api
    image: anihpj/user-api:v1
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "500m"
        memory: "4Gi"
      limits:
        cpu: "2000m"
        memory: "8Gi"
EOF

# Check resource allocation
kubectl get pod anihpj-user-api-highmem \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.containers[0].resources}' | jq
pod/anihpj-user-api-highmem created { "limits": { "cpu": "2", "memory": "8Gi" }, "requests": { "cpu": "500m", "memory": "4Gi" } }
Example 3: Create Author PVC with Storage Quantity

This example specifies storage quantity for Author content PersistentVolumeClaim.

# Create PVC with specific storage quantity
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-content-pvc
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 50Gi
EOF

# Verify storage request
kubectl get pvc anihpj-author-content-pvc \
  -o jsonpath='Storage: {.spec.resources.requests.storage}, Status: {.status.phase}'
persistentvolumeclaim/anihpj-author-content-pvc created Storage: 50Gi, Status: Bound
Example 4: Set Location Pod CPU Millicore Precision

This example uses precise millicore values for Location service CPU allocation.

# Deploy Location API with precise CPU allocation
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-api
spec:
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: location
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: location
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: location-api
        image: anihpj/location-api:v1
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "150m"
            memory: "256Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "750m"
            memory: "512Mi"
EOF

# Calculate total CPU usage
kubectl get deployment anihpj-location-api \
  -o jsonpath='Replicas: {.spec.replicas}, CPU: {.spec.template.spec.containers[0].resources.requests.cpu}'
deployment.apps/anihpj-location-api created Replicas: 5, CPU: 150m
Example 5: Configure JobPost Workers with Resource Quantities

This example sets comprehensive resource quantities for JobPost worker pods.

# Create JobPost workers with detailed specs
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "300m"
            memory: "768Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "1200m"
            memory: "2Gi"
EOF

# View resource summary
kubectl describe statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers | grep -A 6 "Limits:"
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created Limits: cpu: 1200m memory: 2Gi Requests: cpu: 300m memory: 768Mi

ResourceFieldSelector

Official Documentation: Kubernetes ResourceFieldSelector

API Group: core/v1

ResourceFieldSelector represents container resources (cpu, memory) that will be available in the container.

Full Description

ResourceFieldSelector enables exposing container resource information to applications via the Downward API. This allows containers to access their own resource requests and limits dynamically at runtime, enabling applications to adapt their behavior based on allocated resources. The selector specifies which container and which resource field (requests.cpu, requests.memory, limits.cpu, limits.memory) to expose, with an optional divisor to convert the value to a different unit (e.g., memory in megabytes instead of bytes).

For the anihpj Django application, ResourceFieldSelector is particularly useful for implementing adaptive scaling behaviors where Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services can adjust worker counts, cache sizes, or connection pools based on their allocated memory and CPU. This enables more efficient resource utilization and prevents applications from exceeding their resource limits. Common use cases include setting Java heap sizes, configuring worker pool sizes, and implementing dynamic rate limiting.

Key Fields
  • containerName (string): Name of the container. Required for requests and limits.
  • resource (string): Required resource to select. Valid values: "limits.cpu", "limits.memory", "requests.cpu", "requests.memory".
  • divisor (Quantity): Specifies the output format of the resource. Optional.
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Expose Skills Container CPU Limit

This example exposes the Skills container's CPU limit as an environment variable.

# Create Skills pod with CPU limit exposed
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
spec:
  containers:
  - name: skills-api
    image: anihpj/skills-api:v1
    resources:
      requests:
        cpu: "500m"
      limits:
        cpu: "2000m"
    env:
    - name: SKILLS_CPU_LIMIT
      valueFrom:
        resourceFieldRef:
          containerName: skills-api
          resource: limits.cpu
EOF

# Check exposed CPU limit
kubectl exec anihpj-skills-api -- printenv SKILLS_CPU_LIMIT
pod/anihpj-skills-api created 2
Example 2: Expose User Container Memory Request in Megabytes

This example exposes the User container's memory request converted to megabytes.

# Deploy User API with memory in MB
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
spec:
  containers:
  - name: user-api
    image: anihpj/user-api:v1
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "2Gi"
      limits:
        memory: "4Gi"
    env:
    - name: USER_MEMORY_REQUEST_MB
      valueFrom:
        resourceFieldRef:
          containerName: user-api
          resource: requests.memory
          divisor: "1Mi"
    - name: USER_MEMORY_LIMIT_MB
      valueFrom:
        resourceFieldRef:
          containerName: user-api
          resource: limits.memory
          divisor: "1Mi"
EOF

# View memory in megabytes
kubectl exec anihpj-user-api -- sh -c \
  'echo "Request: $USER_MEMORY_REQUEST_MB MB, Limit: $USER_MEMORY_LIMIT_MB MB"'
pod/anihpj-user-api created Request: 2048 MB, Limit: 4096 MB
Example 3: Configure Author Workers Based on CPU Requests

This example uses CPU requests to dynamically set Author worker count.

# Deploy Author API with adaptive workers
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: author
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: author
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: author-api
        image: anihpj/author-api:v1
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "1000m"
          limits:
            cpu: "2000m"
        env:
        - name: AUTHOR_CPU_REQUEST
          valueFrom:
            resourceFieldRef:
              containerName: author-api
              resource: requests.cpu
        command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
        args:
          - |
            WORKERS=\$((\${AUTHOR_CPU_REQUEST%m} / 250))
            echo "Starting with \$WORKERS workers based on \${AUTHOR_CPU_REQUEST} CPU"
            gunicorn --workers \$WORKERS anihpj.wsgi:application
EOF

# Check deployment
kubectl get deployment anihpj-author-api
deployment.apps/anihpj-author-api created NAME READY UP-TO-DATE AVAILABLE AGE anihpj-author-api 3/3 3 3 15s
Example 4: Expose Location Pod Memory for Cache Configuration

This example uses memory limits to configure Location service cache size.

# Create Location pod with adaptive cache
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
spec:
  containers:
  - name: location-api
    image: anihpj/location-api:v1
    resources:
      requests:
        memory: "1Gi"
      limits:
        memory: "2Gi"
    env:
    - name: LOCATION_MEMORY_LIMIT_MB
      valueFrom:
        resourceFieldRef:
          containerName: location-api
          resource: limits.memory
          divisor: "1Mi"
    command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
    args:
      - |
        CACHE_SIZE=\$((\$LOCATION_MEMORY_LIMIT_MB / 4))
        echo "Configuring Redis cache: \${CACHE_SIZE}MB"
        redis-server --maxmemory \${CACHE_SIZE}mb
EOF

# Verify pod startup
kubectl logs anihpj-location-api --tail=5
pod/anihpj-location-api created Configuring Redis cache: 512MB * Ready to accept connections
Example 5: JobPost StatefulSet with Resource-Aware Configuration

This example creates JobPost workers that adapt based on both CPU and memory resources.

# Deploy JobPost workers with complete resource visibility
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "1Gi"
          limits:
            cpu: "2000m"
            memory: "4Gi"
        env:
        - name: JOBPOST_CPU_LIMIT
          valueFrom:
            resourceFieldRef:
              containerName: jobpost-worker
              resource: limits.cpu
        - name: JOBPOST_MEMORY_LIMIT_MB
          valueFrom:
            resourceFieldRef:
              containerName: jobpost-worker
              resource: limits.memory
              divisor: "1Mi"
        - name: JOBPOST_CPU_REQUEST
          valueFrom:
            resourceFieldRef:
              containerName: jobpost-worker
              resource: requests.cpu
        command: ["/bin/sh", "-c"]
        args:
          - |
            WORKERS=\$((\${JOBPOST_CPU_LIMIT%m} / 500))
            CONNECTIONS=\$((\$JOBPOST_MEMORY_LIMIT_MB / 10))
            echo "CPU: \$JOBPOST_CPU_LIMIT, Memory: \${JOBPOST_MEMORY_LIMIT_MB}MB"
            echo "Starting \$WORKERS workers with \$CONNECTIONS DB connections"
            celery -A anihpj worker --concurrency=\$WORKERS
EOF

# View StatefulSet pods
kubectl get pods -l model=jobpost
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-jobpost-workers-0 1/1 Running 0 20s anihpj-jobpost-workers-1 1/1 Running 0 18s anihpj-jobpost-workers-2 1/1 Running 0 16s anihpj-jobpost-workers-3 1/1 Running 0 14s anihpj-jobpost-workers-4 1/1 Running 0 12s

Status

Official Documentation: Kubernetes Status

API Group: meta/v1

Status is a return value for calls that don't return other objects.

Full Description

Status is the standard error response object returned by the Kubernetes API when operations fail or when requesting information about API errors. It provides detailed information about what went wrong, including a human-readable message, a machine-readable reason code, HTTP status code, and optionally detailed information about the resource that caused the error. The Status object is particularly important for error handling in client applications, allowing them to programmatically detect and handle specific failure conditions.

For the anihpj Django application, understanding Status responses is critical for implementing robust error handling when managing Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost Kubernetes resources. Common scenarios include handling NotFound errors when resources don't exist, AlreadyExists errors during creation, Invalid errors for malformed specifications, Forbidden errors for permission issues, and Conflict errors during concurrent updates. Proper status handling enables graceful degradation, meaningful error messages to users, and automated retry logic.

Key Fields
  • status (string): Status of the operation. Values: "Success" or "Failure".
  • message (string): Human-readable description of the status.
  • reason (string): Machine-readable description of why the operation failed.
  • code (integer): HTTP status code.
  • details (StatusDetails): Extended data about the failure.
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Handle Skills Pod NotFound Status

This example demonstrates handling a NotFound error when querying a non-existent Skills pod.

# Try to get non-existent Skills pod
kubectl get pod anihpj-skills-nonexistent -o json 2>&1 || true

# Or use API directly
kubectl get --raw /api/v1/namespaces/default/pods/anihpj-skills-nonexistent 2>&1 | jq
{ "kind": "Status", "apiVersion": "v1", "metadata": {}, "status": "Failure", "message": "pods \"anihpj-skills-nonexistent\" not found", "reason": "NotFound", "details": { "name": "anihpj-skills-nonexistent", "kind": "pods" }, "code": 404 }
Example 2: Handle User Deployment AlreadyExists Status

This example shows the Status returned when trying to create a User deployment that already exists.

# Create User deployment
kubectl create deployment anihpj-user-api --image=anihpj/user-api:v1

# Try to create again (will fail)
kubectl create deployment anihpj-user-api --image=anihpj/user-api:v1 2>&1 || true

# View error via API
kubectl get --raw /apis/apps/v1/namespaces/default/deployments/anihpj-user-api 2>&1 | jq '.status' 2>/dev/null || echo "Already exists"
deployment.apps/anihpj-user-api created Error from server (AlreadyExists): deployments.apps "anihpj-user-api" already exists { "kind": "Status", "apiVersion": "v1", "status": "Failure", "message": "deployments.apps \"anihpj-user-api\" already exists", "reason": "AlreadyExists", "details": { "name": "anihpj-user-api", "group": "apps", "kind": "deployments" }, "code": 409 }
Example 3: Handle Author ConfigMap Invalid Status

This example demonstrates Invalid status when creating an Author ConfigMap with invalid keys.

# Try to create ConfigMap with invalid key name
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f - 2>&1 || true
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-config
data:
  "invalid key with spaces": "value"
EOF
The ConfigMap "anihpj-author-config" is invalid: data[invalid key with spaces]: Invalid value: "invalid key with spaces": a valid config key must consist of alphanumeric characters, '-', '_' or '.' (e.g. 'key.name', or 'KEY_NAME', or 'key-name', regex used for validation is '[-._a-zA-Z0-9]+') { "kind": "Status", "apiVersion": "v1", "status": "Failure", "message": "ConfigMap \"anihpj-author-config\" is invalid", "reason": "Invalid", "details": { "name": "anihpj-author-config", "kind": "ConfigMap", "causes": [ { "reason": "FieldValueInvalid", "message": "Invalid value: \"invalid key with spaces\": must match regex '[-._a-zA-Z0-9]+'", "field": "data[invalid key with spaces]" } ] }, "code": 422 }
Example 4: Handle Location Namespace Forbidden Status

This example shows Forbidden status when attempting to access Location resources without permission.

# Create a limited ServiceAccount
kubectl create serviceaccount anihpj-location-readonly

# Try to delete deployment without permission (simulated)
# In practice, this would be done with the ServiceAccount token
kubectl auth can-i delete deployments --as=system:serviceaccount:default:anihpj-location-readonly

# Attempt deletion (would fail with Forbidden)
echo "Simulated Forbidden response:"
cat <<EOF
{
  "kind": "Status",
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "status": "Failure",
  "message": "deployments.apps \"anihpj-location-api\" is forbidden: User \"system:serviceaccount:default:anihpj-location-readonly\" cannot delete resource \"deployments\" in API group \"apps\" in the namespace \"default\"",
  "reason": "Forbidden",
  "details": {
    "name": "anihpj-location-api",
    "group": "apps",
    "kind": "deployments"
  },
  "code": 403
}
EOF
serviceaccount/anihpj-location-readonly created no Simulated Forbidden response: { "kind": "Status", "apiVersion": "v1", "status": "Failure", "message": "deployments.apps \"anihpj-location-api\" is forbidden: User \"system:serviceaccount:default:anihpj-location-readonly\" cannot delete resource \"deployments\" in API group \"apps\" in the namespace \"default\"", "reason": "Forbidden", "details": { "name": "anihpj-location-api", "group": "apps", "kind": "deployments" }, "code": 403 }
Example 5: Handle JobPost StatefulSet Conflict Status

This example demonstrates Conflict status during concurrent JobPost StatefulSet updates.

# Create JobPost StatefulSet
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1
EOF

# Get current resource version
RESOURCE_VERSION=$(kubectl get statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers -o jsonpath='{.metadata.resourceVersion}')

# Simulate outdated update (using old resourceVersion)
echo "Conflict occurs when resourceVersion is outdated:"
cat <<EOF
{
  "kind": "Status",
  "apiVersion": "v1",
  "status": "Failure",
  "message": "Operation cannot be fulfilled on statefulsets.apps \"anihpj-jobpost-workers\": the object has been modified; please apply your changes to the latest version and try again",
  "reason": "Conflict",
  "details": {
    "name": "anihpj-jobpost-workers",
    "group": "apps",
    "kind": "statefulsets"
  },
  "code": 409
}
EOF
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created Conflict occurs when resourceVersion is outdated: { "kind": "Status", "apiVersion": "v1", "status": "Failure", "message": "Operation cannot be fulfilled on statefulsets.apps \"anihpj-jobpost-workers\": the object has been modified; please apply your changes to the latest version and try again", "reason": "Conflict", "details": { "name": "anihpj-jobpost-workers", "group": "apps", "kind": "statefulsets" }, "code": 409 }

TypedLocalObjectReference

Official Documentation: Kubernetes TypedLocalObjectReference

API Group: core/v1

TypedLocalObjectReference contains enough information to let you locate the typed referenced object inside the same namespace.

Full Description

TypedLocalObjectReference extends LocalObjectReference by adding type information through the kind and apiGroup fields. This allows referencing any Kubernetes resource type in a type-safe manner while ensuring the reference stays within the same namespace. Unlike ObjectReference which can span namespaces and clusters, TypedLocalObjectReference is explicitly scoped to the local namespace, making it ideal for configuration where cross-namespace references would be a security concern or architectural violation.

For the anihpj Django application, TypedLocalObjectReference is commonly used in Ingress backends, HorizontalPodAutoscaler targets, NetworkPolicy references, and PodDisruptionBudget selectors to reference Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services and deployments. The typed nature ensures that references are validated by the API server, preventing misconfigurations where incompatible resource types might be referenced. This is particularly important for autoscaling and traffic routing configurations.

Key Fields
  • kind (string, required): Kind of the referent. Examples: "Service", "Deployment", "StatefulSet".
  • name (string, required): Name of the referent.
  • apiGroup (string): APIGroup of the referent. Empty for core/v1 resources like Service.
5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Reference Skills Service in Ingress Backend

This example creates an Ingress that references the Skills Service using TypedLocalObjectReference.

# Create Skills Service
kubectl create service clusterip anihpj-skills-api --tcp=8000:8000

# Create Ingress with TypedLocalObjectReference
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-ingress
spec:
  rules:
  - host: skills.anihpj.com
    http:
      paths:
      - path: /
        pathType: Prefix
        backend:
          service:
            name: anihpj-skills-api
            port:
              number: 8000
EOF

# Verify Ingress backend reference
kubectl get ingress anihpj-skills-ingress \
  -o jsonpath='{.spec.rules[0].http.paths[0].backend}' | jq
service/anihpj-skills-api created ingress.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-skills-ingress created { "service": { "name": "anihpj-skills-api", "port": { "number": 8000 } } }
Example 2: Configure User Deployment HPA with Typed Reference

This example creates an HPA that targets the User Deployment using TypedLocalObjectReference.

# Create User Deployment
kubectl create deployment anihpj-user-api --image=anihpj/user-api:v1
kubectl set resources deployment anihpj-user-api --requests=cpu=200m

# Create HPA with scaleTargetRef
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-hpa
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-user-api
  minReplicas: 2
  maxReplicas: 10
  metrics:
  - type: Resource
    resource:
      name: cpu
      target:
        type: Utilization
        averageUtilization: 70
EOF

# View HPA target reference
kubectl get hpa anihpj-user-hpa \
  -o jsonpath='Target: {.spec.scaleTargetRef.kind}/{.spec.scaleTargetRef.name}'
deployment.apps/anihpj-user-api created deployment.apps/anihpj-user-api resource requirements updated horizontalpodautoscaler.autoscaling/anihpj-user-hpa created Target: Deployment/anihpj-user-api
Example 3: Create Author NetworkPolicy with Typed Pod Selector

This example uses TypedLocalObjectReference in NetworkPolicy to reference Author pods.

# Create Author Deployment
kubectl create deployment anihpj-author-api \
  --image=anihpj/author-api:v1 \
  --replicas=3

# Add labels
kubectl label deployment anihpj-author-api app=anihpj model=author

# Create NetworkPolicy
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-netpol
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: author
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: anihpj
          model: user
    ports:
    - protocol: TCP
      port: 8000
EOF

# Verify NetworkPolicy
kubectl describe networkpolicy anihpj-author-netpol
deployment.apps/anihpj-author-api created deployment.apps/anihpj-author-api labeled networkpolicy.networking.k8s.io/anihpj-author-netpol created Name: anihpj-author-netpol PodSelector: app=anihpj,model=author Allowing ingress traffic: To Port: 8000/TCP From: PodSelector: app=anihpj,model=user
Example 4: Configure Location VerticalPodAutoscaler Target

This example creates a VPA that references the Location Deployment.

# Create Location Deployment
kubectl create deployment anihpj-location-api --image=anihpj/location-api:v1
kubectl set resources deployment anihpj-location-api \
  --requests=cpu=100m,memory=128Mi \
  --limits=cpu=500m,memory=512Mi

# Create VPA with typed target reference
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: autoscaling.k8s.io/v1
kind: VerticalPodAutoscaler
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-vpa
spec:
  targetRef:
    apiVersion: apps/v1
    kind: Deployment
    name: anihpj-location-api
  updatePolicy:
    updateMode: "Auto"
  resourcePolicy:
    containerPolicies:
    - containerName: location-api
      minAllowed:
        cpu: 50m
        memory: 64Mi
      maxAllowed:
        cpu: 1000m
        memory: 2Gi
EOF

# View VPA target
kubectl get vpa anihpj-location-vpa \
  -o jsonpath='Target: {.spec.targetRef.kind}/{.spec.targetRef.name}'
deployment.apps/anihpj-location-api created deployment.apps/anihpj-location-api resource requirements updated verticalpodautoscaler.autoscaling.k8s.io/anihpj-location-vpa created Target: Deployment/anihpj-location-api
Example 5: Create JobPost PodDisruptionBudget with Typed Selector

This example sets up a PDB for JobPost workers using TypedLocalObjectReference.

# Create JobPost StatefulSet
kubectl create -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 10
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1
EOF

# Create PodDisruptionBudget
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-pdb
spec:
  minAvailable: 7
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  unhealthyPodEvictionPolicy: AlwaysAllow
EOF

# Verify PDB
kubectl get pdb anihpj-jobpost-pdb \
  -o jsonpath='MinAvailable: {.spec.minAvailable}, CurrentHealthy: {.status.currentHealthy}'
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created poddisruptionbudget.policy/anihpj-jobpost-pdb created MinAvailable: 7, CurrentHealthy: 10

⚙️ Common Parameters

Common Parameters are standard query and request parameters used across all Kubernetes API operations. These parameters provide consistent ways to control API behavior including pagination, filtering, field selection, dry-run operations, and resource versioning. Understanding these parameters is essential for efficient API usage and proper resource management.

allowWatchBookmarks - Request watch events with BOOKMARK type
continue - Token for paginated list continuation
dryRun - Validate without persisting changes
fieldManager - Name of entity making changes
fieldSelector - Filter objects by field values
fieldValidation - Handle unknown or duplicate fields
force - Force apply operations
gracePeriodSeconds - Deletion grace period duration
ignoreStoreReadErrorWithClusterBreakingPotential - Unsafe deletion option
labelSelector - Filter objects by labels
limit - Maximum responses for list calls
namespace - Object scope and authorization
pretty - Pretty print API responses
propagationPolicy - Garbage collection behavior
resourceVersion - Resource version constraints
resourceVersionMatch - How resourceVersion applies
sendInitialEvents - Send current state in watch
timeoutSeconds - Operation timeout duration
watch - Stream resource changes

allowWatchBookmarks

Type: boolean (query parameter)

Default: false

allowWatchBookmarks requests watch events with type "BOOKMARK" for checkpoint markers.

Full Description

allowWatchBookmarks enables the server to send BOOKMARK events during watch operations, providing checkpoint markers that clients can use to resume watches from specific points. Servers that don't implement bookmarks may ignore this flag, and bookmark events are sent at the server's discretion. Clients should not assume bookmarks are returned at any specific interval or that the server will send any BOOKMARK event during a session.

For the anihpj Django application, bookmark events are useful when watching Skills, User, Author, Location, or JobPost resources for long periods. Bookmarks help maintain efficient watch operations by providing periodic checkpoints that can be used to resume watches after disconnections, reducing the need to restart from the beginning and improving application resilience.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Watch Skills Deployments with Bookmarks

This example watches Skills deployments with bookmark support.

# Watch Skills deployments with bookmarks enabled
kubectl get deployments -l model=skills --watch \
  --allow-watch-bookmarks=true -v=6

# Alternative using curl
curl -X GET "https://kubernetes.default.svc/apis/apps/v1/deployments?watch=true&allowWatchBookmarks=true&labelSelector=model=skills" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
ADDED anihpj-skills-api 3/3 3 3 1m BOOKMARK {"type":"BOOKMARK","object":{"kind":"Status","apiVersion":"v1","metadata":{"resourceVersion":"12345"}}} MODIFIED anihpj-skills-api 3/3 3 3 2m
Example 2: Watch User Pods with Bookmarks

This example demonstrates watching User pods with bookmark checkpoints.

# Watch User pods with bookmarks
kubectl get pods -l model=user --watch --allow-watch-bookmarks=true

# Check for bookmark events in output
kubectl get pods -l model=user --watch --allow-watch-bookmarks=true -o json | \
  jq 'select(.type=="BOOKMARK")'
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-user-api-abc123 1/1 Running 0 1m {"type":"BOOKMARK","object":{"kind":"Status","metadata":{"resourceVersion":"12346"}}}
Example 3: Resume Author Watch from Bookmark

This example shows resuming an Author watch from a bookmark resource version.

# Start watching Author services from specific resource version
RESOURCE_VERSION="12346"
kubectl get services -l model=author --watch \
  --resource-version=$RESOURCE_VERSION \
  --allow-watch-bookmarks=true

# API call with resource version
curl "https://kubernetes.default.svc/api/v1/services?watch=true&resourceVersion=12346&allowWatchBookmarks=true&labelSelector=model=author"
BOOKMARK {"type":"BOOKMARK","object":{"metadata":{"resourceVersion":"12347"}}} ADDED anihpj-author-api ClusterIP 10.96.1.123 8000/TCP 30s
Example 4: Location ConfigMap Watch with Bookmarks

This example watches Location ConfigMaps with bookmark events for checkpointing.

# Watch Location ConfigMaps with bookmarks
kubectl get configmaps -l model=location --watch --allow-watch-bookmarks=true -o wide

# Filter only bookmark events
kubectl get configmaps --watch --allow-watch-bookmarks=true -o json | \
  jq -r 'select(.type=="BOOKMARK") | .object.metadata.resourceVersion'
NAME DATA AGE anihpj-location-config 3 5m BOOKMARK: resourceVersion 12348 MODIFIED anihpj-location-config 3 6m
Example 5: JobPost StatefulSet Watch with Bookmark Handling

This example shows comprehensive JobPost StatefulSet watching with bookmark processing.

# Watch JobPost StatefulSets with comprehensive bookmark handling
kubectl get statefulsets -l model=jobpost --watch --allow-watch-bookmarks=true -o json | \
  while read event; do
    TYPE=$(echo "$event" | jq -r '.type')
    if [ "$TYPE" = "BOOKMARK" ]; then
      RV=$(echo "$event" | jq -r '.object.metadata.resourceVersion')
      echo "Bookmark checkpoint: $RV"
    else
      NAME=$(echo "$event" | jq -r '.object.metadata.name')
      echo "Event: $TYPE for $NAME"
    fi
  done
Event: ADDED for anihpj-jobpost-workers Bookmark checkpoint: 12349 Event: MODIFIED for anihpj-jobpost-workers Bookmark checkpoint: 12350

continue

Type: string (query parameter)

Default: empty

continue token enables pagination by retrieving the next set of results from a previous query.

Full Description

The continue parameter should be set when retrieving more results from the server after a paginated list operation. Since this value is server-defined, clients may only use the continue value from a previous query result with identical query parameters (except for the continue value itself). The server may reject a continue value it doesn't recognize. If the continue value is no longer valid due to expiration (typically 5-15 minutes) or server configuration changes, the server responds with a 410 ResourceExpired error along with a new continue token.

For the anihpj Django application, continue tokens are essential when listing large numbers of Skills, User, Author, Location, or JobPost resources. This enables efficient pagination through thousands of objects without overwhelming server resources or client memory. If consistent results are required, restart the list without continue; otherwise, use the new token from the 410 error to continue from the latest snapshot.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Paginate Skills Pods with Continue Token

This example demonstrates pagination through Skills pods using continue tokens.

# Get first page of Skills pods (limit 10)
kubectl get pods -l model=skills --limit=10 -o json > page1.json

# Extract continue token
CONTINUE_TOKEN=$(cat page1.json | jq -r '.metadata.continue // empty')

# Get next page using continue token
if [ -n "$CONTINUE_TOKEN" ]; then
  kubectl get pods -l model=skills --limit=10 --continue="$CONTINUE_TOKEN" -o json
fi
{ "metadata": { "continue": "eyJ2IjoibWV0YS5rOHMuaW8vdjEi...", "remainingItemCount": 25 }, "items": [ { "metadata": { "name": "anihpj-skills-api-1" } } ] }
Example 2: User ServiceAccounts Complete Pagination

This example shows complete pagination through User ServiceAccounts.

# Function to paginate through User ServiceAccounts
paginate_user_serviceaccounts() {
  local continue_token=""
  local page=1
  
  while true; do
    echo "Fetching page $page..."
    
    if [ -n "$continue_token" ]; then
      response=$(kubectl get serviceaccounts -l model=user --limit=5 --continue="$continue_token" -o json)
    else
      response=$(kubectl get serviceaccounts -l model=user --limit=5 -o json)
    fi
    
    # Process items
    echo "$response" | jq -r '.items[].metadata.name'
    
    # Get next continue token
    continue_token=$(echo "$response" | jq -r '.metadata.continue // empty')
    [ -z "$continue_token" ] && break
    
    ((page++))
  done
}

paginate_user_serviceaccounts
Fetching page 1... anihpj-user-sa-1 anihpj-user-sa-2 Fetching page 2... anihpj-user-sa-3 anihpj-user-sa-4
Example 3: Author Secrets with Continue Error Handling

This example handles continue token expiration when listing Author secrets.

# List Author secrets with continue error handling
list_author_secrets() {
  local continue_token="$1"
  
  result=$(kubectl get secrets -l model=author --limit=3 \
    ${continue_token:+--continue="$continue_token"} \
    -o json 2>&1)
  
  if echo "$result" | grep -q "410 ResourceExpired"; then
    echo "Continue token expired, restarting from beginning"
    kubectl get secrets -l model=author --limit=3 -o json
  else
    echo "$result"
  fi
}

# Test with expired token
list_author_secrets "invalid_token_12345"
Continue token expired, restarting from beginning { "metadata": { "continue": "eyJ2IjoibWV0YS5rOHMuaW8vdjEi...", "remainingItemCount": 8 }, "items": [...] }
Example 4: Location ConfigMaps Consistent Pagination

This example demonstrates consistent pagination of Location ConfigMaps.

# Consistent pagination - restart if continue fails
paginate_location_configmaps() {
  echo "Starting consistent pagination of Location ConfigMaps"
  
  # First request without continue token for consistency
  response=$(kubectl get configmaps -l model=location --limit=2 -o json)
  echo "Page 1:"
  echo "$response" | jq -r '.items[].metadata.name'
  
  continue_token=$(echo "$response" | jq -r '.metadata.continue // empty')
  
  if [ -n "$continue_token" ]; then
    echo "Page 2 (continue: ${continue_token:0:20}...):"
    kubectl get configmaps -l model=location --limit=2 --continue="$continue_token" \
      -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\n"}{end}'
  fi
}

paginate_location_configmaps
Starting consistent pagination of Location ConfigMaps Page 1: anihpj-location-config-1 anihpj-location-config-2 Page 2 (continue: eyJ2IjoibWV0YS5rOHM...): anihpj-location-config-3 anihpj-location-config-4
Example 5: JobPost Events Large Dataset Pagination

This example handles large JobPost events dataset with continue tokens.

# Paginate through large JobPost events dataset
paginate_jobpost_events() {
  local total_items=0
  local page=1
  local continue_token=""
  
  echo "Paginating JobPost events..."
  
  while true; do
    if [ -n "$continue_token" ]; then
      response=$(kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-jobpost-workers \
        --limit=10 --continue="$continue_token" -o json 2>/dev/null)
    else
      response=$(kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-jobpost-workers \
        --limit=10 -o json)
    fi
    
    # Count items in this page
    page_items=$(echo "$response" | jq '.items | length')
    total_items=$((total_items + page_items))
    
    echo "Page $page: $page_items events (Total: $total_items)"
    
    # Show recent events
    echo "$response" | jq -r '.items[0:2][] | "\(.firstTimestamp) \(.reason): \(.message)"'
    
    continue_token=$(echo "$response" | jq -r '.metadata.continue // empty')
    [ -z "$continue_token" ] && break
    
    ((page++))
  done
  
  echo "Total JobPost events: $total_items"
}

paginate_jobpost_events
Paginating JobPost events... Page 1: 10 events (Total: 10) 2025-01-11T10:30:00Z Scheduled: Successfully assigned default/anihpj-jobpost-workers-0 2025-01-11T10:30:05Z Pulling: Pulling image "anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1" Page 2: 5 events (Total: 15) Total JobPost events: 15

dryRun

Type: array of strings (query parameter)

Valid Values: "All"

dryRun indicates that modifications should not be persisted, only validated.

Full Description

When present, dryRun indicates that modifications should not be persisted to storage, allowing validation of requests without making actual changes. An invalid or unrecognized dryRun directive will result in an error response and no further processing. The only valid value is "All", which processes all dry run stages including validation, admission control, and mutation, but skips the final persistence step.

For the anihpj Django application, dry run is invaluable for testing configurations of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost resources before applying them to production. It enables validation of YAML manifests, testing admission webhooks, checking quota limits, and verifying that resources will be accepted without risking actual cluster state changes. This is essential for CI/CD pipelines and configuration validation workflows.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Validate Skills Deployment Configuration

This example validates Skills deployment YAML without creating the actual deployment.

# Create Skills deployment YAML
cat <<EOF > skills-deployment.yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: skills
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v2
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
EOF

# Validate with dry run
kubectl apply -f skills-deployment.yaml --dry-run=server -o yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: anihpj-skills-api namespace: default # ... full validated manifest # No actual deployment created
Example 2: Test User Service Creation

This example tests User service creation with dry run validation.

# Test User service creation
kubectl create service clusterip anihpj-user-api \
  --tcp=8000:8000 \
  --dry-run=server -o yaml

# Alternative with explicit manifest
kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
spec:
  selector:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
  ports:
  - port: 8000
    targetPort: 8000
    protocol: TCP
EOF
service/anihpj-user-api configured (dry run) # Service validated but not created
Example 3: Validate Author ConfigMap Update

This example validates Author ConfigMap updates without applying changes.

# Create initial ConfigMap (if not exists)
kubectl create configmap anihpj-author-config \
  --from-literal=max-authors=100 \
  --from-literal=cache-timeout=300 \
  --dry-run=server

# Test updating existing ConfigMap
kubectl patch configmap anihpj-author-config \
  --type='merge' \
  -p='{"data":{"max-authors":"200","new-setting":"enabled"}}' \
  --dry-run=server -o yaml

# Verify current ConfigMap unchanged
kubectl get configmap anihpj-author-config -o yaml
configmap/anihpj-author-config configured (dry run) # Patch validated, original ConfigMap unchanged data: cache-timeout: "300" max-authors: "100"
Example 4: Location Secret Dry Run Testing

This example tests Location secret operations with comprehensive dry run validation.

# Test creating Location API secret
kubectl create secret generic anihpj-location-secret \
  --from-literal=api-key="test-key-12345" \
  --from-literal=database-url="postgresql://test" \
  --dry-run=server -o json | jq '.metadata.name'

# Test secret update
kubectl patch secret anihpj-location-secret \
  --type='merge' \
  -p='{"data":{"new-key":"'$(echo -n "new-value" | base64)'"}}' \
  --dry-run=server

# Validate secret deletion
kubectl delete secret anihpj-location-secret --dry-run=server
"anihpj-location-secret" secret/anihpj-location-secret patched (dry run) secret "anihpj-location-secret" deleted (dry run)
Example 5: JobPost StatefulSet Complex Validation

This example performs comprehensive JobPost StatefulSet validation with dry run.

# Create comprehensive JobPost StatefulSet for validation
kubectl apply --dry-run=server -o yaml -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v2
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 1Gi
          limits:
            cpu: 2000m
            memory: 4Gi
        env:
        - name: DATABASE_URL
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: anihpj-jobpost-secret
              key: database-url
  volumeClaimTemplates:
  - metadata:
      name: worker-storage
    spec:
      accessModes: [ "ReadWriteOnce" ]
      resources:
        requests:
          storage: 10Gi
EOF

# Check validation results
echo "StatefulSet validated successfully with dry run"
apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: StatefulSet metadata: name: anihpj-jobpost-workers # ... complete validated manifest # StatefulSet validated successfully with dry run

fieldManager

Type: string (query parameter)

Length Limit: ≤ 128 characters

fieldManager identifies the actor or entity making changes to resource fields.

Full Description

fieldManager is a name associated with the actor or entity making changes to resource fields. The value must be less than or equal to 128 characters long and contain only printable characters. This parameter is essential for Server-Side Apply (SSA) operations, where Kubernetes tracks field ownership to prevent conflicts between different managers. Each field in a resource can be owned by one manager, enabling safe collaborative editing of resources.

For the anihpj Django application, setting appropriate fieldManager values helps track which system or operator is managing different aspects of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost resources. This is particularly important in environments with multiple deployment tools, operators, or team members managing the same resources. Proper field management prevents conflicts and enables safe automated updates.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Skills Deployment with Field Manager

This example applies Skills deployment with a specific field manager for tracking.

# Apply Skills deployment with field manager
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="anihpj-skills-manager" <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
    managed-by: anihpj-skills-manager
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: skills
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 250m
            memory: 512Mi
EOF

# Check field ownership
kubectl get deployment anihpj-skills-api -o yaml | grep -A 5 managedFields
deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api configured managedFields: - manager: anihpj-skills-manager operation: Apply apiVersion: apps/v1
Example 2: User Service with Multiple Managers

This example demonstrates different field managers for User service configuration.

# Apply User service base configuration
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="user-service-operator" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
  ports:
  - port: 8000
    targetPort: 8000
EOF

# Apply additional annotations with different manager
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="monitoring-operator" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port: "8000"
    prometheus.io/path: "/metrics"
EOF

# View field ownership
kubectl get service anihpj-user-api -o yaml | grep -A 10 managedFields
service/anihpj-user-api configured managedFields: - manager: user-service-operator operation: Apply fields: f:spec: f:type: {} f:ports: {} - manager: monitoring-operator operation: Apply fields: f:metadata: f:annotations: {}
Example 3: Author ConfigMap Managed Updates

This example shows Author ConfigMap updates with different field managers.

# Apply Author ConfigMap with application manager
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="anihpj-app-manager" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-config
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
data:
  max-authors: "100"
  cache-timeout: "300"
