  api-version: "v1"
EOF

# Update configuration with ops manager
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="ops-manager" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-config
data:
  log-level: "INFO"
  monitoring-enabled: "true"
  backup-frequency: "daily"
EOF

# Check which manager owns which fields
kubectl get configmap anihpj-author-config -o yaml | \
  yq '.metadata.managedFields[] | {"manager": .manager, "fields": .fieldsV1}'
configmap/anihpj-author-config configured manager: anihpj-app-manager fields: f:data: f:max-authors: {} f:cache-timeout: {} f:api-version: {} --- manager: ops-manager fields: f:data: f:log-level: {} f:monitoring-enabled: {} f:backup-frequency: {}
Example 4: Location Secret with Security Manager

This example manages Location secrets with security-focused field manager.

# Apply Location secret with security manager
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="security-manager" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-secret
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    security-level: high
type: Opaque
data:
  api-key: $(echo -n "loc-api-key-12345" | base64)
  database-password: $(echo -n "secure-db-pass" | base64)
EOF

# Update with additional credentials using different manager
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="integration-manager" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-secret
type: Opaque
data:
  redis-password: $(echo -n "redis-pass-67890" | base64)
  cache-token: $(echo -n "cache-token-abcdef" | base64)
EOF

# Verify field management
kubectl get secret anihpj-location-secret -o yaml | \
  grep -A 20 managedFields
secret/anihpj-location-secret configured managedFields: - manager: security-manager operation: Apply fieldsType: FieldsV1 fieldsV1: f:data: f:api-key: {} f:database-password: {} f:metadata: f:labels: f:security-level: {} - manager: integration-manager fieldsV1: f:data: f:redis-password: {} f:cache-token: {}
Example 5: JobPost StatefulSet with CI/CD Manager

This example shows JobPost StatefulSet management with CI/CD pipeline field manager.

# Apply JobPost StatefulSet with CI/CD manager
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="anihpj-cicd-pipeline" <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    deployed-by: cicd-pipeline
    version: v1.2.3
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
        version: v1.2.3
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1.2.3
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 1Gi
EOF

# Update with monitoring configuration from different manager
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="monitoring-operator" --force-conflicts <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port: "9090"
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        env:
        - name: METRICS_PORT
          value: "9090"
        - name: METRICS_ENABLED
          value: "true"
EOF

# Show field ownership structure
kubectl describe statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers | grep -A 5 "Managed Fields"
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers configured Managed Fields: anihpj-cicd-pipeline (Apply): spec.replicas, spec.template.spec.containers[0].image monitoring-operator (Apply): metadata.annotations, spec.template.spec.containers[0].env

fieldSelector

Type: string (query parameter)

Default: "" (everything)

fieldSelector restricts the list of returned objects by their field values.

Full Description

fieldSelector enables filtering resources based on their field values using equality-based selectors. Unlike labelSelector which filters by labels, fieldSelector filters by actual resource fields such as metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, status.phase, etc. The syntax supports equality (=, ==) and inequality (!=) operators, and multiple selectors can be combined with commas. Field selectors are resource-type specific, meaning different resources support different fields for selection.

For the anihpj Django application, fieldSelector is essential for precise filtering of Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost resources based on their actual properties rather than labels. This enables efficient queries for resources in specific states, on particular nodes, with certain names patterns, or meeting specific criteria. Field selectors are particularly useful for monitoring, debugging, and automated management tasks.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Filter Skills Pods by Status and Node

This example filters Skills pods by their running status and specific node.

# List Skills pods that are running
kubectl get pods -l model=skills \
  --field-selector status.phase=Running

# List Skills pods on specific node
kubectl get pods -l model=skills \
  --field-selector spec.nodeName=worker-node-1

# Combine multiple field selectors
kubectl get pods -l model=skills \
  --field-selector status.phase=Running,spec.nodeName=worker-node-1

# List Skills pods NOT in running state
kubectl get pods -l model=skills \
  --field-selector status.phase!=Running
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE NODE anihpj-skills-api-abc123 1/1 Running 0 10m worker-node-1 anihpj-skills-api-def456 1/1 Running 0 8m worker-node-1 NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE NODE anihpj-skills-api-ghi789 0/1 Pending 0 2m
Example 2: User Events by Involved Object

This example filters events related to User resources using field selectors.

# Get events for specific User deployment
kubectl get events \
  --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-user-api

# Get events for User resources by kind
kubectl get events \
  --field-selector involvedObject.kind=Deployment,involvedObject.name=anihpj-user-api

# Filter User pod events by reason
kubectl get events \
  --field-selector involvedObject.kind=Pod,reason=Failed \
  --all-namespaces

# Get recent User service events
kubectl get events \
  --field-selector involvedObject.kind=Service,involvedObject.name=anihpj-user-service \
  --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
LAST SEEN TYPE REASON OBJECT MESSAGE 5m Warning FailedScheduling pod/anihpj-user-api-xyz789 0/3 nodes available 3m Normal Scheduled pod/anihpj-user-api-xyz789 Successfully assigned default/anihpj-user-api-xyz789 to worker-node-2 1m Normal Pulling pod/anihpj-user-api-xyz789 Pulling image "anihpj/user-api:v1"
Example 3: Author Resources by Namespace and Name Pattern

This example uses field selectors to filter Author resources by namespace and name patterns.

# List Author resources in specific namespace
kubectl get configmaps --all-namespaces \
  --field-selector metadata.namespace=author-system

# Get Author resources with specific name
kubectl get services --all-namespaces \
  --field-selector metadata.name=anihpj-author-api

# Combine namespace and name selectors
kubectl get secrets --all-namespaces \
  --field-selector metadata.namespace=default,metadata.name=anihpj-author-secret

# List all Author-related resources in default namespace
for resource in pods services configmaps secrets; do
  echo "=== Author $resource in default namespace ==="
  kubectl get $resource -l model=author \
    --field-selector metadata.namespace=default
done
=== Author pods in default namespace === NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-author-api-abc123 1/1 Running 0 15m === Author services in default namespace === NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE anihpj-author-api ClusterIP 10.96.123.45 8000/TCP 15m === Author configmaps in default namespace === NAME DATA AGE anihpj-author-config 5 20m
Example 4: Location Pods with Resource Failures

This example identifies Location pods with failures using field selectors.

# Find Location pods in failed state
kubectl get pods -l model=location \
  --field-selector status.phase=Failed

# Get Location pods with restart count > 0
kubectl get pods -l model=location \
  --field-selector status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount!=0 2>/dev/null || \
  kubectl get pods -l model=location \
    -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount>0)]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount}{"\n"}{end}'

# Find Location pods not scheduled (pending)
kubectl get pods -l model=location \
  --field-selector status.phase=Pending

# Get Location events for troubleshooting
kubectl get events \
  --field-selector involvedObject.kind=Pod \
  --field-selector reason=FailedScheduling \
  | grep location
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-location-api-fail123 0/1 Failed 0 5m NAME RESTARTS anihpj-location-api-abc123 3 NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-location-api-pending456 0/1 Pending 0 2m
Example 5: JobPost StatefulSet Pods by Ordinal

This example filters JobPost StatefulSet pods using field selectors for pod management.

# List all JobPost StatefulSet pods with ordinal pattern
kubectl get pods \
  --field-selector metadata.name=anihpj-jobpost-workers-0

# Get JobPost pods that are ready
kubectl get pods -l model=jobpost \
  --field-selector status.phase=Running \
  -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,READY:.status.conditions[?(@.type==\"Ready\")].status

# Find JobPost pods with specific container status
kubectl get pods -l model=jobpost \
  -o jsonpath='{range .items[?(@.status.containerStatuses[0].ready==false)]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[0].state}{"\n"}{end}'

# Get JobPost events filtered by specific pod ordinals
for i in {0..2}; do
  echo "=== Events for anihpj-jobpost-workers-$i ==="
  kubectl get events \
    --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-jobpost-workers-$i \
    --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' \
    --no-headers | tail -3
done
NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-jobpost-workers-0 1/1 Running 0 10m NAME READY anihpj-jobpost-workers-0 true anihpj-jobpost-workers-1 true anihpj-jobpost-workers-2 false anihpj-jobpost-workers-2 {"waiting":{"reason":"ImagePullBackOff"}} === Events for anihpj-jobpost-workers-0 === 1m Normal Started Started container jobpost-worker === Events for anihpj-jobpost-workers-1 === 2m Normal Pulled Successfully pulled image === Events for anihpj-jobpost-workers-2 === 30s Warning Failed Error: ImagePullBackOff

fieldValidation

Type: string (query parameter)

Valid Values: "Ignore", "Warn", "Strict"

fieldValidation controls how the server handles unknown or duplicate fields in requests.

Full Description

fieldValidation instructs the server on how to handle objects in requests (POST/PUT/PATCH) containing unknown or duplicate fields. "Ignore" silently drops unknown fields and keeps only the last duplicate field (pre-v1.23 behavior). "Warn" sends warnings for unknown fields and duplicates while still processing the request successfully (default in v1.23+). "Strict" fails the request with a BadRequest error if any unknown fields or duplicates are present.

For the anihpj Django application, fieldValidation is crucial for maintaining data integrity when deploying Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost resources. Using "Strict" mode helps catch configuration errors, typos, and API version mismatches during development. "Warn" mode provides feedback while maintaining compatibility, and "Ignore" mode ensures backward compatibility with older manifests.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Skills ConfigMap with Strict Validation

This example tests Skills ConfigMap creation with strict field validation.

# Create Skills ConfigMap with valid fields
kubectl apply --field-validation=Strict -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-config
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
data:
  max-skills: "500"
  cache-timeout: "600"
  api-endpoint: "https://skills.anihpj.com"
EOF

# Try with invalid field (will fail in Strict mode)
kubectl apply --field-validation=Strict -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-config-invalid
data:
  max-skills: "500"
  invalidField: "should-fail"
  duplicateKey: "value1"
  duplicateKey: "value2"
EOF
configmap/anihpj-skills-config created error validating data: ValidationError(ConfigMap.data): unknown field "invalidField" in io.k8s.api.core.v1.ConfigMap.data; strict decoding as requested
Example 2: User Deployment with Warning Validation

This example applies User deployment with warning field validation.

# Apply User deployment with warnings for unknown fields
kubectl apply --field-validation=Warn -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: user
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: user
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: user-api
        image: anihpj/user-api:v1
        oldImageField: "deprecated-field"  # Unknown field
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 250m
            memory: 512Mi
EOF
Warning: unknown field "spec.template.spec.containers[0].oldImageField" deployment.apps/anihpj-user-api created
Example 3: Author Service with Ignore Validation

This example creates Author service ignoring unknown fields silently.

# Apply Author service with ignore validation
kubectl apply --field-validation=Ignore -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
  unknownMetadataField: "ignored"
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  invalidSpecField: "also-ignored"
  selector:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
  ports:
  - port: 8000
    targetPort: 8000
    protocol: TCP
    unknownPortField: "ignored-too"
EOF

# Verify service was created without unknown fields
kubectl get service anihpj-author-api -o yaml | grep -v "unknownField\|invalidField"
service/anihpj-author-api created apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: anihpj-author-api labels: app: anihpj model: author spec: type: ClusterIP selector: app: anihpj model: author
Example 4: Location Secret Validation Comparison

This example compares different validation modes for Location secrets.

# Test Location secret with different validation modes
echo "=== Testing with Strict validation ==="
kubectl apply --field-validation=Strict --dry-run=server -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-secret-strict
type: Opaque
data:
  api-key: $(echo -n "location-key" | base64)
  invalidSecretField: "invalid"
EOF

echo "=== Testing with Warn validation ==="
kubectl apply --field-validation=Warn --dry-run=server -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-secret-warn
type: Opaque
data:
  api-key: $(echo -n "location-key" | base64)
  invalidSecretField: "invalid"
EOF

echo "=== Testing with Ignore validation ==="
kubectl apply --field-validation=Ignore --dry-run=server -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-secret-ignore
type: Opaque
data:
  api-key: $(echo -n "location-key" | base64)
  invalidSecretField: "invalid"
EOF
=== Testing with Strict validation === error validating data: ValidationError(Secret.data): unknown field "invalidSecretField" === Testing with Warn validation === Warning: unknown field "data.invalidSecretField" secret/anihpj-location-secret-warn configured (dry run) === Testing with Ignore validation === secret/anihpj-location-secret-ignore configured (dry run)
Example 5: JobPost StatefulSet Validation Pipeline

This example shows JobPost StatefulSet validation in CI/CD pipeline.

# CI/CD pipeline validation script for JobPost StatefulSet
validate_jobpost_statefulset() {
  local validation_mode="$1"
  echo "Validating JobPost StatefulSet with $validation_mode mode..."
  
  kubectl apply --field-validation="$validation_mode" --dry-run=server -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    version: v1.3.0
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 5
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: jobpost
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: jobpost
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1.3.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 1Gi
        env:
        - name: WORKER_TYPE
          value: "jobpost-processor"
        - name: LOG_LEVEL
          value: "INFO"
EOF
}

# Run validation with all modes
for mode in Strict Warn Ignore; do
  echo "========================================="
  validate_jobpost_statefulset "$mode"
  echo ""
done
========================================= Validating JobPost StatefulSet with Strict mode... statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers configured (dry run) ========================================= Validating JobPost StatefulSet with Warn mode... statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers configured (dry run) ========================================= Validating JobPost StatefulSet with Ignore mode... statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers configured (dry run)

force

Type: boolean (query parameter)

Default: false

force enables re-acquiring conflicting fields owned by other managers in Apply operations.

Full Description

force is used to "force" Apply requests by allowing users to re-acquire conflicting fields owned by other field managers. When set to true, the current field manager takes ownership of all specified fields, even if they're currently managed by different managers. The force flag must be unset (false) for non-apply patch requests. This parameter is essential for resolving field ownership conflicts in Server-Side Apply operations.

For the anihpj Django application, the force parameter is crucial when multiple systems manage Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost resources. It enables override scenarios where CI/CD pipelines, operators, or administrative tools need to take control of specific fields from other managers. Use force carefully as it can lead to unexpected changes if multiple systems compete for the same fields.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Force Skills Deployment Update

This example forces a Skills deployment update when field conflicts exist.

# Initial Skills deployment by CI/CD
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="cicd-pipeline" <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 250m
            memory: 512Mi
EOF

# Manual update by admin (will create conflict)
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="admin-operator" <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
spec:
  replicas: 5
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 1Gi
EOF

# Force CI/CD to take control back
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="cicd-pipeline" --force-conflicts <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
spec:
  replicas: 3
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1.1
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 300m
            memory: 768Mi
EOF
deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api configured deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api configured deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api configured
Example 2: User Service Force Conflicts Resolution

This example resolves User service field conflicts using force.

# Service managed by infrastructure team
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="infra-team" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
  annotations:
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: "nlb"
spec:
  type: LoadBalancer
  selector:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
  ports:
  - port: 80
    targetPort: 8000
EOF

# Monitoring team adds annotations (conflict potential)
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="monitoring-team" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
  annotations:
    prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
    prometheus.io/port: "8000"
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: "alb"  # Conflict!
EOF

# Infrastructure team forces control of load balancer annotation
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="infra-team" --force-conflicts <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-api
  annotations:
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-type: "nlb"
    service.beta.kubernetes.io/aws-load-balancer-scheme: "internet-facing"
EOF
service/anihpj-user-api configured service/anihpj-user-api configured service/anihpj-user-api configured
Example 3: Author ConfigMap Force Override

This example shows forcing ConfigMap field ownership for Author configuration.

# Author ConfigMap managed by application
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="author-app" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-config
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
data:
  max-authors: "100"
  cache-timeout: "300"
  api-version: "v1"
  debug-mode: "false"
EOF

# Operations team tries to update without force (will show conflict)
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="ops-team" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-config
data:
  max-authors: "200"  # Conflict with author-app
  log-level: "INFO"
  monitoring-enabled: "true"
EOF 2>&1 || echo "Conflict detected, using force..."

# Operations team forces the update
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="ops-team" --force-conflicts <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-config
data:
  max-authors: "200"
  log-level: "INFO"
  monitoring-enabled: "true"
  cache-timeout: "600"  # Also taking ownership of this field
EOF
configmap/anihpj-author-config configured Conflict detected, using force... configmap/anihpj-author-config configured
Example 4: Location Secret Force Security Update

This example forces security updates for Location secrets.

# Initial Location secret by application
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="location-app" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-secret
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
type: Opaque
data:
  api-key: $(echo -n "location-api-key-old" | base64)
  database-url: $(echo -n "postgresql://old-db" | base64)
EOF

# Security team tries to update credentials (potential conflict)
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="security-team" <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-secret
  labels:
    security-reviewed: "true"
type: Opaque
data:
  api-key: $(echo -n "location-api-key-new-secure" | base64)
EOF 2>&1 || echo "Conflict with existing field manager"

# Security team forces the security update
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="security-team" --force-conflicts <<EOF
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-secret
  labels:
    security-reviewed: "true"
    last-updated: "$(date +%Y-%m-%d)"
type: Opaque
data:
  api-key: $(echo -n "location-api-key-new-secure" | base64)
  database-url: $(echo -n "postgresql://new-secure-db" | base64)
EOF

# Verify security team now owns the fields
kubectl get secret anihpj-location-secret -o yaml | grep -A 5 managedFields
secret/anihpj-location-secret created Conflict with existing field manager secret/anihpj-location-secret configured managedFields: - manager: security-team operation: Apply fieldsV1: f:data: f:api-key: {} f:database-url: {}
Example 5: JobPost StatefulSet Emergency Force Update

This example demonstrates emergency JobPost StatefulSet updates using force.

# JobPost StatefulSet managed by GitOps
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="gitops-operator" <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
    managed-by: gitops
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 5
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1.0
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 1Gi
          limits:
            cpu: 1000m
            memory: 2Gi
EOF

# Emergency fix needed - SRE team forces critical update
kubectl apply -f - --field-manager="sre-emergency" --force-conflicts <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  labels:
    emergency-patch: "$(date +%Y%m%d-%H%M)"
    managed-by: sre-emergency
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1.0-hotfix
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: 200m  # Emergency resource reduction
            memory: 512Mi
          limits:
            cpu: 500m
            memory: 1Gi
        env:
        - name: EMERGENCY_MODE
          value: "true"
        - name: MAX_WORKERS
          value: "2"
EOF

# Check field ownership after emergency update
kubectl describe statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers | grep -A 3 "Managed Fields"
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers configured statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers configured Managed Fields: sre-emergency: spec.template.spec.containers[0].image, spec.template.spec.containers[0].resources, spec.template.spec.containers[0].env GitOps Operator: spec.replicas, spec.serviceName

gracePeriodSeconds

Type: integer (query parameter)

Default: per-resource default

gracePeriodSeconds sets the duration before an object should be forcefully deleted.

Full Description

gracePeriodSeconds specifies the duration in seconds before an object should be deleted. The value must be a non-negative integer, where zero indicates immediate deletion. If this value is nil, the default grace period for the specified resource type will be used. During the grace period, the object receives a SIGTERM signal to allow for graceful shutdown, followed by SIGKILL if the process doesn't terminate within the grace period.

For the anihpj Django application, gracePeriodSeconds is critical for ensuring Skills, User, Author, Location, and JobPost services shut down gracefully. This allows time for finishing active requests, closing database connections, and saving state. Different workloads may require different grace periods: web APIs might need 30 seconds to finish requests, while batch jobs might need several minutes to complete processing and cleanup.

5 Practical Django Examples
Example 1: Skills API Graceful Shutdown

This example configures Skills API pods with appropriate grace period for request completion.

# Create Skills deployment with custom grace period
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-skills-api
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: skills
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: anihpj
      model: skills
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: skills
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60  # 60 seconds for graceful shutdown
      containers:
      - name: skills-api
        image: anihpj/skills-api:v1
        lifecycle:
          preStop:
            exec:
              command: ["/bin/sh", "-c", "sleep 10 && /app/graceful_shutdown.py"]
        ports:
        - containerPort: 8000
EOF

# Delete pod with custom grace period
kubectl delete pod -l model=skills --grace-period=30

# Delete immediately (no grace period)
kubectl delete pod anihpj-skills-api-abc123 --grace-period=0 --force
deployment.apps/anihpj-skills-api created pod "anihpj-skills-api-abc123" deleted pod "anihpj-skills-api-def456" deleted
Example 2: User Database Connection Cleanup

This example ensures User service pods have time to close database connections.

# User StatefulSet with database connection cleanup
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-user-workers
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: user
spec:
  serviceName: user-workers
  replicas: 3
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: user
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 90  # Extra time for DB cleanup
      containers:
      - name: user-worker
        image: anihpj/user-worker:v1
        lifecycle:
          preStop:
            exec:
              command:
              - /bin/sh
              - -c
              - |
                echo "Stopping new requests..."
                kill -USR1 1  # Signal Django to stop accepting requests
                sleep 30
                echo "Closing database connections..."
                python /app/manage.py close_db_connections
                echo "Cleanup complete"
        env:
        - name: DJANGO_GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN
          value: "true"
EOF

# Test graceful deletion with monitoring
kubectl delete statefulset anihpj-user-workers --grace-period=120

# Monitor deletion progress
kubectl get pods -l model=user -w
statefulset.apps/anihpj-user-workers created statefulset.apps "anihpj-user-workers" deleted NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE anihpj-user-workers-0 1/1 Terminating 0 5m anihpj-user-workers-1 1/1 Terminating 0 5m anihpj-user-workers-2 1/1 Terminating 0 4m
Example 3: Author Batch Job with Extended Grace Period

This example configures Author batch jobs with extended grace periods for data processing completion.

# Author batch job with long grace period
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: batch/v1
kind: Job
metadata:
  name: anihpj-author-batch-processor
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: author
    job-type: batch
spec:
  template:
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 300  # 5 minutes for batch completion
      restartPolicy: Never
      containers:
      - name: author-processor
        image: anihpj/author-processor:v1
        command:
        - /bin/sh
        - -c
        - |
          trap 'echo "Received termination signal, finishing current batch..."; SHUTDOWN=true' TERM
          
          SHUTDOWN=false
          while [ "$SHUTDOWN" = false ]; do
            echo "Processing author batch..."
            python /app/process_authors.py --batch-size=100
            sleep 60
          done
          
          echo "Graceful shutdown complete"
        env:
        - name: BATCH_TIMEOUT
          value: "240"  # Shorter than grace period
EOF

# Delete job with specific grace period
kubectl delete job anihpj-author-batch-processor --grace-period=180

# Monitor job termination
kubectl describe job anihpj-author-batch-processor
job.batch/anihpj-author-batch-processor created job.batch "anihpj-author-batch-processor" deleted Events: Type Reason Age Message Normal SuccessfulDelete 1m Deleted pod: anihpj-author-batch-processor-xyz789
Example 4: Location Cache Flush During Shutdown

This example ensures Location services flush cache data during graceful shutdown.

# Location service with cache flush on shutdown
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: anihpj-location-cache
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: location
    component: cache
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: anihpj
        model: location
        component: cache
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 45
      containers:
      - name: location-cache
        image: anihpj/location-cache:v1
        lifecycle:
          preStop:
            exec:
              command:
              - /bin/bash
              - -c
              - |
                echo "Starting graceful shutdown..."
                # Stop accepting new cache requests
                redis-cli CONFIG SET stop-writes-on-bgsave-error no
                # Trigger background save
                redis-cli BGSAVE
                # Wait for save to complete (max 30 seconds)
                for i in {1..30}; do
                  if redis-cli LASTSAVE | grep -q "$(date +%s)"; then
                    echo "Cache flush completed"
                    break
                  fi
                  sleep 1
                done
                echo "Cache shutdown complete"
        ports:
        - containerPort: 6379
EOF

# Rolling restart with grace period monitoring
kubectl rollout restart deployment anihpj-location-cache

# Watch graceful termination
kubectl get events --field-selector involvedObject.name=anihpj-location-cache --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
deployment.apps/anihpj-location-cache created deployment.apps/anihpj-location-cache restarted LAST SEEN TYPE REASON MESSAGE 2m Normal Killing Stopping container location-cache 1m Normal Started Started container location-cache
Example 5: JobPost Workers Coordinated Shutdown

This example coordinates JobPost worker shutdown with job completion.

# JobPost workers with coordinated shutdown
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: StatefulSet
metadata:
  name: anihpj-jobpost-workers
  labels:
    app: anihpj
    model: jobpost
spec:
  serviceName: jobpost-workers
  replicas: 5
  template:
    spec:
      terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 120  # 2 minutes for job completion
      containers:
      - name: jobpost-worker
        image: anihpj/jobpost-worker:v1
        lifecycle:
          preStop:
            exec:
              command:
              - python
              - -c
              - |
                import os, time, signal, subprocess
                print("Initiating graceful shutdown...")
                
                # Signal Celery to stop accepting new jobs
                subprocess.run(['pkill', '-USR1', 'celery'])
                
                # Wait for current jobs to complete (max 100 seconds)
                for i in range(100):
                    result = subprocess.run(['celery', 'inspect', 'active'], 
                                          capture_output=True, text=True)
                    if 'empty' in result.stdout:
                        print(f"All jobs completed after {i} seconds")
                        break
                    time.sleep(1)
                
                print("Graceful shutdown complete")
        env:
        - name: CELERY_GRACEFUL_SHUTDOWN
          value: "true"
        - name: CELERY_TASK_SOFT_TIME_LIMIT
          value: "90"  # Shorter than grace period
EOF

# Scale down with grace period monitoring
kubectl scale statefulset anihpj-jobpost-workers --replicas=3

# Monitor workers shutdown
kubectl get pods -l model=jobpost -o wide -w
statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers created statefulset.apps/anihpj-jobpost-workers scaled NAME READY STATUS NODE anihpj-jobpost-workers-0 1/1 Running worker-1 anihpj-jobpost-workers-1 1/1 Running worker-2 anihpj-jobpost-workers-2 1/1 Running worker-3 anihpj-jobpost-workers-3 1/1 Terminating worker-1 anihpj-jobpost-workers-4 1/1 Terminating worker-2
